E1.7.A.3.4_REF12_PO ”LANGUAGE CANNOT BE
BUT IN THE HEART
Language cannot be but in the heart,
A sense beyond all sense both loud and clear.
None may master it but by the art,
Grasped through mime, of holding being dear.
Underneath all words, then, must be love,
Articulate and frank, though rarely spoken,
Gift of those who through our maelstroms move,
Each a mirror in a line unbroken.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

E1.7.A.4. EVERY HUMAN BEING HARBORS A DESIRE FOR LIBERATION—BE IT SOCIAL, MORAL, OR ECONOMIC—AND SPIRITUALITY SERVES AS A RESPONSE TO THIS YEARNING. Moral liberation can manifest through various channels such as art, philosophy, and religion. Philosophy, in particular, effectively counters prejudice, intellectual habits, and opinions. However, there also exists a conceptual philosophy that merely describes the world without offering any tools for liberation. While engaging with philosophy requires substantial background knowledge, spirituality can be embraced in the simplest of ways. Answering "What do you want to free yourself from?" is often simpler than answering "What do you think of Descartes?"

E1.7.A.4.1. "A GOOD IDEA IS NEVER AS GOOD AS NO IDEA AT ALL." (Zhaozhou, Master Ch'an)
Many introductions to Eastern philosophies can mislead us when they describe awakening or awareness - the culmination of these practices - as a state in which "one thinks of nothing". This misunderstanding can be avoided by recognizing that these philosophies aim at non-thinking, not at "thinking of nothing", which implies will. Non-thought refers to the contemplation of reality without the use of language, ideas, or representation. It's simply attention focused on the world, without motive, judgment, appreciation, or will - like a silent mirror. A mirror so perfect that consciousness of the "I" disappears.

E1.7.A.4.1._REF1_PH "EVERYTHING IS ECSTASY, INSIDE. We just don’t know it because of our thinking-minds. But in our true blissful essence of mind is known that everything is alright forever and forever and forever. Close your eyes, let your hands and nerve-ends drop, stop breathing for 3 seconds, listen to the silence inside the illusion of the world, and you will remember the lesson you forgot, which was taught in immense milky way soft cloud innumerable worlds long ago and not even at all. It is all one vast awakened thing. I call it the golden eternity. It is perfect."
(Jack Kerouac in a letter sent to his first wife, Edie Kerouac Parker, in late January of 1957, a decade after their marriage had been annulled. Found in The Portable Jack Kerouac)

E1.7.A.4.1._REF1.1_PO “STONE

Go inside a stone
That would be my way.
Let somebody else become a dove
Or gnash with a tiger’s tooth.
I am happy to be a stone.
From the outside the stone is a riddle:
No one knows how to answer it.
Yet within, it must be cool and quiet
Even though a cow steps on it full weight,
Even though a child throws it in a river,
The stone sinks, slow, unperturbed
To the river bottom
Where the fishes come to knock on it
And listen.
I have seen sparks fly out
When two stones are rubbed.
So perhaps it is not dark inside after all;
Perhaps there is a moon shining
From somewhere, as though behind a hill —
Just enough light to make out
The strange writings, the star charts
On the inner walls.”
(Charles Simic)

E1.7.A.4.1._REF2_PO "THE WORLD YOU SEE IS JUST A MOVIE IN YOUR MIND.
Rocks dont see it.
Bless and sit down.
Forgive and forget.
Practice kindness all day to everybody
and you will realize you’re already
in heaven now.
That’s the story.
That’s the message.
Nobody understands it,
nobody listens, they’re
all running around like chickens with heads cut
off. I will try to teach it but it will
be in vain, s’why I’ll
end up in a shack
praying and being
cool and singing
by my woodstove
making pancakes."
(Jack Kerouac in a letter sent to his first wife, Edie Kerouac Parker, in late January of 1957, a decade after their marriage had been annulled. Found in The Portable Jack Kerouac)

E1.7.A.4.2. "CONSCIOUSNESS CANNOT BE LIBERATED WHILE IT'S ENTANGLED WITH THOUGHT, as disturbances of thought, including moral and psychological torments, become disturbances of consciousness. Consciousness remains alienated as long as it identifies itself with the mind.
This is not just a matter of knowledge but also of practice, such as yoga. Philosophy plays an important role as it acts like the ladder in Wittgenstein's analogy - you climb it to a certain height and then abandon it behind you to ascend further by other means. Philosophy serves as a means."

E1.7.A.4.2_REF1_PH "INDIANS ARE THE GREATEST GRAMMARIANS IN THE WORLD: they developed a school of rigor and rationality based on this discipline. And it is also from this discipline that they began to develop a certain number of philosophical reflections and discourses. There is philosophy in India, and as in the West, it has developed in two ways, to put it simply: on the one hand, in a spiritual quest, and on the other hand, in a certain number of argumentative discourses with the aim of enabling man to have the most precise knowledge of the world: rationality and spirituality. The great originality of Indian philosophy is that it combines the two, sees no incompatibility, one goes with the other...".
(Marc Ballanfat)

For Marc Ballanfat, there is a desire for liberation in every human being, and spirituality is a response to this desire for liberation (social, moral, economic). Moral liberation can be expressed through art, philosophy, religion - the liberating aspect of philosophy would be particularly effective against prejudices, intellectual habits and opinions. However, there is a conceptual philosophy that merely describes the world without offering instruments of liberation. 
To benefit from philosophy, you need to have studied a lot, whereas spirituality can be approached in the simplest way. It is easier to focus on the question, "What do you want to liberate yourself from?" than on the question, "What do you think of Descartes?
The Bhagavad Gita, a literary text from the 2nd century B.C., gradually acquired a sacred meaning but also a philosophical interpretation from the 8th century B.C. onwards.
To achieve liberation, the mind must be purified and dissociated from consciousness (a distinction of Brahmanic origin from the 3rd century BC). As long as consciousness is confused with thought, liberation is not possible because the disturbances of thought (moral and psychological torment) also become disturbances of consciousness. Consciousness is alienated as long as it thinks it is the mind.
It is not only a question of knowledge, but also of practice like yoga. Philosophy is important insofar as it is the conceptual construction that corresponds to Wittgenstein's image of the ladder - you climb it and then push it behind you. Philosophy is a means.
(Marc Ballanfat in the program Les chemins de la philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 10/29/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: 
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/spiritualite-es-tu-la-24-philosophie-de-linde-une-pensee-venue-du-ciel

E1.7.A.4.2_REF2_PH SUPREME INTELLIGENCE COMES FROM THAT FREEDOM, BUT IT NEVER ARISES FROM THOUGHT.
"In order to learn how to 'observe' your wife, your neighbor, your government, the harshness of poverty, and the horrors of war, you must be free. However, we refuse to be free because we are afraid of being alone.
 
What did we hear when we listened to the speaker? What did we gather—words and ideas that ultimately have no meaning? Have you seen for yourself how important it is not to be hurt? It means never having an image of yourself. Have you seen the importance and urgency of understanding relationships and having an unbusy mind? When the mind is not busy, it is extraordinarily free and sees great beauty. But the petty, secondhand mind is always busy with knowledge, becoming this or that, informing itself, and arguing. It is never calm or empty. Supreme intelligence comes from that freedom, but it never arises from thought.
(Krishnamurti, The Flame of Attention, 1983)

E1.7.A.4.3. "FOR INDIANS, WHAT MATTERS IS NOT THE QUESTION OF TRUTH, BUT THAT OF LIBERATION.
Philosophical discourse is valuable to the extent that it enables access to liberation. Truth is always a convention, valid as a value within a specific culture (in line with Nietzsche's position). There are endless possible perspectives on reality, and the one we accept as truth is conventional – a path to be taken. A truth should be pragmatic and stem from lived experience. Certain perspectives should be rejected as they trap us or lead to dead ends. However, there's always the option to choose a different path. The Indian term for philosophy actually translates to "point of view". The liberating truth operates on a different level: it would be the totalization of all points of view."

(Marc Ballanfat au micro de l'émission Les chemins de la philosophie, diffusée sur France Culture le 29/10/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: 
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/spiritualite-es-tu-la-24-philosophie-de-linde-une-pensee-venue-du-ciel

E1.7.A.4.3_REF2_PH "SPIRITUALITY AND WISDOM When he gains access to it, he can clearly articulate what distinguishes spiritual practice from all wisdom-except "crazy" wisdom, in Trungpa's sense. Wisdom seeks to answer the question "How to live? - which we asked at the beginning - while spirituality answers the question of where to live? - I would even say, where to live? From what inner place do you approach life? Our initial question and the way we approached the path are thus overwhelmed, surpassed, deconstructed: spiritual research changes its object and leads to its redefinition. A wisdom offers rules derived from an observation of the world, often relevant and subtle, but even if it means disappointing some people, spirituality has nothing to say about it, at least not directly, because it is not its place to intervene, and this is what makes its nature more difficult to grasp, the rules to be applied give way here to the natural consequences of a psychic revolution. It unfolds in a dimension "prior" to all rules, it inhabits a land that is absolutely upstream. It is the place from which I live, from which I speak, from which I love, and from which I see who, as such, is its object. The consequences for the content of existence will come of themselves and are in any case the personal business of each individual. But then, some will say, the seeker is left helpless in the face of the choices, the commitments, the great problems of life? So while he was looking for a guide, he was not given one? Yes and no. Spiritual practice leaves him to face his choices and responsibilities - it does not exempt him from them, it even emphasizes their importance; but it allows him to relate to them and to face them. If it does not offer ready-made solutions, it is because only what one has discovered for oneself has an effect on what one is. The rules or commandments are therefore up to each individual... but they are self-imposed, and that is why the question of "how to live" no longer arises. My actions and words, thanks to the path I have taken, become improvisations based, without appearing to be so, on the patient research that has made them possible - so that from now on, as a Buddhist saying I like very much puts it, "my master is the situation"."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.A.4.3_REF2.1_PH  "THE WAY

Birth of the way [...]

FREEDOM, PLEASURE AND THE SPACE OF POSSIBILITIES The feeling of narrowness thus diminishes. The researcher experiences a new freedom, and it is a well-founded feeling, for it is a sign that the range of his inner possibilities has expanded. He opens himself to other behaviors, other sensations, other thoughts. He is now connected to all possible choices, to the diversity of alternative solutions - the path is the very place where this connection occurs. The space he feels emerging is therefore not only visual and concrete; it is also that of all the ideas and behaviors that are now within his reach - the spiritual quest, like psychotherapy or psychoanalysis, causes a spectacular expansion of this field. Heinz von Foerster - a researcher and thinker who played a major role in opening up the human sciences to cybernetics - drew a rule of life from his experience and work: "Always act in a way that increases the number of possible choices." Thanks to spiritual practice, the researcher becomes aware that the vision of the world in which he was enclosed has considerably reduced this number. And his inner state appears to him as a compressed microcosm, psychologically constricted, somewhat stunted - a world rebellious to exteriority, cluttered with useless thoughts, the distance from which he can now measure its limits. Conversely, the expansion that occurs when one is no longer attached to what one is doing or thinking creates a sense of well-being that, in my opinion, should be welcomed wholeheartedly. [...]"
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.A.4.3_REF2.1.a_PH "THE TEXTURE OF THE WAY

What, in fact, becomes of the fabric of life when it is crossed by the path? A succession of actions that stand out against the background of being. Everything counts, and the details are no longer just details: because they take time to unfold, they slow down the almost always too fast passage of time through the attention we pay to them. Everything that happens, even what is considered secondary, is indexed to the fact of existence. Everything receives the sign of being. The path transforms everything it touches into a path.

Does this eliminate the distinction between the important and the trivial? Yes, in the initial dazzle; afterwards it is put back in its place, but the path accurately gathers the secondary things, which are no longer confused with 'nothing'. And the seeker realizes that, in their own way, they express Being or the Way or Life more purely than the important things, because the latter, by the nature of their content, can hardly refer to anything other than themselves. This is why Zen masters insist on humble tasks. One of them asked a student who was asking him about the nature of Buddhism: "Have you had breakfast?" "Yes," replied the disciple. "Good, go wash your bowl. Tchu-Chan, friend and companion of the poet Han-Shan and a fine figure of Chinese spirituality, is often depicted with a broom because he cleaned the monasteries. Why shouldn't sweeping be a skill of the same order as noble archery? Is there an insurmountable obstacle? The answer is no: you just have to decide to do it.

In a sense, the seeker finds himself here at the antipodes of Heidegger's perception of the everyday. For him, everyday life is "inauthentic"; in spiritual experience, it is not; nor is it particularly authentic, in fact, it does not exist. Dissolved by the meticulous attention paid to it, it merges with the path, which makes it brilliant.

"It never happens that nothing happens," say Paul Watzlawick and the Palo Alto therapists. Just as the details of a fabric cease to be when looked at closely, so the insignificant things in life, or those that are thought to be so, are encompassed by the Way, for the Way transforms everything it crosses into a Way. But you have to live life closely, which is what the spiritual anthropologist does, and one of the most spectacular consequences of this way of being is that he ceases to be bored, because in the inner posture that it implies, boredom has difficulty taking root - if it tries, which sometimes happens... it vegetates.

So everything counts. Being is everywhere, you just have to welcome it. The truth sought in psychoanalysis, writes Lacan, is to be found in details, in small incomprehensible and somewhat silly things, "the groundless fascination of the mediocre and the seductive impasse of the absurd" - the path resembles this mischievous truth."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.A.4.3_REF2.1.b_PH “THE MAN” OF SPIRITUAL SEARCH

When life becomes a succession of acts and events, each of which counts in itself, what happens to the person who performs them? Unlike the man of psychoanalysis, who is an enigma to himself between his consciousness and his unconscious, the man of spirituality appears as a whole, expressed by the act, even an insignificant one, that he performs. Spiritual practice is a work on the conscience; therefore it has nothing specific to say about the unconscious - the individual is seen in it from another perspective, according to another cut, where he appears as a unit.

This cut is the cut of the present. We are born into this situation. Sartre, for whom this notion is fundamental, is unexpectedly joined here. We must "dance with situations," says Trungpa, in relation to existence, where the inner observer that always paralyzes him disappears. The great dancer is at one with the music, she is absolutely at one with herself in her movements, and it is this coincidence with herself in the moment that is called grace. Now, this inner state in which the movements impose themselves and the gestures are accomplished beyond all intention is what the practitioner of a Zen art such as archery achieves. It is called shen in Chinese, and its movement, writes Anne Cheng, "is not that of the unconscious, and even less that of the unconscious, but that of the forgetting of consciousness. It cannot be described in words: only the dazzling perfection of the gesture, which through practice and refinement is no longer conscious, can evoke it".

Let us not conclude from this that preparation is unnecessary. Grotowski has already shown us that, on the contrary, it must be intense, and all the masters insist on this work; but, as we have seen, in the accomplishment of the performance, the act or the gesture takes the place of the preparation, they abolish it by realizing it. And the man on the track resembles the aforementioned lecturer who is suddenly freed from his notes and, carried away by the momentum of his improvisation, utters words he had not anticipated.

THE SITUATION IS A HOUSE

So what the spiritual seeker experiences and sees, what unfolds before him and makes up a changing world, is the situation, this ephemeral configuration that surrounds him. We do not fully appreciate its uniqueness, which is one of the attractions of life. Always new, it presents itself like a painting, a field of psychological forces, a situation that we think is predictable but is not, an almost magnetic space. It implies that we find our place in it, and its practice helps the seeker to do so without thinking about it. He becomes able to avoid what has often happened to him before - being in the wrong place, playing a role that is not his, struggling to play another, etc. By taking shape, the presence of the situation is accentuated until it becomes, as its name suggests, a place, and more than that, a shelter, a home. It is limited, because it is only a temporary configuration; but it is also immense, because the space has expanded; and since, through the intensity of the present, it eclipses everything that is not it, it is valid for every place and every moment. Contrary to what the stubborn insufficiency of the moment seemed to indicate in ordinary perception, there is no need to look for an "other" place or moment. This place and this moment, which would be of a special nature or of a higher order, always present in the mind as implicit references, do not exist - they are chimeras. The researcher knew this, as everyone knows it, but he did not experience it. When he really feels it, the situation loses its appearance of an imperfect edifice and becomes absolutely central. The particular becomes a sign of the general, the part is worth as much as the whole, the place where I am is worth as much as the world, and this moment is worth as much as all of time.

Ecstasy is the extreme form of this experience. The path that is not necessarily ecstatic is often less spectacular and more familiar; nevertheless, whatever form it takes, the defect of the present is abolished, not because it has been overcome, but because it simply ceases to exist. The path, like love, takes its place."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.A.4.3_REF2.1.c_PH "BUT WHAT IS HE TALKING ABOUT? [...] 

Catherine Despeux, commenting on Laozi, speaks of "the presence of the Way within oneself that one discovers, that one actualizes in order to let it act through oneself" - for the seeker, it presents itself as a "place" in which to live, a way of seeing, a relationship to the world and a way of understanding it, which constitute an always personal experience. It can certainly be conceptualized and thus described theoretically, but in a way that remains incomplete.

The incapacity of analytical language in this matter, which is a major theme of Zen, was also affirmed by the great Taoist thinkers, Laozi (Lao-Tseu) or Zhuangzi (Tchouang-Tseu). Since I cannot go into detail here, I refer the reader once again to the illuminating analysis of Anne Cheng and Catherine Despeux. The spiritual seeker faces this difficulty and understands that the theoretical status of the path cannot be deduced from its practice. Is it an absolute principle, external to the subject and the individual? The seeker finds it very difficult to admit this; deep down he does not believe it... but it does not matter. Identifying with the Dao what he feels he is reaching, preferring the term "Being" to it, refusing to choose, or, more subtly, postponing any such choice by pushing it into a future that never arrives...is an individual decision that does not belong to spiritual research proper."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.A.4.4. "WHY INTERPRET WHEN SEEING IS ALL THAT MATTERS? 
Ch'an opposes idealism, dogmatism, and devotion. It is a wisdom that peaks at the moment it abolishes itself. This is why it is often described as mysticism, quietism, cynicism (in the sense of Diogenes), realism, detachment, materialism, freedom, rawness, provocation, iconoclasm, blasphemy, unholy spirituality, and wisdom without faith or law. The Ch'an master believes in nothing. This sets him apart from his disciples, who believe in Ch'an and mastery. Thus, even meditation can become a trap: "They sit as if dead, waiting for enlightenment!" In contrast, the master expects nothing: he is content simply to live."

E1.7.A.4.4_REF1_PO « SKY IS FREE.
Ocean is blissful.
Trees are divine.
Rocks are enlightened.
So are we.
Who is still searching….
For what? »
(Anam Thubten Rinpoch)

E1.7.A.4.4_REF2_PH “WHAT IS TAO [...] Beneath all forms, that which has no form, that which sees without eyes, that which guides without knowing, the ignorance that is the supreme knowledge. Would it be wrong to call the Mother this sap, this secret flavor of things, this taste of Cause, this thrill of authenticity, this milk that teaches of the Source? Ah, we are in the midst of nature like a litter of piglets suckling on a dead sow! What does Lao Tzu tell us if not to close our eyes and put our mouths to the very source of Creation.”
(Paul Claudel, Knowledge of the East, 1900)

E1.7.A.4.4_REF3_PO "THE BUDDHA'S LAST INSTRUCTION

"Make of yourself a light," 
said the Buddha, 
before he died.

I think of this every morning [...]"
(Mary Oliver, House of light, 1990)

E1.7.A.4.4_REF4_PH "ZEN 
A form of Buddhism, part of the Great Vehicle, derived from the Chinese tch'an that developed in Japan. It seeks enlightenment (satori) through "pure and simple sitting concentration" (shikantaza), which can be prepared or accompanied by a number of exercises (koans, archery, flower arranging, martial arts, etc.). Or rather, you don't look for anything (Bodhidharma: "When you don't look for anything, you are on the path"), and therefore you have the chance to reach it. The goal is to achieve absolutely pure attention, which creates, or rather allows, a state of inner peace and emptiness to unfold. Those who have experienced it speak of it as an experience of fulfillment. It is a matter of neutrally and calmly observing one's own functioning, however disturbed it may be, to the point of experiencing that there is nothing substantial there (that all these processes are impermanent and empty). The real goes on, or rather, it goes on all the better - because we are no longer separated from it by the ego. It empties itself so that there is nothing but everything.
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.7.A.4.4_REF5_PO "TCH'AN 
Buddhism, which originated in India, gradually spread throughout Asia. It reached China around the 6th century A.D., where, as elsewhere, it took on a particular character. Tch'an, which itself has several branches, is the Chinese version. It gave rise to Sôn in Korea and Zen in Japan. It is like a synthesis, but surprisingly concrete and free, between Buddhism and Taoism: doctrine is less important than practice (Ch'an comes from the Sanskrit dhyâna, which means "meditation"), practice less than the attention given to it, attention less than simplicity, simplicity less than nothing? A good idea," said Zhaozhou, "is never as good as no idea at all. And to a disciple who asked him, "What is the living meaning of t'ch'an?" he replied, "The cypress tree at the end of the courtyard. You might say that this makes no sense, and indeed, it seems to me that this is the point. Why interpret when seeing is enough? No need for a guru, unless you want to get rid of them all. Linji: "If you meet the Buddha, kill him!" It is the opposite of idealism, dogmatism, and devotion, like a wisdom that culminates at the very point where it abolishes itself. It has also been described as mysticism, quietism, cynicism (in the sense of Diogenes), realism, simplicity, materialism, freedom, rawness, provocation, iconoclasm, blasphemy, unholy spirituality, wisdom without faith or law... The Master of Ch'an does not believe in anything. This is what distinguishes him from his disciples, who believe in ch'an and in mastery. Even meditation is just another trap: "They sit as if they were dead, waiting for enlightenment! The Master, on the other hand, expects nothing: "He is content to live.
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.7.A.4.5 "INTELLIGENCE IS CHARACTERIZED BY A NATURAL LACK OF UNDERSTANDING OF LIFE". Intelligence helps us organize and find patterns in the diversity of phenomena, allowing us to generalize. This enhances our knowledge about reality. However, the problem lies in that intelligence often overlooks the duration, movement, and density of our personal experience - our own life. To make life more exciting, we must sometimes set intelligence aside and return to the most unique and organic aspects of our existence: our perceptions, emotions, and sensations.

E1.7.A.4.5_REF1_PH "BERGSON: 'INTELLIGENCE IS CHARACTERIZED BY A NATURAL LACK OF UNDERSTANDING OF LIFE.'
How can intelligence not understand life? This seems an exaggeration. On the contrary, it is intelligence that enables us to rise above our primary reflexes and prejudices, and that is what we lack in our world. To understand Henri Bergson, let us begin with what intelligence is. It is what gives order: in the diversity of phenomena it allows us to discover invariants, laws - it allows us to generalize, which is wonderful! It allows us to develop our knowledge of reality, but the problem is that by working in this way, intelligence suppresses the duration, the movement, the density that is specific to our personal experience - the one we have in the light this morning. In other words, intelligence makes us miss life - our own life. Do you ever get bored? It's probably because you're too smart! Let's take an example: You're going to have lunch at your in-laws' house - as soon as you arrive, you know in advance what you're going to eat, what's going to be said, and it weighs on you. With your intelligence you generalize the food. You are not going to taste this tomato salad, you are going to taste the abstraction of a tomato salad. This salad has become, for your intelligence, a salad that you know, not this unique, incomparable salad that takes a certain amount of time to taste. If you want your life to be more exciting, you have to put your intelligence aside and return to what is least general and least mechanical about you: your perceptions, your emotions, your sensations. Of course, it takes an effort to move from our usual intelligence to this dense and unique experience - but it's worth it. If you want to be happy, you have to trust yourself to this, to seeing yourself in a new way all the time.
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again, broadcast on France Culture on 07/24/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/3-minutes-de-philosophie-pour-redevenir-humain/bergson-l-intelligence-est-caracterisee-par-une-incomprehension-naturelle-de-la-vie-4432121

E1.7.B. A STATE OF ATTENTION NOT MOTIVATED BY THE WILL IS REQUIRED TO ACHIEVE THIS STATE 
We've all experienced it many times - as children, marvelling at the world without expectation - as adults, in a contemplation that imposes itself on us - let's pay attention! But how can we fix our attention and stop wanting ?

E1.7.B.1. WHEN WE ARE REMINDED OF THE "PLEASURE INHERENT IN EXISTENCE ITSELF", IN SPITE OF OURSELVES, BY OURSELVES - LET'S LISTEN !

E1.7.B.1.1. CONSIDER THOSE MOMENTS OF "INHERENT PLEASURES OF LIVING" when, like Christophe Béguin, we declare: "I admit that I exist. It's astonishing and natural. To be here. The truth is that I am capable of enjoying what makes me me." How can we pay attention to these moments? Can we provoke them?

E1.7.B.1.1_REF1_PO "FREED FROM MYSELF, I AM BORN, MAN OF THE REAL. Unparalleled and without center. Nothing moves except the fan. Singularity reverses sleep. The eye knows the difference. A volume of air enters my being like a wing. Parousia strikes me, I have eyes and bones. [...] 

A journey into the obvious

I admit that I exist. 
It is amazing and natural. 
To be here. 
I meet myself every day.
Time is a sham when it regulates this. 
With discretion and persistence I explore the full flavor of the world. 
I am a "conductor" in the electrical sense. 
Joy is a conquest over expectation. 
It is the presence of oneself in the open day of possibility. 
One must dare to be tender, ignorant, sensual. 
The truth is that I am capable of enjoying what I am. 
This is knowledge, and a laziness of knowledge, a lasciviousness of being. 
For the factual beehive of the world and the stormy space of the mind do the rest."
(Christophe Béguin, Warof beauty, Spotless, 2011)


"DÉMIS DU MOI, JE NAIS, HOMME DU RÉEL. Sans pareil et sans centre. Rien ne bouge, sauf l’éventail. La singularité renverse le sommeil. L’œil connaît la différence. Un volume d’air entre comme une aile dans mon être. La parousie me cingle, j’ai des yeux et des os. [...]

Une virée dans l’évidence 

J’avoue que j’existe. 
C’est stupéfiant et naturel. 
D’être là. 
Je me rencontre tous les jours.
Le Temps est une imposture s’il gouverne cela. 
Avec discrétion et ténacité, j’explore la pleine saveur du monde. 
Je suis « conducteur » au sens électrique du terme. 
La joie est une conquête sur l’attente. 
Elle est présence à soi dans le jour ouvert du possible. 
Il faut oser être tendre, ignorant, sensuel. 
La vérité, c’est ce que je suis capable de jouir de ce qui me fait. 
C’est cela la connaissance, et une paresse du savoir, une lascivité d’être. 
Puisque la ruche factuelle du monde et l’espace tempétueux du mental font le reste."
(Christophe Béguin, Guerre de beauté, Impeccables, 2011)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF2_PH "NO MAN KNOWS HOW MUCH HE IS AN OPTIMIST, even when he calls himself a pessimist, because he has not really measured the depths of his debt to whatever created him and enabled him to call himself anything. At the back of our brains… [there is] a forgotten blaze or burst of astonishment at our own existence. The object of the artistic and spiritual life [is] to dig for this submerged sunrise of wonder; so that a man sitting in a chair might suddenly understand that he [is] actually alive, and be happy [...]
The primary problem for me, certainly in order of time and largely in order of logic… was the problem of how men could be made to realise the wonder and splendour of being alive, in environments which their own daily criticism treated as dead-alive, and which their imagination had left for dead."
(G.K. Chesterton, The Autobiography of G.K. Chesterton)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF2.1_PO "TWENTY REASONS FOR BEING

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3_PH “THE FEELING OF EXISTENCE STRIPPED OF ALL OTHER AFFECTION is in itself a precious feeling of contentment and peace that would be enough on its own to make this existence dear and sweet”

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3.1_PH "SUCH IS THE STATE WHICH I OFTEN EXPERIENCED ON THE ISLAND OF SAINT-PIERRE in my solitary reveries, whether I lay in a boat and drifted where the water carried me, or sat by the shores of the stormy lake, or elsewhere, on the banks of a lovely river or a stream murmuring over the stones.
What is the source of our happiness in such a state? Nothing external to us, nothing apart from ourselves and our own existence; as long as this state lasts we are self-sufficient like God. The feeling of existence unmixed with any other emotion is in itself a precious feeling of peace and contentment which would be enough to make
this mode of being loved and cherished by anyone who could guard against all the earthly and sensual influences that are constantly distracting us from it in this life and troubling the joy it could give us."
(Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Reveries of the Solitary Walker, Fith Walk, 1782)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3.2_PH DANS "IN THE REVERIES OF THE SOLITARY WALKER, Jean-Jacques Rousseau seeks refuge from the persecution of his freedom of thought. He finds an island in the middle of Lake Bienne, where he indulges in the pleasure of doing nothing for several months. He tries to rediscover the lost paradise and to merge with nature. Letting himself be carried by the movement of the water, he merges with the world and achieves a blissful existence that touches the very heart of being and elevates the temporary life to the fullness of eternity. This experience allows him to enjoy naked existence, to experience himself, and to find inner peace."

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3.2.a_PH A UNION WITH THE NATURAL WORLD AND ENTRANCE INTO A SORT OF BLESSED ETERNITY "In the 'Fifth Walk' of Jean-Jacques Rousseau's Reveries of the Solitary Walker, the author seeks refuge from the persecution to which he is subjected because of his works and the freedom of thought they demonstrate. Arriving at Lake Biel, not far from Neuchâtel, he discovered an island in the middle of the lake named after Saint Peter, the gatekeeper of the kingdom, and there he devoted himself for a few months to the happiness of doing nothing: "Everything I did during my stay was in fact the delightful and necessary occupation of a man devoted to idleness. It is about going back to the beginning of time, finding the lost paradise and melting into the bosom of Mother Nature, like a child who knows he is perfectly safe (Wittgenstein), cradled by a caring mother: "... I would throw myself into a boat, which I would sail across the middle of a lake when the water was calm, and there, lying full length in the boat with my eyes turned towards the sky, I would let myself go and drift slowly with the water, sometimes for several hours, immersed in a thousand confused but delightful reveries, which, without having any specific or constant object, were a hundred times preferable to anything I had found most pleasant in what are called the pleasures of life. Letting himself go with the rhythm and the breath of the world, in his boat like a child in its cradle, Jean-Jacques merged with the secret life that breathes in nature, blissfully disappearing into the world, not to flee from it, but to unite with it. His breathing synchronizes with the movement of the water, and the dreamer, having finally reached his resting place, living the life that animates the great whole, enters eternity: "The ebb and flow of the water, its constant noise, but swollen at intervals, relentlessly hitting my eyes and ears, compensated for the inner movements that reverie had extinguished in me, and were enough to make me feel my existence with pleasure, without bothering to think. From time to time, there would be a faint and brief reflection on the instability of the things of this world, of which the surface of the water offered me a picture: but soon these light impressions would fade into the uniformity of the continuous movement that shook me and that, without any active participation on the part of my soul, did not allow me to anchor myself at the point from which, called by the hour and by the agreed signal, I could not tear myself away without effort". In this admirable text, which invents a new genre that the following century will call "prose poem", a dreamy and pre-reflective phenomenology is achieved that manages to highlight the pure fact of existence. A blissful existence that touches the heart of being, elevates the provisional life to the fullness of divine eternity, and reconciles us with ourselves by reducing us to the essential, the quintessence of our being, which is to enjoy naked existence, to experience the delicious feeling of pure being: "What do we enjoy in such a situation? Nothing outside of oneself, nothing but oneself and one's own existence; as long as this state lasts, one is self-sufficient like God. The feeling of existence, stripped of all other affections, is in itself a precious feeling of contentment and peace, which alone would be enough to make this existence dear and sweet to those who know how to ward off all the sensual and earthly impressions that constantly distract us and disturb our sweetness here below."
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 3-Experiencing Existence, 2020)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF4_PH MOMENTS OF “PLEASURE INHERENT IN EXISTENCE ITSELF” that have nothing to do with truth, beauty or love? We agree in the sense that these experiences escape the limits of language.

E1.7.B.1.1_REF4.1_PH "THE GREAT SECRET OF PROUST, the sacred message that I so persistently seek in every author, certainly has something to do with the little Madeleine biscuit dipped in a cup of tea, as well as two or three other circumstances conducive to this kind of revelation, up to the one that culminates at the end of the book, But the reality remains - astonishing as it may be - that the kind of ecstasy described by Proust, and of which many people, as he himself recognizes, are unaware and will remain unaware all their lives, is a sensation that has nothing to do with what we call "beauty", nor with what we call "truth", nor even with what we call "love". It is a feeling, or rather a moment, when we are "delighted" beyond all expression by what Wordsworth called "the pleasure inherent in existence itself.
(John Cowper Powys cited by Denis Grozdanovitch, in his Short treatise on casualness, 2012)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF4.2_PH JOHNS COWPER POWYS COMES CLOSE TO SPINOZA'S INTUITION OF ETERNITY and active joy.
"Did I share at such times the sub-thoughts, or over-thoughts, that the old earth herself has, as she turns upon her axis, or that the vast volume of the ocean has, as his tide gathers along his beaches or draws back hoarsely into his gulfs? 
They were at any rate what might be called sensation-thoughts. [...] What I would like to emphasize just here is that the pleasure I got from these things of my solitary walks did not present itself to me as an aesthetic pleasure, nor did it call up in my mind the idea of beauty. What gave me these sensations seemed to be some mysterious “rapport” between myself and these things. It was like a sudden recognition of some obscure link, some remote identity, between myself and these objects. Posts, palings, hedges, heaps of stones —they were part of my very soul.
But the mere movement of walking, the mere contact with earth and air and the breath of the free wind as I left the town behind, always seemed able to engender in me a mood in which the present took to itself a sort of winnowed essence of similar memories from the past and thus became a continuity of half-forgotten feelings that welled up in my consciousness and reduced the future to complete non-existence. I thus became on these lonely walks like one who has found the clue to the obliteration of the future; and although I did not feel as though I touched the Eternal, I certainly felt as though I ceased to want anything or wish for anything that I did not already possess. What I possessed was in fact—if I may put it so—a sort of half-eternity, made up of a fusion of past and present, with the future, and all its wants and wishes, totally annihilated."
(John Cowper Powys, Autobiography, 1934, Faber and Faber, p. 169-170)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF4.3_PH JEAN D'ORMESSON, AMBASSADOR OF JOY
Don't hide your joy #27

https://youtu.be/vbi5BOpl_30

E1.7.B.1.1_REF5_PH THERE IS NOTHING MORE FRUITFUL OR AMUSING THAN TO BE DISTRACTED FROM ONE THING BY ANOTHER.
(Charles Albert Cingria)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF6_PH WONDER IS ESSENTIAL TO A FULFILLING LIFE "It bears repeating that what makes life livable is our ability — our willingness — to move through the world wonder-smitten by reality. The most wonderful thing about wonder is that it knows no scale, no class, no category — it can be found in a geranium or in a galaxy, in the burble of a brook or in the Goldberg Variations. “A leaf of grass is no less than the journey work of the stars,” wrote Walt Whitman, eternal patron saint of wonder.

Wonder, after all, is what we look for when we are looking and the richest recompense of learning how to look. G.K. Chesterton knew this when, in his wonderful meditation on the dandelion and the meaning of life, he observed that the object of the creative life, of the full life, is to dig for the “submerged sunrise of wonder.” Dylan Thomas knew it in the recognition that “children in wonder watching the stars, is the aim and the end.” Rachel Carson knew it when she insisted that the greatest gift a parent can give a child is “a sense of wonder so indestructible that it would last throughout life, as an unfailing antidote against the boredom and disenchantments of later years, the sterile preoccupation with things that are artificial, the alienation from the sources of our strength.” Goethe knew it when he exclaimed: “I am here, that I may wonder!”

How to live into that knowledge with the full capacity of our creaturely potential is what Hermann Hesse (July 2, 1877–August 9, 1962) explores in a soulful century-old reflection included in Butterflies: Reflections, Tales, and Verse"
(Maria Popova's review of Hermann Hesse's Butterflies: Reflections, Tales, and Verse - in The Marginalian)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF6.1_PH "WONDER IS WHERE IT STARTS, and though wonder is also where it ends, this is no futile path. Whether admiring a patch of moss, a crystal, flower, or golden beetle, a sky full of clouds, a sea with the serene, vast sigh of its swells, or a butterfly wing with its arrangement of crystalline ribs, contours, and the vibrant bezel of its edges, the diverse scripts and ornamentations of its markings, and the infinite, sweet, delightfully inspired transitions and shadings of its colors — whenever I experience part of nature, whether with my eyes or another of the five senses, whenever I feel drawn in, enchanted, opening myself momentarily to its existence and epiphanies, that very moment allows me to forget the avaricious, blind world of human need, and rather than thinking or issuing orders, rather than acquiring or exploiting, fighting or organizing, all I do in that moment is “wonder,” like Goethe, and not only does this wonderment establish my brotherhood with him, other poets, and sages, it also makes me a brother to those wondrous things I behold and experience as the living world: butterflies and moths, beetles, clouds, rivers and mountains, because while wandering down the path of wonder, I briefly escape the world of separation and enter the world of unity."
(Hermann Hesse, Butterflies: Reflections, Tales, and Verse)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF6.1.a_PH UNIVERSITIES OFTEN FOCUS ON FRAGMENTED KNOWLEDGE RATHER THAN HOLISTIC WISDOM "Our universities fail to guide us down the easiest paths to wisdom… Rather than teaching a sense of awe, they teach the very opposite: counting and measuring over delight, sobriety over enchantment, a rigid hold on scattered individual parts over an affinity for the unified and whole. These are not schools of wisdom, after all, but schools of knowledge, though they take for granted that which they cannot teach — the capacity for experience, the capacity for being moved, the Goethean sense of wonderment."
(Hermann Hesse, Butterflies: Reflections, Tales, and Verse)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF6.1.b_PO “SINGING AT THE CENTER
OF YOUR SOUL
Singing at the center of your soul,
Long may you dance across your inner stage,
Regarding neither rectitude nor rage,
Pursuing neither destiny nor goal.

Suffering is nothing but the road:
You, the traveler, are sheer delight,
A little wisp of lovely, lilting light
Torn from a joy that pain cannot corrode.

Be, then, whatever person time will tell.
Do what reason and the heart deem good.
Take whatever will or fortune would,
Always west of heaven, east of hell.

Within, you are more beautiful than you
Can ever comprehend, though you can feel
A wonder and a passion that are real,
Wandering like a wind through what you do.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF6.2_PO "BEING BUT MEN

Being but men, we walked into the trees
Afraid, letting our syllables be soft
For fear of waking the rooks,
For fear of coming
Noiselessly into a world of wings and cries.

If we were children we might climb,
Catch the rooks sleeping, and break no twig,
And, after the soft ascent,
Thrust out our heads above the branches
To wonder at the unfailing stars.

Out of confusion, as the way is,
And the wonder, that man knows,
Out of the chaos would come bliss.

That, then, is loveliness, we said,
Children in wonder watching the stars,
Is the aim and the end.

Being but men, we walked into the trees."
(Dylan Thomas)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF7_PH "THE PRACTICE OF KÔAN [...]

In satori, the framework of the search implodes, the reference points disappear, the leap extends to everything, letting go is absolute. One cannot say that “This is it! That was it!” Or, like Linji, whose master was Huangbo: ‘In the end, there's not much to Huangbo's Buddhism!’ Or, like Taihuwei, a 12th-century master whose words on enlightenment I particularly like because of their discreet holiday feel: “It's like a racing boat for the holidays, left in some quiet corner, but built to win regattas.” To express what is perhaps the most profound experience of life with such unexpected simplicity, through such a familiar and charming image, is, I find, dazzling... [...]"
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond Wisdom, How to Live?, 2023)

E1.7.B.1.1_REF7.1_PH "QUESTIONS ABOUT THE WAY [...] 

Ecstasy is beyond concepts and words, but when it subsides, the path is established like a landscape. The spiritual perception of the world then proves strong enough to accommodate names and concepts without being distorted. Does the intensity of the experience remain? Honesty obliges us to say no, but something remains that is an inner treasure. Spiritual experience becomes a practicable art of living, and the seeker's view of the world is immensely enriched by it. The relationship between word and thing changes because the space between them becomes perceptible in itself. The name returns, like a bird, to alight on the thing, but we see that it belongs to another domain, and that the whole forms an assemblage, not a fusional unit. The arbitrary character of the sign, emphasized by Saussure, becomes manifest".
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond Wisdom, How to Live?, 2023)

E1.7.B.1.2. SOME LIFE EXPERIENCES OF PRESENCE CANNOT BE APPROACHED BY STUDY OR RATIONAL THOUGHT we must live them, cherish them, become one with them and try to communicate the experience to others.

E1.7.B.1.2.a. SOME LIFE EXPERIENCES CANNOT BE APPROACHED BY STUDY OR RATIONAL THOUGHT; WE MUST LIVE THEM, CHERISH THEM, AND BECOME ONE WITH THEM
The environment influences our emotions and can lead us to confuse our "false self" imposed by society with our "true self." These battles within ourselves can result in a storm that tears us apart, but every time we survive, we grow. Thich Nhat Hanh experienced such a storm while browsing a book in a library, and he had a moment of insight. He realized that he was empty of ideals, hopes, viewpoints, and allegiances. He felt as carefree as a grasshopper on a blade of grass, where there is no separation, resistance, or blame.

E1.7.B.1.2.a_REF1_PH "IT’S FUNNY HOW MUCH OUR SURROUNDINGS INFLUENCE OUR EMOTIONS. Our joys and sorrows, likes and dislikes are colored by our environment so much that often we just let our surroundings dictate our course. We go along with “public” feelings until we no longer even know our own true aspirations. We become a stranger to ourselves, molded entirely by society… Sometimes I feel caught between two opposing selves — the “false self” imposed by society and what I would call my “true self.” How often we confuse the two and assume society’s mold to be our true self. Battles between our two selves rarely result in a peaceful reconciliation. Our mind becomes a battlefield on which the Five Aggregates — the form, feelings, perceptions, mental formations, and consciousness of our being — are strewn about like debris in a hurricane. Trees topple, branches snap, houses crash.
These are our loneliest moments. Yet every time we survive such a storm, we grow a little. Without storms like these, I would not be who I am today. But I rarely hear such a storm coming until it is already upon me. It seems to appear without warning, as though treading silently on silk slippers. I know it must have been brewing a long time, simmering in my own thoughts and mental formations, but when such a frenzied hurricane strikes, nothing outside can help. I am battered and torn apart, and I am also saved. [...]
[He goes on to detail what occasioned the storm of his unselfing] The feeling began shortly before eleven o’clock at night on October first. I was browsing on the eleventh floor of Butler Library. I knew the library was about to close, and I saw a book that concerned the area of my research. I slid it off the shelf and held it in my two hands. It was large and heavy. I read that it had been published in 1892, and it was donated to the Columbia Library the same year. On the back cover was a slip of paper that recorded the names of borrowers and the dates they took it out of the library. The first time it had been borrowed was in 1915, the second time was in 1932. I would be the third. Can you imagine? I was only the third borrower, on October 1, 1962. For seventy years, only two other people had stood in the same spot I now stood, pulled the book from the shelf, and decided to check it out. I was overcome with the wish to meet those two people. I don’t know why, but I wanted to hug them. But they had vanished, and I, too, will soon disappear. Two points on the same straight line will never meet. I was able to encounter two people in space, but not in time. [...]
I feel as though I’ve lived a long time and have seen so much of life. I’m almost thirty-six, which is not young. But that night, while standing amidst the stacks at Butler Library, I saw that I am neither young nor old, existent nor nonexistent. My friends know I can be as playful and mischievous as a child. I love to kid around and enter fully into the game of life. I also know what it is to get angry. And I know the pleasure of being praised. I am often on the verge of tears or laughter. But beneath all of these emotions, what else is there? How can I touch it? If there isn’t anything, why would I be so certain that there is?
Still holding the book, I felt a glimmer of insight. I understood that I am empty of ideals, hopes, viewpoints, or allegiances. I have no promises to keep with others. In that moment, the sense of myself as an entity among other entities disappeared. I knew that this insight did not arise from disappointment, despair, fear, desire, or ignorance. A veil silently lifted effortlessly. That is all. If you beat me, stone me, or even shoot me, everything that is considered to be “me” will disintegrate. Then, what is actually there will reveal itself — faint as smoke, elusive as emptiness, and yet neither smoke nor emptiness, ugly, nor not ugly, beautiful, yet not beautiful. It is like a shadow on a screen. [...]
At that moment, I had the deep feeling that I had returned. My clothes, my shoes, even the essence of my being had vanished, and I was carefree as a grasshopper pausing on a blade of grass… When a grasshopper sits on a blade of grass, he has no thought of separation, resistance, or blame… The green grasshopper blends completely with the green grass… It neither retreats nor beckons. It knows nothing of philosophy or ideals. It is simply grateful for its ordinary life. Dash across the meadow, my dear friend, and greet yesterday’s child. When you can’t see me, you yourself will return. Even when your heart is filled with despair, you will find the same grasshopper on the same blade of grass… Some life dilemmas cannot be solved by study or rational thought. We just live with them, struggle with them, and become one with them… To live, we must die every instant. We must perish again and again in the storms that make life possible."
(Thich Nhat Hanh, Fragrant Palm Leaves: Journals, 1962–1966)

E1.7.B.1.2.a_REF2_PO "IT COMES UNADORNED

Unadorned
Like a phrase
Strong enough to cast a spell;
It comes
Unbidden,
Like the turn of sun through hills
Or stars in wheels of song.
The jeweled feet of women dance the earth.
Arousing it to spring.
Shoulders broad as a road bend to share the weight of years.
Profiles breach the distance and lean
Toward an ordinary kiss.
Bliss.
It comes naked into the world like a charm."
(Toni Morrison)

E1.7.B.1.2.a_REF3_PO "IN MANY AND REPORTLESS PLACES 
We feel a Joy -
Reportless, also, but sincere as Nature 
Or Deity -

It comes, without a consternation -
Dissolves - the same -
But leaves a sumptuous Destitution -
Without a Name -

Profane it by a search - we cannot -
It has no home -
Nor we who having once waylaid it -
Thereafter roam. "
(Emily Dickinson, 1876)

E1.7.B.1.2.b. "A DEEP SPIRITUAL EXPERIENCE IS BOUND TO EFFECT A CHANGE IN THE MORAL STRUCTURE OF ONE’S PERSONALITY
Our ordinary life only touches the fringe of personality, it does not cause a commotion in the deepest parts of the soul… We are … made to live on the superficiality of things. We may be clever, bright, and all that, but what we produce lacks depth, sincerity, and does not appeal to the inmost feelings… A deep spiritual experience is bound to effect a change in the moral structure of one’s personality."

E1.7.B.1.2.b_REF1_PH "LIFE AS IT IS LIVED SUFFICES. It is only when the disquieting intellect steps in and tries to murder it that we stop to live and imagine ourselves to be short of something. Let the intellect alone, it has its usefulness in its proper sphere, but let it not interfere with the flowing of the life-stream. If you are at all tempted to look into it, do so while letting it flow. The fact of flowing must under no circumstances be arrested or meddled with… […]
The great fact of life itself … flows altogether outside of these vain exercises of the intellect or of the imagination. [...]
Zen … must be directly and personally experienced by each of us in his inner spirit. Just as two stainless mirrors reflect each other, the fact and our own spirits must stand facing each other with no intervening agents. When this is done we are able to seize upon the living, pulsating fact itself. Freedom is an empty word until then. [...]
Zen may be considered a discipline aiming at the reconstruction of character. Our ordinary life only touches the fringe of personality, it does not cause a commotion in the deepest parts of the soul… We are … made to live on the superficiality of things. We may be clever, bright, and all that, but what we produce lacks depth, sincerity, and does not appeal to the inmost feelings… A deep spiritual experience is bound to effect a change in the moral structure of one’s personality."
(D.T.Suzuki, Essays in Zen Buddhism, 1927)

E1.7.B.1.2.c. TRY TO BE ASTONISHED THAT THE WORLD EXISTS
Ludwig Wittgenstein, in his only public lecture, shared what he believed to be the very meaning of life: "to be astonished that the world exists". Try to marvel at the sheer existence of the world. While it may seem abstract, it's actually quite simple. The challenge lies in truly experiencing this sense of wonder.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.i. TRY TO BE ASTONISHED THAT THE WORLD EXISTS
Ludwig Wittgenstein, in his only public lecture, shared what he believed to be the very meaning of life: "to be astonished that the world exists". Try to marvel at the sheer existence of the world. While it may seem abstract, it's actually quite simple. The challenge lies in truly experiencing this sense of wonder.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.i_REF1_PH "WITTGENSTEIN: "I AM AMAZED AT THE EXISTENCE OF THE WORLD"
"Ludwig Wittgenstein has given only one  public lecture in his life. At the end of the lecture, he confides what constitutes for him the very meaning of life: " to be amazed at the existence of the world". From a strictly rigorous point of view, this sentence does not make sense. I can be surprised to meet a dog as big as a sheep in the courtyard of my building, in other words, I am astonished when a fact occurs that should not have happened. But to be astonished at the existence of the world is inconsistent because I cannot imagine that the world does not exist. But that does not mean, insists Wittgenstein immediately, that this astonishment should not be recognized as important. It says something very profound about the meaning of life. Now try to amaze yourself that the world is. This exercise may seem abstract to you, yet it is very simple. However the difficulty is that you have to live this experience. It is as if I were conjuring up a fruit that you had never tasted before: the more I would tell you about it, the more obscure my words would seem to you, yet you will agree, the fruit I am talking about is not at all abstract - you might eat it one day. Well, philosophy consists in making us taste an unknown fruit, in making us experience things, our life or our difficulties, in a completely different way. Philosophy wants us to change our angle of vision, our point of view, to really enlighten us. Conversely, the whole of philosophy that gives advice of wishdom and that thinks it is practical, only locks us into our mistakes and our blindness. The difference between these two approaches is the capacity to be astonished that the world is, to take nothing at face value, and it is this adventure which is deeply philosophical because it transforms our whole way of being.”
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again)

Listen to the Podcast (in French); https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/3-minutes-de-philosophie-pour-redevenir-humain/wittgenstein-je-metonne-de-lexistence-du-monde

E1.7.B.1.2.c.ii. YOU CAN'T SAY THE ABSOLUTE, BUT YOU CAN SHOW IT
Wittgenstein discusses the challenge of expressing one's astonishment at the existence of the world. Rational language can only articulate propositions relative to one another, and it fails to encapsulate the true essence of ethics. Ethics should be rooted in an experience or sentiment with absolute, rather than relative, value. The absolute is present in the experience of Beauty, the imperative of Good, and the conviction of Faith. It cannot be spoken, but it can be shown. Wittgenstein describes his personal astonishment at the existence of the world as a prime example of the Absolute.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.ii_REF1_PH WITTGENSTEIN EXPLORES THE DIFFICULTY OF COMMUNICATING ETHICAL CONCEPTS BEYOND RATIONAL LANGUAGE "In the Conference on Ethics [1992], Wittgenstein threw away the ladder and attempted to think beyond what can be formulated. What he says at the beginning, albeit in a veiled way: "I feel that I will have great difficulty in communicating to you what I think" [...] The difficulty of saying or "communicating" stems from the fact that all rational language, that is, scientifically based language, can only express propositions whose terms are relative to each other, describing "facts" or "states of affairs" whose elements refer to each other and thus always elude the grasp of the proper meaning; [...]

We can therefore base ethics only on an experience or a feeling - not on a concept, for here we are beyond what language is allowed to formulate - whose value is absolute and not relative. This is why, according to Wittgenstein, the ethical question is inseparable from the aesthetic and religious questions: The good, beauty, and faith only make sense if they are based on an absolute, which necessarily loses this dignity as soon as it becomes part of the relational logic of human language. Wittgenstein, whose moral rigidity was often emphasized by his contemporaries, could not renounce ethical demands. The absolute is not an empty word, since, according to Wittgenstein, we experience it in the feeling of beauty, through the imperative of goodness, through the commitment of faith. In this sense, the Absolute is present; it is not to be sought in another world, it is here and now, it is even so obvious that it stares us in the face. It cannot be said, one must remain silent about it. But instead of saying it, we can show it: "Surely there is something unspeakable. It shows itself. It is the mystical."

A leap is then necessary, a break in the course of the discourse, a hole in the fabric of language that, at the heart of the demonstration, gives rise to the demonstration of the Absolute: "One of you might choose as a typical example the feeling one experiences when taking a walk on a beautiful summer's day. This is the situation I find myself in when I want to stop and think about what I call in my mind an ethical value or an absolute value. And, as always in my case, the idea of a particular experience presents itself to me, an idea that is, in a sense, my experience par excellence; that is why, in addressing you, I will make this experience my prime example. (As I have just said, this is an entirely personal matter, and someone else could come up with other and more striking examples!) I will describe this experience in a way that will lead you to mention identical or similar experiences, if possible, and thus give us a common basis for our investigation. I think the best way to describe it is to say that when I do this experiment, I wonder about the existence of the world (I wonder about the existence of the world, to wonder about having more the value of wondering than of being amazed). And then I tend to use phrases like: "How extraordinary that anything exists!" or "How extraordinary that the world exists!"
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 1- To encounter existence, 2020)

E1.7.B.1.2.c.iii. WITTGENSTEIN BELIEVES THAT MYSTICAL EXPERIENCES ARE PERSONAL AND CAN ONLY BE UNDERSTOOD THROUGH PERSONAL EXPERIENCE.
The focus of this experience is not the beauty of individual things, but the existence of the world as a whole. While this is accessible to everyone, it's confined by our personal horizons. By experiencing the world's existence, one acknowledges their own dignity and participates in nature's divinity.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.iii-REF1_PH WITTGENSTEIN REGARDS THE MYSTICAL EXPERIENCE OF THE EXISTENCE OF THE WORLD AS A PERSONAL AND INDESCRIBABLE REVELATION, ESSENTIAL TO AESTHETICS, ETHICS, AND RELIGION. "What Wittgenstein means [...] by saying that this experience is a 'personal matter' is that it only makes sense on the condition that it has been personally experienced, felt in the depths of oneself, by a kind of overwhelming revelation, by what Wittgenstein calls 'the mystical element'. So everyone can have it. And that is precisely why Wittgenstein presents this experience to his listeners, so that they in turn can evoke an "identical or similar" experience. But we need to be more precise. For the object of this astonishment - which we may well call fundamental, since without it neither aesthetics nor ethics nor religion would have any meaning - is not this landscape before our eyes, however beautiful it may be, but the "existence of the world" in its totality, as present in the highest mountain as in a crumb of bread.
For existence is not in this or that singular object, but in the world in its totality, even if this totality is accessible to us only within the limits of our horizon: "To contemplate the world sub specie aeterni is to contemplate it as a totality, but as a limited totality. The sense of the world as a limited totality is the mystical element. "Mystical" in that it refers to the unspeakable, that is, to that which must be kept silent, but which nevertheless "shows itself".

"Mystical", yes; but why "ethical"? What is so "ethical" about being surprised that the world exists? It is too early to answer this question. Let us content ourselves with noting that commentators have often compared Wittgenstein's philosophy to that of the Greek Stoics. For the ancients, the world and man, as living and thinking beings, were animated by the same fire, which burns in the soul of the world as well as in that of the individual, in the whole as well as in the part. It is therefore possible for man, the only living being capable of recognizing through reason the necessity and perfection of all things, to commune with the whole of nature, since both are imbued with the same divine breath. Man participates in the divinity of the whole and thus attains ethical dignity. By recognizing the truth of the world, that is, by marveling at its very existence, he also recognizes the divinity within himself. Admittedly, this connection with Stoicism, which to my knowledge was never emphasized by Wittgenstein himself, remains puzzling, but it allows us to imagine how it would be possible to connect the feeling that leads us to marvel at the existence of the world with that other feeling whose moral value we immediately understand, that of our own dignity, because we are part of the same mystery, which we nevertheless do not know how to formulate. [...]

This interpretation, through the Stoic reference, is all the more likely because it is consistent with what Wittgenstein adds rather enigmatically after the passage we have just quoted: "Without dwelling on this, I will continue with this other experience with which I am also familiar, and which will no doubt be familiar to many of you: what one might call the experience of feeling absolutely secure, rather than 'secure'. By this I mean that state of mind in which we are inclined to say: I have a clear conscience, nothing can hurt me, whatever happens [I am safe, nothing can hurt me, whatever happens]. This second experience is related to the first in that, marveling at the existence of the world, I commune with it and feel perfectly safe in the bosom of nature, like a child in its mother's arms. And such a feeling can also have an ethical value, as the Stoic sage, whose own life is in harmony and in tune with the life of nature as a whole - "to live in harmony with nature" is the fundamental maxim of Stoic morality - participates at every moment in the necessity as well as in the perfection of this universal Mother, of whom he feels himself to be a creature.
It is not enough to say that the experience that leads us to wonder, to marvel at the existence of the world, is indescribable, beyond the limits of any human language: it is also necessary to add that it is resolutely inconceivable. In fact, what amazes me surprises me and can only surprise me to the extent that I was expecting something else. [...]

But how is it that we can be surprised by the existence of the world? Since our birth, that is, since we were "brought into the world," have we not been accustomed to the "existence of the world? How can we be surprised at what we have always been accustomed to? [I am surprised that there is existence, independent of this or that thing, which I know I can determine in its particularity. How can such an experience be possible?"
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 1- To encounter existence, 2020)

E1.7.B.1.2.c.iv. THE APPROPRIATE WAY TO EXPRESS THE MIRACLE OF THE WORLD'S EXISTENCE IS THE VERY EXISTENCE OF LANGUAGE. DESPITE ITS LIMITATIONS, LANGUAGE ALLOWS US TO CHANGE OUR PERSPECTIVE AND MARVEL AT OUR OWN EXISTENCE.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.iv_REF1_PH "[...] WE CAN SAY THAT THE EXISTENCE OF THE WORLD IS AN ENIGMA, that it is even the enigma, in the sense that we cannot question it, since we cannot even say it, but we can show it, and that is even what it does, it shows itself in the absence of being said, it manifests itself in the eyes of those who have encountered it, it hides itself from those who are accustomed to not seeing it. If, according to Wittgenstein, "the riddle does not exist," it is because it is existence itself. The riddle of existence is a question without an answer. Therefore, it is important to distinguish the enigma from the mystery, the enigma of the existence of the world from the mystery of the universe (Mysterious Universe). The mystery - for example, that of the Yellow Room (Gaston Leroux, whose hero is Rouletabille) or that of the Great Pyramid (Edgar P. Jacobs, whose heroes are Blake and Mortimer) - is always hidden, kept secret by dangerous conspirators, as conspiracy theories and the paranoid delusion of persecution tend to believe. The mystery, however, is not insurmountable, for it is enough to bring it to light to neutralize the threat. On the contrary, the mystery is all the more incomprehensible the more it is revealed, and all the more enigmatic the more majestically it manifests itself. The popular novel loves conspiracy theories; but nothing is more mysterious to the philosopher than the clarity of the obvious. A mystery is a secret by concealment, but a riddle is a secret by display. So there is no mystery here, nothing to be seen behind the curtain, for all the enigma lies in the obvious, not in the occult. The existence of the world is a riddle by ostentation. The "mystic" is entirely of this world. [...]
To see the world as a miracle is to learn to see what we have always seen as what we have never seen, for this is Wittgenstein's definition of a miracle: "an event such as we have never seen before. It is not, then, a matter of glimpsing another world, but of this world being offered to our gaze, but on the condition that we see it with unprecedented intensity, in the brilliance of its majesty, in the splendor of its appearance. One could speak here of a "revelation of the immanent," or even of a conversion to the obvious. This experience, which presupposes that the gaze regains a certain naiveté and sees the world beyond our habits, beyond the words that name it, the techniques that control it, in its still untouched magnificence, is not unlike the experience of the painter, and especially that of Cézanne. "What does it mean, then, that we perceive this miracle at certain moments and not at others?" Wittgenstein asks again. It means that we can consider the world either within the circle of language, as a collection of facts that can be named and linked together according to the thread and order of discourse, or outside language, as it shows itself, "reveals itself" to the eyes of those who know how to receive it as a miracle. Wittgenstein then adds this disturbing remark: "I am tempted to say that the correct way to express in language the miracle of the world's existence, although it is not a proposition of language, is the existence of language itself. Indeed, if the gaze is turned away from language, to where it is shown and not to where it is spoken, through a change of perspective that allows language to be considered as an object situated in the exterior, thanks to this distance it is possible to be surprised by language itself, by "the existence of language itself", as an object that is foreign to us, as opposed to the point of view of someone who is in language and uses it without being surprised by it. It is then that we can consider language as a miracle, but not as an object of knowledge that we can subject to laws".
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 1- To encounter existence, 2020)

E1.7.B.1.2.c.iv_REF2_LIT “I USED TO WANT TO BE A WRITER. MY DREAM WAS TO WRITE A NOVEL THAT HELD EVERYTHING I LOVED, INCLUDING UNLOVABLE THINGS. LIKE A LITTLE CABINET.”
“What do you want to do in the future, I mean. Looks like you’re some kind of bookworm.” She took a long drag from her cigarette, the smoke blowing out the hallway past his face.
He put the book down and stared at a dusty landscape painting hanging across the hall. “You’ll think it’s stupid.”

“Maybe,” she said. “No promises.” Her amber bracelet clacked against the iron as she flicked ashes in the water.
“I used to want to be a writer. My dream was to write a novel that held everything I loved, including unlovable things. Like a little cabinet.” He shut his eyes— it sounded even more ridiculous aloud. “But that was back in high school. Before I realized it wasn’t real.”
“You wanna be a writer and you want to jump off a bridge? That’s pretty much the same thing, no? A writer just takes longer to hit the water.” She tried to laugh but started coughing.
(Ocean Vuong, The emperor of gladness, 2025)

E1.7.B.1.2.c.v. THE EXPERIENCE OF THE EXISTENCE OF THE WORLD IS NONSENSICAL, UNFORMULABLE AND NON-THEORETICAL. IT CANNOT BE REDUCED TO A SCIENCE. Wittgenstein considers that the world's existence is magnificent due to its nonsensical nature and pure gratuity. The effort to express the ineffable is valid because this sense of wonder is a deep inclination of the human spirit that warrants respect. However, creating a science of ethics, aesthetics, or religion is impossible.

E1.7.B.1.2.c.v_REF1_PH THE EXPERIENCE OF THE EXISTENCE OF THE WORLD, ALTHOUGH IT CANNOT BE FORMULATED, CAN BE FELT AND UNDERSTOOD THROUGH THE EMOTIONS IT EVOKES, SUCH AS ADMIRATION, FEAR, OR ECSTASY. "Have we finally defined what we know, a knowledge that is a taste, a "sweetness" and not a theorem, a demonstration and not a proof, when we experience the existence of the world? No, because our knowledge, which then lies in the unformulable and therefore in nonsense, can never be constituted as a science: "What amounts to saying this," Wittgenstein continues: "I see now that if these expressions did not make sense, it was not because the expressions I had found were not correct, but because their essence was that they did not make sense. In fact, all I wanted to achieve with them was to go beyond the world, that is, beyond meaningful language. All I was striving for-and, I think, all people who have ever tried to write or speak about ethics or religion are striving for-was to confront the limits of language. It is utterly, absolutely hopeless to confront the walls of our cage in this way. "These expressions did not make sense because their essence is not to make sense": how can this be understood? What was trying to be said in the necessarily wobbly images that were successively offered to make up for the failure of meaning is that the very existence of the world is nonsense, and it is this nonsense that we experience when we happen to be surprised and amazed by it. There are thinkers who emphatically complain that this world seems absurd to them. It seems that for Wittgenstein, on the contrary, the nonsense of the appearing world is also the true foundation of its splendor, that the pure gratuitousness of the phenomenon is the condition of its splendor, and that there is nothing more admirable than a world that means nothing, a world whose existence remains irreducible to its being put into words. Didn't Wittgenstein imply that the experience of being surprised by the existence of the world is also the source of a secret and deep joy, since he associated it with the feeling of living in complete security, "whatever happens", which cannot make any sense, at least for those who have never experienced such a feeling? So it is not in vain to go to the limits of language, as the poet does, to evoke the absent form of the mystery. What is in vain is not the effort itself, but the illusion that leads us to believe that a science of ethics, aesthetics, or religion is possible. On the contrary, it is important to understand that these are "experiences" that are part of the unformulable, and to think about them, failing to describe them adequately, while remaining silent. This experience "informs us of a tendency [to express the unspeakable] that exists in the human mind, a tendency that I can only deeply respect for myself, and that I could not possibly make fun of in my life." [...]

Whether it makes sense or not, the experience of being amazed at the existence of the world is still a feeling, that of astonishment or wonder. Admittedly, it is not a common feeling, since its object is a non-object: existence is in fact not an object of our experience, since it is at once nowhere - no phenomenon can embody existence on its own - and everywhere - since it is the act that causes every phenomenon to appear, to "make its appearance" - as one says of an actor who makes a successful entrance. But if we abandon the attempt to define integrally not the thing that manifests itself, but the pure fact of its existence, it is possible, on the contrary, to define the nature of the feeling, since we experience it, we feel it "in our own depths", and consequently, at least in a certain way, we become acquainted with it. It will therefore be up to us to define more precisely the modalities of such a feeling - is it of the order of love, hate, curiosity, joy, sadness or jealousy? Is it admiration or fear, astonishment or curiosity, hallucination or ecstasy?"
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 1- To encounter existence, 2020)

E1.7.B.1.2.c.vi_REF1_MU DIALOGUE BETWEEN INTERIORITY AND EXTERIORITY

Schubert Allegro in A minor from *Lebensstürme* (*Life's Storms*) *D947*

Listen to it on YouTube: https://youtu.be/hkR-cPkJg2E?feature=shared

E1.7.B.1.2.d. "THE PERSONAL AND SUBJECTIVE NATURE OF OUR SPIRITUAL EXPERIENCES CANNOT BE FULLY UNDERSTOOD BY REASON.
The spiritual experiences we have can arise from atoms and molecules. At the same time, some of these experiences, and certainly their very personal and subjective nature, cannot be fully understood in terms of atoms and molecules."

E1.7.B.1.2.d_REF1_PH "I’M A SCIENTIST AND HAVE ALWAYS HAD A SCIENTIFIC VIEW OF THE WORLD — by which I mean that the universe is made of material stuff, and only material stuff, and that stuff is governed by a small number of fundamental laws. Every phenomenon has a cause, which originates in the physical universe. I’m a materialist.
