The hero’s quest  or journey has been represented in mythology and ritual in numerous ways, but the  manifold representations appear in accordance with the myth of the way, as previously  described: a harmonious community or way of life, predictable and stable in structure and  function, is unexpectedly threatened by the emergence of (previously harnessed)  unknown and dangerous forces. An individual of humble and princely origins rises, by  free choice, to counter this threat. This individual is exposed to great personal trials and  risks or experiences physical and psychological dissolution. Nonetheless, he overcomes  the threat, is magically restored (frequently improved) and receives a great reward, in  consequence. He returns to his community with the reward, and (re)establishes social  order (sometimes after a crisis engendered by his return).”
(Jordan Peterson, Maps of Meaning The Architecture of Belief, 1999)

E1.7.B.3.4. THERE ARE INDIVIDUALS FOR WHOM A CONSTANT INTERNAL PEACE AND A HARMONIOUS ARRANGEMENT OF ALL THEIR FACULTIES ARE SO HABITUAL THAT THEY FIND ANY ACTIVITY AIMED AT A GOAL REPUGNANT.

E1.7.B.3.4_REF1_PH  "WITHOUT MELODY. There are people for whom a constant inner repose and a harmonious ordering of all their capabilities is so characteristic that any
goal-directed activity goes against their grain. They are like a piece of music consisting entirely of sustained harmonious chords, with no evidence of even the beginning of a structured, moving melody. At any movement from the outside, their boat at once gains a new equilibrium on the sea of harmonic euphony. Modern people are usually extremely impatient on meeting such natures, who do not become anything—though it may not be said that they are not anything. In certain moods, however, their presence
evokes that rare question: why have melody at all? Why are we not satisfied when life mirrors itself peacefully in a deep lake?
The Middle Ages was richer in such natures than we are. How seldom do we now meet a person who can keep living so peacefully and cheerfully with himself even amidst the turmoil, saying to himself like Goethe: “The best is the deep quiet in which I live and grow against the world, and harvest what they cannot take from me by fire or sword.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 626, 1878)

E1.7.B.3.4_REF2_LIT "DO I SPEND TOO MUCH TIME AT THIS EPHEMERAL TASK [GARDENING]? In spring, summer, and autumn I work harder at it than at writing, and I expect that looks crazy, but what it does is balance all the anxieties and tensions and keep me sane. Sanity (plus flowers) does make sense. [...]
Gardening is like poetry in that it is gratuitous, and also that it cannot be done on will alone. What will can do, and the only thing it can do, is make time in which to do it. Young poets, enraged because they don’t get published right away, confuse what will can do and what it can’t. It can’t make a tree peony grow to twelve feet in a year or two, and it can’t force the attention of editors and publishers. What it can do is create the space necessary for achievement, little by little."
(May Sarton, The House by the Sea, 1977)

E1.7.B.3.5. "YOU SHY AWAY FROM THE FRUITS OF YOUR LABOR, WISHING THEY WERE DIFFERENT, AND STRAY FROM YOUR TRUE LIFE PATH BECAUSE IT DOESN'T LEAD TO THESE ALTERNATE OUTCOMES. However, this perception of labor and its results - the desire for certain outcomes as a motivator for action, and work as a means to satisfy this desire - is the servitude of the ignorants. They do not understand the true nature of labor, its real source, its true purpose, or its profound value.

Once you embark on the path of Yoga, you'll realize that no step is wasted. Each tiny movement will become an advantage, and no obstacle will hinder your progress. This is a bold and absolute promise, one that a hesitant and fearful mind, besieged and stumbling at every turn, may find difficult to trust."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.a. "THE WILL, ENGAGED IN MULTIPLE ACTIVITIES ON VARIOUS PATHS, CONCERNS ITSELF WITH MANY THINGS WHILE NEGLECTING THE ONLY ONE NECESSARY. It is subject to the restless, scattered action of the mind, distributing itself among external life, labor, and its fruits."
(Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.a_REF1_PH "THE YOGA OF INTELLIGENT WILL

[...] what [the philosophy of the Gitâ] teaches is above all a practical system of Yoga, and it introduces metaphysical ideas only to explain this practical system.

You shrink from the results of your works, you desire others and you turn away from your true path in life because it does not lead you there. But this conception of works and their result – desire for the result as the motive for action, work as the means of satisfying desire – is the bondage of the ignorant who knows neither what works are, nor their true source, nor their true purpose, nor their high utility. My yoga, the divine Instructor said to Arjuna, will free you from all bondage of the soul to its works.

40 . – On this path effort never goes to waste, and there is no failure. Even a little effort toward spiritual awareness will protect you from the greatest fear.

My yoga, the Divine Teacher told him, will free you from great fear, and even a little of this yoga will bring you liberation. Once you set out on this path, you will see that no step is lost; each of your smallest movements will be an advantage gained; you will find no obstacle that can frustrate your progress. A bold and absolute promise, a promise that the hesitant and fearful mind, besieged and stumbling on every path, cannot easily place its complete trust in."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.a_REF2_PH THE WILL IS DISPERSED AMONG EXTERNAL LIFE, WORKS AND THEIR FRUITS
"Buddhi - this is the term used here - strictly speaking means the mental power of understanding, but this term is obviously used by the Gîtâ in a broad philosophical sense to designate the whole action of the discriminating and deciding mind which determines both the direction and use of our thoughts and the direction and use of our actions; Thought, intelligence, judgment, choice of perception and purpose are all included in the activity of buddhi; for what characterizes unified intelligence is not only the concentration of the knowing mind, but above all the concentration of the deciding mind and its persistence in its decision, vgavasâya; while the sign of dissipated intelligence is not so much a scattering of ideas and perceptions as a scattering of goals and desires, and consequently of will. Will and knowledge are therefore the two functions of buddhi. Unified intelligent will is fixed in the enlightened soul, concentrated in the inner knowledge of the Self; will, on the contrary, whose multiple activities engage in many ways, deals with many things, neglecting the only necessary one; it is subject to the agitated, scattered action of the mind, and disperses in outer life, works, and their fruits."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)


E1.7.B.3.5.b. "YOU ARE ENTITLED TO ACTION, BUT ONLY TO ACTION, AND NEVER TO ITS FRUITS; let not the fruits of your actions be your motive; and yet do not allow in you any attachment to inaction." This illustrates the originality and strength of the Gitâ - it advocates for ongoing work of the soul while avoiding the major flaw of quietist and ascetic philosophies.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.b_REF1_PH "WHAT CAN BE THE ACQUISITIONS AND POSSESSIONS OF THE FREE SOUL? Possessing the Self, we possess all things. And yet the free soul never ceases work and action. After affirming that, for the liberated soul, what exists is this static condition, this superiority over Nature, this emptiness of all that usually constitutes the action of Nature, the Gita can still—and this is its originality and power—claim for this soul the continuation of work, and even command it, thus avoiding the great flaw of purely quietist and ascetic philosophies, a flaw from which we see them striving to escape today.

47 . – You have the right to work, but never to the fruit of work. You should never engage in action for the sake of reward, nor should you long for inaction.

It is because he acts in ignorance, with a distorted intelligence, and consequently with a distorted will, that man is enslaved, or appears to be enslaved, by his works; otherwise works are not bondage to the free soul. It is because of this distorted intelligence that man experiences hope and fear, anger and sorrow, and transient joy; otherwise he can perform the works in perfect serenity and freedom. Therefore, what is first ordained to Arjuna is the yoga of buddhi, intelligence. To act with true intelligence and consequently true will, firmly established in the One, conscious of the unique Self in all and acting from its serene equality, without running in all directions under the thousand impulses of the superficial mental self, is the yoga of intelligent will. Is action not perplexed when faced with the choice between relative good and relative evil, between the fear of sin and the difficult effort of virtue? This is true of the action of ordinary people, but not of the action of the yogi.

49 . – Seek refuge in the attitude of detachment and you will amass the wealth of spiritual awareness. Those who are motivated only by desire for the fruits of action are miserable, for they are constantly anxious about the results of what they do."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.c. “EVEN THE MIND OF THE WISE MAN WHO STRIVES FOR PERFECTION IS DRIVEN BY THE VEHEMENT INSISTENCE OF THE SENSES.” Peace of mind cannot be accomplished perfectly by an act of the intellect alone or by mental discipline alone; it can only be achieved through yoga, which is something higher than oneself and inherently involves calm and self-control.

“He attains peace, whom all desires penetrate like waters into the ocean (an ocean of being and consciousness fills, yet remains still) - not he who (like muddy, troubled waters ) is troubled by the slightest influx of desire.
He who abandons all desires and lives and acts devoid of longing, who has neither "I" nor "mine" (who has dissolved his individual ego into the One, living in that unity), he attains pprofound peace."
Nirvana is not a negative state; it is not the Buddhist annihilation of self, but rather the profound immersion of the distinct personal self into the vast reality of infinite Existence, unified and impersonal.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.c_REF1_PH "EVEN THE MIND OF THE WISE MAN WHO STRIVES FOR PERFECTION is driven, O son of Kunti, by the vehement insistence of the senses.

Certainly, self-discipline and self-control are never easy. Every intelligent man knows that he must exercise some control over himself, and nothing is more common than the advice to control the senses; but in general it is imperfectly given and imperfectly put into practice, in the most limited, most inadequate way. And even the wise man, the man whose soul is clear, full of wisdom and discernment, who truly strives to acquire complete self-control, even he finds himself pushed, hurried, dragged along by the senses.

61 . – They live in wisdom who subdue their senses and keep their minds ever absorbed in me.

It cannot be achieved perfectly by an act of the mind alone, by mental discipline alone; it can only be achieved through yoga with something higher than oneself and with inherent calm and self-control.

62 . - When you keep thinking about sense objects, attachment comes. Attachment breeds desire, the lust of possession that burns to anger. 63 . -  Anger clouds the judgment; you can no longer learn from past mistakes. Lost is the power to choose between what is wise and what is unwise, and your life is utter waste. 64 . -  But when you move amidst the world of sense, free from attachment and aversion alike, 65 . -  there comes the peace in which all sorrows end, and you live in the wisdom of the Self.

It is to this unchangeability of buddhi in equilibrium and self-knowledge - calm unchangeability, without desire, without sorrow - that the Gîtâ gives the name of samâdhi.

70 . – As rivers flow into the ocean but cannot make the vast ocean overflow, so flow the streams of the sense-world into the sea of peace that is the sage. But this is not so with the desirer of desires. 71 . - They are forever free who renounce all selfish desires and break away from the ego-cage of “I,” “me,” and “mine” to be united with
the Lord.

Nirvana is not negative; it is not the self-annihilation of Buddhists, but the great immersion of the distinct personal self in the vast reality of infinite, one, impersonal existence."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)


E1.7.B.3.5.d. “THE POSITIVE AND IMPERATIVE DOCTRINE OF LABOR: ACTION IS NOT JUST PREPARATION, IT IS THE MEANS OF LIBERATION.
The Gîtâ has emphasized withdrawing the will from the ordinary motive of human activity. Yet, labor is insistently presented as part of Yoga, so that there seems to be a radical inconsistency in this teaching. To address this perceived contradiction, the Gîtâ elaborates its positive and imperative doctrine of labor.

"In this world, the soul's effort upon itself (by which it enters the Brahmic condition) is twofold: that of knowledge, and that of labor."

According to the Yoga of the Gîtâ, action is not just preparation; it is the means of liberation. The Gîtâ continually emphasizes and validates this theory. Regrettably, this emphasis couldn't withstand Buddhism's powerful influence in India and was later lost amidst the ascetic delusion popularized by Shankara and the fervor of devotees and saints who renounced the world.

Renunciation is crucial, but true renunciation involves the inner rejection of desire and egoism. Without it, the external abandonment of labor is substanceless and ineffective. With it, such abandonment becomes unnecessary, though not forbidden. Knowledge is vital as the highest force of liberation, but labor, paired with knowledge, is just as crucial. Through the union of knowledge and labor, the soul remains in the Brahmic condition, not only in rest and tranquility but also in the heart of action, its intensity, and violence.”
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.d_REF1_PH "SELFLESS SERVICE
ARJUNA
1 . - O Krishna, you have said that knowledge is greater than action; why then do you ask me to wage this terrible war? 

Hitherto the Gîtâ has mainly emphasized the withdrawal of the will from the ordinary motive of human activity (desire), from the normal human temperament (thought and will directed towards the senses, accompanied by their passions and their ignorance), and from its inveterate habit of agitated ideas and desires scattered along various paths - in order to direct it towards the calm and desireless unity and passionless serenity of Brahmanic equilibrium.
And yet, works are persistently presented as part of yoga, so that there seems to be a radical inconsistency in this teaching. In response to this objection, the Gîtâ immediately begins to develop more clearly its positive and imperative doctrine of works.

2 . - Your advice seems inconsistent. Give me one path to follow to the supreme good.

For the yoga of the Gîtâ, as for the Vedic yoga of the works, action is not only a preparation, it is itself the means of liberation; and it is the correctness of this theory that the Gîtâ seeks to emphasize with incessant force and insistence. Unfortunately, this insistence could not prevail in India against the powerful wave of Buddhism; it was subsequently lost in the intensity of the ascetic illusionism popularized by Shankara, in the fervor of the devotees and saints fleeing the world.

Renunciation is essential, but true renunciation is the inner renunciation of desire and selfishness; without it, the material, external renunciation of works is without reality and without effect; with it, this renunciation is not even necessary, though it is not forbidden. Knowledge is essential; there is no higher power of liberation, but works combined with knowledge are also necessary. Through the union of knowledge and works, the soul remains entirely in the Brahmanic state, not only in the rest and tranquility of inactivity, but in the very heart of action, in its intensity and violence. Devotion is of the utmost importance, but so are the works associated with devotion. This is the synthesis of Gîtâ.

KRISHNA

3 . - At the beginning of time I declared two paths for the pure heart: jnana yoga, the contemplative path of spiritual wisdom, and karma yoga, the active path of selfless service.

4 . - One who shirks action does not attain freedom; no one can gain perfection by abstaining from work.

If the works of prakriti continue, how can the soul not be involved? How can I fight and yet not think in my soul, not feel that I, the individual, am fighting, not desire victory, nor be inwardly affected by defeat? It is the teaching of the Samkhians that the intelligence of the man engaged in the activities of nature is caught in the nets of egoism, ignorance, and desire, and is consequently drawn to action; on the contrary, when the intelligence withdraws, action necessarily ceases with the cessation of desire and ignorance. Consequently, the renunciation of life and works is a necessary part, an inevitable circumstance, and an ultimate indispensable means of the movement toward liberation. The Teacher immediately foresees this objection of current logic. No, he says, such renunciation, far from being indispensable, is not even possible.

5 . – Indeed, there is no one who rests for even an instant; all creatures are driven to action by their own nature."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.d_REF2_PH "PEACE IS HAPPINESS AT REST AND HAPPINESS IS PEACE IN MOTION. You can convert peace to happiness anytime you want."
(Naval Ravikant)

E1.7.B.3.5.e. “BUT IS IT POSSIBLE TO ENGAGE IN ACTION WITHOUT DESIRE?
There is an essential difficulty that needs resolving. Given our nature, and since desire is a common principle of action, can we truly initiate action without desire? What we often refer to as disinterested action isn't truly action without desire; it's merely substituting small personal desires for broader ones that seem impersonal, such as virtue, patriotism, or humanity. So, how can we achieve a true absence of desire? We can do this by dedicating all our efforts solely to the divine.

Just as those who are ignorant act with attachment to their actions, those who are knowledgeable must act without attachment, with the intention of uniting nations. They should not unsettle the ignorant who are attached to their labor. Instead, they should engage them in all actions, which they themselves perform with knowledge and yoga.”
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.e_REF1_PH IS IT POSSIBLE TO INITIATE AN ACTION THAT IS TRULY DESIRELESS? 
"But one essential difficulty remains to be resolved. Our nature being what it is, and desire being the common principle of its action, is it possible to initiate an action that is truly desireless? For what we ordinarily call disinterested action is not really action without desire; it is only the substitution of certain small personal desires for other larger desires which only have the appearance of being impersonal, virtue, country, humanity. How can we achieve the real absence of desire? By doing all works with sacrifice as the sole object - this is the answer of the Divine Teacher.

9 . – Selfish action imprisons the world. Act selflessly, without any thought of personal profit.

It is obvious that in this spirit all works can be performed, and not only sacrifices and social duties; all actions can be dictated either by the sense of the ego, narrow or broad, or by the love of the Divine. All existence and all action of Prakriti [nature, creative energy (almost synonymous with Shakti)] exist only for the love of the Divine; that is where they come from, that is where they last, that is where they are led.

25 . – The ignorant work for their own profit, Arjuna; the wise work for the welfare of the world, without thought for themselves. 26 . - By abstaining from work you will confuse the ignorant, who are engrossed in their"
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.f. "HE WHO IN ACTION CAN SEE INACTION, AND WHO CAN SEE ACTION CONTINUING STILL IN THE CESSATION OF LABOR, IS AMONG MEN THE ONE WITH TRUE REASON AND JUDGEMENT": There's no need for external distinctions, to avoid any necessary labor in this world, or to set limits or barriers around human activities. Instead, all actions should be performed by a soul in yoga with the Divine. Complete inaction is not the solution; a person who has achieved the inner vision of supreme reason understands that such inaction is in itself a constant action, a state subject to nature and its modes.

Outwardly, the liberated man appears to engage in all kinds of actions like other men, perhaps on a larger scale or with greater force and impetus, given the divine will acting in his active nature. However, his efforts completely lack desire. He has renounced any attachment to the outcomes of his actions. When one acts not for the result, but purely as an impersonal instrument of nature, the outcome of this knowledge, this absence of desire, and this impersonality, is perfect equality in soul and nature.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.f_REF1_PH "THE DETERMINISM OF NATURE

In fact, there are several planes of our conscious existence, and what is a practical truth on one of these planes takes on a completely different appearance and consequently ceases to be true as soon as we rise to a higher level from where we can have a more general view.

27 . – All actions are performed by the gunas of prakriti. Deluded by identification with the ego, a person thinks, “I am the doer.” 
28 . - But the illumined man or woman understands the domain of the gunas and is not attached. Such people know that the gunas interact with each other; they do not claim to be the doer.
29 . - Those who are deluded by the operation of the gunas become attached to the results of their action. Those who understand these truths should not unsettle the ignorant."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.f_REF2_PH "If a man loves life, and feels the sacredness and mystery of life, then he knows that life is full of strange and subtle and even conflicting imperatives. And a wise man learns to recognize the imperatives as they arise — or nearly so — and to obey. But most men bruise themselves to death trying to fight and overcome their own, new, life-born needs, life’s ever strange imperatives. The secret of all life is obedience: obedience to the urge that arises in the soul, the urge that is life itself, urging us to new gestures, new embraces, new emotions, new combinations, new creations. [...]
I offer no creed. I offer myself, my heart of wisdom, strange warm cavern where the voice of the oracle steams in from the unknown; I offer my consciousness, which hears the voice; and I offer my mind and my will, for the battle against every obstacle to respond to the voice of life."
(D.H. Lawrence, Kangaroo, 1923)

"Outwardly, the liberated man seems to undertake works of all kinds like other men, on a larger scale perhaps and with more force and impetus, for the power of the divine will acts in his active nature; but his works are all devoid of desire. He has given up all attachment to the fruits of his actions; and when one acts, not for the fruit, but solely as an impersonal instrument of the Master of the works, desire cannot find a place, not even the desire to serve successfully - for the fruit is the Lord's, is determined by Him and not by personal will and effort - nor the desire to serve meritoriously and to the satisfaction of the Master, for the real author is the Lord Himself, [...] on a mission in nature, and not to the limited human personality. The result of this knowledge, absence of desire and impersonality is perfect equality in soul and nature. Equality is the fourth sign of the divine worker. [...]
Once revealed, this knowledge must be accepted with the assent of the mind, the consent of the will, the joy and submission of the heart - the three elements of complete mental faith."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.g. “FOR A SAGE WHO IS CLIMBING THE SLOPE OF YOGA, ACTION IS THE CAUSE; FOR THE SAME SAGE, WHEN HE HAS REACHED THE SUMMIT OF YOGA, SELF-CONTROL IS THE CAUSE.
Labor is the cause of self-perfection, liberation, and nirvana in Brahman. When one engages in labor with persistent inner renunciation, this perfection, this liberation, this conquest of the desire-mind, ego in yoga, and the lower nature is easily achieved. And when you've reached the summit? Then labor is no longer the cause; the tranquility obtained from self-mastery and self-possession through labor becomes the cause. What does it cause? It causes stability in the self, in Brahman consciousness, and perfect equanimity, which enables the divine labor of a liberated man.”
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.g_REF1_PH "THE SUPREME WORD OF GITA 

Understanding and knowledge and liberation from the confusion of ignorance, forgiveness and truth and self-control and the tranquility of inner mastery, sorrow and joy, creation and destruction, fear and fearlessness, glory and shame, non-violence and equanimity, contentment and austerity and charity, all these things in their separate diversity are subjective becomings of existences, and all emanate from Me.

This knowledge, transferred to the affective and emotional level, to the level of temperament, becomes a serene love and an intense worship of the original and transcendent Divine, above us, ever-present Master of all things here below, God in man, God in nature. It is primarily a wisdom of the intellect, buddhi [reason, intelligence, mental power of understanding], but it is accompanied by a state of spiritualized emotion of the affective nature, bhâva [subjective state or feeling; becoming]. This change of heart and mind is the beginning of a total change in one's entire nature. New inner birth and becoming prepare us for union with the ultimate object of our love and worship, madbhâvâya. There is an intense joy of love in the grandeur, beauty, and perfection of the Divine Being, which is henceforth seen everywhere in the world and above it, prîti [joy, joy of love]. This deeper ecstasy takes the place of the outward and scattered joy of the mind in existence; or rather, it draws all other joy into itself and transforms, by a marvelous alchemy, the feelings of the mind and heart and all the movements of the senses.

9 . – Their thoughts are all absorbed in me, and all their vitality flows to me. Teaching one another, talking about me always, they are happy and fulfilled."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h." HE WHO PRACTICES EXCLUSIVELY THE YOGA OF KNOWLEDGE IMPOSES UPON HIMSELF A PAINFUL STRUGGLE AGAINST THE MULTIPLE DEMANDS OF HIS NATURE; he refuses even to satisfy its noblest demands, and repudiates even the highest impulses of his spirit, whenever they imply relationships, and do not rise to an absolute negation. The living path of the Gîtâ, on the other hand, uncovers the most intense exalting tendency of our entire being, and, directing it towards God, employs knowledge, will, feelings and the instinct for perfection as powerful wings for the liberating ascent.

- "Better in truth is knowledge than effort; better than knowledge is meditation; better than meditation is renunciation of the fruit of action; from renunciation comes peace."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1_PH DON'T THINK THAT THE MORE DIFFICULT THE PROCESS, THE MORE EFFECTIVE IT IS.
"The Way of Love

KRISHNA

2 . - Those who set their hearts on me and worship me with unfailing devotion and faith are more established in yoga.

The highest faith is that which sees God in everything, and for it manifestation and non-manifestation are one and the same Divine. Perfect union is that in which one touches the Divine in every moment, in every action, with the whole of nature.

3 . - As for those who seek the transcendental Reality, without name, without form, contemplating the Unmanifested, beyond the reach of thought and of feeling, 4 . - with their senses subdued and mind serene and striving for the good of all beings, they too will verily come unto me.

For they are not mistaken in their aspiration, but they follow a more difficult, less complete and less perfect path. The Immutable offers no hold to the mind; it can only be conquered by a spiritual impersonality that is motionless and silent, and those who seek this alone must completely restrain and even completely withdraw the activity of the mind and the senses within.

5 . – Yet hazardous and slow is the path to the Unrevealed, difficult for physical creatures to tread.

And it should not be thought that the more difficult the effort, the higher and more effective the process. The easier path of Gîtâ leads more quickly, more naturally, and more normally to the same absolute liberation. Those who practice only the yoga of knowledge impose upon themselves a painful struggle against the multiple demands of their nature; they refuse to satisfy even its noblest demands and reject even the highest impulses of their spirit whenever they involve relations that do not rise to an absolute of negation. The living path of Gîtâ, on the other hand, discovers the most intense exalting tendency of our entire being and, directing it towards God, uses knowledge, will, feeling, the instinct for perfection as so many powerful wings for the liberating ascent.

12 . – Better indeed is knowledge than mechanical practice. Better than knowledge is meditation. But better still is surrender of attachment to results, because there follows immediate peace.

Abhyâsa, the practice of a method, the repetition of an effort and an experience, is a great and powerful thing; but superior is knowledge, thought directed successfully and in the light to the Truth that is behind things. This knowledge of thought, in turn, is surpassed by a complete silent concentration on the Truth, so that the consciousness can finally live in the Truth, always be one with it. But even more powerful is the renunciation of the fruits of action, because this immediately destroys any cause for disturbance, automatically creating and maintaining inner peace and tranquility. And peace and tranquility are the basis upon which everything becomes perfect, in the sure possession of a tranquil mind."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1_PH HUMAN BEINGS ARE MIXED "FORCES OF EVOLUTION

KRISHNA

1 . - Let me tell you more about the wisdom that transcends all knowledge, through which the saints and sages attained perfection. 2 . - Those who rely on this wisdom will be united with me. For them there is neither rebirth nor fear of death.

3 . - My womb is prakriti; in that I place the seed. Thus all created things are born. 4 . - Everything born, Arjuna, comes from the womb of prakriti, and I am the seed-giving father.

5 . - It is the three gunas born of prakriti – sattva, rajas, and tamas – that bind the immortal Self to the body. 6 . - Sattva – pure, luminous, and free from sorrow – binds us with attachment to happiness and wisdom. 7 . - Rajas is passion, arising from selfish desire and attachment. These bind the Self with compulsive action. 8 . - Tamas, born of ignorance, deludes all creatures through heedlessness, indolence, and sleep.

9 . - Sattva binds us to happiness; rajas binds us to action. Tamas, distorting our understanding, binds us to delusion.

These three qualities of nature are certainly present and active in all human beings, and no one can be considered wholly devoid of one or the other, or free from any of the three; but they are not constant in every man in the quantitative action of their force, or in the combination of their elements: for they are variable and in a constant state of displacement, mutual shock, and interaction. It is only by the general and habitual predominance of one or other of these qualities that a man can be said to be sattvic or rajasic or tamasic in nature; but this can only be a general qualification, not an absolute or exclusive one."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.a_PH "THE MIND IS ALERT AND ENLIGHTENED, THE SENSES SHARPENED, THE WHOLE MIND SATISFIED AND FULL OF BRILLIANCE, AND THE NERVOUS SYSTEM CALMED, FILLED WITH CLARITY AND LUMINOUS EASE, prasâda [clarity and blissful tranquillity]. Knowledge, harmonious ease, pleasure and happiness, these are the characteristic results of sallva. Can sallva, the power of knowledge and happiness, become a chain? Yes, because it is a principle of mental nature, a principle of limited and limiting knowledge, a principle of happiness dependent on this or that object duly pursued or attained, or even on certain states of mind, on a mental light that can be more or less clear for only half a day. Its pleasure can only be an ephemeral intensity or a muted satisfaction. Infinite spiritual knowledge and the free, intrinsic joy of our spiritual being are quite different.

10 . – Sattva predominates when rajas and tamas are transformed. Rajas prevails when sattva is weak and tamas overcome. Tamas prevails
when rajas and sattva are dormant.

11 . - When sattva predominates, the light of wisdom shines through every gate of the body."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.b_PH EVEN WHEN THE PLEASURE OF POSSESSION IS REALISED, WE REMAIN UNSTABLE AND RESTLESS IN THE ABSENCE OF CLEAR KNOWLEDGE AND TRUE ENJOYMENT. "Appetite, impulse of lust, initiative of action, fear, desire - this is what increases in us when rajas increases.

It is the force of desire which is the motive for all ordinary personal initiative of action, for all that movement in our nature - agitation, seeking, impulse - which drives us to action and works, pravritti [impulse to action]. Rajas, then, is obviously the kinetic force in the modes of nature. Its fruit is the thirst for action, but it is also sorrow, pain, suffering of all kinds; for it does not have the right possession of its object - desire actually implies non-possession; and even when its pleasure of possession is realised, it is unstable and realised, it is unstable and disturbed because it has no clear knowledge, does not know how to possess, cannot find the secret of harmony and true enjoyment. All ignorant and passionate pursuit of life belongs to the rajasic mode of nature.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.c_PH IGNORANCE AND INACTION "Ignorance, indolence, negligence and aberration - this is what happens when tamas predominates, O Joy of the Kurus.

It is the darkness of tamas that obscures knowledge and causes all confusion and aberration. That is why tamas is the opposite of sallva, for the essence of sallva is illumination, prakâsha, and the essence of tamas is the absence of light and knowledge, aprakâsha. But as well as incapacity and carelessness in error, inattention, misunderstanding or non-understanding, tamas produces incapacity and carelessness in action; indolence, sluggishness and sleep belong to this guna. This is why tamas is also the opposite of rajas; for the essence of rajas is movement, impulse and kinetic force, pravritti, while the essence of tamas is inertia, apravritti. Tamas, doubly negative, is the inertia of ignorance and the inertia of inactivity".
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.d_PH THE Gîtâ INSISTS ON THE NECESSITY OF ACTION "The Gîtâ, while demanding that the soul be freed from fashions, insists on the necessity of action. This is why the Gîtâ's insistence on the renunciation of fruits is so important; for it is the desire for fruits that is the most powerful cause of the soul's bondage; abandoning this desire, it can be free in action.

Ignorance is the result of tamasic action; pain, the result of rajasic work - pain in its many forms, reaction, disappointment, dissatisfaction, the feeling of impermanence - and therefore there is nothing to be gained by clinging to the fruits of this kind of activity, for they are followed by these undesirable consequences. But the fruit of rightly performed works is pure and sattvic, the inner result is knowledge and happiness. And yet attachment, even to these sources of pleasure, must be completely abandoned; first, because they are limited and limiting forms in the mind; and second, because, since sallva is constantly mixed with rajas and tamas, which besiege it and can dominate it at any moment, there is a perpetual insecurity in the possession of these pleasures.

But even if one is free from all attachment to the fruit, one may be attached to the work itself, either for its own sake - and this is the essence of the rajasic hindrance - or through weak submission to the impulses of nature - and this is the tamasic hindrance - or because the thing done is right and therefore attractive - and this is the cause of the sattvic attachment which is strong in the man of virtue or the man of knowledge. It is then, without doubt, necessary to have recourse to this other injunction of the Gîtâ: "to surrender the action itself to the Lord of the works, and to be nothing more than an instrument, equal and without desire, of His will" (XIV, 19).
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.e_PH UNE ACTION IMPERSONNELLE ET UNIVERSELLE POUR LE BIEN DU MONDE "THE POWER OF FAITH

3 . – Our faith conforms to our nature, Arjuna. Human nature is made of faith. A person is what his shraddha is.

If we look closely at this fruitful idea, we find that this single line, with its few powerful words, implies almost the entire theory of the modern pragmatic gospel. For if a man, or the soul in a man, consists in the faith that is in him - understood in this profound sense - it follows that the truth which he sees and wants to live is for him the truth of his being, his own truth, which he has created or is creating, and for him there can be no other real truth. This truth belongs to his inner and outer action, to his future, to the dynamism of his soul, and not to that which never changes within him. He is what he is today because of a past will of his nature, sustained and continued by a present will to know, to believe, and to be in his intelligence and vital force; and whatever new direction this will and faith, acting in the very substance of his being, take, that is what he will tend to become in the future. We create our own truth of existence through our own actions of mind and life, that is, we create our own being, we make ourselves. But this is, of course, only one aspect of the truth.

The sattvic sacrifice is very close to the ideal and leads directly to the quality of action required by the Gîtâ; but it is not the ultimate and highest ideal, it is not yet the action of the man who has become perfect and lives in the divine nature. For it is accomplished as a specific dharma and offered as a sacrifice or service to the gods, to some power or partial aspect of the Divine manifested in ourselves or in the universe. Work done in selfless religious faith, or even without selfishness, for humanity, or even impersonally, out of devotion to right or truth, is sattvic in nature, and such action is necessary for our perfection; for it purifies our thought, our will, and the substance of our nature. The culmination of sattvic action to which we must aspire is of an even broader and freer quality; it is the ultimate high sacrifice offered by us to the Supreme Divine in His Integral Being, with the aspiration of Purushottama, with the vision of Vâsudéva in all that is; it is action done impersonally, universally, for the good of the world, for the fulfilment of the divine will in the universe. This culmination leads sattvic action to transcend itself, leading to the immortal Dharma. For then comes a freedom in which there is no personal action, no sattvic rule of dharma, no limitation by shastra; lower reason and will are themselves transcended; it is not they but a higher wisdom that dictates and guides action and assigns its object. There is no question of personal fruit, for it is not our will that is at work, but a Supreme Will of which our soul is the instrument. There is neither self-consciousness nor self-forgetfulness, for the Jiva, the eternal portion of the Divine, is united with the supreme Self of His existence, and He and all are one in that Self, in that Spirit. There is no personal action, for we offer all actions to the Master of our works, and it is He who does the action through the divinised Prakriti. There is no sacrifice - unless we say that the Master of Sacrifice, in His cosmic form, offers to Himself the works of His own energy in the Jiva. It is the supreme state of surpassing oneself that we attain through action, which is sacrifice; it is the perfection of the soul that has attained full consciousness in the divine nature".
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.f_PH TRUE RENUNCIATION CONSISTS IN ABANDONING ATTACHMENT TO THE RESULTS OF ONE'S ACTIONS AND ACTING SELFLESSLY.
"FREEDOM & RENUNCIATION

KRISHNA

2 . – To refrain from selfish acts is one kind of renunciation, called sannyasa; to renounce the fruit of action is another, called tyaga.

Sannyâsa, in the terminology used by the sages, refers to the renunciation of desirous actions. In this sense, it is not Sannyâsa but Tyâga that is the best path. For what is to be rejected is not actions tainted with desire, but the desire that attaches to them. The fruit of action may come by the dispensation of the Master of Works, but our selfishness must not demand it as a reward, nor make it a condition of the work. It may also be that this fruit will never come, and yet the work must be done as an action to be done, kartavyam karma, an action required of us by the Master within us. Success or failure is in His hands and He will order it according to His omniscient will and His unfathomable plans. In truth, action, any action, must in the end be surrendered, not materially by abstention, immobility, inertia, but spiritually to the Master of our being, whose power alone can bring about the accomplishment of any action. We must abandon the misconception that we are the author of action, for in reality it is the Universal Shakti that works through our personality and ego. The spiritual transfer of all our works to the Master and His Shakti is, in the teaching of the Gîtâ, true Sannyâsa.

The sattvic principle of renunciation is to renounce not the action but the personal demand, the ego factor behind the action. It is to perform works dictated not by desire but by the law of righteous living, or by the essential nature, its knowledge, its ideal, its faith in itself and the truth it sees, its shraddhâ. Or, on a higher spiritual level, one lets the will of the Master dictate these actions and accomplishes them, the mind in yoga, without personal attachment to the action or the fruit of the action. One must completely renounce all desire, all choice, all selfish impulses where one thinks only of oneself, and finally that much more subtle selfishness of the will which says: "The work is mine, I am its author", or even "The work is God's, but I am its author". One should not be attached to work that is pleasant, desirable, lucrative or successful, and one should not do it because of that nature; but even this work must be done, totally, in self-denial, with the consent of the spirit, if it is the action required from above and within us, kartavyam karma. There must be no aversion to unpleasant, undesirable or futile actions that bring or are likely to bring suffering, danger, harshness or disastrous consequences; for this too must be accepted, totally, in self-denial, with a deep understanding of its necessity and its meaning, if it is the work that must be done, kartavyam karma.

26 . – Sattvic workers are free from egotism and selfish attachments, full of enthusiasm and fortitude in success and failure alike.

He who acts in sattva is filled with a high enthusiasm, pure and without selfishness, for the action to be done. At the summit of Sattva and beyond, this determination, this zeal, this enthusiasm, becomes the spontaneous play of spiritual tapas, and ultimately the supreme power of the soul, the direct power of God, the powerful and firm movement of a divine energy in the human instrument, the sure steps of the seer's will, the Gnostic intelligence, and with it the abundant joy of the free spirit in the works of liberated nature.

30 . – To know when to act and when to refrain from action, what is right action and what is wrong, what brings security and what insecurity, what brings freedom and what bondage: these are the signs of a sattvic intellect."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.5.h_REF1.1.g_PH THE THREE KINDS OF HAPPINESS
"36 . - Now listen, Arjuna: there are also three kinds of happiness. By sustained effort, one comes to the end of sorrow. 37 . - That which seems like poison at first, but tastes like nectar in the end – this is the joy of sattva, born of a mind at peace with itself. 38 . - Pleasure from the senses seems like nectar at first, but it is bitter as poison in the end. This is the kind of happiness that comes to the rajasic. 39. - Those who are tamasic draw their pleasures from sleep, indolence, and intoxication. Both in the beginning and in the end, this happiness is a delusion.

What the sattvic nature seeks is the satisfaction of the higher mind and spirit; and when this great object of its quest is attained, then comes a clear and pure happiness of the soul, a state of fulfilment, a lasting ease and peace. This happiness does not depend on external things, but only on ourselves, on the flowering of the best, of the most intimate in us. But it is not, at first, our normal possession; it must be conquered by self-discipline, by work on the soul, by a powerful and arduous effort. And this means, first of all, much lost habitual pleasure, much suffering and struggle, a poison born of the agitation of our nature, a painful conflict of forces, and, against this change, much revolt and resistance due to the ill will of the elements of being or the obstinacy of the vital movements; but in the end the Nectar of Immortality replaces all this bitterness, and as we ascend to the higher spiritual nature we reach the end of pain, the euthanasia of sorrow and suffering. This is the incomparable happiness that descends upon us at the point or summit where sattvic discipline culminates. This surpassing of oneself in sattvic nature comes to us when we go beyond sattvic pleasures, which are noble but still inferior, beyond the pleasures of knowledge, virtue and mental peace, and when we attain the eternal tranquillity of the Self and the spiritual ecstasy of divine unity. This spiritual joy is no longer sattvic happiness, sukham, but absolute ananda - ananda [spiritual delight, the bliss of the mind], the secret delight from which all things are born, by which all things are sustained, and to which all things can rise in spiritual culmination. Man can possess it only when, free, liberated from the ego, he lives one with his supreme self, one with all beings, one with God, in the absolute bliss of the Spirit.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.B.3.6. WHY IS IT SO DIFFICULT TO ESCAPE THE MODE OF THE WILL and find peace in contemplation or in action detached from its fruits, as described by ante-predictive philosophies? It's because we are the heirs of over two thousand years of rationalist philosophy - we are conditioned to will.

E1.7.B.3.6.b_REF1_PH "A LOT OF PEOPLE SAY, “IF YOU WANT TO BE HAPPY, DON’T HAVE EXPECTATIONS.” And I think that’s a really good way to be unhappy for the rest of your life because … if you don’t have expectations, you will just put up with everything. I mean, you don’t expect anything for yourself. With high expectations, with [a] kind of enforceability around that, you’re not going to put up with a bad relationship, a bad work environment, a bad employee, all of these types of things."

"If you are looking for a partner to build with, you’re going to look for different traits and qualities than somebody who just clocks in and clocks out."

"You have to care deeply to be able to challenge directly, and … somebody has to know that you care deeply about who they are in their role, that you care about their performance, that this is coming from a place that’s meant to make them better. And if it’s not coming from that place, and it’s coming from a place of punishing or shaming or humiliating or dominating, it’s not a really effective thing to do. But people can take hard feedback from people that they know are in their corner."

"We all have bad moments, bad minutes, bad hours, but we don’t have to have bad days."
(Dr. Julie Gurner cited in FS | BRAIN FOOD)

E1.7.B.3.6.b_REF2_PO "HURRY

We stop at the dry cleaners and the grocery store   
and the gas station and the green market and   
Hurry up honey, I say, hurry,   
as she runs along two or three steps behind me   
her blue jacket unzipped and her socks rolled down.   

Where do I want her to hurry to? To her grave?   
To mine? Where one day she might stand all grown?   
Today, when all the errands are finally done, I say to her,   
Honey I'm sorry I keep saying Hurry—   
you walk ahead of me. You be the mother.   

And, Hurry up, she says, over her shoulder, looking   
back at me, laughing. Hurry up now darling, she says,   
hurry, hurry, taking the house keys from my hands."
(Marie Howe, 2008)

Listen to the poem read by Marie Howe:
https://onbeing.org/poetry/hurry/

E1.7.B.3.6.b_REF3_PO ”I’M NOT IN A HURRY. IN A HURRY FOR WHAT?
The sun and moon aren’t in a hurry; they’re right.
To hurry is to suppose we can overtake our legs
Or leap over our shadow.
No, I’m not in a hurry.
If I stretch out my arm, I’ll reach exactly as far as my arm
     reaches
And not half an inch farther.
I touch where my ﬁnger touches, not where I think.
I can only sit down where I am.
This sounds ridiculous, like all absolutely true truths,
But what’s really ridiculous is how we’re always thinking of
     something else,
And we’re always outside it, because we’re here.”
(Fernando Pessoa, 20 June 1919 - signed as Albert Caeiro) 

E1.7.B.3.6.b_REF4_PH IN THE CHAPTER “APPRENTICESHIP AND ENCULTURATION” OF MAPS OF MEANING (1999), JORDAN PETERSON PROVIDES A COMPLEX DEVELOPMENTAL THEORY OF FREEDOM, WHICH HE CALLS THE "PHILOSOPHY OF APPRENTICESHIP." 
1. It Outlines a Complete "Path": The text describes a clear developmental path toward "true freedom." This path is not immediate but proceeds through necessary stages: from childhood dependency, to "apprenticeship" and "subjugation to lawful authority" (identification with the group/tradition), and finally to the potential for mature, creative self-mastery.
2. It Defines the "Technique of the Self": The core technique described is discipline through submission. Drawing heavily on Nietzsche, the text argues that a "long compulsion" and obedience "over a long period of time and in a single direction" is the "indispensable means of spiritual discipline and cultivation." This "tyranny," whether from a church, a philosophy, or a craft, is what "trains the spirit to strength, ruthless curiosity, and subtle mobility."
3. It Is Aimed at "Inner Transformation": The entire purpose of this "apprenticeship" is to instigate a profound inner transformation. It is designed to turn the "undisciplined" individual, who is paralyzed by the "too much unknown" of childhood, into a mature individual capable of "self-mastery" and heroic, creative action.
4. The Goal Is a Higher "Liberation": The text makes it clear that submission to tradition is not the end goal, but a necessary phase. The ultimate liberation is the ability to "transcend tradition and dogma" after one has mastered the discipline it teaches. This is a sophisticated model where a period of "long unfreedom of the spirit" becomes the paradoxical precondition for a higher, more potent form of freedom.
5. It Critiques "Incomplete" Paths: By contrasting genuine myth with ideologies ("incomplete myths—as partial stories"), the text also explains the pitfalls on this path. It critiques simplistic worldviews (like the romantic idea that "man is by nature good, and that only our institutions have made him bad") as inadequate guides, reinforcing the need for a more complete and difficult engagement with the totality of experience.
(Pierre Schindler)

E1.7.B.3.6.c.  ... VERSUS THE LETTING-GO OF EASTERN PHILOSOPHIES.
Wu-wei refers to a state of mind where an individual feels as if they are doing nothing, yet they are optimally active and effective. It represents dynamic, effortless, and unselfconscious action that encompasses the integration of body, emotions, and mind. Many desirable states are best pursued indirectly, as conscious thought and strenuous effort can hinder their achievement.

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF1_PH TRYING TOO HARD CAN HINDER SUCCESS "Slingerland frames the paradoxical premise at the heart of his book with an illustrative example: a game called Mindball at his local science museum in Vancouver, in which two players sit opposite one another, each wearing an electrode-equipped headband that registers general activity in the brain, and try to mentally push a metal ball from the center of the table to the other player; whoever does this first wins. There is, of course, a rub:
"The motive force — measured by each player’s electrodes, and conveyed to the ball by a magnet hidden underneath the table—is the combination of alpha and theta waves produced by the brain when it’s relaxed: the more alpha and theta waves you produce, the more force you mentally exert on the ball. Essentially, Mindball is a contest of who can be the most calm. It’s fun to watch. The players visibly struggle to relax, closing their eyes, breathing deeply, adopting vaguely yogic postures. The panic they begin to feel as the ball approaches their end of the table is usually balanced out by the overeagerness of their opponent, both players alternately losing their cool as the big metal ball rolls back and forth. You couldn’t wish for a better, more condensed illustration of how difficult it is to try not to try." [...]

Some of the most elusive objects of our incessant pursuits are happiness and spontaneity, both of which are strikingly resistant to conscious pursuit. Two ancient Chinese concepts might be our most powerful tools for resolving this paradox — wu-wei (pronounced oooo-way) and de (pronounced duh). Slingerland explains:
"Wu-wei literally translates as “no trying” or “no doing,” but it’s not at all about dull inaction. In fact, it refers to the dynamic, effortless, and unselfconscious state of mind of a person who is optimally active and effective. People in wu-wei feel as if they are doing nothing, while at the same time they might be creating a brilliant work of art, smoothly negotiating a complex social situation, or even bringing the entire world into harmonious order. For a person in wu-wei, proper and effective conduct follows as automatically as the body gives in to the seductive rhythm of a song. This state of harmony is both complex and holistic, involving as it does the integration of the body, the emotions, and the mind. If we have to translate it, wu-wei is probably best rendered as something like “effortless action” or “spontaneous action.” Being in wu-wei is relaxing and enjoyable, but in a deeply rewarding way that distinguishes it from cruder or more mundane pleasures.  [...]
We have been taught to believe that the best way to achieve our goals is to reason about them carefully and strive consciously to reach them. Unfortunately, in many areas of life this is terrible advice. Many desirable states — happiness, attractiveness, spontaneity — are best pursued indirectly, and conscious thought and effortful striving can actually interfere with their attainment. [...]
Our modern conception of human excellence is too often impoverished, cold, and bloodless. Success does not always come from thinking more rigorously or striving harder."
(Marina Popova, Brain Pickings, on Edward Slingerland's Trying Not to Try: The Art and Science of Spontaneity)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF2_PH "WUWEI 
Non-action. A major concept in Chinese thought, especially in Taoism and Confucianism. It is not inertia, inaction or passivity in the usual sense of the word, but rather the consideration of that which is passive (see this word) in all natural and successful action: it is the right action, therefore also the minimal action, the one that changes only what needs to be changed and does so with the least effort, because it is in harmony with nature or reality. Accompanying the movement, rather than trying to create or reverse it (this is also the logic of judo), letting nature take its course, which can only be commanded by obeying it (Bacon, Taoist master!).), to swim with the current, as the Buddha said, instead of being carried by it or exhausting oneself trying to fight against it, to avoid pointless fights, anger and haste, to act calmly and with "flexibility", as Epictetus also said, to fight peacefully, to let nature or history take its course, if you can, instead of letting your ego or your mind do it... "Lao Tzu said: 'By not doing, there is nothing that is not done. It is the opposite not of will but of voluntarism, not of courage but of pride, not of action but of stupidity. The humility of the wise, the clarity of the man of action. The two are one in wuwei".
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF2.1_PH ONE CAN SAY A "HIGH-QUALITY 'NO'" WITHOUT REACTIVITY, AND ONE'S ACTIONS BECOME INFUSED WITH THE "SOURCE-ENERGY OF BEING," TRANSFORMING THEM INTO A "JOYFUL CELEBRATION."
" Let me illustrate how surrender can work in relationships.  When you become involved in an argument or some conflict situation, perhaps with a partner or someone close to you, start by observing how defensive you become as your own position is attacked, or feel the force of your own aggression as you attack the other person's position.  Observe the attachment to your views and opinions. Feel the mental-emotional energy behind your need to be right and make the other person wrong.  That's the energy of the egoic mind.  You make it conscious by acknowledging it, by feeling it as fully as possible.  Then one day, in the middle of an argument, you will suddenly realize that you have a choice, and you may decide to drop your own reaction -- just to see what happens.  You surrender.  I don't mean dropping the reaction just verbally by saying "Okay, you are right," with a look on your face that says, "I am above all this childish unconsciousness." That's just displacing the resistance to another level, with the egoic mind still in charge, claiming superiority.  I am speaking of letting go of the entire mental-emotional energy field inside you that was fighting for power.
The ego is cunning, so you have to be very alert, very present, and totally honest with yourself to see whether you have truly relinquished your identification with a mental position and so freed yourself from your mind.  If you suddenly feel very light, clear and deeply at peace, that is an unmistakable sign that you have truly surrendered.  Then observe what happens to the other person's mental position as you no longer energize it through resistance.  When identification with mental positions is out of the way, true communication begins.
What about nonresistance in the face of violence, aggression, and the like?
Nonresistance doesn't necessarily mean doing nothing.  All it means is that any "doing" becomes nonreactive.  Remember the deep wisdom underlying the practice of Eastern martial arts: don't resist the opponent's force.  Yield to overcome.
Having said that, "doing nothing" when you are in a state of intense presence is a very powerful transformer and healer of situations and people.  In Taoism, there is a term called wu wei, which is usually translated as "actionless activity" or "sitting quietly doing nothing." In ancient China, this was regarded as one of the highest achievements or virtues.  It is radically different from inactivity in the ordinary state of consciousness, or rather unconsciousness, which stems from fear, inertia, or indecision.  The real "doing nothing" implies inner nonresistance and intense alertness.
On the other hand, if action is required, you will no longer react from your conditioned mind, but you will respond to the situation out of your conscious presence.  In that state, your mind is free of concepts, including the concept of nonviolence.  So who can predict what you will do?
The ego believes that in your resistance lies your strength, whereas in truth resistance cuts you off from Being, the only place of true power.  Resistance is weakness and fear masquerading as strength.  What the ego sees as weakness is your Being in its purity, innocence, and power.  What it sees as strength is weakness.  So the ego exists in a continuous resistance-mode and plays counterfeit roles to cover up your "weakness," which in truth is your power.
Until there is surrender, unconscious role-playing constitutes a large part of human interaction.  In surrender, you no longer need ego defenses and false masks.  You become very simple, very real.  "That's dangerous," says the ego. "You'll get hurt.  You'll become vulnerable." What the ego doesn't know, of course, is that only through the letting go of resistance, through becoming "vulnerable," can you discover your true and essential invulnerability.”
(Eckhart Tolle, The Power Of Now, 1997)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF3_PH "WUWEI

From there we come to the fundamental concept of the Tao: non-action, wuei. Wuwei is obviously not the opposite of the will to live: it is not a will to die: it is what thwarts, evades, disorients the will to live. It is therefore structurally neutral: that which thwarts the paradigm.

In the Tao, Wuwei: sometimes said to mean: favouring the "spontaneous" over the "voluntary". It is true: freedom from will. But "spontaneous" is not good: for us it has a wild, instinctive, anti-intellectual connotation. Wou-wei: not directing, not finalising one's power, leaving it in place. For example: the source of the breath (Lien Ki) is superior to the conduct of the breath (Hing K'i). Or again, not using one's strength: for example, not using one's wisdom or knowledge, or using it to a minimum, purely for protection and prudence. Cf. Pyrrho: "He philosophised according to the reasoning of doubt, without acting recklessly".

The profound attitude of Wuwei Tao = not to choose. Now there are two kinds of "not choosing": a "not choosing" that is thrown around, panicked, shameful, frowned upon, as opposed to a "not choosing" that is accepted, calm, I would say. The latter: extremely difficult because it goes against public opinion, damages the imago → it must therefore be accepted → Tao was very aware of this difficulty: a poem (Tao + Zen) says: "The perfect Tao offers no difficulty, / Except that it avoids choosing". This non-choice is not a sublimating abstinence, an asceticism, a spirituality: "Do not oppose the sensual world". One can clearly see the paradox of Wou-wei (for us Westerners): a powerful subversion of all our moral values, and especially "progressive" values, in this word: "The wise man does not fight" (remember that this is a pagan thought: the absence of fighting is not seen as a gain in heaven), or another form of paradox, a socialised form. For Leang Li (Grenier): "He was ready to follow everything [...] For him, everything was in a state of destruction, everything was under construction. This is called tranquillity in disorder. Calm in disorder means perfection".

3) FIGURES OF THE WEST

Our entire Western world: moral ideology of the will, of wanting (to seize, to dominate, to live, to impose one's truth, etc.). The West: land of proselytism → therefore, obviously, Western figures of Wou-wei are rare and above all partial (if there were a Taoist sage, by definition we would not know him): only moments, tendencies, aspects of a few individuals. [...]

4) THE SACRED

[...] Grenier, Tao, p. 103: “By producing forests, the mountain attracts those who will strip it bare. By letting its fat drip, the roast activates the fire that grills it. The cinnamon tree is cut because its bark is a sought-after condiment. The varnish tree is felled to rob it of its precious sap. Almost all human beings imagine  that it is a good thing to be considered capable of something. In reality, it is an advantage to be considered incapable of anything.

Wonderful! The basic status of the sacred: to be good for nothing! The only danger is that the sacred is not eternal: there may be societies where it is considered immoral to do nothing and where useless and sacred oaks are cut down. The Taoist ideal would be to be sacred without it being seen: a contradiction in terms: an invisible Wuwei, in other words cheating from the moment it is said.”
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, Course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF3.1_PH “TAO: IMAGE OF THE MIRROR
It is not the same theme as in our culture, where the mirror is above all a symbol of the ego, of Narcissus. Chuang-Tzu: “The Perfect One uses his mind as a mirror; he neither follows things up nor goes to meet them (as politeness requires); he responds to them without holding them back. This is what enables him to take charge of all things without them harming him [...] To him who is in himself without things remaining in him, things show themselves as they are; his movement is apathetic as is that of water, his immobility like that of the mirror, his response like that of the echo...”. 

Note: 
a) The Tao mirror does not have the passive and mechanical aspect of the Western mirror (a mirror that speaks: only in fairy magic): it responds (without holding back), it has the beauty, the mysterious activity of “still and clear water”;
b) There is action (responding) but no appropriation (wanting to grasp): “It responds to them without holding them back.”
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, Course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF3.1.a_PH “SITTING DOWN

[...] In Zen
Sitting down is associated with the idea of non-profit: Mushotoku: no profit, no desire to take (mu = no + shotoku = profit) Shikantaza sitting down without aim, without profit. Despite its strong negativity, do not flatten the gesture (the posture): should not be flattened: sitting is active = acting, the antonym of falling on the spot: Beckett (All those who fall): “Ah, to spread oneself on the ground like dung and not move anymore”. For the seated person thinks, watches (viget animus → corpus sentit), enjoys in laziness. → Dream of a whole day, just once, completely seated: without any demands, tasks or responsibilities. A word that I have always wanted to use as an epigraph for texts and books: Zenrin poem: “Sitting peacefully, doing nothing, / spring comes and the grass grows of its own accord. [...] 

"To sit peacefully and do nothing" = in reality means to remove oneself completely from the realm of guilt: perhaps impossible for a Westerner: to do nothing, without guilt, without debt: the persistent idea that one owes time to something, to someone. Christian posture: on your knees. Fascist posture: standing. Asian posture: sitting → therefore: a posture, as I said, that is completely symbolic and completely efficient, therefore one that goes beyond and exhausts the symbolic, without resorting to any empiricism (so to speak).
We can further clarify and update "sitting". Here I am sitting, and this is how I see it: if I don't like what is masterful, if it makes me angry and hurt (although it is regularly thrown in my face as if I were responsible for it), it is not because of the "monologue" (I am convinced that I am actually in dialogue with those who are there, especially if they are willing to come back regularly), it is because of a deviation from "sitting": the "front-sitting"; it is the pulpit-room arrangement that makes the masterful (school: better). In Zen, one does not sit in front of anything; in fact, this is what defines Zen. Sitting in front of nothing: squaring the circle: in restaurants, on trains, in life, there is always someone who comes and sits in front of you."
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, Course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

E1.7.B.3.6.c_REF4_TH A LIFE OF ONE’S OWN: A PENETRATING CENTURY-OLD FIELD GUIDE TO SELF-POSSESSION, MINDFUL PERCEPTION, AND THE ART OF KNOWING WHAT YOU REALLY WANT.
“One must know what one wants to be,” the eighteenth-century French mathematician Émilie du Châtelet wrote in weighing the nature of genius. “In the latter endeavors irresolution produces false steps, and in the life of the mind confused ideas.” And yet that inner knowing is the work of a lifetime, for our confusions are ample and our missteps constant amid a world that is constantly telling us who we are and who we ought to be — a world which, in the sobering words of E.E. Cummings, “is doing its best, night and day, to make you everybody else.” Try as we might not to be blinded by society’s prescriptions for happiness, we are still social creatures porous to the values of our peers — creatures surprisingly and often maddeningly myopic about the things we believe furnish our completeness as human beings, habitually aspiring to the wrong things for the wrong reasons.

In 1926, more than a decade before a team of Harvard psychologists commenced history’s longest and most revelatory study of human happiness and half a century before the humanistic philosopher Erich Fromm penned his classic on the art of living, the British psychoanalyst and writer Marion Milner (February 1, 1900–May 29, 1998) undertook a seven-year experiment in living, aimed at unpeeling the existential rind of all we chronically mistake for fulfillment — prestige, pleasure, popularity — to reveal the succulent, pulsating core of what makes for genuine happiness. Along her journey of “doubts, delays, and expeditions on false trails,” which she chronicled in a diary with a field scientist’s rigor of observation, Milner ultimately discovered that we are beings profoundly different from what we imagine ourselves to be — that the things we pursue most frantically are the least likely to give us lasting joy and contentment, but there are other, truer things that we can train ourselves to attend to in the elusive pursuit of happiness.”
(Maria Popova’s review of Marion Milner’s A Life of One’s Own, 1934 - in The Marginalian)

”Let no one think it is an easy way because it is concerned with moments of happiness rather than with stern duty or high moral endeavour. For what is really easy, as I found, is to blind one’s eyes to what one really likes, to drift into accepting one’s wants ready-made from other people, and to evade the continual day to day sifting of values. And finally, let no one undertake such an experiment who is not prepared to find himself more of a fool than he thought. […]

As soon as I began to study my perception, to look at my own experience, I found that there were different ways of perceiving and that the different ways provided me with different facts. There was a narrow focus which meant seeing life as if from blinkers and with the centre of awareness in my head; and there was a wide focus which meant knowing with the whole of my body, a way of looking which quite altered my perception of whatever I saw. And I found that the narrow focus way was the way of reason. If one was in the habit of arguing about life it was very difficult not to approach sensation with the same concentrated attention and so shut out its width and depth and height. But it was the wide focus way that made me happy. […]

I want to feel myself part of things, of the great drift and swirl: not cut off, missing things, like being sent to bed early as a child, the blinds being drawn while the sun and cheerful voices came through the chink from the garden. […]

I want… the patterns and colourings on the vase on my table took on a new and intense vitality — I want to be so harmonious in myself that I can think of others and share their experiences. […]

I had felt my life to be of a dull dead-level mediocrity, with the sense of real and vital things going on round the corner, out in the streets, in other people’s lives. For I had taken the surface ripples for all there was, when actually happenings of vital importance to me had been going on, not somewhere away from me, but just underneath the calm surface of my own mind. Though some of these discoveries were not entirely pleasant, bringing with them echoes of terror and despair, at least they gave me a sense of being alive. […]

If just looking could be so satisfying, why was I always striving to have things or to get things done? Certainly I had never suspected that the key to my private reality might lie in so apparently simple a skill as the ability to let the senses roam unfettered by purposes. I began to wonder whether eyes and ears might not have a wisdom of their own. […]

I had been continually exhorted to define my purpose in life, but I was now beginning to doubt whether life might not be too complex a thing to be kept within the bounds of a single formulated purpose, whether it would not burst its way out, or if the purpose were too strong, perhaps grow distorted like an oak whose trunk has been encircled with an iron band. I began to guess that my self’s need was for an equilibrium, for sun, but not too much, for rain, but not always… So I began to have an idea of my life, *not as the slow shaping of achievement to fit my preconceived purposes, but as the gradual discovery and growth of a purpose which I did not know.* I wrote: “It will mean walking in a fog for a bit, but it’s the only way which is not a presumption, forcing the self into a theory.” […]

I did not know that I could only get the most out of life by giving myself up to it. […]

Here then was a deadlock. I wanted to get the most out of life, but the more I tried to grasp, the more I felt that I was ever outside, missing things. At that time I could not understand at all that my real purpose might be to learn to have no purposes. […]

I had at least begun to guess that my greatest need might be to let go and be free from the drive after achievement — if only I dared. I had also guessed that perhaps when I had let these go, then I might be free to become aware of some other purpose that was more fundamental, not self-imposed private ambitions but some thing which grew out of the essence of one’s own nature. People said: ‘Oh, be yourself at all costs’. But I had found that it was not so easy to know just what one’s self was. It was far easier to want what other people seemed to want and then imagine that the choice was one’s own. […]

It struck me as odd that it had taken me so long to reach a feeling of sureness that there was something in me that would get on with the job of living without my continual tampering. I suppose I did not really reach it until I had discovered how to sink down beneath the level of chattering thoughts and simply feel what it meant to be alive. […]

I came to the conclusion then that “continual mindfulness” could certainly not mean that my little conscious self should be entirely responsible for marshalling and arranging all my thoughts, for it simply did not know enough. It must mean, not a sergeant-major-like drilling of thoughts, but a continual readiness to look and readiness to accept whatever came…. Whenever I did so manage to win its services I began to suspect that thought, which I had always before looked on as a cart-horse, to be driven, whipped and plodding between shafts, might be really a Pegasus, so suddenly did it alight beside me from places I had no knowledge of. […]

I had just begun to ponder over the fact that all the things which I had found to be sources of happiness seemed to depend upon the capacity to relax all straining, to widen my attention beyond the circle of personal interest, and to look detachedly at my own experience. I had just realized that this relaxing and detachment must depend on a fundamental sense of security, and yet that I could apparently never feel safe enough to do it, because there was an urge in me which I had dimly perceived but had never yet been able to face. It was then that the idea occurred to me that until you have, once at least, faced everything you know — the whole universe — with utter giving in, and let all that is “not you” flow over and engulf you, there can be no lasting sense of security. […]

By continual watching and expression I must learn to observe my thought and maintain a vigilance, not against “wrong” thoughts, but against refusal to recognize any thought. Further, this introspection meant continual expression, not continual analysis; it meant that I must bring my thoughts and feelings up in their wholeness, not argue about them and try to pretend they were something different from what they were.

I had also learnt how to know what I wanted; to know that this is not a simple matter of momentary decision, but that it needs a rigorous watching and fierce discipline, if the clamouring conflict of likes is to be welded into a single desire. It had taught me that my day-to-day personal “wants” were really the expression of deep underlying needs, though often the distorted expression because of the confusions of blind thinking. I had learnt that if I kept my thoughts still enough and looked beneath them, then I might sometimes know what was the real need, feel it like a child leaping in the womb, though so remotely that I might easily miss it when over-busy with purposes. Really, then, I had found that there was an intuitive sense of how to live. For I had been forced to the conclusion that there was more in the mind than just reason and blind thinking, if only you knew how to look for it; the unconscious part of my mind seemed to be definitely something more than a storehouse for the confusions and shames I dared not face. […]

It was only when I was actively passive, and content to wait and watch, that I really knew what I wanted. […]

Blind thinking… could make me pretend I was being true to myself when really I was only being true to an infantile fear and confusion of situations; and the more confused it was the more it would call to its aid a sense of conviction. Yet for all its parade there was as much in common between its certainties and the fundamental sense of my own happiness as between the windy flappings of a newspaper in the gutter and the poise of a hovering kestrel. And only by experience of both, by digging down deep enough and watching sincerely enough, could I be sure of recognizing the difference.

By keeping a diary of what made me happy I had discovered that happiness came when I was most widely aware.
