admitting ourselves to be “Nature” too.
Whenever we lose track of our own obsessions,
our self-concerns, because we drift for a minute,
an hour even, of pure (almost pure)
response to that insouciant life:
cloud, bird, fox, the flow of light, the dancing
pilgrimage of water, vast stillness
of spellbound ephemerae on a lit windowpane,
animal voices, mineral hum, wind
conversing with rain, ocean with rock, stuttering
of fire to coal—then something tethered
in us, hobbled like a donkey on its patch
of gnawed grass and thistles, breaks free.
No one discovers
just where we’ve been, when we’re caught up again
into our own sphere (where we must
return, indeed, to evolve our destinies)
—but we have changed, a little."
(Denise Levertov, Sojourns in parallel world)

E1.7.C.1.3_REF3_PO "BEGINNING MY STUDIES

Beginning my studies the first step pleas'd me so much, 
The mere fact consciousness, these forms, the power of motion, 
The least insect or animal, the senses, eyesight, love, 
The first step I say awed me and pleas'd me so much, 
I have hardly gone and hardly wish'd to go any farther, 
But stop and loiter all the time to sing it in ecstatic songs."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.2. WE CAN OVERCOME THE ILLUSION OF THE SEPARATE EGO THROUGH RECOGNIZING OUR INTERCONNECTEDNESS WITH THE WORLD AROUND US.
Dissolving the ego leads to a feeling of being one with everything and it requires intensified self-consciousness and awareness of the world, not a simple act of will.

E1.7.C.2.1. EVERYTHING THAT HAPPENS REQUIRES SOMEONE OR SOMETHING TO HAPPEN TO, AND THERE IS NO FATE WITHOUT SOMETHING OR SOMEONE IT ACTS UPON.  With the dissolution of the ego, we feel a sense of passivity at first but realize that we are both the leaf and the wind, and the world outside of our skin is as much a part of us as our inner world. We feel lighter and more in tune with our surroundings, and time no longer feels like a force to fight against.

E1.7.C.2.1_REF1_PH "THERE IS NO FATE UNLESS THERE IS SOMEONE OR SOMETHING TO BE FATED. There is no trap without someone to be caught. [...] Thus when the line between myself and what happens to me is dissolved and there is no stronghold left for an ego even as a passive witness, I find myself not in a world but as a world which is neither compulsive nor capricious. What happens is neither automatic nor arbitrary: it just happens [...]
In immediate contrast to the old feeling, there is indeed a certain passivity to the sensation, as if you were a leaf blown along by the wind, until you realize that you are both the leaf and the wind. The world outside your skin is just as much you as the world inside: they move together inseparably, and at first you feel a little out of control because the world outside is so much vaster than the world inside. Yet you soon discover that you are able to go ahead with ordinary activities—to work and make decisions as ever, though somehow this is less of a drag. Your body is no longer a corpse which the ego has to animate and lug around. There is a feeling of the ground holding you up, and of hills lifting you when you climb them. Air breathes itself in and out of your lungs, and instead of looking and listening, light and sound come to you on their own. Eyes see and ears hear as wind blows and water flows. All space becomes your mind. Time carries you along like a river, but never flows out of the present: the more it goes, the more it stays, and you no longer have to fight or kill it."
(Alan Watts, The Book: On the Taboo Against Knowing Who You Are, 1966)

E1.7.C.2.1_REF2_PH "SO LONG AS THE MIND BELIEVES IN THE POSSIBILITY OF ESCAPE FROM WHAT IT IS AT THIS MOMENT, THERE CAN BE NO FREEDOM. […]
It sounds as if it were the most abject fatalism to have to admit that I am what I am, and that no escape or division is possible. It seems that if I am afraid, then I am 'stuck' with fear. But in fact I am chained to the fear only so long as I am trying to get away from it. On the other hand, when I do not try to get away I discover that there is nothing 'stuck' or fixed about the reality of the moment. When I am aware of this feeling without naming it, without calling it 'fear', 'bad', 'negative', etc., it changes instantly into something else, and life moves freely ahead. The feeling no longer perpetuates itself by creating the feeler behind it."
(Alan Watts, The Wisdom of Insecurity: A Message for an Age of Anxiety, 1951)

E1.7.C.2.1_REF4_PH "WE WERE NEVER REALLY BORN, WE WILL NEVER REALLY DIE. It has nothing to do with the imaginary idea of a personal self, other selves, many selves everywhere: Self is only an idea, a mortal idea. That which passes into everything is one thing. It’s a dream already ended. There’s nothing to be afraid of and nothing to be glad about. I know this from staring at mountains months on end. They never show any expression, they are like empty space. Do you think the emptiness of space will ever crumble away? Mountains will crumble, but the emptiness of space, which is the one universal essence of mind, the vast awakenerhood, empty and awake, will never crumble away because it was never born."
(Jack Kerouac in a letter sentto his first wife, Edie Kerouac Parker, in late January of 1957, a decade after their marriage had been annulled. Found in The Portable Jack Kerouac)

E1.7.C.2.1_REF5_PH TRUE FREEDOM AND SELF-MASTERY "The man of virtue, of knowledge, has his ego of the virtuous man, his ego of knowledge, and it is this sattvic ego that he seeks to satisfy; it is for himself that he seeks virtue and knowledge. But it is only when we cease to satisfy the ego, to think and want from the ego, from the limited 'I' within us, that there is real freedom. In other words, freedom, the supreme mastery of the self, begins when we see and hold above the natural self the supreme self for which the ego is an obstructive veil, an impenetrable shadow. And this can only be when we see the unique Self within us sitting above nature, when we make our individual being one with it in being and in consciousness, and when it is no longer in its individual nature of action that it is the instrument of a supreme will, the only will that is truly free".
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.C.2.1_REF6_PO “THE WORLD HAS NEED OF YOU

everything here 
seems to need us

Rainer Maria Rilke

I can hardly imagine it
as I walk to the lighthouse, feeling the ancient 
prayer of my arms swinging
in counterpoint to my feet.
Here I am, suspended
between the sidewalk and twilight,
the sky dimming so fast it seems alive.
What if you felt the invisible
tug between you and everything?
A boy on a bicycle rides by,
his white shirt open, flaring
behind him like wings.
It’s a hard time to be human. We know too much 
and too little. Does the breeze need us?
The cliffs? The gulls?
If you’ve managed to do one good thing,
the ocean doesn’t care.
But when Newton’s apple fell toward the earth, 
the earth, ever so slightly, fell
toward the apple.”
(Ellen Bass, Like a beggar, 2014)

E1.7.C.2.1_REF7_PH NICOLAS GRIMALDI ARGUES THAT TRUE FREEDOM REQUIRES LIBERATING OURSELVES FROM THE "TENACIOUS ILLUSION" OF THE SUBSTANTIAL EGO—which traps us in the anguish of time and desire—by humbly recognizing that we are merely transient mediators through whom the anonymous life of humanity flows.

"Because we represent everything in time, we can only conceive of a relationship between the present and the future as that between cause and effect. Thus, in the present, we perceive only the means by which we can accomplish our goals. Here, pure immediacy is transformed into mediation for us. Unable to recognize reality in the present, we can no longer see anything in the real world except the material of the possible. Our desire, the relationship we project onto the present, is at the center of our representations, just as our self is at the center of the world. The contradictions of our desires make us experience the world as contradictory and torn, and the anguish of our selves darkens the world.
We understand that we can never be free of anything unless we are free of time. However, it is equally clear that we cannot be freed from time without first being freed from desire. Furthermore, we will never be freed from desire unless we are freed from our ego. In this regard, Schopenhauer's thinking aligns with that of Ashtâvakra and the Bhagavad Gita. As the most original representation, the ego is also the most spontaneous and tenacious illusion. Since our consciousness is the origin of all possible representations, it is only natural that we experience it as the foundation of what it represents. The world ceases to exist for me when I close my eyes. I am equally free to look at the Louvre on my right or the hill of Chaillot on my left. The sensations and feelings they arouse are not qualities or properties of what I see. No representation would be possible if the evanescent diversity of all intuitions did not relate to the permanence and identity of a self. All consciousness is therefore transcendent and irreducible to its object. It is only natural that I move from the reflexive certainty of being the subject of my representations to the metaphysical belief of being their substance. As consciousness, I am a logical and psychological subject. But as an individual, I am merely a transition, a passage, a mediation. I am not a substance in any way.
What is truly mine that I did not receive? My life, my language, my culture, its models, its myths, its prejudices, and its most ordinary representations; its technology; and all of those objects, each of which shapes one of my gestures—everything was tradition to me. No matter how freely or how I appropriated them, my use of them spread imperceptibly around me until it vanished and became indistinguishable in the anonymous life of humanity. Whether we like it or not, life wears itself out and destroys itself within each individual while simultaneously being transmitted, diffused, and spread through them. History, life, and time are fulfilled through us. If a first freedom consists of wanting to exercise one's reign, then a second freedom, humble and lucid, consists of not presuming or claiming anything and serving life while knowing that life uses us. While the first freedom, that of egotism, claims to transform one's life into destiny, the second freedom, freed from this naïveté, fulfills a destiny that is not its own and has no more importance than responsibility.
 Recognizing ourselves as mere mediators and knowing that we have no reality or destiny of our own, like a wave on the sea, grants our ego only the identity of a tenacious appearance. It frees us from it. Of course, an appearance of reality has the reality of an appearance. We are not saying that the self is nothing; we are saying that it is nothing worth obsessing over. Once this illusion is lifted, everything will be freedom."
(Nicolas Grimaldi, Ambiguities of Freedom, 1999)

E1.7.C.2.2. THERE IS NO WAY OF GETTING RID OF THE FEELING OF SEPARATENESS BY A SO-CALLED “ACT OF WILL,” BY TRYING TO FORGET YOURSELF, OR BY GETTING ABSORBED IN SOME OTHER INTEREST.
"In recognizing this lies the cure for the illusion of the separate ego — but this recognition can’t be willed into existence, since the will itself is part of the ego: Just as science overcame its purely atomistic and mechanical view of the world through more science, the ego-trick must be overcome through intensified self-consciousness."

E1.7.C.2.2_REF1_PH "OUR WHOLE KNOWLEDGE OF THE WORLD IS, IN ONE SENSE, SELF-KNOWLEDGE. For knowing is a translation of external events into bodily processes, and especially into states of the nervous system and the brain: we know the world in terms of the body, and in accordance with its structure."
(Alan Watts, The Book: On the Taboo Against Knowing Who You Are, 1966)
"In recognizing this lies the cure for the illusion of the separate ego — but this recognition can’t be willed into existence, since the will itself is part of the ego: Just as science overcame its purely atomistic and mechanical view of the world through more science, the ego-trick must be overcome through intensified self-consciousness. For there is no way of getting rid of the feeling of separateness by a so-called “act of will,” by trying to forget yourself, or by getting absorbed in some other interest."
(Maria Popova, Brain Pickings)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF1.1_PH "DECREATION 
[...]  Creation is an act of love and it is perpetual. At each moment our existence is God’s love for us. But God can only love himself. His love for us is love for himself through us. Thus, he who gives us our being loves in us the acceptance of not being.

[...]  Renunciation. Imitation of God’s renunciation in creation. In a sense God renounces being everything. We should renounce being something. That is our only good.

[...]  We participate in the creation of the world by decreating ourselves.

[...]  We only possess what we renounce; what we do not renounce escapes from us. In this sense, we cannot possess anything whatever unless it passes through God.

[...]  We have to be nothing in order to be in our right place in the whole."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF1.2_PH "PROPOSITION 36
The intellectual love of the mind towards God is that very love of God whereby God loves himself, not in so far as he is infinite, but in so far as he can be explained through the essence of the human mind regarded under the form of eternity ; in other words, the intellectual love of the mind towards God is part of the infinite love wherewith God loves himself.
Proof.
This love of the mind must be referred to the activities of the mind [...] ; it is itself, indeed, an activity whereby the mind regards itself accompanied by the idea of God as cause [...] ; that is [...], an activity whereby God, in so far as he can be explained through the human mind, regards himself accompanied by the idea of himself ; therefore [...], this love of the mind is part of the infinite love wherewith God loves himself."
(Baruch Spinosa, Ethics part five)

Read the Ethics online: http://app.ethica-spinoza.net/en/text

E1.7.C.2.2_REF2_PH "THE PROCESS OF 'DECREATION,' WHICH IS THE ONLY PATH TO SALVATION, IS THE WORK OF GRACE AND NOT OF WILL. Man does not rise to heaven by pulling himself up by his bootstraps. The will is only good for menial tasks: it ensures the correct exercise of the natural virtues, which are prerequisites for the work of grace, like the plowman's effort in sowing. But the divine seed comes from somewhere else... Like Plato and Malebranche, Simone Weil attaches much more importance to attention than to will in this area. "One must be indifferent to good and evil, but one must be truly indifferent, that is, one must shine the light of attention on both equally."
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF2.1_PH "GRAVITY AND GRACE
[...] Attitude of supplication: I must necessarily turn to something other than myself since it is a question of being delivered from self.
Any attempt to gain this deliverance by means of my own energy would be like the efforts of a cow which pulls at its hobble and so falls onto its knees."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF2.2_PH "ILLUSIONS
[...] Why is the determination to fight against a prejudice a sure sign that one is full of it? Such a determination necessarily arises from an obsession. It constitutes an utterly sterile effort to get rid of it. In such a case the light of attention is the only thing which is effective, and it is not compatible with a polemical intention."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF3_PH "CONSENT FOR A MOMENT TO NOT KNOWING ANYTHING, to not deciding anything, to consent to staying where you are. To consent is something that is somewhat neglected, it is to allow life to act fully, to come to us as if we have cut ourselves and all we have to do is wait for the wound to heal - that is what trust is. At first this gesture may seem absurd, but it is necessary to go through it in order to discover the advantage of trusting something of which one cannot be absolutely sure. If I trust what you tell me, it is precisely because I am not absolutely sure, and that is the beauty of life: "It is time for the stone to finally decide to blossom".
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again, program broadcast on France Culture on 07/03/2019)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF3.1_PH "PAUL CELAN WROTE: 'IT IS TIME FOR THE STONE TO FINALLY DECIDE TO BLOSSOM' How can what is closed open itself? How can the seemingly dead become alive? Paul Celan invites us to find out. This seems extraordinary, but unrealistically promising. Paul Celan invites us to an experiment so profound that it requires a little attention. To understand it, let's think about sleep. Have you ever suffered from insomnia? If this has happened to you, you know that it is not enough to want to fall asleep; in fact, the more you want to sleep, the less it happens. You feel like a stone that nothing can make blossom. For sleep to take you, it is important, for example, to trust your body. It only wants to sleep, it is your will that holds it back - that is the key - but we mistakenly believe that an increase in will is necessary here. If we are anxious, we should do something to stop being so; if we have some kind of addiction, a little willpower would enable us to overcome it; if we suffer from procrastination, we also believe that it is because we lack willpower - which is completely wrong. And it is because he knows this that Paul Celan invites us to put our trust in the life that is there in the stone of our sorrows, but which remains latent, invisible. This takes patience and trust. Do you want to do it? It's very easy, consent for a moment to not knowing anything, to not deciding anything, to consent to staying where you are. To consent is something that is somewhat neglected, it is to allow life to act fully, to come to us as if we have cut ourselves and all we have to do is wait for the wound to heal - that is what trust is. At first this gesture may seem absurd, but it is necessary to go through it in order to discover the advantage of trusting something of which one cannot be absolutely sure. If I trust what you tell me, it is precisely because I am not absolutely sure, and that is the beauty of life: "It is time for the stone to finally decide to blossom".
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again, program broadcast on France Culture on 07/03/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/3-minutes-de-philosophie-pour-redevenir-humain/paul-celan-ecrivait-il-est-temps-que-la-pierre-se-resolve-enfin-a-fleurir-8011896

E1.7.C.2.2_REF4_PH REMBRANDT'S SELF-PORTRAITS SEEK PRESENCE RATHER THAN SUBJECTIVE EXPLORATION OF THE SELF Rembrandt's portraits of Narcissus are a search for presence. For Rembrandt, it is not about vanity but about self-love, a deep sense of peace that allows us to recognise our own humanity and make peace with our own weaknesses, inadequacies and even clumsiness. Historians have discovered that Rembrandt's self-portraits were not self-portraits, but portraits of Rembrandt himself, because at that time self-portraiture was not a subjective exploration of our states of mind, but rather a search for presence. In short, the myth of Narcissus is important for understanding the search for presence in painting and in our relationship with ourselves. Being narcissistic is not a problem in itself, but rather a way of turning inwards in order to love oneself and others better.


E1.7.C.2.2_REF4.1_PH "NARCISSUS, NOT GUILTY. NARCISSUS TO CELEBRATE BEAUTY AND REDISCOVER CULTURE When I set out to study the various ways in which Narcissus has nourished our history, I was most surprised to discover the great role he played in the Renaissance. At that time, Narcissus became the myth that allowed us to understand the meaning of painting. Alberti wrote: "I used to tell my friends that the inventor of painting, according to the formula of the poets, was that Narcissus who saw himself transformed into a flower. What is painting if not the art of embracing the surface of a fountain? In the Renaissance, therefore, Narcissus is not only innocent but also the bard of the forgotten truth of painting. This invocation of Narcissus is unprecedented; Alberti invents a new theme and links it to what led the Renaissance to give painting a new role. The dignity of painting, he tells us, is represented by Narcissus. And why? Because Narcissus marvels at a face he believes to be real, before realising that it is only his own image. Like the mirror, painting shows us the world, but unlike the mirror, which only reproduces and duplicates reality, painting imitates and brings to life. The meaning of this word, imitation, has played an important role in the history of the West - and especially at this moment. To imitate is not to copy slavishly, mechanically, but to render true. Narcissus reminds us that painting should not be content with merely reflecting what it shows. In this sense, the philosopher and historian Louis Marin explains that "painting is the transformation of the flesh of things into the fragile surface of colours and the fiction of their presence", there like Narcissus at the water's edge. The most astonishing thing for me was to see these lines written by Alberti, whom I had identified as the thinker of the art of representation. You know the criticism of modern artists in the face of this Renaissance illusionism, whether it be Pablo Picasso, Gicometti, Fernand Léger - it is that the Renaissance illusionist project was a limited project, and it was the adventure of poetic modernism that opened up our horizons. But when I thought about the link that Alberti makes between painting and Narcissus, I realised that I had not paid enough attention to the fact that the Renaissance project is certainly a certain objectification of reality, linked to a mathematisation of the world - but in the name of a search for presence. Basically, the logic of representation that the Renaissance promotes is in the name of this presence, it is what is being sought... It is with this understanding of the Renaissance that we must begin to understand the portraits that Rembrandt painted of himself (around a hundred in 40 years). Why did he paint so many portraits of himself? Rembrandt shows no vanity in these portraits. But what does it mean in the everyday sense to be narcissistic? Is there a danger of loving oneself too much? To think so is to misunderstand the meaning of love. You can never love too much. You can love in a jealous, perverse, possessive way, which are distortions, even counterfeits of love. The problem with being narcissistic is that you don't turn to others? But is that true? The opposite is also true: how can you love someone if you don't love yourself? The problem with being narcissistic is that you are obsessed with yourself? But loving yourself means that you are no longer obsessed with yourself; it is a deep sense of peace. Being obsessed with one's image, identity and social status is more a sign of difficulty in being oneself than of overconfidence. What would be the problem with being narcissistic? None. On the other hand, the benefits are obvious: to know and make peace with the humanity within, which means making peace with our own weaknesses, inadequacies and, sometimes, our own mistakes.

Historians have discovered that in Rembrandt's time it was not so much a self-portrait as a portrait of Rembrandt himself. What difference does that make? The self-portrait appeared in the Romantic period and implied a subjective exploration of our states of mind, which was a completely new approach at the time. Portraiture at that time was the representation of an individual in his or her social function; Rembrandt inherited this concept from his illustrious predecessors. But one only has to look at Rembrandt's portraits to see that, although he was familiar with this tradition and sometimes alluded to it, in his self-portraits, even those directly inspired by these models, he departed fundamentally from it; he made any social distance between them disappear completely. We are not confronted with a social type, but with a human being; he shows us a man without a history. What is striking about these portraits is their existential intensity, their gravity, their depth, which goes beyond any particular expression. He devotes himself to exploring the enigma of our own existence, in a movement of well-understood narcissism - as did Montaigne, who simply painted himself in order to see the man in himself. The works of Rembrandt and Montaigne are thus our mirrors, showing us how we are, not how they were. They narcissise us by revealing the adventure of the depth of the encounter with oneself, which opens up unprecedented paths of truth, authenticity and tolerance.

How does Rembrandt manage to show us the human condition? He renounces what had hitherto constituted the art of painting in the West: he renounces the exuberance of colour (from now on there is no red or blue), he renounces the precision of the line that defines great art, he even renounces the representation of material, which he had mastered like no other in his youth. It was the simplification of the means that allowed him to accentuate the tension he wanted to achieve. He now used thin layers of very smooth paint, superimposed on each other, but also reliefs that left very visible marks. Then we see the brushstroke - this juxtaposition in the same work of finished and seemingly improvised parts that create an imbalance, a tension. Because life is tension, conflict, trial, Rembrandt is here the antithesis of the cult of cuteness so prevalent today. It is the negation of all tension, the sweet illusion that all would be well, that all should be well... It may not seem like a big deal, but cuteness is the Trojan horse of nihilism, because by denying the tension at the heart of existence, the difficulties that every human being has to go through, we are lying. To say that life is free of conflict is to deceive us, to lead us astray, to prevent us from finding the impetus to move forward. In this sense, the aesthetics of the cute, or rather the ideology of the cute, invites us to helplessness, to an irresponsible infantilism. In this sense, we should ask ourselves what it means to place a sculpture by Jeff Koons, whose work is precisely the epitome of the cute, in the centre of Paris? [Jeff Koons, Tulip Bouquet in Paris: Is this kitsch bouquet the best way to pay tribute to the victims of the attacks? Does it pay tribute to the tragedy and hardship that ended their lives?"
(Fabrice Midal, Narcisse accused but not guilty, series of programs broadcast on France Culture in July and August 2018)

Listen to the podcat in French: https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/narcisse-accuse-non-coupable

E1.7.C.2.2_REF4.1.a_PH "DELIGHT IN ONESELF. “Delight in an enterprise,” they say; but in truth it is delight in oneself, by means of an enterprise."
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 501, 1878)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF4.1.b_CUR REMBRANDT'S SELF-PORTRAITS SEEK PRESENCE RATHER THAN SUBJECTIVE EXPLORATION OF THE SELF
47 paintings, 31 engravings & 6 drawings 
Rembrandt's self-portraits

Access the gallery: https://www.wikiwand.com/fr/Autoportraits_de_Rembrandt

E1.7.C.2.2_REF5_PH NARCISSISM CAN BE SEEN AS A HUMANISTIC APPROACH THAT CAN LEAD TO THE LOSS OF OUR CERTAINTIES AND OUR BLOCKAGES. Encountering oneself can mean losing oneself, no longer knowing who we are, letting go of habits and restrictions. The artist Henri Michaux explored this reality by showing that entering into oneself is not self-knowledge, but rather an exploration of the unknown. Gaston Bachelard and Roland Barthes also explored the idea that encountering ourselves is essential to changing our relationship with others and with the world. Generalised narcissism can make all people aware of their beauty and the beauty of the world.
(Fabrice Midal, Narcisse accused but not guilty, series of programs broadcast on France Culture in July and August 2018)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF5.1_PH "NARCISSISM AS A HUMANISTIC APPROACH ALSO MEANS SOMETIMES LOSS OF OUR CERTIFICATIONS, OUR BENCHMARKS - everything we think we know about ourselves. In fact, encountering ourselves also means losing this. Sometimes Narcissus invites us not to know ourselves, but to forget ourselves. Henri Michaux is one of the artists who has explored this reality most scrupulously. He has constantly undermined everything that could fix meaning, that could define us as "someone somewhere".
That somebody is us; and we are all those somebodies. Michaux shows that to enter into oneself is not to know oneself, but, on the contrary, to enter into the unknown, to no longer know who one is, to free oneself from rules, traps, habits of thought and behaviour, from all confinement. Michaux thus approaches the enigma of being, not to solve it, but to explore it, to celebrate it, the exact opposite of what we are told by the sloppy psychology that always wants to define us, to profile us, to put us in boxes. When I meet myself, I am seized by an immense vertigo, I am so huge!
Narcisse, who sees himself in the fountain, feels that his beauty is not complete; so he looks at himself and discovers the profound sense of imagination that is at the heart of the poetic and creative experience. As Gaston Bachelard says: "Narcisse does not say I love myself as I am, he says I am as I love myself". I am exuberant because I love myself passionately, I want to appear, so I must increase my adornment. In this way life illustrates itself, life covers itself with images, life grows, it transforms being. The imagination opens to the most distant metaphors, it participates in the life of all flowers. Is this not the meaning of culture? Culture today is so damaged, reduced to a kind of general, vague culture - references and intellectual games, peripheral problems. But culture is the space in which we encounter ourselves, in which we discover what we did not know, or did not know very well, about ourselves. No one can live without such an encounter, no one can live without being spoken to about oneself in one way or another. To believe, as we do, that this movement is a mistake, an evil, is suicidal - we need it to be able to meet ourselves, to meet the other that is in us: more courageous than we are, and sometimes more fragile. This was understood so well by Roland Barthes, who spent his life trying to understand how the relationship with oneself could be not a mistake but a virtue. He helps us to understand the deeper meaning of art. "A kind of flattering complicity is established between the writer and myself," he writes. "I feel chosen: the artist discovers me, he sings to me, he sings my sorrow, my joy, my curiosity, and he sings it well. He has seen and felt everything and other things that I have neither seen nor felt. Like a Narcissus who cannot speak, he is my inspired double; his confidence enlightens me, but these revelations are so decent, he sings this individual song with such aptitude, with such a full yet modest beauty, that I have no reason to be ashamed of it, and to the pleasure of having been guessed, I add that of not having been betrayed". In this respect, I wonder if the rejection of Narcissus, so typical of our time, is not the rejection of this demand: the demand to always find oneself in someone else, always in someone else. It's a pity, because a beautiful encounter with oneself profoundly changes our relationship with others and with the world, as Gaston Bachelard discovered: "Generalised narcissism transforms all beings into flowers and gives all flowers an awareness of their beauty."
(Fabrice Midal, Narcisse accused but not guilty, series of programs broadcast on France Culture in July and August 2018)

Listen to the podcat in French: https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/narcisse-accuse-non-coupable

E1.7.C.2.2_REF5.1.a_PO "SOMEWHERE SOMEONE IS A DOG AND BARKS AT THE MOON
Someone was born Chinese and is now seventeen
Someone is a blonde and her sister is lively, truly vivacious
Someone his father is a Highlander
Someone. . .and then it hit him in the kidneys and now he's finished, he says he might as well die in hospital
Someone has big beams at home
Someone wants some more cream. But the other someone is quibbling about the existence of God
Someone has just had a moment of pride that he will atone for harshly
Someone, it's raining
Someone, this time it's raining hard
Someone the people next door are just coming in
Someone there was no breeze today, and the swell is still strong
Someone, it's still raining; bad for the roof [...]"
(Henri Michaux, somewhere someone, 1938)


"QUELQUE PART QUELQU’UN EST CHIEN ET ABOIE À LA LUNE
Quelqu’un est né chinoise et maintenant elle a dix-sept ans
Quelqu’un c’est une blonde et sa soeur est vive, véritablement pétulante
Quelqu’un son père est highlander
Quelqu’un. ..et puis ça lui a retenti sur les reins et maintenant fini, il dit qu’il aime autant mourir à l’hôpital
Quelqu’un il a de grosses solives à la maison

E1.7.C.2.2_REF6_PO WALT WHITMAN UNDERSTAND LOVING ONESELF AS A GATEWAY TO LOVING OTHERS AND THE WHOLE UNIVERSE since "for every atom belonging to me as good belongs to you".

E1.7.C.2.2_REF6.1_PO "I CELEBRATE MYSELF, AND SING MYSELF, 
And what I assume you shall assume,
For every atom belonging to me as good belongs to you.

I loafe and invite my soul,
I lean and loafe at my ease observing a spear of summer grass. 

My tongue, every atom of my blood, form'd from this soil, this air,
Born here of parents born here from parents the same, and their parents the same"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF6.2_PO "I AM OF OLD AND YOUNG, OF THE FOOLISH AS MUCH AS THE WISE,
Regardless of others, ever regardful of others,
Maternal as well as paternal, a child as well as a man,
Stuff’d with the stuff that is coarse and stuff’d with the stuff that is fine,
One of the Nation of many nations, the smallest the same and the largest the same, [...]
A learner with the simplest, a teacher of the thoughtfullest,
A novice beginning yet experient of myriads of seasons,
Of every hue and caste am I, of every rank and religion,
A farmer, mechanic, artist, gentleman, sailor, quaker,
Prisoner, fancy-man, rowdy, lawyer, physician, priest.

I resist any thing better than my own diversity,
Breathe the air but leave plenty after me,
And am not stuck up, and am in my place.

(The moth and the fish-eggs are in their place,
The bright suns I see and the dark suns I cannot see are in their place,
The palpable is in its place and the impalpable is in its place.) "
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF6.3_PO "THIS IS THE MEAL EQUALLY SET, this the meat for natural hunger,
It is for the wicked just same as the righteous, I make appointments with all,
I will not have a single person slighted or left away,
The kept-woman, sponger, thief, are hereby invited,
The heavy-lipp’d slave is invited, the venerealee is invited;
There shall be no difference between them and the rest.
This is the press of a bashful hand, this the float and odor of hair,
This the touch of my lips to yours, this the murmur of yearning,
This the far-off depth and height reflecting my own face,
This the thoughtful merge of myself, and the outlet again.
Do you guess I have some intricate purpose?
Well I have, for the Fourth-month showers have, and the mica on the side of a rock has.
Do you take it I would astonish?
Does the daylight astonish? does the early redstart twittering through the woods?
Do I astonish more than they?
This hour I tell things in confidence,
I might not tell everybody, but I will tell you. "
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF7_PH "KNOWING ANOTHER IS ENDLESS. And I have discovered that man’s experience of them enlarges rock, flower and bird. The thing to be known grows with the knowing. I believe that I now understand in some small measure why the Buddhist goes on pilgrimage to a mountain. The journey is itself part of the technique by which the god is sought. It is a journey into Being; for as I penetrate more deeply into the mountain’s life, I penetrate also into my own. For an hour I am beyond desire. It is not ecstasy, that leap out of the self that makes man like a god. I am not out of myself, but in myself. I am. To know Being, this is the final grace accorded from the mountain."
(Nan Shepherd, The Living Mountain)

E1.7.C.2.2_REF8_PH “WHAT CONSTITUTES THE FREEDOM, THE SOUL OF AN INDIVIDUAL LIFE, IS ITS UNIQUENESS. The reflection of the universe in someone’s consciousness is the foundation of his or her power, but life only becomes happiness, is only endowed with freedom and meaning when someone exists as a whole world that has never been repeated in all eternity. Only then can they experience the joy of freedom and kindness, finding in others what they have already found in themselves.”

[Responding to William James Sidis’ daring definition of life:

“There is a boundary limiting the infinity of the universe — life itself. This boundary’s nothing to do with Einstein’s curvature of space; it lies in the opposition between life and inanimate matter… Life can be defined as freedom. Life is freedom. Freedom is the fundamental principle of life. That is the boundary — between freedom and slavery, between inanimate matter and life.”]

“On this very day the Germans are slaughtering Jewish children and old women… And we ourselves have endured 1937 and the horrors of collectivization — famine, cannibalism and the deportation of millions of unfortunate peasants… You say life is freedom. Is that what people in the camps think? What if the life expanding through the universe should use its power to create a slavery still more terrible than your slavery of inanimate matter? Do you think this man of the future will surpass Christ in his goodness? That’s the real question. How will the power of this omnipresent and omniscient being benefit the world if he is still endowed with our own fatuous self-assurance and animal egotism? Our class egotism, our race egotism, our State egotism and our personal egotism? What if he transforms the whole world into a galactic concentration camp? What I want to know is — do you believe in the evolution of kindness, morality, mercy? [...]

When a person dies, they cross over from the realm of freedom to the realm of slavery. Life is freedom, and dying is a gradual denial of freedom. Consciousness first weakens and then disappears. The life-processes — respiration, the metabolism, the circulation — continue for some time, but an irrevocable move has been made towards slavery; consciousness, the flame of freedom, has died out.

The stars have disappeared from the night sky; the Milky Way has vanished; the sun has gone out; Venus, Mars and Jupiter have been extinguished; millions of leaves have died; the wind and the oceans have faded away; flowers have lost their colour and fragrance; bread has vanished; water has vanished; even the air itself, the sometimes cool, sometimes sultry air, has vanished. The universe inside a person has ceased to exist. This universe is astonishingly similar to the universe that exists outside people. It is astonishingly similar to the universes still reflected within the skulls of millions of living people. But still more astonishing is the fact that this universe had something in it that distinguished the sound of its ocean, the smell of its flowers, the rustle of its leaves, the hues of its granite and the sadness of its autumn fields both from those of every other universe that exists and ever has existed within people, and from those of the universe that exists eternally outside people. What constitutes the freedom, the soul of an individual life, is its uniqueness. The reflection of the universe in someone’s consciousness is the foundation of his or her power, but life only becomes happiness, is only endowed with freedom and meaning when someone exists as a whole world that has never been repeated in all eternity. Only then can they experience the joy of freedom and kindness, finding in others what they have already found in themselves.”
(Vasily Grossman, Life and Fate, 1959)

E1.7.C.2.3. "L’OUBLI DE SOI EST LE CRITÈRE DU RÉEL": SELF-OBLIVION IS THE CRITERION OF REALITY: The extent to which you align with reality can be gauged by your ability to recede into the background.

E1.7.C.2.3_REF1_PH "FICHTE: "FORGETTING ONESELF IS THE CRITERION OF REALITY."
How do we avoid a formidable pitfall that threatens philosophy: to forget that man is first of all a being in relation, that is to say in relation with the world, things, other men? It is with this in mind that Fichte writes this dialogue with himself: "as you read this book, when you contemplate this object, when you speak with your friend, do you think about what you are doing, what you are reading, what you contemplate, what you hear or see? Not at all! I do not think of myself, I completely forget myself in the book, in the object, in the conversation - the formulation is correct when we say that we are absorbed in what we do or we are immersed in it". Fichte's remarks are decisive: the criterion for checking that you are in relation to reality is that you have gone to the background.
Thsi explains my astonishment with the school of meditation we call in France "full consciousness". Mindful walking, mindful eating - in a strict sense, is torture and a confinement. I am all the more surprised that the experience of meditation that I discovered about thirty years ago precisely taught me to free myself from full consciousness; my consciousness goes to the background: it is the world, it is things, beings which suddenly appear more fully and it is this movement which makes one happy - to forget oneself. We've all experienced it, there are times when you don't really know why or how, things just happen on their own, you just feel like you're serving the situation"
(Fabrice Midal, 3 minutes of philosophy to become human again, broadcast on France Culture on 08/19/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: Écouter le podcast: https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/3-minutes-de-philosophie-pour-redevenir-humain/fichte-loubli-de-soi-est-le-critere-du-reel

E1.7.C.2.3_REF2_PH "YOU HAVE TO PLAY WITH THE "I". WHEN IT IS ARROGANT, WHICH IS ALMOST ALWAYS, GENTLY RESIST IT. What if putting ourselves last, far from making us suffer and humiliate ourselves, allowed us to access a broader state of mind? A state of mind that welcomes everything, without focusing on the small self, without dwelling on the state of mind of the moment".
(Alexandre Jollien, La Pharmacopée n°22)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF2.1_PH "GANDHI SAID: 'UNTIL A MAN PUTS HIMSELF AT THE LAST PLACE OF HIS OWN FREE WILL, THERE IS NO SALVATION FOR HIM.'Far from any reward, perhaps daring to put oneself at the last place is a way out of the stupidest tyranny of all, that of the ego. What if we learned to disobey that capricious self that always wants to be at the centre of the world, judging it, commenting on it, taking everything for itself? To put oneself in the last place may be to let the other person exist as he is, to see that the world is bigger than my opinions, to see that the universe is bigger than my desires. Asceticism here, as always, can be joyful. Playing with the self. Seeing when it is pretentious, which is almost always the case, gently resisting it. What if putting ourselves last, far from making us suffer and humiliate ourselves, allowed us to access a broader state of mind? A state of mind that welcomes everything, without focusing on the small self, without dwelling on the state of mind of the moment".
(Alexandre Jollien, La Pharmacopée n°22)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF3_PH "THE CRITIC OF THE EGO IN THE KINGDOM OF THE WAY [...] 

The same is true of spiritual experience. In ecstasy, as in any extreme state in which the framework explodes and bearings disappear, the ego as such is abolished; then it is reborn, but its reappearance is not prohibited if its place is no longer central. But it is no longer central because the path, which establishes a new inner geometry, takes it over and the self becomes for the seeker one of the elements of the terrain with which he is dealing. From then on it can be observed and one can relate to it while maintaining a distance.

Seen in this way, the ego is not so bad. It is almost touching, because it is childlike. An archaic entity, it comes from far back, from early childhood, whose features it retains. Of course, it is stupid, I would even say silly - which among other things means that I am too; but above all it is pathetic: always on its guard, constantly on the lookout, it constitutes an inexhaustible reservoir of potential suffering. Insecure because he is by nature subject to the approval of others, he tries, in the harshness of adult life, to maintain the privileged position of the child-king, the center of a world that unfolds around him and from him. When the spiritual anthropologist is no longer the plaything of the aberrations that this geometry entails, he can study the self and observe it without condemning it. What is its function? What are the defects of its point of view? What is the nature of its blindness? “Criticism” in the Kantian sense of the term, that is to say the examination without value judgment of its limits and possibilities, seems to be the appropriate attitude here.”
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond Wisdom, How to Live?, 2023)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF4_PH "TO SPEAK TRULY, FEW ADULT PERSONS CAN SEE NATURE. Most persons do not see the sun. At least they have a very superficial seeing. The sun illuminates only the eye of the man, but shines into the eye and the heart of the child. The lover of nature is he whose inward and outward senses are still truly adjusted to each other; who has retained the spirit of infancy even into the era of man- hood. His intercourse with heaven and earth becomes part of his daily food. In the presence of nature a wild delight runs through the man, in spite of real sorrows. Nature says he is my creature, and maugre all his impertinent griefs, he shall be glad with me. Not the sun or the summer alone, but every hour and season yields its tribute of delight; for every hour and change corre- sponds to and authorizes a different state of the mind, from breathless noon to grimmest midnight. Nature is a setting that fits equally well a comic or a mourning piece. In good health, the air is a cordial of incredible virtue. Crossing a bare common, in snow puddles, at twilight, under a clouded sky, without having in my thoughts any occurrence of special good fortune, I have en- joyed a perfect exhilaration. I am glad to the brink of fear. In the woods, too, a man casts off his years, as the snake his slough, and at what period soever of life is always a child. In the woods is perpetual youth. Within these plantations of God, a decorum and sanctity reign, a perennial festival is dressed, and the guest sees not how he should tire of them in a thousand years. In the woods, we return to reason and faith. There I feel that nothing can be- fall me in life-no disgrace, no calamity (leaving me my eyes), which nature cannot repair. Standing on the bare ground-my bead bathed by the blithe air and uplifted into infinite space-all mean egotism vanishes. I become a transparent eyeball; I am nothing; I see all; the currents of the Universal Being circulate through me; I am part or parcel of God. The name of the nearest friend sounds then foreign and accidental: to be brothers, to be acquaintances, master or servant, is then a trifle and a disturb- ance. I am the lover of uncontained and immortal beauty. In the wilderness I find something more dear and connate than in streets or villages. In the tranquil landscape, and especially in the distant line of the horizon, man beholds somewhat as beautiful as his own nature.

The greatest delight which the fields and woods minister is the suggestion of an occult relation between man and the vegetable. I am not alone and unacknowledged. They nod to me, and I to them. The waving of the boughs in the storm is new to me and old. It takes me by surprise, and yet is not unknown. Its effect is like that of a higher thought or a better emotion coming over me, when I deemed I was thinking justly or doing right.
Yet it is certain that the power to produce this delight does not reside in nature, but in man, or in a harmony of both. It is necessary to use these pleasures with great temperance. For nature is not always tricked in holiday attire, but the same scene which yesterday breathed perfume and glittered as for the frolic of the nymphs is overspread with melancholy to-day. Nature always wears the colors of the spirit. To a man laboring under calamity, the heat of his own fire hath sadness in it. Then there is a kind of contempt of the landscape felt by him who has just lost by death a dear friend. The sky is less grand as it shuts down over less worth in the population.
(Ralph Waldo emerson, Nature, 1836)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF5_PH "CE QUI NE ME TUE PAS ME REND PLUS FORT"
Il y a une joie tragique du Dionysiaque chez Nietzsche qui appelle à affirmer l’éternité de la vie par delà les limites de son individualité, par l'individu se dépossédant de lui-même. Cela implique de changer d'échelle temporelle, au delà du destin de l'individu. La pleine libération pour Nietzsche passe par la remise en question de la notion même d'identité qui est une fiction en grande partie grammaticale quand on est plongé dans une société stable qui maintient cette fiction - il n'y a en fait qu'un flux qui se cristallise par moment."
(Emmanuel Salanskis au micro de l'émission Les Chemins de la Philosophie, Quatre malentendus nietzschéens (3/4), diffusée sur France Culture le 11/09/2019)

Écouter le podcast: https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/quatre-malentendus-nietzscheens-34-ce-qui-ne-me-tue-pas-me-rend-plus-fort

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6_PH IT IS IMPERATIVE THAT THE INDIVIDUAL ENLARGE HIS OR HER VISION "This axis [the recognition of the Self as a microcosm of the universe] is framed by the cosmic mystery which transcends the mystery of individual incarnation. Without some relationship to the cosmic drama, we are constrained to lives of transience, superficiality and aridity. Since the culture most of us have inherited offers little mythic mediation for the placement of self in a larger context, it is all the more imperative that the individual enlarge his or her vision."
(James Hollis, The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, 1993) 

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.1_PH WHEN THE TRUE SELF CLASHES WITH THE ACQUIRED PERSONA "“In the middle of the journey of our life I found myself within a dark woods where the straight way was lost,” Dante wrote in the Inferno. “The perilous time for the most highly gifted is not youth,” the visionary Elizabeth Peabody cautioned half a millennium later as she considered the art of self-renewal, “the perilous season is middle age.”

In The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife (public library), Jungian analyst James Hollis offers a torch for turning the perilous darkness of the middle into a pyre of profound transformation — an opportunity, both beautiful and terrifying, to reimagine the patterns of thought, feeling, and behavior acquired in the course of adapting to life’s traumas and demands, and finally inhabit the authentic self beneath the costume of this provisional personality.

One has entered the Middle Passage when the demands of the true self press restive and uprising against the acquired persona, eventually colliding to produce untenable psychic ache — a “fearsome clash,” Hollis writes, leaving one “radically stunned into consciousness.” A generation after James Baldwin contemplated how myriad chance events infuse our lives with the illusion of choice, Hollis considers our unexamined conditioning as a root cause of this clash."
(Maria Popova's review on James Hollis's The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, in The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.1.a_PH MIDLIFE CRISES OFTEN STEM FROM THE CONFLICT BETWEEN OUR INNER SELF AND OUR ACQUIRED PERSONALITY "Perhaps the first step in making the Middle Passage meaningful is to acknowledge the partiality of the lens we were given by family and culture, and through which we have made our choices and suffered their consequences. If we had been born of another time and place, to different parents who held different values, we would have had an entirely different lens. The lens we received generated a conditional life, which represents not who we are but how we were conditioned to see life and make choices… We succumb to the belief that the way we have grown to see the world is the only way to see it, the right way to see it, and we seldom suspect the conditioned nature of our perception. ...]

We all live out, unconsciously, reflexes assembled from the past. [...]

Most of the sense of crisis in midlife is occasioned by the pain of that split. The disparity between the inner sense of self and the acquired personality becomes so great that the suffering can no longer be suppressed or compensated… The person continues to operate out of the old attitudes and strategies, but they are no longer effective. Symptoms of midlife distress are in fact to be welcomed, for they represent not only an instinctually grounded self underneath the acquired personality but a powerful imperative for renewal… In effect, the person one has been is to be replaced by the person to be. The first must die… Such death and rebirth is not an end in itself; it is a passage. It is necessary to go through the Middle Passage to more clearly achieve one’s potential and to earn the vitality and wisdom of mature aging. Thus, the Middle Passage represents a summons from within to move from the provisional life to true adulthood, from the false self to authenticity. [...]

The second adulthood… is only attainable when the provisional identities have been discarded and the false self has died. The pain of such loss may be compensated by the rewards of the new life which follows, but the person in the midst of the Middle Passage may only feel the dying… The good news which follows the death of the first adulthood is that one may reclaim one’s life. There is a second shot at what was left behind in the pristine moments of childhood."
(James Hollis, The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, 1993) 

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.2_PH JAMES HOLLIS'S CONCEPT OF SHIFTING IDENTITIES "Hollis envisions [our] shifting identities as a change of axes, moving from the parent-child axis of early life to the ego-world axis of young adulthood to the ego-Self axis of the Middle Passage — a time when “the humbled ego begins the dialogue with the Self.” On the other side of it lies the final axis: “Self-God” or “Self-Cosmos,” embodying philosopher Martin Buber’s recognition that “we live our lives inscrutably included within the streaming mutual life of the universe” — the kind of orientation that led Whitman, who lived with uncommon authenticity and made of it an art, to call himself a “kosmos,” using the spelling Alexander von Humboldt used to denote the interconnectedness of the universe reflected in his pioneering insistence that “in this great chain of causes and effects, no single fact can be considered in isolation.” The fourth axis is precisely this recognition of the Self as a microcosm of the universe — an antidote to the sense of insignificance, alienation, and temporality that void life of meaning."
(Maria Popova's review on James Hollis's The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, in The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.2.a_PH IT IS IMPERATIVE THAT THE INDIVIDUAL ENLARGE HIS OR HER VISION "This axis [the recognition of the Self as a microcosm of the universe] is framed by the cosmic mystery which transcends the mystery of individual incarnation. Without some relationship to the cosmic drama, we are constrained to lives of transience, superficiality and aridity. Since the culture most of us have inherited offers little mythic mediation for the placement of self in a larger context, it is all the more imperative that the individual enlarge his or her vision. [...]

It takes courage to face one’s emotional states directly and to dialogue with them. But therein lies the key to personal integrity. In the swamplands of the soul there is meaning and the call to enlarge consciousness. To take this on is the greatest responsibility in life… And when we do, the terror is compensated by meaning, by dignity, by purpose. […]

Our task at midlife is to be strong enough to relinquish the ego-urgencies of the first half and open ourselves to a greater wonder."
(James Hollis, The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, 1993) 

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.2.b_PH JAMES HOLLIS'S CONCEPT OF SHIFTING IDENTITIES "Hollis envisions [our] shifting identities as a change of axes, moving from the parent-child axis of early life to the ego-world axis of young adulthood to the ego-Self axis of the Middle Passage — a time when “the humbled ego begins the dialogue with the Self.” [...]

These shifting axes are marked by several “sea-changes of the soul,” the most important of which is the withdrawal of projections — those mental figments that “embody what is unclaimed or unknown within ourselves,” born of the tendency to superimpose the unconscious on external objects, nowhere more pronounced than in love: What is so often mistaken for love of another is a projection of the unloved parts of oneself.

Drawing on the work of Jungian psychologist Marie-Louise von Franz, Hollis describes the five stages of projection — a framework strikingly similar to the seven stages of falling in and out of love that Stendhal outlined two centuries ago. "
(Maria Popova's review on James Hollis's The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, in The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF6.2.c_PH BY LETTING GO OF PROJECTIONS AND FOCUSING ON INNER DEVELOPMENT, INDIVIDUALS CAN EXPERIENCE A TRANSCENDENT RELATIONSHIP WHERE BOTH PARTNERS GROW BEYOND THEIR LIMITATIONS, FORMING A DYNAMIC AND ENRICHING UNION. "First, the person is convinced that the inner (that is, unconscious) experience is truly outer. Second, there is a gradual recognition of the discrepancy between the reality and the projected image… Third, one is required to acknowledge this discrepancy. Fourth, one is driven to conclude one was somehow in error originally. And, fifth, one must search for the origin of the projection energy within oneself. This last stage, the search for the meaning of the projection, always involves a search for a greater knowledge of oneself. [...]

The loss of hope that the outer will save us occasions the possibility that we shall have to save ourselves… Life has a way of dissolving projections and one must, amid the disappointment and desolation, begin to take on the responsibility for one’s own life… Only when one has acknowledged the deflation of the hopes and expectations of childhood and accepted direct responsibility for finding meaning for oneself, can the second adulthood begin. [...]

We learn through the deflation of the persona world that we have lived provisionally; the integration of inner truths, joyful or unpleasant, is necessary to bring new life and the restoration of purpose. […]

The truth about intimate relationships is that they can never be any better than our relationship with ourselves. How we are related to ourselves determines not only the choice of the Other but the quality of the relationship… All relationships… are symptomatic of the state of our inner life, and no relationship can be any better than our relationship to our own unconscious. [...]

When one has let go of the projections and the great hidden agenda, then one can be enlarged by the otherness of the partner. One plus one does not equal One, as in the fusion model; it equals three — the two as separate beings whose relationship forms a third which obliges them to stretch beyond their individual limitations. Moreover, by relinquishing projections and placing the emphasis on inner growth, one begins to encounter the immensity of one’s own soul. The Other helps us expand the possibilities of the psyche. […]

Loving the otherness of the partner is a transcendent event, for one enters the true mystery of relationship in which one is taken to the third place — not you plus me, but we who are more than ourselves with each other."
(James Hollis, The Middle Passage: From Misery to Meaning in Midlife, 1993) 

E1.7.C.2.3_REF7_CUR
“EVERY MORNING WHEN I WAKE UP, I EXPERIENCE AN EXQUISITE JOY — the joy of being Salvador Dalí— and I ask myself in rapture: What wonderful things is this Salvador Dalí going to accomplish today?”
(Salvador Dalí)

E1.7.C.2.3_REF8_PO “JOURNEY INTO THE INTERIOR
In the long journey out of the self,
There are many detours, washed-out interrupted raw places
Where the shale slides dangerously
And the back wheels hang almost over the edge
At the sudden veering, the moment of turning.
Better to hug close, wary of rubble and falling stones.
The arroyo cracking the road, the wind-bitten buttes, the canyons,
Creeks swollen in midsummer from the flash-flood roaring into the 
narrow valley. Reeds beaten flat by wind and rain,
Grey from the long winter, burnt at the base in late summer.
-- Or the path narrowing,
Winding upward toward the stream with its sharp stones,
The upland of alder and birchtrees,
Through the swamp alive with quicksand,
The way blocked at last by a fallen fir-tree,
The thickets darkening,
The ravines ugly.”
(Theodore Roethke)

E1.7.C.2.4. SPIRITUALITY INVOLVES A QUEST FOR THE "SELF," A CRITICAL CONCEPT IN INDIAN PHILOSOPHY. THIS CONCEPT CONTRASTS WITH THE "I," which is often the focus of personal development and certain psychoanalytic discourses. "If a man knows the "Self" by understanding "I am the Self'', what plan or desire will enable him to free himself from the miseries of the body? The one who has awoken to the 'Self,' having found it within the body's intricate labyrinth, becomes the universe's creator, the author of all things. It's on Earth that we must have this experience, otherwise, there will only be ignorance and profound misery.

E1.7.C.2.4.a. "PARADOXICALLY, THE TERM "INDIVIDUAL" ALIGNS WELL WITH INDIAN PHILOSOPHY. THE INDIVIDUAL IS BORN WITH THE PERSON WHO SEEKS LIBERATION, the one who breaks from societal norms and chooses to be a renouncer - an individual defined by their "Self". Those who define themselves by their "I" live in conflict - in opposition to society, to others - and thus, live in suffering. Only the "Self" can originate the desire for liberation. The "Self", a reflexive pronoun, refers back to the subject without conflict. It's crucial to continuously return to the distinction between the "Self" and the "I", as the "I" often overshadows the "Self". The "I" is the mirror in which we see ourselves. Conversely, there's no mirror for the "Self", as it requires us to decentralize."

E1.7.C.2.4.a_REF1_PH "SPIRITUALITY IS THE QUEST FOR THE 'SELF' [âtman]- an essential concept in Indian philosophy - the very opposite of the 'ego' [ahamkara] which is the focus of personal development and a certain kind of psychoanalysis.
"If a man knows the 'Self' by understanding 'I am it', under what plan, under what desire will he be able to remain in the bondage of the body and its miseries? He who has awakened to the 'Self', who has found it in the dark labyrinth of the body, becomes the creator of the universe, the author of all things. It is here on earth that we have to learn this, otherwise there would only be ignorance and great misery. When one has encountered the evidence of the 'Self' [âtman], of this God who rules the future as well as the past, one is less tempted to turn away from it, for it is under its aegis that the year unfolds the course of its days. May the discerning Brahmin, having discerned it, practise wisdom without exhausting himself in long speeches. This vast beginningless 'Self' [âtman], which resides in the form of consciousness between the breaths, rests in the space within the heart, commanding and presiding over all things. It is to this that the Brahmins seek to approach through the study of the Vedas, through sacrifice, offering and asceticism. Precisely because they knew this, the sages of old did not wish to have descendants. "What would we do with posterity, they said, we who have already found the place of salvation in the 'Self' [âtman]?" And they accepted the condition of religious beggars. To desire children is in fact the same as to desire riches; and to desire riches is to desire a place of salvation. As for the 'self', it can only be said that it is not like that... not like that; elusive, because it cannot be perceived anywhere; invulnerable, because nothing can harm it; unattached, because it is not attached to anything - immune to suffering, unattainable".
(Extrait de Brihadaranyaka Upanishad IV, VIe siècle BC)
The word "individual," paradoxical at first glance, is perfectly suited to Indian philosophy, for it is with the one who wishes to liberate himself that the individual begins - the one who breaks with the social order and decides to become a renunciate. - An individual who defines himself by "Self" [âtman]. The person who defines himself by "I" [ahamkara] lives in conflict - I am against society, against others - therefore I will spend my life in suffering. Only the "Self" [âtman] can be at the origin of the desire for liberation - Self, a reflexive pronoun that returns to the subject. It arises without resistance. We will always have to return to the source of the distinction between the "Self" and the "Ego" because the "Self" is always covered by the "Ego". The ego is the mirror in which we see ourselves, whereas there is no mirror of the self, because it forces us to decentralize.
(Marc Ballanfat in the program Les chemins de la philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 10/29/2019)

Listen to the podcast in French: https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/spiritualite-es-tu-la-24-philosophie-de-linde-une-pensee-venue-du-ciel

E1.7.C.2.4.a_REF2_PH PROPRIOCEPTION OF THOUGHT [...]
If we can see what all of our opinions mean, then we are sharing a common content, even if we don't agree entirely. It may turn out that the opinions are not really very important — 
they are all assumptions. And if we can see them all, we may then move more creatively in a different direction. We can just simply share the appreciation of the meanings; and out of
this whole thing, truth emerges unannounced not that we have chosen it.
If each of us in this room is suspending, then we are all doing the same thing. We are all looking at everything together. The content of our consciousness is essentially the same. Accordingly, a different kind of consciousness is possible among us, a participatory consciousness - as indeed consciousness always is, but one that is frankly acknowledged to be participatory and can go that way freely. Everything can move between us. Each person is participating, is partaking of the whole meaning of the group and also taking part in it. We can call that a true dialogue."
(David Bohm, On dialogue, 1996)

E1.7.C.2.4.b. "ZEN INVOLVES EMPTYING ONESELF OF THE SELF, so that there is nothing left but everything."

E1.7.A.4.4_REF4_PH.wE1.7.C.2.4.b.

E1.7.C.2.4.c. "AS HUMAN BEINGS, WE CAN LIVE LIVES OF CONSTANT VIGILANCE AND ATTENTION, WHERE OUR MINDS REMAIN ALERT, OBSERVING THE EGO IN MOTION. Authentic religion involves the cessation of the "I", and actions born from this silence. Such a life is sacred and full of meaning."

E1.7.B.3.2_REF1_PH.wE1.7.C.2.4.c.

E1.7.C.2.5. "I’VE COME TO THINK THIS IS A VERY SATISFYING VIEW OF SELF, THAT ALL THESE DIFFERENT ELEMENTS OF WHAT IT IS TO BE ME OR TO BE YOU CAN BE UNDERSTOOD AS FORMS OF PERCEPTION  that play some role in guiding the behavior of the organism. And it can also help us understand what happens when these processes go wrong. There’s so many conditions in psychiatry and neurology, and that we can induce in the lab using virtual reality, where we can tease apart these elements of self so that some remain and some are altered. The more you do this, the less sense it makes to presume that there is such a thing as a unified, stable, unchanging essence of anyone."

E1.7.C.2.5_REF1_SCI THE EXPERIENCE OF SELF IS A FORM OF CONTROLLED HALLUCINATION, SIMILAR TO HOW THE BRAIN PREDICTS AND INTERPRETS SENSORY SIGNALS FROM THE EXTERNAL WORLD 
"Strogatz (32:37): Well, alright, so as we move toward the end of our discussion here, I think we should start talking about the self. I’m  reminded of expressions that you have been using earlier in this conversation where you say things like, “the brain’s best guess,” or “the brain is trying to make sense of…” It almost makes me picture what people used to call the omunculus. Like, there’s a little person inside your head who’s looking out through your eyes, trying to make sense of the world. It’s sort of: the self, what is it? I mean, who’s in  there? Who is it that’s trying to make sense?

Seth (33:09): Yeah, I mean, so this is why when I say the brain believes this, or the brain predicts this, I tried to be careful to say the brain rather than you. Because I think the experience of being you, the experience of self, is another kind of controlled hallucination. It’s another kind of brain-based best guess, OK? And this is another challenge to this sort of naive how-things-seem view of perception.

(33:32) Now, just as it seems as though there’s a real world out there that just pours itself into our minds, it might also seem to us that the self is the thing that does the perceiving. This quiddity, this essence of you or me, maybe the soul that resides somewhere homunculus-like inside the skull, doing all the perceiving and then deciding what to do and then pulling various meat strings in the body to make actions happen.

(34:00) And I think this is fundamentally mistaken. And again, this is not a new thing to say. Here, the philosopher David Hume, Scottish philosopher, had what he called “the bundle theory of self,” which is the idea that the nature of self is just an ongoing collection of perceptions. It’s not the thing that does the perceiving. It’s another kind of perception, but this time based at least in part on the body. So just as the brain, in trying to make sense of what’s out there in the world, is making predictions about the causes of signals that come into
the eyes and the ears and so on, it’s also trying to figure out what’s happening inside the body and trying to also control the body too. And this also happens, in my view anyway, through a process of the brain creating predictions and using them to update sensory signals, but in this case from the body. Or when making actions to use predictions to actually, you know, overwhelm sensory data so they become selffulfilling predictions, so that if I move my arm to pick up the cup again, now that’s a kind of self-fulfilling prediction about where my arm will be.

(35:10) But the key point is that there’s a common principle here underlying experiences of the world, and experiences of the self. They’re both forms of perceptual prediction. Neither of them reflect things as they really are. And both can be subject to illusions — they can both go awry in various ways.

(35:32) So lots of the book is kind of taking this idea that we build up about how our perception of the world works, and then turning the lens inward to understand how the same core principle can explain experiences of selfhood. And these include not just the sort of highlevel aspects of self that “I’m Anil Seth, and I live in Brighton” and so on and so on, and “I remember this funny story about getting a ticket in San Diego.” But much more basic elements of self too, like the fact that this object that I call my body is part of myself — emotions and moods are part of self, experiences of free will. Now, they are not the consequences of some almost supernatural causal power that sweeps in and changes the course of physical events in the world. No, that’s another kind of perception of what my body is doing.

(36:23) I’ve come to think this is a very satisfying view of self, that all these diferent elements of what it is to be me or to be you can be understood as forms of perception that play some role in guiding the behavior of the organism. And it can also help us understand what happens when these processes go wrong. There’s so many conditions in psychiatry and neurology, and that we can induce in the lab using virtual reality, where we can tease apart these elements of self so that some remain and some are altered. The more you do this, the less sense it makes to presume that there is such a thing as a unified, stable, unchanging essence of anyone."
(From the transcript of the podcast: What is the nature of consciousness, The joy of why, Quanta Magazine, May31, 2023)

Listen to the podcast: https://www.quantamagazine.org/what-is-the-nature-of-consciousness-20230531/

E1.7.C.2.5_REF2_SCI THE BIRD BRAIN MAY BE WHERE DREAMS EVOLVED - A SPACE WHERE WE CONTINUE PROCESSING DAYTIME PROBLEMS. REM SLEEP, BRIDGING CONSCIOUSNESS AND UNCONSCIOUSNESS, MAY BE WHERE WE REHEARSE POSSIBILITIES INTO REALITY.

“There are two primary groups of living birds: the flightless Palaeognathae, including the ostrich and the kiwi, which have retained certain ancestral reptilian traits, and Neognathae, comprising all other birds. EEG studies of sleeping ostriches have found REM-like activity in the brainstem — a more ancient part of the brain — while in modern birds, as in mammals, this REM-like activity takes place primarily in the more recently developed forebrain.

Several studies of sleeping monotremes — egg-laying mammals like the platypus and the echidna, the evolutionary link between us and birds — also reveal REM-like activity in the brainstem, suggesting that this was the ancestral crucible of REM before it slowly migrated toward the forebrain.

If so, the bird brain might be where evolution designed dreams — that secret chamber adjacent to our waking consciousness where we continue to work on the problems that occupy our days. Dmitri Mendeleev, after puzzling long and hard over the arrangement of atomic weights in his waking state, arrived at his periodic table in a dream. “All the elements fell into place as required,” he recounted in his diary. “Awakening, I immediately wrote it down on a piece of paper.” Cosmologist Stephon Alexander dreamed his way to a groundbreaking insight about the role of symmetry in cosmic inflation that earned him a national award from the American Physics Society. For Einstein, the central revelation of relativity took shape in a dream of cows simultaneously jumping up and moving in wavelike motion.

As with the mind, so with the body. Studies have shown that people learning new motor tasks “practice” them in sleep, then perform better while awake. This line of research has also shown how mental visualization helps athletes improve performance. Renata Adler touches on this in her novel *Speedboat*: “That was a dream,” she writes, “but many of the most important things, I find, are the ones learned in your sleep. Speech, tennis, music, skiing, manners, love — you try them waking and perhaps balk at the jump, and then you’re over. You’ve caught the rhythm of them once and for all, in your sleep at night.”

It may be that in REM, this gloaming between waking consciousness and the unconscious, we practice the possible into the real. It may be that we evolved to dream ourselves into reality — a laboratory of consciousness that began in the bird brain.”
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.2.6. BEFORE WE CAME TO BELIEVE HUMANS WERE SO IMPORTANT... ALL   EVERYTHING   HOME
"SINGULARITY
(after Stephen Hawking)

Do you sometimes want to wake up to the singularity
we once were?

so compact nobody
needed a bed, or food or money —

nobody hiding in the school bathroom
or home alone

pulling open the drawer
where the pills are kept.

For every atom belonging to me as good
Belongs to you.   Remember?

There was no   Nature.    No
 them.   No tests

to determine if the elephant
grieves her calf    or if

the coral reef feels pain.    Trashed
oceans don’t speak English or Farsi or French;

would that we could wake up   to what we were
— when we were ocean    and before that

to when sky was earth, and animal was energy, and rock was
liquid and stars were space and space was not

at all — nothing

before we came to believe humans were so important
before this awful loneliness.

Can molecules recall it?
what once was?    before anything happened?

No I, no We, no one. No was
No verb      no noun
only a tiny tiny dot brimming with

is is is is is

All   everything   home"
(Marie Howe)
