E1.7.C.3.3.b. WE SHOULD GO OUT AND SAVE THE WORLD BY SPREADING UNSOLICITED KINDNESS.
Nick Cave believes that the emptiness that we occasionally feel is an indicator of our own self-absorption, indicating a need for love and meaning. He views us as facilitators on a shared journey towards meaning, which brings us closer to love and beauty. Even the smallest acts of kindness and tolerance can contribute to this collective effort to enhance our lives.

E1.7.C.3.3.b_REF1_PH THE EMPTINESS PEOPLE FEEL IS A CALL TO MEANING AND LOVE "So, the next Red Hand File is #200! What are The Red Hand Files? I mean what are you trying to do with them? By the way, I love them. They help.
JOHN, LANCASHIRE, UK

I just feel so fucking empty. So hollow. What can I do?
MARINA, MADRID, SPAIN

Dear John and Marina,

It might be worth bearing in mind, Marina, that this emptiness that you feel, that we all feel to a greater or lesser degree, is not a condition in itself, rather it is an indicator of our own self-absorption, and signals a need. It asks something of us, this emptiness, this hollowness. It is a call. It is a call to meaning, and a call to love. It requires of us that we reach beyond our own dejection and attend to the condition of the world.

For me, personally, this is the gift of The Red Hand Files. By their very nature your questions draw me out of my own self-absorption, by demanding an engagement with you all. This is not always easy, but a call to meaning rarely is – how happy we can feel in our own misery! How cosy! How safe! – and so I read the hundreds of questions that come in each week and do my best to reply, regardless of my state of mind, truthfully and in good faith.

You ask me, John, what I want from The Red Hand Files. Well, the answer is this – I want to facilitate, in some small way, a mutual journey toward meaning; to decrease the dimensions of our emptiness and draw us closer to love and to beauty. I understand that these sound like grandiose claims, but they are not. This common project – to improve matters – is available to all of us, at every moment, and in a multitude of ways, and exists in the smallest kindness, the most rudimentary act of tolerance, or in the simplest generosity.

Today, John and Marina, my advice is to go out and save the world. Smile and say ‘hello’ to the mean old bastard who lives next door, or the cranky cow at the corner shop, for they suffer too, and watch this small act of unsolicited kindness gather momentum and begin its journey around the world – watch it thunder and roar through the ages and change the nature of the cosmos itself.

More grandiose claims? Hey, it’s my 200th post! Today, I can say what I like. Thank you everyone for improving the general condition of my life! I hope The Red Hand Files go some small way to improving yours. It is impossible to exaggerate just how unbelievably grateful I am to you all.

With endless love, Nick"
(Nick Cave, The Red Hand Files, ISSUE #200 / JULY 2022)

E1.7.C.3.3.b_REF2_PH "NOBLE SANS LE VOULOIR. —  L’homme se comporte noblement sans le vouloir, quand il s’est accoutumé à ne vouloir rien des hommes et à leur donner toujours. [...]"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Humain, trop humain, Aphorisme 497, 1878)

E1.7.C.3.4. THE GITA ADDRESSES THE VISION OF DIVINE YOGA, WHICH LIBERATES US FROM ALL DOUBTS AND CONFUSION. It encourages us to acknowledge reality as it exists, and to advance through discord to achieve the ultimate harmony of Spirit with the Whole.

E1.7.C.3.4.a. HUMANS MUST FIGHT FOR WHAT IS RIGHT AND EXPERIENCE THE BLISS OF THE COSMIC AND TRANSCENDENT SPIRIT. The text poses the question of life's meaning and the creative, all-consuming flame of mortal existence. The Divine responds that everything occurs according to His will, and time must demolish old structures to build a new kingdom.

E1.7.C.3.4.a_REF1_PH "THE COSMIC VISION

So this is the central meaning, the keynote. It is the vision of the one in the many, the many in the one-and all are the one. It is this vision that, in the eyes of divine yoga, liberates, justifies, and explains all that is, all that has been, and all that will be. Once perceived and held, it strikes with the glittering axe of God at the root of all doubt and perplexity, and annihilates all denial and opposition. If the soul can achieve unity with the Divine in this vision - Arjuna has not yet done so, which is why he is terrified when he sees it - everything in the world, even the terrible, loses its terror.

KRISHNA
5 . - Behold, Arjuna, a million divine forms, with an infinite variety of color and shape. 6 . - Behold the gods of the natural world, and many more wonders never revealed before. 7 . - Behold the entire cosmos turning within my body, and the other things you desire to see. 8 . - But these things cannot be seen with your physical eyes; therefore I give you spiritual vision to perceive my majestic power.

ARJUNA
30 . - You lap the worlds into your burning mouths and swallow them. Filled with your terrible radiance, O Vishnu, the whole of creation bursts into flames.
31 . - Tell me who you are, O Lord of terrible form. I bow before you; have mercy! I want to know who you are, you who existed before all creation. Your nature and workings confound me.

The weakness of the human heart wants only pleasant and comforting truths, or, failing that, nice fables; it does not want the whole truth, where so many things are neither clear nor pleasant nor comfortable, but hard to understand and harder to bear. To place the responsibility for everything that seems bad or terrible on the shoulders of a semi-omnipotent demon, or to see it as only a part of nature, thus creating an irreducible opposition between the nature of the world and that of God, as if nature were independent of God, or even to place the responsibility on man and his sins, as if he played a dominant role in the construction of this world or could create anything against the will of God-these are comfortable but clumsy solutions, in which the religious thought of India has never sought refuge. We must boldly face reality and see that it is God and no one else who has created this world in His being and made it as it is. The agony of the bed of suffering and evil on which we are tortured is His touch as well as happiness and sweetness and joy. The discords of the worlds are the discords of God, and it is only by accepting them and passing through them that we can reach the greatest harmonies of His supreme harmony, the summits, the vibrant immensities of His cosmic and transcendental ananda [spiritual delight, the bliss of the mind].

The problem raised by the Gîtâ and the solution it offers require this character of the vision of the Spirit of the World. Why does the Spirit - All - manifest itself in nature in this way? What is the meaning of this creative and all-consuming flame that is mortal existence, of this struggle for the whole world, of these constant, disastrous revolutions, of this effort and agony, of this painful birth and death of creatures? Here Arjuna asks the ancient question and utters the eternal prayer.

I have a far-sighted plan, says the Divine in substance, a plan that is infallibly accomplished, and neither the participation nor the abstention of any man can prevent, change or modify it; everything is already accomplished by Me in the eternal eye of My Will before man on earth can even undertake it. I, as Time, must destroy the old structures and build a new, powerful and glorious kingdom. You, as a human instrument of divine power and wisdom, must fight for the law in this battle that you cannot prevent, and kill and defeat your adversaries. And also, as a human soul in nature, you must enjoy in nature the fruits given by Me, the kingdom of law and justice. Let this be enough for you - to be one with God in your soul, to receive His commandments, to do His will, to calmly see a supreme plan fulfilled in the world.
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.C.3.4.b. FOR FREE SPIRITS, DIVINITY IS A SOURCE OF POWER AND SUBLIME VISION, BUT FOR ORDINARY HUMANS, IT IS TERRIFYING AND INCOMMUNICABLE. However, the mediator form of divinity, Nârâyana, is accessible to humans and provides support. This concept can be compared with Nietzsche's ideas of Dionysianism and Apollonianism.

E1.7.C.3.4.b_REF1_PH "THE DOUBLE ASPECT

ARJUNA
45 . - I rejoice in seeing you as you have never been seen before, yet I am filled with fear by this vision of you as the abode of the universe. Please let me see you again as the shining God of gods. 46 . - Though you are the embodiment of all creation, let me see you again not with a thousand arms but with four, carrying the mace and
discus and wearing a crown.

The form of the universal and transcendental Being is, to the strength of the liberated mind, a powerful thing, encouraging and empowering, a source of strength, a vision that sublimes, that equates, that justifies everything; but to the ordinary man it is overwhelming, terrifying, incommunicable. But there is also the mediating form, the form of grace of the divine Nârâyana [epithet applied to Krishna, meaning "he who has made water his home"], the God so close to man and in man, the charioteer in battle and on the road, with his four arms of protective power, a humanized symbol of the divine and no longer a universality with millions of arms. It is this mediating aspect that man must always have before him to sustain him. For it is this face of Nârâyana that symbolizes reassuring truth. It makes close, visible, alive, graspable, the vast spiritual joy in which, supremely, universal achievements culminate for the inner life and spirit of man - beyond their formidable cyclical movement of retrogression and progression - in their marvelous and hopeful flight.

KRISHNA
49 . -  Do not be troubled; do not fear my terrible form. Let your heart be satisfied and your fears dispelled in looking at me as I was before.

But since the lower nature in you is not yet ready to contemplate this terrible vision with strength and tranquility, I will once again assume for you my form of Nârâyana, in which the human mind, isolated and adapted to its humanity, sees the tranquility, help, and joy of a friendly divinity.

ARJUNA
51 . - O Krishna, now that I have seen your gentle human form my mind is again composed and returned to normal.

KRISHNA
52 . - It is extremely difficult to obtain the vision you have had; even the gods long always to see me in this aspect. 53 . - Neither knowledge of the Vedas, nor austerity, nor charity, nor sacrifice can bring the vision you have seen. 54 . - But through unfailing devotion, Arjuna, you can know me, see me, and attain union with me. 55 . - Those who make me the supreme goal of all their work and act without selfish attachment, who devote themselves to me completely and are free from ill will for any creature, enter into me."
(The Bhagavad Gitâ, Commented by Aurobindo)

E1.7.C.3.5. ATTENTION CAN DEFEAT EVIL
Simone Weil suggests that by being indifferent to good and evil, we can project the light of attention on both, and good will always prevail. This is because attention turned toward God or something authentically beautiful can render certain behaviors impossible.

E1.7.C.3.5.a. "AUTHENTIC VALUES OF TRUTH, BEAUTY AND GOODNESS ARE PRODUCED BY ONE AND THE SAME ACT, A CERTAIN APPLICATION TO THE OBJECT OF ATTENTION."

E1.7.C.3.5.a_REF1_PH "ATTENTION AND WILL

[...] We should be indifferent to good and evil but, when we are indifferent, that is to say when we project the light of our attention equally on both, the good gains the day. This phenomenon comes about automatically. There lies the essential grace. And it is the definition, the criterion of good.

A divine inspiration operates infallibly, irresistibly, if we do not turn away our attention, if we do not refuse it. There is not a choice to be made in its favour, it is enough not to refuse to recognize that it exists.

The attention turned with love towards God (or in a lesser degree, towards anything which is truly beautiful) makes certain things impossible for us. 

[...] The poet produces the beautiful by fixing his attention on something real. It is the same with the act of love. To know that this man who is hungry and thirsty really exists as much as I do — that is enough, the rest follows of itself.

The authentic and pure values — truth, beauty and goodness — in the activity of a human being are the result of one and the same act, a certain application of the full attention to the object.

[...] Purity is the power to contemplate defilement [Disfigurement]."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.5.a_REF2_PO " SONG OF THE OPEN ROAD

[...] 5

From this hour I ordain myself loos’d of limits and imaginary lines,
Going where I list, my own master total and absolute,
Listening to others, considering well what they say,
Pausing, searching, receiving, contemplating,
Gently, but with undeniable will, divesting myself of the holds that would hold me.

I inhale great draughts of space,
The east and the west are mine, and the north and the south are mine.

I am larger, better than I thought,
I did not know I held so much goodness.

All seems beautiful to me,
Can repeat over to men and women You have done such good to me I would do the same to you,
I will recruit for myself and you as I go,
I will scatter myself among men and women as I go,
I will toss a new gladness and roughness among them,
Whoever denies me it shall not trouble me,
Whoever accepts me he or she shall be blessed and shall bless me."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.3.5.b. EDUCATION MUST FOCUS ON CULTIVATING ATTENTION, AS ALL OTHER BENEFITS OF INSTRUCTION BECOME IRRELEVANT WITHOUT IT. Studying should serve as an exercise for attention, with the technique involving examining images, symbols, etc. until their meaning becomes clear without any deliberate interpretation. This approach aids in distinguishing reality from illusion. The vital premise is that attention should be an observational act, not an attachment.

E1.7.C.3.5.b_REF1_PH "ATTENTION AND WILL
[...] Teaching should have no aim but to prepare, by training the attention, for the possibility of such an act. All the other advantages of instruction are without interest.

Studies and faith. Prayer being only attention in its pure form and studies being a form of gymnastics of the attention, each school exercise should be a refraction of spiritual life. There must be method in it. A certain way of doing a Latin prose, a certain way of tackling a problem in geometry (and not just any way) make up a system of gymnastics of the attention calculated to give it a greater aptitude for prayer.

Method for understanding images, symbols, etc. Not to try to interpret them, but to look at them till the light suddenly
dawns.

Generally speaking, a method for the exercise of the intelligence, which consists of looking.

Application of this rule for the discrimination between the real and the illusory. In our sense perceptions, if we are not sure of what we see we change our position while looking, and what is real becomes evident. In the inner life, time takes the place of space. With time we are altered, and, if as we change we keep our gaze directed towards the same thing, in the end illusions are scattered and the real becomes visible. This is on condition that the attention be a looking and not an attachment."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.5.b_REF2_PO " SONG OF THE OPEN ROAD

[...] 6 [...]

Now I see the secret of the making of the best persons,
It is to grow in the open air and to eat and sleep with the earth.

Here a great personal deed has room,
(Such a deed seizes upon the hearts of the whole race of men,
Its effusion of strength and will overwhelms law and mocks all authority and all argument against it.)

Here is the test of wisdom,
Wisdom is not finally tested in schools,
Wisdom cannot be pass’d from one having it to another not having it,
Wisdom is of the soul, is not susceptible of proof, is its own proof,
Applies to all stages and objects and qualities and is content,
Is the certainty of the reality and immortality of things, and the excellence of things;
Something there is in the float of the sight of things that provokes it out of the soul.

Now I re-examine philosophies and religions,
They may prove well in lecture-rooms, yet not prove at all under the 
     spacious clouds and along the landscape and flowing currents.

Here is realization,
Here is a man tallied—he realizes here what he has in him,
The past, the future, majesty, love—if they are vacant of you, you are vacant of them.

Only the kernel of every object nourishes;
Where is he who tears off the husks for you and me?
Where is he that undoes stratagems and envelopes for you and me?

Here is adhesiveness, it is not previously fashion’d, it is apropos;
Do you know what it is as you pass to be loved by strangers?
Do you know the talk of those turning eye-balls?"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.C.3.5.c. "IN THE REALM OF INTELLIGENCE, THE VIRTUE OF HUMILITY IS NOTHING OTHER THAN THE POWER OF ATTENTION."

E1.7.C.3.5.c_REF1_PH "INTELLIGENCE AND GRACE

[...] In the intellectual order, the virtue of humility is nothing more nor less than the power of attention.

The wrong humility leads us to believe that we are nothing in so far as we are ourselves—in so far as we are certain particular human beings.

True humility is the knowledge that we are nothing in so far as we are human beings as such, and, more generally, in so far as we are creatures.
The intelligence plays a great part in this. We have to form a conception of the universal.

[...] There is nothing nearer to true humility than the intelligence. It is impossible to be proud of our intelligence at the moment when we are really exercising it."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.5.c_REF2_PH “THE OPPONENT OF GRACE.—The impatient and arrogant man does not care for grace, feeling it to be a corporeal, visible reproach against himself. For grace is heartfelt toleration in movement and gesture.”
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human II, Aphorism 258, 1878)

E1.7.C.3.6. THE ONLY FREE ACTION WE ARE ALLOWED IS THE DESTRUCTION OF OUR EGO.
According to Simone Weil, God withdrew so that we could exist, he relinquished being everything to let us be something. We are a part that should mirror the whole. God's grace is the sole escape from the gravity within us. To delve into the depths of reality, one must be vulnerable, without defense. The only free action we are granted is the annihilation of our ego.

E1.7.C.3.6.a. GOD WITHDREW SO THAT WE COULD EXIST, HE RELINQUISHED BEING EVERYTHING SO THAT WE COULD BE SOMETHING. Simone Weil suggests that while human beings bear the imprint of God, they are also separated from Him. The central law of this world is gravity, compelling every creature to seek anything that can preserve or reinforce it. Psychologically, this translates into motives for affirmation or restitution of the self, which keeps us external and in opposition to God.

E1.7.C.3.6.a_REF1_PH CREATION REFLECTS BOTH THE BEAUTY AND THE ABSENCE OF GOD "Creation reflects God through its beauty and harmony, but through the evil and death that inhabit it and the insensitive necessity that governs it, it also reveals the absence of God. We have departed from God: this means that we bear His mark, and it also means that we are separated from Him. The etymology of the word "exist" (to be outside) is very illuminating in this regard: we exist, we are not. God, who is Being, has, in a certain sense, erased himself so that we may exist; he has renounced being everything so that we may be something; he has, for our sake, renounced his necessity, which is indistinguishable from the good, in order to allow another necessity, which is alien and indifferent to the good, to reign. The central law of this world, from which God withdrew by his very act of creation, is the law of gravity, which is analogously found at all levels of existence. Gravity is the "divine" force par excellence. It urges every creature to seek everything that can preserve or increase it and, in the words of Thucydides, to exercise all the power of which it is capable. Psychologically, it translates into all the motives of affirmation or restitution of the self, in all the deceptions (internal lies, escape into dreams and false ideals, imaginary incursions into the past and the future, etc.) that we use to consolidate our shaken existence from within, that is, to remain external and opposed to God. ) that we use to consolidate our shaken existence from within, that is, to remain external and opposed to God."
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.a_REF2_CUR FILM: DAN KWAN, DANIEL SCHEINERT, EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE, 2022
A reflection on choosing kindness as a result of gaining a "cosmic conscciousness" and choosing teh here and now over all other possibilities.

Watch the trailer on YouTube: https://youtu.be/wxN1T1uxQ2g

E1.7.C.3.6.b. "WE ARE A PART THAT SHOULD MIRROR THE WHOLE. 
Identify with the universe itself. Anything less than the universe is subject to suffering. 
Through every sensation, feel the universe. What does it matter whether it's pleasure or pain?"

E1.7.C.3.6.b_REF1_PH "MEANING OF THE UNIVERSE

We are a part which has to imitate the whole.

The atman. Let the soul of a man take the whole universe for its body. Let its relation to the whole universe be like that of a collector to his collection

[...] We should identify ourselves with the universe itself. Everything that is less than the universe is subject to suffering.

Even though I die, the universe continues. That does not console me if I am anything other than the universe. If, however, the universe is, as it were, another body to my soul, my death ceases to have any more importance for me than that of a stranger. The same is true of my sufferings.

Let the whole universe be for me, in relation to my body, what the stick of a blind man is in relation to his hand. His sensibility is really no longer in his hand but at the end of the stick. An apprenticeship is necessary.

[...] To change the relationship between ourselves and the world in the same way as, through apprenticeship, the workman changes the relationship between himself and the tool. Getting hurt: this is the trade entering into the body. May all suffering make the universe enter into the body.\

Habit, skill: a transference of the consciousness into an object other than the body itself.

[...] We do not become detached, we change our attachment. We must attach ourselves to the all.

We have to feel the universe through each sensation. What does it matter then whether it be pleasure or pain? If our hand is shaken by a beloved friend when we meet again after a long separation, what does it matter that he squeezes it hard and hurts us?

[...] We have to associate the rhythm of the life of the body with that of the world, to feel this association constantly and to feel also the perpetual exchange of matter by which the human being bathes in the world.

Things which nothing can take from a human being as long as he lives: in the way of movement over which his will has a hold, respiration; in the way of perception, space (even in a dungeon, even with our eyes blinded and our ear-drums pierced, as long as we live we are aware of space).

We have to attach to these things the thoughts which we desire that no circumstances should be able to deprive us of."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.b_REF2_PH "PRETENDING TO BE MORAL. You never want to be dissatisfied with yourself, never to suffer because of yourself, - and you call this your moral inclination! But one thing is certain, you will never make the trip around the world (that you are yourself) and you will remain, in yourself, a chance, a clod of earth on a clod of earth. Do you believe, then, that we who are of another persuasion expose ourselves by sheer folly to the journey through our own nothingness, our swamps and our ice peaks, that we have voluntarily chosen pain and disgust like the stylistic anchorites?"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Aurora, Aphorism 343, 1881)

E1.7.C.3.6.c. GOD'S GRACE IS THE ONLY WAY TO OVERCOME THE INHERENT GRAVITY WITHIN US. For Simone Weil, this grace does not alter the law of necessity and chance that governs the world, but it permeates our souls to aid us in becoming divine again. To accomplish this, we must eradicate our egos and embrace life's emptiness and imbalance, without desiring recompense. We need also to let go of the past and future to stay true to the present moment. All sins are attempts to evade emptiness, and the imagination impedes the acceptance of grace. By choosing to be nothing, one can open oneself to the gates of eternity.

E1.7.C.3.6.c_REF1_PH "SIMONE WEIL PRESENTS THE PROBLEM OF SALVATION IN THESE TERMS: 'How can we escape what is like gravity within us? Only through grace. God passes through the infinite thickness of time and space to come to us; his grace does not change anything in the blind games of necessity and chance that govern this world: it penetrates our souls like a drop of water that penetrates geological strata without altering their structure, and there it waits in silence for our consent to become God again. Gravity was the law of creation, the work of grace is to "uncreate" us. God, out of love, consented to cease to be everything so that we could be something; we, out of love, must consent to cease to be nothing so that God can become everything again. It is therefore a matter of abolishing the ego in us, "this shadow cast by sin and error, which blocks the light of God and which we take to be a being". Outside of this total humility, this unconditional acceptance of being nothing, all forms of heroism and self-sacrifice remain subject to gravity and lies. "One can only offer the self. Otherwise, everything that is called a sacrifice is nothing more than a label on the revenge of the self. To kill the ego, one must expose oneself naked and defenseless to all the bites of life, accepting the emptiness, the imbalance, never seeking compensation for misfortune, and above all, suspending in oneself the work of the imagination, "which constantly tends to close the cracks through which grace would pass. All sins are attempts to escape emptiness. It is also necessary to renounce the past and the future, because the self is nothing more than a concretion of the past and the future around an always failing present. Memory and hope cancel out the beneficial effect of misfortune by opening up an unlimited field for imaginary exaltations (I was, I will be...), but fidelity to the present moment truly reduces man to nothing and thus opens the doors to eternity".
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.c_REF1.1_PH "THE SELF MUST BE KILLED FROM WITHIN BY LOVE. But it can also be killed from without by extreme suffering and humiliation. There are tramps and prostitutes who have no more self-respect than saints, and whose whole life is limited to the present moment. This is the tragedy of abjection: what makes it irreparable is not that the self it destroys is precious, for it is made to be destroyed; it is that it prevents God from destroying it himself, that it deprives the love that makes things eternal of its prey".
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.c_REF2_PH "TO ACCEPT THE VOID
[...] 'experience shows us as regards men that, by a necessity of nature, every being invariably exercises all the power of which it is capable’ (Thucydides).

[...] Not to exercise all the power at one’s disposal is to endure the void. This is contrary to all the laws of nature. Grace alone can do it.

[...] To accept a void in ourselves is supernatural. Where is the energy to be found for an act which has nothing to counterbalance it? The energy has to come from elsewhere. Yet first there must be a tearing out, something desperate has to take place, the void must be created. Void: the dark night."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d. TO TRULY UNDERSTAND THE DEPTHS OF REALITY, ONE MUST BE VULNERABLE, WITHOUT DEFENSES. The hero's armor may shield from harm, but it also obstructs direct interaction with reality and the realm of supernatural love. Consolations and illusions merely occupy the gaps left by the sting of reality.

E1.7.C.3.6.d.i. "IF YOU ONLY DESIRE THE GOOD, YOU ARE IN OPPOSITION TO THE LAW WHICH BINDS GOOD TO EVIL like the lighted object to the shadow, and, being in opposition to the universal law of the world, it is inevitable that you will fall into unhappiness". As long as the soul is not completely emptied of itself, this thirst for pure goodness leads to expiatory suffering.

E1.7.C.3.6.d.i_REF1_PH "HE WHO REFUSES CONFUSION IS CONDEMNED TO SUFFER. From Antigone, whom the guardian of the temporal city invites to go and love among the dead, to Simone Weil herself, whom human injustice crucified to the grave, misfortune is the lot of all lovers of the absolute lost in the relative: "If one desires only the good, one is in opposition to the law that binds good to evil, like the enlightened object to the shadow, and since one is in opposition to the universal law of the world, it is inevitable that one falls into misfortune. As long as the soul is not completely emptied of itself, this thirst for pure goodness produces expiatory suffering.

[Purity does not abolish suffering; on the contrary, it deepens it infinitely, but gives it eternal meaning".
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.i_REF2_PH IN ORDER FOR THINGS TO REALLY EXIST FOR US, THEY MUST PENETRATE US, WHICH REQUIRES BEING VULNERABLE 
"Supernatural love has no contact with force, but neither does it protect the soul from the cold of force, the cold of iron. Only an earthly attachment, if it contains enough energy, can protect against the cold of iron. Armor, like a sword, is made of metal. If you want a love that protects the soul from wounds, you have to love something other than God. The hero wears armor, the saint is naked. Well, armor protects against blows, but it prevents direct contact with reality and, above all, access to the third dimension, which is that of supernatural love. For things to really exist for us, they must penetrate us. Hence the need to be naked: nothing can penetrate us if the armor protects us both from wounds and from the depths they bring. Every sin is an attack on the third dimension, an attempt to return to the plane of the unreal, of the painless, a feeling that wants to penetrate the depths. This is a strict law: the more one exhausts one's intimate and direct communion with reality, the less one suffers. At the limit, life is spread out on the surface: one suffers no more than in a dream, because existence, reduced to two dimensions, becomes as flat as a dream. The same goes for consolations, illusions, boasting, and all the compensatory reactions with which we try to fill the voids that the bite of reality digs into us".
(Gustave Thibon, preface to Gravity and Grace, Février 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.i_REF3_PH THE DEVIL IS THE CONTRADICTORINESS OF OUR NATURE.
“Satan—The Adversary […]
We can understand the devil as those intimidating fiscal, social, political, and religious powers, which we reify into such entities as the Economy, Society, the Government, or the Church, and then treat as though they possessed a personal agency that has the power to condemn or destroy us.
“For the demonic,” reflects the theologian Paul Tillich, “is the elevation of something conditional to unconditional significance.” Each time something contingent and impermanent is raised to the status of something necessary and permanent, a devil is created. Whether it be an ego, a nation-state, or a religious belief, the result is the same. This distortion severs such things from their embeddedness in the complexities, fluidities, and ambiguities of the world and makes them appear as simple, fixed, and unambiguous entities with the power to condemn or save us. Far from being consciously chosen by individuals, such perceptions seem wired into the structure of our psychological, social, religious, and biological makeup. […]

The devil is the contradictoriness of our nature. As soon as we make a foolhardy commitment to “enlightenment” or “salvation,” we start being torn apart by diabolic forces we only dimly understand and can scarcely control. For when we choose to follow a path that Buddha described as “going against the stream,” we choose to confront those fears and desires that hitherto we had either repressed or acted out. Like someone who has been swimming downstream with minimal effort only to discover how exhausting, uncomfortable, and unrewarding it is to swim against the current, so one who embarks on such a path will invariably encounter a legion of obstacles.
The devil extends from that inner enemy who voices our most private doubts, conceits, and loathings to that outer enemy who afflicts us with disease, terrorizes us with real and imagined dangers, and ultimately kills us. […]

Rather than face the contingency of my existence, I flee it. This existential flight is the diabolic undercurrent of human life. It is that bewildered and fearful recoil against having been born and having to die, that brooding anxiety that is not anxious about anything in particular. Its quivering unease is like the lazy collision of two rings of ripples on water: one a reverberation from the shock of birth, the other an intimation of the shock of death. […]

Fear is the longing not to be hurt; the craving not to suffer misfortune; the yearning not to be contingent. It is the fundamental aversive reaction to the threats with which life confronts us. As well as being an emotion in its own right, fear pervades all self-centered emotion. Whether I am consumed by hatred or riddled with doubts, in both cases I am afraid—I want to avoid the pain inflicted by an enemy’s barbed remarks as much as I do the anguish of my own uncertainty.”
(Stephen Batchelor, Living with the Devil, 2004)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii. “OUR ATTACHMENT CONSTRUCTS THE REALITY OF THE WORLD. IT IS THE REALITY OF THE SELF THAT WE PROJECT ONTO THINGS. It is not an external reality. This can only be perceived through total detachment. Even if only a thread remains, attachment still exists. Attachment creates illusions, so anyone seeking reality must be detached.”

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF1_PH "DETACHMENT
Affliction in itself is not enough for the attainment of total detachment. Unconsoled affliction is necessary. There must be no consolation—no apparent consolation. Ineffable consolation then comes down.

[...] The reality of the world is the result of our attachment. It is the reality of the self which we transfer into things. It has nothing to do with independent reality. That is only perceptible through total detachment. Should only one thread remain, there is still attachment.

Affliction which forces us to attach ourselves to the most wretched objects exposes in all its misery the true character of attachment. In this way the necessity for detachment is made more obvious.

Attachment is a manufacturer of illusions and whoever wants reality ought to be detached.

[...] Attachment is no more nor less than an insufficiency in our sense of reality. We are attached to the possession of a thing because we think that if we cease to possess it, it will cease to exist. A great many people do not feel with their whole soul that there is all the difference in the world between the destruction of
a town and their own irremediable exile from that town.

[...] All suffering which does not detach us is wasted suffering. Nothing is more frightful, a desolate coldness, a warped soul"
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF1.1_PH "DÉSIRER SANS OBJET 

La purification est la séparation du bien et de la convoitise .

Descendre à la source des désirs pour arracher l’énergie à son objet . C’est là que les désirs sont vrais en tant qu’énergie . C’est l’objet qui est faux . Mais arrachement indicible dans l’âme à la séparation d’un désir et de son objet .

Si l’on descend en soi - même , on trouve qu’on possède exactement ce qu’on désire ."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF1.2_PH "TO DESIRE WITHOUT AN OBJECT

[...] To lose someone: we suffer because the departed, the absent, has become something imaginary and unreal. But our desire for him is not imaginary. We have to go down into ourselves to the abode of the desire which is not imaginary. Hunger: we imagine kinds of food, but the hunger itself is real: we have to fasten on to the hunger. The presence of the dead person is imaginary, but his absence is very real: henceforward it is his way of appearing.

[...] The handshake of a friend on meeting again after a long absence. I do not even notice whether it gives pleasure or pain to my sense of touch: like the blind man who feels the objects directly at the end of his stick, I feel the presence of my friend directly. It is the same with life’s circumstances, whatever they may be, and God.

This implies that we should never seek consolation for pain. Because felicity is beyond the realm of consolation and pain. We become aware of it through a sense which is different, just as the perception of objects at the end of a stick or an instrument is different from touch in the strict sense of the word. This other sense is formed by a shifting of the attention through an apprenticeship in which the whole soul and body participate. [...] "
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF1.3_PH "THE SELF
[...] Everything without exception which is of value in me comes from somewhere other than myself, not as a gift but as a loan which must be ceaselessly renewed. Everything without exception which is in me is absolutely valueless; and, among the gifts which have come to me from elsewhere, everything which I appropriate becomes valueless immediately I do so.

Perfect joy excludes even the very feeling of joy, for in the soul filled by the object no corner is left for saying ‘I’. We cannot imagine such joys when they are absent, thus the incentive for seeking them is lacking."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF2_LIT WHAT IT’S LIKE TO BE A FALCON: THE PEREGRINE AS A PORTAL TO A WAY OF SEEING, A STATE OF BEING, EXEPRIMENTING DETACHMENT
"Standing in the fields near the north orchard, I shut my eyes and tried to crystallise my will into the light-drenched prism of the hawk’s mind. Warm and firm-footed in long grass smelling of the sun, I sank into the skin and blood and bones of the hawk. The ground became a branch to my feet, the sun on my eyelids was heavy and warm. Like the hawk, I heard and hated the sound of man, that faceless horror of the stony places. I stifled in the same filthy sack of fear. I shared the same hunter’s longing for the wild home none can know, alone with the sight and smell of the quarry, under the indifferent sky. I felt the pull of the north, the mystery and fascination of the migrating gulls. I shared the same strange yearning to be gone. I sank down and slept into the feather-light sleep of the hawk."
(J.A. Baker, The Peregrine, 1967)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF2.1_LIT A TIMELESS REMINDER OF THE BEAUTY AND WONDER FOUND IN CLOSE, ATTENTIVE OBSERVATION 
"In the mid-1950s, a near-sighted English office worker set out to do for the sky what Rachel Carson had done for the sea thirty years earlier — invite our human imagination, grounded yet boundless, into the world of another creature dwelling in another sphere. J.A. Baker (August 6, 1926–December 26, 1987) spent a decade following earth’s fastest flying bird on bicycle and on foot, possessed by its “restless brilliance.” When he unloosed The Peregrine (public library) into the atmosphere of culture in 1967 — an atmosphere shaped by the new ecological conscience awakened by Carson’s Silent Spring five years earlier — it was a clarion call and a consecration, entirely original, yet emanating Thoreau’s meticulous observation, Whitman’s ecstatic language, and Carson’s soulful reverence for the realities of nature in all their brutal beauty. An epoch later, it remains an ode to wonder, a field guide to observation as devotional practice, a passionate and poetic reminder that by attending closely and tenderly to any one thing, we recover our natural reverence for everything, our love of the world in all its strangeness and splendor."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.ii_REF2.2_LIT "YOU CANNOT KNOW WHAT FREEDOM MEANS till you have seen a peregrine loosed into the warm spring sky to roam at will through all the far provinces of light. [...]

The eyes of a falcon peregrine weigh approximately one ounce each; they are larger and heavier than human eyes. If our eyes were in the same proportion to our bodies as the peregrine’s are to his, a twelve-stone man would have eyes three inches across, weighing four pounds. The whole retina of a hawk’s eye records a resolution of distant objects that is twice as acute as that of the human retina. Where the lateral and binocular visions focus, there are deep-pitted foveal areas; their numerous cells record a resolution eight times as great as ours. This means that a hawk, endlessly scanning the landscape with small abrupt turns of his head, will pick up any point of movement; by focusing upon it he can immediately make it flare up into larger, clearer view.

The peregrine’s view of the land is like the yachtsman’s view of the shore as he sails into the long estuaries. A wake of water recedes behind him, the wake of the pierced horizon glides back on either side. Like the seafarer, the peregrine lives in a pouring-away world of no attachment, a world of wakes and tilting, of sinking planes of land and water. We who are anchored and earthbound cannot envisage this freedom of the eye. The peregrine sees and remembers patterns we do not know exist: the neat squares of orchard and woodland, the endlessly varying quadrilateral shapes of fields. He finds his way across the land by a succession of remembered symmetries. [...]

Slowly he drifted above the orchard skyline and circled down wind, curving upward and round in long steep glides. He passed from the cold white sky of the south, up to the warm blue zenith, ascending the wind-bent thermal with wonderful ease and skill. His long-winged, blunt-headed shape contracted, dwindled, and darkened to the flinty point of a diamond as he circled high and far over; hanging and drifting above; indolent, watchful, supreme. Looking down, the hawk saw the big orchard beneath him shrink into dark twiggy lines and green strips; saw the dark woods closing together and reaching out across the hills; saw the green and white fields turning to brown; saw the silver line of the brook, and the coiled river slowly uncoiling; saw the whole valley flattening and widening; saw the horizon staining with distant towns; saw the estuary lifting up its blue and silver mouth, tongued with green islands. And beyond, beyond all, he saw the straight-ruled shine of the sea floating like a rim of mercury on the surface of the brown and white land. The sea, rising as he rose, lifted its blazing storm of light, and thundered to freedom to the land-locked hawk… I watched him with longing, as though he were reflecting down to me his brilliant unregarded vision of the land beyond the hill… He sank forward into the wind, and passed slowly down across the sun. I had to let him go. When I looked back, through green and violet nebulae of whirling light, I could just see a tiny speck of dusk falling to earth from the sun, flashing and turning and falling through an immense silence that crashed open in a tumult of shrilling, wing-beating birds."
(J.A. Baker, The Peregrine, 1967)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.iii. "THE SELF
[...] Once we have begun the process of destroying the I, we can prevent any misfortune from doing harm. For the I is not destroyed by external pressure without extreme revolt. If we refuse this revolt out of love for God, then the destruction of the I does not come from without, but from within."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.iv. WE MUST RENOUNCE BEING SOMETHING. THIS IS THE ONLY GOOD FOR US. “[Suffering] is God withdrawing from us out of love so we can love Him. If we were exposed to the direct radiation of His love, without the protection of space, time, and matter, we would evaporate like water in the sun. There wouldn't be enough 'I' in us to abandon the 'I' out of love. Necessity is the screen put between God and us, allowing us to exist. It's up to us to pierce this screen and cease to exist. This is renunciation. It is imitating God's renunciation in creation. God, in a sense, relinquished being everything. We must relinquish being something. This is the only good for us. We must be nothing in order to be in our true place in the whole."
(Simone Weil)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.iv_REF1_PH "DECREATION

[...]  Creation is an act of love and it is perpetual. At each moment our existence is God’s love for us. But God can only love himself. His love for us is love for himself through us. Thus, he who gives us our being loves in us the acceptance of not being.

[...]  Relentless necessity, wretchedness, distress, the crushing burden of poverty and of labour which wears us out, cruelty, torture, violent death, constraint, disease—all these constitute divine love. It is God who in love withdraws from us so that we can love him. For if we were exposed to the direct radiance of his love, without
the protection of space, of time and of matter, we should be evaporated like water in the sun; there would not be enough ‘I’ in us to make it possible to surrender the ‘I’ for love’s sake. Necessity is the screen set between God and us so that we can be. It is for us to pierce through the screen so that we cease to be.

[...]  Renunciation. Imitation of God’s renunciation in creation. In a sense God renounces being everything. We should renounce being something. That is our only good.

Everything which is grasped by our natural faculties is hypothetical. It is only supernatural love that establishes anything. Thus we are co-creators.

We participate in the creation of the world by decreating ourselves.

[...]  We only possess what we renounce; what we do not renounce escapes from us. In this sense, we cannot possess anything whatever unless it passes through God.

[...]  For men of courage physical sufferings (and privations) are often a test of endurance and of strength of soul. But there is a better use to be made of them. For me then, may they not be that. May they rather be a testimony, lived and felt, of human misery. May I endure them in a completely passive manner. Whatever happens, how could I ever think an affliction too great, since the wound of an affliction and the abasement to which those whom it strikes are condemned opens to them the knowledge of human misery, knowledge which is the door of all wisdom?

But pleasure, happiness, prosperity, if we know how to recognize in them all that comes from outside (chance, circumstances, etc.), likewise bear testimony to human misery. They should be used in the same way. This applies even to grace, in so far as it is a sensible phenomenon.

We have to be nothing in order to be in our right place in the whole."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.iv_REF1.1_PH "HE WHOM WE MUST LOVE IS ABSENT

[...] The irreducible character of suffering which makes it impossible  for us not to have a horror of it at the moment when we are undergoing it is destined to bring the will to a standstill, just as absurdity brings the intelligence to a standstill, and absence love, so that man, having come to the end of his human faculties, may stretch out his arms, stop, look up and wait."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.iv_REF2_PH NICOLAS GRIMALDI ARGUES THAT TRUE FREEDOM IS FOUND NOT IN NIHILISM OR SELF-INTEREST, BUT IN AN ACTIVE "ABNEGATION" THAT SHEDS THE ISOLATING OBSESSION WITH OUR PRECARIOUS "SINGULARITY," allowing us to escape the prison of desire and participate in a "vaster life" of universal truth and eternal communion.
"Beyond desire and time, amazed to exist, active and carefree—such is the freedom of which various wisdoms speak. However, this freedom is opposed to the most spontaneous tendencies of each individual, the domineering and predatory attitude that technology prolongs, and the mirror games without which society could no longer exist. Thus, is this freedom not a subtle form of nihilism, shielding us from both the demands of life and those of society? However, self-denial is not nihilism. It is not even renunciation. On the contrary, it is the simple negation within us of all the negations that tend to destroy us. If it refuses to take the side of the individual, it is because it has taken the side of a broader life.
Yet, such an experience is less exceptional than it seems. Although contemplation is required, withdrawal is not. Almost everyone has sensed it, recognized it, felt called to it, and promised to devote their lives to it at one time or another.
Sometimes, an earthquake of history strips us of all the appearances we believed to be inseparable from our identity. By turning us into former beings, it reveals a truth we spent our time hiding from ourselves: we die as we are born—in essential destitution and poverty—and everything not devoted to the service of life is lost in the meantime.
[...] For the life of the spirit is freedom itself. Spinoza believed that knowledge of the truth gives us access to freedom by allowing us to participate in the eternal understanding of the infinite. However, more than temporality, it is solitude from which the experience of truth frees us. In it, we experience ourselves as more deeply and indissolubly united with others through the universality of what possesses us than with ourselves through the particularity of what we possess. Thus, an unbreakable community can be founded on the universality of truth and the joy of eternal communion based on this community's principles.
However, as with Spinoza, this joy would merely be intellectual if we did not feel ourselves participating in a greater life that grounds and justifies our own. Spinoza invites us to recognize infinite understanding as the original reflexivity through which the eternal simplicity of God becomes life. Each of us feels this principle of life's universal prodigality within ourselves, and we are free to make ourselves mediators of it. 
Only our ordinary concern for our individuality prevents us from abandoning ourselves to it. This concern imprisons us in desire and its contradictions. This concern also turns our irremediable incompleteness into loneliness. However, when an excess of ingenuity or lucidity renders our precarious and transitory singularity indifferent or almost insubstantial, nothing that matters to others matters to us anymore. Only this spiritual life, to which every act of self-denial bears witness, manifests a freedom in time that is free from the ambiguities of temporality.
Note:
(1) Ashtâvakra, op. cit., XIII, 3, p. 47: "Nothing can be accomplished. When one has deeply understood this, whatever one has to accomplish, one is always serene." "
(Nicolas Grimaldi, The Ambiguities of Freedom, 1999)

in the world of love,
it was freed
from both belief
and from disbelief.

On this journey,
I found the problem
to be myself.

When I went beyond myself,
the pathway finally opened ».
(Mahsati Ganjavi)

E1.7.C.3.6.d.  THE ONLY FREE ACTION WE ARE PERMITTED IS THE DESTRUCTION OF OUR EGO.
For Simone Weil, in this world, the only thing we truly possess is the ability to say "I". This ability must be surrendered to God, meaning it needs to be destroyed. However, severe misfortune can strip us of this ability, leaving us devoid of free will and solely focused on survival.

E1.7.C.3.6.d_REF1_PH "THE SELF

We possess nothing in the world—a mere chance can strip us of everything—except the power to say ‘I’. That is what we have to give to God—in other words, to destroy. There is absolutely no other free act which it is given us to accomplish—only the destruction of the ‘I’.

[...] Nothing in the world can rob us of the power to say ‘I’. Nothing except extreme affliction. Nothing is worse than extreme affliction which destroys the ‘I’ from utside, because after that we can no longer destroy it ourselves.

[...] In affliction the vital instinct survives all the attachments which have been torn away, and blindly fastens itself to everything which can provide it with support, like a plant fastens its tendrils. Gratitude (except in a base form) and justice are not conceivable in this state. Slavery. There is no longer the extra amount of energy required to support free-will by which man takes the measure of things. Affliction, from this point of view, is hideous as life in its nakedness always is, like an amputated stump, like the swarming of insects. Life without form. Survival is then the only attachment. That is where extreme affliction begins—when all other attachments are replaced by that of survival. Attachment appears then in its nakedness without any other object but
itself—Hell.

It is by this mechanism that to those in affliction life appears as the one thing desirable, at the very time when their life is in no way preferable to death.

In this state, to accept death is total detachment."
(Simone Weil, Gravity and Grace, 1947)

E1.7.C.4. “DOES THE AESTHETIC PERSPECTIVE ESSENTIALLY INVOLVE VIEWING THE WORLD WITH A JOYFUL GAZE? [...] I have a clear conscience; nothing can harm me, no matter what [...]. These two feelings, astonishment and security, are deeply connected. They are affective experiences that are hard to adequately express because both require a disinterested and indifferent attitude towards specific objects in the world.”

E1.7.C.4.1. WITTGENSTEIN ASSIGNS A DUAL ETHICAL AND AESTHETIC DIMENSION TO THE DISINTERESTED CONTEMPLATION OF THE WORLD. The aesthetic perspective involves viewing the world from the outside, completely disinterested in its practical utility.

E1.7.C.4.1_REF1_PH WITTGENSTEIN COMBINES THE NOTION OF DISINTERESTED KNOWLEDGE AND CONSCIOUSNESS OF THE WORLD IN THE PERSPECTIVE OF ETERNITY WITH AN AESTHETIC AND ETHICAL VISION OF THE WORLD, IN WHICH TIMELINESS AND DISINTERESTED CONSCIOUSNESS PROVIDE SALVATION. This approach emphasizes that science does not satisfy all our desires and leaves fundamental questions unresolved.
"Wittgenstein's account of the mystical, as well as of the aesthetic and, in a sense, the ethical, is located in Schopenhauer's problematic. What Wittgenstein says about the mystical corresponds, in fact, to what Schopenhauer says about knowledge that frees itself from the service of will (1) and individuality. Wittgenstein is certainly thinking about this theme when he writes in Proposition 6.45: "The intuition of the world sub specie aeterni is the intuition of the world as totality - but limited totality. The feeling of the world as limited totality is the mystical feeling."
Let us note in parentheses the rapid shift that takes place here from the notion of intuition to that of feeling. First of all, the use of the term "limited totality" is reminiscent of the author of The World as Will and Representation, who writes that the subject and the object, like two halves, constitute the totality of the world, but are limited by each other. The world appears as a limited totality when it is considered from the point of view of a transcendental subject, which, according to Wittgenstein, is neither man, nor the human body, nor the human soul, but the metaphysical subject, which is the limit of the world and not a part of it, an object that would be in the world (5.632, 5. 633 and 5.641, Notebooks, August 7, 1916). This situation is realized precisely in the disinterested intuition of the world, freed from the principle of sufficient reason, of which Schopenhauer speaks (2). We no longer consider, says Schopenhauer, "neither the place, nor the time, nor the why, nor the what-good of things, but purely and simply their nature"; one is no longer oneself, but a "pure subject" (is this not the metaphysical subject of which Wittgenstein speaks? ), lost in the object. On this point, Schopenhauer quotes Spinoza: "The mind is eternal insofar as it conceives of things in the perspective of eternity (sub specie aeternitatis)," which for Schopenhauer means that those who see things in this perspective transcend their individuality and identify with the eternal subject of knowledge. Wittgenstein's Notebooks (October 7, 1916) shed some light on what this formula of Schopenhauer and Spinoza means to him. First, it corresponds to an aesthetic and ethical vision of the world:

The work of art is the object seen sub specie aeternitatis, and the good life is the world seen sub specie aeternitatis. This is the connection between art and ethics. In the ordinary way of seeing, one looks at objects, so to speak, by placing oneself among them; in the way of seeing sub specie aeternitatis, one looks at them from the outside. In such a way that they have the whole world as a background. 
Does the aesthetic view of the world consist essentially in looking at the world with a happy gaze? (October 20, 1916). 

One could say that to see from the perspective of eternity, that is, "from the outside," is in a sense to see from above, with a total disinterest in practical utility, or even to see from the perspective of an eternal present. For, as Wittgenstein (6.4311) tells us, following Schopenhauer, eternity should not be understood as an infinite period of time, but as timelessness: "He lives eternally who lives in the present". For Wittgenstein, as for Schopenhauer, the disinterested contemplation of the world that brings salvation therefore has a dual ethical and aesthetic dimension.
From this perspective, we can better understand the profound intentions of the Tractatus, as we are invited to do by a note in the Notebooks dated May 25, 1915: "The tendency to the mystical comes from the fact that science leaves our desires unsatisfied. We feel that even if all scientific questions were solved, our problem would still not be solved."

Note: 
(1) Schopenhauer, The World as Will and Representation.
(2) Idem
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004, extract from the 2014 preface)

E1.7.C.4.2. WITTGENSTEIN EMPHASIZES THE LIMITATIONS OF LANGUAGE AND THE CONCEPT THAT SILENT WISDOM IS ATTAINED BY TRANSCENDING THE PROPOSITIONS OF PHILOSOPHY. Conversely, Pierre Hadot expresses skepticism about the feasibility of leading a life of silent wisdom. He underscores that philosophy should be both a critical discourse and a practice of self-transformation.

E1.7.C.4.2_REF1_PH IS WITTGENSTEIN'S IDEAL OF SILENT WISDOM ATTAINABLE?" It is also interesting to note that in the Conference on Ethics, in addition to the feeling of astonishment at the existence of the world, which he says is his experience par excellence, he also alludes to another experience, the feeling of absolute security, which is also familiar to him; it can be expressed as follows (though he notes that this expression is logically nonsense): "I have a clear conscience, nothing can happen to me, no matter what", a phrase with a stoic tone, because the person who admits that there is no evil other than moral evil also knows that, if he wants, nothing bad can happen to him. These two feelings, these two experiences of astonishment and security, which cannot be adequately expressed because they are affective experiences, are deeply related in that they both imply a disposition of disinterest and indifference toward the determined objects of the world. If Wittgenstein insists so much on the limits of language, it is because he ultimately wants to offer a glimpse of a state of silent wisdom that would be attained by those who have gone beyond the propositions of the Tractatus. All the discourse of the book will have been rejected, like a ladder that has become useless and destroys itself after fulfilling its therapeutic function (like the philosophical discourse of the skeptics of antiquity, which they considered a simple laxative to be eliminated with bad moods), to give way to the silence of a life of wisdom, in which the problem of life will be solved by its own disappearance. He must overcome these propositions," says Wittgenstein (6.54), "then he sees the world correctly" (would this not be a return to the contemplation of the world, freed from the principle of sufficient reason, of which Schopenhauer spoke?)
Personally, I doubt that this ideal of a life of quiet wisdom is attainable. Wittgenstein's own life shows that he could not adhere to it. I would be more inclined to think, as I have said elsewhere, that one should not oppose a philosophy conceived as pure discourse to a wisdom that would be a silent way of life. For wisdom is not a state that would put an end to philosophy, but an unattainable ideal that motivates the philosopher's endless search. Philosophy, therefore, since it is an aspiration to wisdom, must be at once and indissolubly a critical discourse and the exercise of self-transformation."
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004, extract from the 2014 preface)

E1.7.C.4.3. "I ADMIT THAT I EXIST. 
IT'S AMAZING AND NATURAL. 
TO BE HERE."

E1.7.C.4.3_REF1_PO I AM A « CONDUCTOR » IN THE ELECTRICAL SENSE OF THE WORD.

"A trip into the obvious

I admit that I exist. 
It's amazing and natural. 
To be here. 
I meet myself every day.
Time is a sham if it governs this. 
With discretion and tenacity, I explore the full flavor of the world. 
I am a « conductor » in the electrical sense of the word. 
Joy is a conquest over waiting. 
It is presence to oneself in the open day of possibilities. 
One must dare to be tender, ignorant, sensual. 
Truth is that I am able to rejoice from what makes me. 
This is knowledge, and a laziness of knowledge, a lasciviousness of being. 
Since the factual hive of the world and the tempestuous space of the mind do the rest."
(Christophe Béguin, War of Beauty, 2011)

"Une virée dans l’évidence 

J’avoue que j’existe. 
C’est stupéfiant et naturel. 
D’être là. 
Je me rencontre tous les jours.
Le Temps est une imposture s’il gouverne cela. 
Avec discrétion et ténacité, j’explore la pleine saveur du monde. 
Je suis « conducteur » au sens électrique du terme. 
La joie est une conquête sur l’attente. 
Elle est présence à soi dans le jour ouvert du possible. 
Il faut oser être tendre, ignorant, sensuel. 
La vérité, c’est ce que je suis capable de jouir de ce qui me fait. 
C’est cela la connaissance, et une paresse du savoir, une lascivité d’être. 
Puisque la ruche factuelle du monde et l’espace tempétueux du mental font le reste."
(Christophe Béguin, Guerre de beauté, Impeccables, 2011)

E1.7.C.5. “THE ENERGY THAT IS THE TOTAL AWARENESS OF ONE’S OWN EXISTENCE CAN BECOME  THE STRONGEST ENERGY IN HUMAN LIFE. It comes together with the need to obey that energy. You are objectively obliged to give, to serve, to manifest that energy in action and understanding.”

E1.7.C.5_REF1_PH "Because you are struggling, your question begins to deepen… What you will discover, always for the first time, always new, in the fleeting moment of wonder — before that moment is captured by the ambitions of personality. You, I, in that moment, will discover the need to serve the energy, uniquely human and also sacred, that starts as the pure awareness of one’s own existence. And even as this idea — this beginning idea — of what is human, even as this idea of what is man, begins to appear — even in that fleeting moment of the pure awareness of my existence given now by a great idea — in that moment in front of a living idea, an awakening idea, a glimpse appears of the uniquely human yearning to serve; the need appears, the need to obey that energy, the need to attend to it, to be nourished by it, to receive the help that comes then and only then, when you are objectively obliged to give, to serve, to manifest that energy in action and understanding. It is only that energy of conscious existence that gives you, a human being, real strength. The energy that is the total awareness of one’s own existence is — or can become, can be — the strongest energy in human life."
(Jacob Needleman, I Am Not I, 2016)

E1.7.C.5_REF1.1_PH "ACKNOWLEDGING THE ILLUSORINESS OF FREE will liberates us rather than tak[e] away our freedom. Pointing out how impossible it is to understand freedom without understanding the influences acting upon us, the laws of the universe, and the nature of reality, [Needleman] considers the source of real freedom"
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.7.C.5_REF1.2_PH "ASK YOURSELF WHAT IS YOUR UNDERSTANDING OF THE INFLUENCES ACTING UPON US — of the universal laws in nature? What are your thoughts about that? And the teachings of religion — the idea of faith, obedience to the higher, responsibility for others and oneself, the deceptions and revelations of sleep and dreaming, the very idea of man’s place in the living, breathing, sentient cosmos, our place on our planet, the demand for morality, the nature of animal instinct and intuition within us and around us, the function and the meaning of pain and pleasure, the idea and the experience of consciousness and conscience, the subtle nourishment in the air we breathe, the food we eat, the genuine and the fabricated needs and desires of the body, the powerful influences of symbols, the cosmic and intimate force of sex, the inevitability of death, the illusion and the reality of time. […]

Working like this, and maintaining the fundamental attitude of sincerity about yourself and your discoveries, you will become disillusioned not only with your certainties, but with the structure of your mind itself. You will realize that what you need is not new beliefs, new information, new theories, but an entirely new mind. [...]

Real ideas open the mind to the heart, to the heart of the mind, to another level of reality within ourselves… This is the taste, the beginning, of inner freedom. Only fools imagine that freedom means getting what one happens to desire. Real freedom begins with obedience to a higher influence — a higher, finer energy within oneself. […]

What is higher in yourself? That way of thinking about the question is the beginning of the answer — because it involves a real idea which has been handed down to humanity over thousands of years… At such a point you yourself will find the answer — not as a thought, but as an experience.

You will for a moment become the answer! You will not only have a taste of real freedom; you will for a moment be freedom."
(Jacob Needleman, I Am Not I, 2016)

E1.7.C.5_REF2_LIT TO LOOK ON ANYTHING, SUCH AS A MOUNTAIN, WITH THE LOVE THAT PENETRATES TO ITS ESSENCE, IS TO WIDEN THE DOMAIN OF BEING IN THE VASTNESS OF NON-BEING. MAN HAS NO OTHER REASON FOR HIS EXISTENCE.
"A scatter of white flowers in grass, looked at through half-closed eyes, blaze out with a sharp clarity as though they had actually risen up out of their background. Such illusions, depending on how the eye is placed and used, drive home the truth that our habitual vision of things is not necessarily right: it is only one of an infinite number, and to glimpse an unfamiliar one, even for a moment, unmakes us, but steadies us again. [...] 
How can I number the worlds to which the eye gives me entry? — the world of light, of colour, of shape, of shadow: of mathematical precision in the snowflake, the ice formation, the quartz crystal, the patterns of stamen and petal: of rhythm in the fluid curve and plunging line of the mountain faces. Why some blocks of stone, hacked into violent and tortured shapes, should so profoundly tranquillise the mind I do not know. Perhaps the eye imposes its own rhythm on what is only a confusion: one has to look creatively to see this mass of rock as more than jag and pinnacle — as beauty… A certain kind of consciousness interacts with the mountain-forms to create this sense of beauty. Yet the forms must be there for the eye to see. And forms of a certain distinction: mere dollops won’t do it. It is, as with all creation, matter impregnated with mind: but the resultant issue is a living spirit, a glow in the consciousness, that perishes when the glow is dead. It is something snatched from non-being, that shadow which creeps in on us continuously and can be held off by continuous creative act. So, simply to look on anything, such as a mountain, with the love that penetrates to its essence, is to widen the domain of being in the vastness of non-being. Man has no other reason for his existence."
(Nan Shepherd,  The Living Mountain, 1977)

E1.7.C.5_REF2.1_LIT "THERE IS NO PURE PERCEPTION — of a flower, of a mountain, of a person. In everything we look at, we see partly a reflection of ourselves — a projection of an internal model seeking to approximate the actuality. If we are conscious enough and unafraid enough of being surprised, we will keep testing the model against reality, incrementally ceding the imagined to the actual. One measure of love — perhaps the deepest measure — is the willingness to remove the projection in order to perceive what is truly there. There is both sorrow and consolation in knowing that although we can only ever glimpse parts of the totality beyond us, we can keep trying to see more clearly in order to love more deeply."
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.7.D. THE CONVERGENT INTUITIONS OF THOUGHT BETWEEN THE PRE-SOCRATIC PHILOSOPHIES OF ASIA AND THE WEST WERE FORGOTTEN AFTER PLATONIC METAPHYSICS AND CHRISTIANITY. They resurfaced, very peripherally, in the 19th century, and the convergence of thought was reinforced in the 20th century. However, this thinking remains marginal today, as it is difficult to understand and its apparent detachment is often perceived as a threat to the productivity and result-oriented mindset that dominates our times.

E1.7.D.1 THE PRESOCRATICS AND ASIAN PHILOSOPHIES BEFORE OUR ERA: Similar intuitions emerged almost concurrently in the 5th century B.C. This occurred among the Presocratics, particularly Heraclitus, in the West, as well as among the early philosophies of Asia.

E1.7.D.1.1. HERACLITUS (around 500 B.C.)
The West favored Plato over Heraclitus and overlooked the East for two millennia.

E1.7.D.1.3.a. THE GREEK PHILOSOPHER HERACLITUS PROMOTED THE IMPORTANCE OF ATTENTION AND MINDFULNESS, the relativity of rationality, and the concept of the world as a constant flux underscored by a principle of unity. He perceived identity as a result of flux rather than a static essence. Considered a monist, Heraclitus saw a fundamental unity in the world's diversity and change. His influence is evident in later philosophers such as the Stoics, Hegel, Nietzsche, and Heidegger. Plato's world of forms can be viewed as a response to Heraclitus' ongoing process of becoming.

E1.7.D.1.3.a_REF1_PH "HERACLITUS
Analyzing Heractlus' fragments, it is remarkable how his parallel those of Buddhist philosophy: 
- The importance of attention and mindfulness conscience
- The relativism of rationality
- The world as a perpetual flux underlined by a principle of unity
- Identity as the produce of a flux and not as a still essence

"The bow, its name is life, its work is death" (a pun on words since in Greek bow = biòs and life = bios), the idea that opposite things are coinciding.
"The bow turns back on itself", the idea that a bow consists of being pulled in two directions at the same time.
"It is wise to agree that all things are one", Heraclitus is thought of as a monist, seeing an underlying unity to diversity and change that we see in the world.
"This account (logos) being always the case, always men fail to understand it, both before they hear it and when first they have heard. For  although all things come to be according to this account, men seem to be without experience of it even when they try out all the words and deeds of the kind I set out when I distinguish each thing according to its nature and say how it is. Other men do not pay attention to what they do when they are awake, just as they do not pay attention to what they do when they are asleep." This is the Introduction of his book. Logos has several meanings and we can consider Heraclitus is not talking about his book but also about the universe, that is reality. He seems to believe there is an ordering principle of the world under what appears to us only flux and opposing principles.
"You never bathe twice in the same river" (grammatically in Greek, it is not clear if the change applies to the change in You or the passing of the water). As all is flux, on the one hand we have identities created through flux - as in the barley drink H. makes reference to and that need to be stirred continuously as you drink it - "The barley drink stands still when it moves" - or the river that would be a lake and not a river if it was not flowing; on the other hand, to what extend does something changes so that it is not itself anymore? To what extend flux destroys identity? Cratylus, one of H. 'disciple push the reasoning further and said that because of flux you cannot have an identity in the first place, and so you cannot even bathe in the river ounce, you cannot even name a river!
If we reduce H. to the idea of flux without mitigating with the idea of unifying opposites, we are drown to Cratylus' position - however this is probably an inadequate reduction of his thought. H. gives us many occurrences of relativism, where contraries are unified in the same thing:
"We value gold. Donkeys don't, but they value trash that we do not value", "the road up and down, one and the same". H. might answer Cratylus that identity is actually secured by the unity of opposites. These differences of opposites and flux in one and the same thing should not alarm us, because these characteristics are part and parcel of reality. We should recognize that our human perspective is just one possible perspective available on the world (he contrasts human perspective with that of animal - donkey, fish).
At the same period, Parmenides - an extreme monist - promotes a conflicting view that there is only being and nothing besides that.
"Eternity is a child at play playing drafts, the kingdom is a child's" would mean you need a child's fresh attitude to gain understanding of unity.
"Eyesight is a better witness than hearing", might mean look for yourself, don't just listen to what other people think.
"If lots of learning made people wise then it would have made these people wise" and he proceeds to cite Pythagorus, Xenophonus and others.
Some of Heraclitus' influence on latter philosophers:
- Plato's world of the forms in reaction to Heraclitus' continuous process of becoming
- The stoics picked up from Heraclitus the unifying rational divine principle guiding the world, 
- Hegel incorporated as a central principle to his philosophy the resolution of opposites into a syntheses, 
- In Ecce Homo Nietzsche says that the doctrine of becoming of Zarathustra was anticipated by Heraclitus,
- Heidegger loves the pre-Socratics since for him, western philosophy goes wrong with Plato's metaphysics"
(Heraclitus, Program brodcast in In Our Time on BBC4 on 8 Dec 2011)

Listen to the program: https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b017x3p4

E1.7.D.1.3.b. PLATO INTERRUPTS THE GREAT PHILOSOPHICAL ADVENTURE BEGUN BY THE PRESOCRATICS.

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF1_PH "WE CAN FIND AN INAUGURAL MOMENT OF THE BIFURCATION OF WESTERN PHILOSOPHY IN PLATO'S PHILEBUS. Protarchus and Socrates are walking in the countryside and Socrates asks if what he sees in the distance is a stone or a man. Instead of sticking to this presence where something has opened up, he declares that as long as we don't know what it is, then it is nothing. The problem is the status we give to identity, the way the mind freezes and believes in the labels placed on things - creating informational causality: It is the identities I have identified that identify and enclose me. The proposition of Aristotelian metaphysics that to be is to be something - that is to have a meaning - is challenged head-on by Buddhism, which works against assigning meaning."
(Alexis Lavis in the porgram Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 08/06/2018)

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF2_PH NIETZSCHE LAMENTS THE LOSS OF A SUPERIOR FORM OF PHILOSOPHICAL LIFE AND SUGGESTS THAT A GREAT CATASTROPHE INTERRUPTED THIS EVOLUTION WITH PLATO, LEAVING A VOID AND AN UNREVEALED MYSTERY.
"TYRANTS OF THE SPIRIT. [...]

With the Greeks, things go forward swiftly, but also as swiftly downwards; the movement of the whole mechanism is so intensified that a single stone, thrown into its wheels, makes it burst. Such a stone was Socrates, for example; in one night, the development of philosophical science, until then so wonderfully regular but, of course, all too swift, was destroyed. It is no idle question to wonder whether Plato, if he had stayed free of the Socratic spell, might not have found an even higher type of the
philosophical man, now lost to us forever. We look into the ages before him as into a sculptor’s workshop, full of such types. The sixth and fifth centuries, however, seem to promise even more and greater things than they produced; but it remained at promises and declarations. And yet there is hardly a heavier loss than the loss of a type, the loss of a new, previously undiscovered, supreme possibility of philosophical life. Even of the older types, most have been handed down to us inadequately; it seems to me extraordinarily difficult to see any philosopher from Thales to Democritus clearly; but the man who is successful in recreating these figures strolls among creatures of the mightiest and purest type. Of course, this ability is rare; even the later Greeks who studied the older philosophers did not have it. Aristotle, particularly, seems not to have his eyes in his head when he is faced with them. And so it seems as if these marvelous philosophers had lived in vain, or even as if they had only been meant to prepare the way for the combative and garrulous hordes of the Socratic schools. As we said, there is a gap here, a break in development; some great misfortune must
have occurred, and the sole statue in which we might have recognized the sense and purpose of that great creative preparatory exercise must have broken or been nsuccessful. What actually happened has remained forever a secret of the workshop. [...]"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Human, All Too Human, Aphorism 261, 1878)

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF3.1_PH "ZETETIC [...] 
Words are only a moment of pure and universal appearance, as Marcel Conche says again, a moment of becoming, as I would prefer to say, which they can neither contain in their entirety nor transform - except illusorily - into fixed or immutable essences. Thus, for Pyrrhon, ataraxia (wisdom, peace of mind) was not without aphasia (non-speech, silence). Not because one cannot speak (aphasia is not aphasia), nor because one should not speak, but because "words can never erase silence" (M. Conche, Pyrrhon ou l'apparence, X, 1).
(André Comte-Sponville, Philosophical Dictionary, 2013)
