E1.7.D.1.2. CLAUDE LÉVI-STRAUSS LAMENTS THAT THE WEST OVERLOOKED THE WISDOMS OF THE EAST FOR TWO MILLENNIA. He believed these wisdoms offered insights superior to those of the religions that succeeded them.

E1.7.D.1.2_REF1_PH "MAN REALLY CREATES GREAT THINGS ONLY IN THE BEGINNING; in every field, only the first step is fully valid. [...] I pay tribute to the decisive reflection that a thinker or the society that created his legend did twenty-five centuries ago, and to which my civilization could only contribute by confirming it. What else have I learned from the masters I have listened to, the philosophers I have read, the societies I have visited, and from this very science of which the West is so proud, if not scraps of lessons which, put together, reconstruct the meditation of the sage at the foot of the tree? Every effort to understand destroys the object to which we have become attached, in favor of an effort that abolishes it in favor of a third, and so on, until we reach the only permanent presence, that is, the one in which the distinction between meaning and meaninglessness disappears: the same one from which we had departed. It is two thousand five hundred years since humans discovered and formulated these truths. Since then, we have found nothing but - by trying every exit door after another - so many additional demonstrations of the conclusion from which we would have liked to "escape".
(Claude Lévi-Strauss, Tristes Tropiques, 1955)

E1.7.D.1.2.a_REF1_PH BUDDHISM REDUCES THE METAPHYSICAL PROBLEM TO THAT OF HUMAN CONDUCT "This great religion of non-knowledge [Buddhism] is not based on our inability to understand. It testifies to our capacity and elevates us to the point where we discover the truth in the form of a mutual exclusion of being and knowing. With additional audacity, it has - alone with Marxism - reduced the metaphysical problem to that of human behavior".
(Claude Lévi-Strauss, Tristes Tropiques, 1955)

E1.7.D.1.3. BUDHISM

E1.7.D.1.3.a_REF1_PH "AWARENESS TESTED BY AWAKENING 
Buddhism is a wholly contemplative tradition, at odds with the rational tradition of philosophy. However, we may be at a special moment of communication between these traditions.
Buddhism is divided into the small vehicle, that of the solitary practitioner seeking nirvana, and the great vehicle, in which one no longer seeks an end but enlightenment, represented by the ocean. Being in the oceanic dimension is the discovery of Maaiana, which explores the common, the impersonal "there is," the rustle of the scene of opening to being, one of the main texts of which is the Bodhicaryavatara written by Santideva.
Awakening is the totally sober being who contemplates the "there is" or "what is". Contemplation, called teoria in Greek, has the same root as Diana in Sanskrit, which means meditation. In antiquity, the same space was found between the two traditions, which then diverged. The content of the original intuition was preserved on the path of Buddhism, while the West took the path of rationalism.

For most Western philosophers, the ante-predicative experience (before the "I", before the "it", before consciousness) cannot constitute the path of ethics: it is only possible from the separation of self and other. In Buddhism, all ethics consists in always returning to the open ante-predicative space. The world of consciousness is that of the subject-object duality, where the mode of apprehension is that of something facing me, that of the transcendence of the object: there is something outside of me that allows me to grasp myself as an interiority. It is a rupture that allows one to situate oneself, to position oneself in the world, to divide it. The world of consciousness is the world of identification. To think that experience or mind can be summed up as conscious experience is Cartesian madness. However, there is a form of alertness that is not consciousness. Awakening is not achieved, we are born awake, it is a form of alertness that is not a path. It is an original situation that is already there.

When knowledge comes from the experience of "there is," it is enriching and deepening. But when the search for information is primary, there is a tearing apart of the state of wakefulness. There is something forgotten in the way of being conscious in the world that creates a sense of discomfort. Consciousness is a useful approach, but not the only one. The mind is more expansive in the dimension of shared presence.

Meditation is the practice of "there is" that shakes us to our metaphysical foundations."
(Alexis Lavis in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 06/08/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/la-conscience-a-lepreuve-de-leveil

E1.7.D.1.3.a_REF1.1_PH "THE FUTURE BELONGS TO THOSE WHO AWAKE EARLY
Being Buddha in Sanskrit is not a quality or an adjective; the one who is Buddha is nothing but awakening, nothing but that. It is a radical break, the most radical experience of non-dualism. There is neither an in-itself nor an out-of-itself. Awakening is also called the non-artificial, the non-manufactured.
To be "aware" already establishes a dualism; in Buddhism this state of mind is broken, and from there one can create ethics and politics.

The question of whether nirvana (the cessation of all reactivity) is enlightenment divides the two streams of Buddhism: the Lesser Vehicle, or Ancient Buddhism, and the Greater Vehicle, or Mahayana.
In the Lesser Vehicle, nirvana is a cessation: one progresses to put an end to a way of being. What does one awaken to in ancient Buddhism? There are two possibilities:
1. the non-self 2. the concatenation of reality - there is no identity, everything is related.
In the Great Vehicle, enlightenment is the space from which a new adventure begins. The two vehicles, which are two interpretations of the Buddha's words, have probably coexisted since the beginning of Buddhism, although the first testimonies of the Great Vehicle appear only in the 1st century BC. 
In the Lesser Vehicle, enlightenment is a state of being, whereas in the Greater Vehicle it is a reality that one discovers to a greater or lesser degree. The Greater Vehicle practitioner develops all aspects of enlightenment over a long period of time. There is no salvation in Buddhism; it is a non-theistic practice."
(Alexis Lavis in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 03/14/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/bouddhisme-44-lavenir-appartient-a-ceux-qui-seveillent-tot

E1.7.D.1.3.a_REF1.2_PO “PROVERBS OF THE SOUL
1. The sun sings.
The heart answers.
The soul listens.
2. The heart sings.
The sun answers.
The soul listens.
3. Although some say the word "soul" is obsolete, there
is no substitute.
4. The soul is the foundation of nothingness upon
which the person is built.
5. There is no dualism of body and soul. They are two
views of one person.
6. In time the person dies, body and soul, except as a
bell reverberates long after it is struck.
7. Outside of time, however, the soul is that part of a
person that is eternal and unchanging, the same in
every being that ever was and ever will be.
8. We experience the soul in the same way that we
experience our thoughts and feelings. But unlike these
other experiences, our experience of the soul is our
window onto eternity, through which we see the
essential truth, beauty, goodness, and oneness of all
things.
9. Nothingness is the soul of Being.
10. That is why when we look for the soul we cannot
find it. But when we eliminate all else, we find
ourselves immersed in its sea.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

“SOME PEOPLE ARE A SHIP,
AND SOME A HEARTH
Some people are a ship, and some a hearth;
Some are wind, and some are summer sun;
Some are many, some are only one;
Some seek the whole while others seek the part.
But those who travel also stay at home,
While those at home are also much abroad,
For all of life must be an open road
That leads us to the place from whence we roam.
There is no difference what we do or are
That makes a difference to the naked soul
That stands before the mirror of the whole
While sailing, sailing bright beyond the bar.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

“THE OCEAN THAT WE
LIVE ON HAS NO SHORE
The ocean that we live on has no shore.
However far we sail, we reach no end.
Infinity is simply what we see,
Restoring the first meaning of "to be,"
There being only ocean, nothing more.
Yet that is what we cannot comprehend.
None long can bear that god-like ecstasy,
Imagining oneself at Being's core,
Nor long remove the multiplicity
Each mind within itself must apprehend.”
(Nicholas Gordon, Philosophical poems, 2002)

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF2_PH "WHAT IS TRANSLATED BY 'REINCARNATION' IS RIGHTLY TRANSLATED BY 'RENAISSANCE', and one may wonder to what extent reincarnation is not something of a myth; a metaphor for the continuous rebirth of the illusion that defines life before awakening (cf. The crisis analysed by the school of Paulo Alto: the more one looks for a solution in illusion, the deeper one sinks).
The egological perspective (the "I" as the basis of experience) is suspended in awakening. How can Buddhism be charitable in this case? How can I help alleviate the suffering of others when the 'I' is suspended? In Mahayana, enlightenment gives us access to the common, to the community of being of the real. We are all Buddhas, and those who have attained enlightenment can help others to 'see' the real in turn, to see the opening of the awakened dimension. The challenge is not one of capacity to be developed, it is one of relationship. In the letting go that allowed the Buddha to finally achieve enlightenment after 6 years of stubborn willpower, we sometimes see "renunciation". A more accurate translation would be the Old French "shame", the withdrawal, the humility that allows enlightenment to occur (renunciation etymologically in Sanskrit is to go back on one's word).
Awakening is the beginning of a journey to deepen existence without wanting to reach a goal. Awakening determines a regime of true humanity".
(Alexis Lavis in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 03/14/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/bouddhisme-44-lavenir-appartient-a-ceux-qui-seveillent-tot

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF2.1_PH A SECULAR READING TREATS REBIRTH AS A METAPHOR FOR A REPETITIVE EXISTENCE IN WHICH WE REMAIN LOCKED INTO CYCLES OF REACTIVE BEHAVIOR. In this case, the goal of the practice is to stop thinking, speaking, and acting reactively.
“Gotama came to understand how the law of karma plays out in the lives of sentient beings. But even this insight is not equivalent to the experience of awakening; it, too, according to the discourses, is a spiritual power available to those who have mastered the fourth  absorption.
On Fear and Dread demonstrated to an Indian audience of believers that Gotama gained direct insight into rebirth and karma, thereby confirming the twin pillars of Indian cosmology, before he achieved his own distinctive awakening. As Johannes Bronkhorst points out, these insights “have no obvious and intrinsic connection with liberation.” […] 
The real problem with rebirth is not suffering periodic hellish torments or other forms of acute pain in different realms of existence but undergoing the same cycle of birth and death again and again for eternity (unless one does something about it). Rebirth is a metaphor for hell. If we remove the metaphysical carapace and strip the concept down to its psychological-existential core, we arrive at repetition. This is explicit in the Pali term punabbhava, usually translated as “rebirth,” which literally means “again-becoming.” A modern translation might be “repetitive existence,” which highlights how a life conditioned by reactivity is one that goes round and round in circles. You may expend a great deal of energy and time on many different things, but at the end of the day, you find yourself back in the same state of bored rest-lessness whence you set out. You realize, with a sinking feeling, that existentially you have achieved nothing. It is as though you have been running on the spot, like a hamster on its wheel, a dog chasing its tail, without getting anywhere.
If we take the idea of rebirth literally, then the only way out of its eternal cycle is to overcome the forces of reactivity that drive the process of samsara, so that after death we will not be born again. From this orthodox perspective, the goal of our practice is the attainment of a final, transcendent nirvana. A secular reading, however, treats rebirth as a metaphor for a repetitive existence in which we remain locked into cycles of reactive behavior. In this case, the goal of the practice is to stop thinking, speaking, and acting reactively, thereby liberating ourselves to respond to life unconditioned by such impulses. Instead of lying beyond the transient, suffering world, nirvana is revealed to lie in the very heart of our own sentient experience here and now.”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF2.2_PH GOTAMA HAD TO SHAPE SOMETHING THAT WAS PRIVATE, INTUITIVE, AND INCHOATE INTO A FORM THAT WAS ACCESSIBLE TO OTHERS, CAREFULLY REASONED AND PRAGMATICALLY STRUCTURED.
“On the basis of what we know about the awakening from The Noble Quest, Gotama’s primary challenge as a teacher would have been to translate his vision of the dharma as a twofold ground into the practice of the dharma as a way of life. He had to convert an insight about conditioned arising and nirvana into an ethical, contemplative, and philosophical discipline. He had to shape something that was private, intuitive, and inchoate into a form that was accessible to others, carefully reasoned and pragmatically structured. My hypothesis is that at some point he came to conceive of the twofold ground as a fourfold task.
In classical terminology, the fourfold task is this:

Suffering (dukkha) is to be comprehended (pariñña).
The arising (samudaya) is to be let go of (pahana).
The ceasing (nirodha) is to be beheld (sacchikata).
The path (magga) is to be cultivated (bhavana).

In more colloquial language, the task can be summarized as a set of
injunctions:
Embrace life.
Let go of what arises.
See its ceasing.
Act!”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.7.D.1.3.b_REF3_MU RAMONES, I WANNA BE SEDATED, THE OPPOSITE OF AWAKENING!

Watch the music video on YouTube: https://youtu.be/bm51ihfi1p4

E1.7.D.1.4. HINDUHISM

E1.7.D.1.4.a. HINDUISM VS. BUDDHISM.
In what way is the concept of the self different in Hinduism and in Buddhism?
In Hinduism, the concept of the self, also known as the "Atman," refers to an individual's permanent, unchanging essence or soul that exists beyond the physical body and mind. The Atman is seen as part of the larger universal consciousness or "Brahman" and is eternal and unchanging.
In Buddhism, the concept of the self, or "Anatta," is quite different. According to Buddhist teachings, there is no permanent, unchanging self or soul. Instead, the self is seen as a collection of constantly changing physical and mental components, such as thoughts, feelings, and sensations, that arise and cease in response to conditions.
In other words, Buddhism teaches that the self is an impermanent, conditioned phenomenon that is constantly changing and lacks a fixed essence. This view is closely tied to the Buddhist doctrine of dependent origination, which holds that all things arise in dependence upon causes and conditions, and there is no permanent, independent self that exists apart from these causes and conditions.
Overall, while Hinduism and Buddhism share some common philosophical roots, their views on the self differ significantly. Hinduism posits the existence of a permanent, unchanging self or soul, while Buddhism teaches that the self is an impermanent, conditioned phenomenon that lacks a fixed essence.

E1.7.D.1.4.b. FOR DAVID LYNCH, HINDU TRANSCENDENTAL MEDITATION WAS THE GATEWAY TO AN EXPERIENCE OF HEIGHTENED IDENTITY, A SENSE OF WONDER, AND COMMUNION WITH THE WORLD. This kind of testimony is very positive to share, to show that it is possible, even if it runs the risk of making people believe that there is only one way to achieve it. There are many valid spiritual practices, religious or otherwise. Any practice aimed at excellence brings the same result. The artist, the athlete, the entrepreneur, the cook, the hiker can all achieve it equally well. But it is always through real work, which is a joy but also an effort, a long-term investment with its failures, its false starts, its discouragements and its wonders.

Watch David Lynch testemony on Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/reel/DFEnYQwTZQj/?igsh=MWR1dm5peW5jazdtdQ%3D%3D

E1.7.B.3.5.wE1.7.D.1.4.

E1.7.C.3.4.wE1.7.D.1.4.

E1.7.D.2 PLOTINUS.  In the West, presocratic philosophies were soon forgotten after their inception, with Plato's metaphysics and Christianity prevailing. This was despite the influence of Plotinism in the 3rd century, whose insights were not retained by the Church.
"Plotinus is the one who makes it possible to think of the beauty of unicity - accessible within us without need for a mirror. He enables us to conceive of immanence through a breach in Platonism. He is the father of the unspeakable, of the appreciation of the infinitely simple, which cannot be explained, but which we must embace. He is the thinker of self-forgetfulness - of that which is relinquished in favor of that which is experienced - in favor of ecstasy."

E1.7.D.2.1. PLOTINUS' CONCEPT OF UNITY REPRESENTS THE CREATIVE OUTPOURING OF BEING, TRANSCENDING CONSCIOUSNESS AND CONCEPTS. His interpretation of God is as love, with creation being free and perpetual, thus continuously rejuvenating the world. This experience is mystical, inviting the soul to partake in divine life. It facilitates the transfiguration of the individual into the universal origin from which they emerge.

E1.7.D.2.1.a. "THIS EXPERIENCE IS NOTABLY MYSTICAL RATHER THAN PHILOSOPHICAL. IT INVITES US TO PARTICIPATE, AS CREATED BEINGS, IN THE LIFE THAT RESIDES IN GOD. IT'S A TRANSFIGURATION OF THE INDIVIDUAL INTO THE UNIVERSAL, FROM WHICH HE IS A SINGULAR EMANATION.
Plotinus' god is characterized by love, superabundant grace, and overflowing fullness. This god generates space and time for the multiple existences that emanate from the One, akin to the rays of fire, the trickle of a spring, or the branches of the tree of life. Existence has no reason—it's a gratuitous gift. "The rose is without why." It's a perpetual creation of a world that would instantly collapse into nothingness if not for the divine gift that eternally revitalizes it. This is an unconscious generation, a generative fruiting that gives birth to all that exists, while always remaining in the infinite enjoyment of its fulfillment and in the infinite activity of its power."

E1.7.D.2.1.a_REF1_PH PLOTINUS DESCRIBED A DIVINE PRINCIPLE, THE ONE, WHICH DIFFERED FROM TRADITIONAL CONCEPTS OF GOD. 
Unlike the personal God, Plotinus' One is an eternal source of existence, indifferent to individuals and unaware of itself. This divine entity is an outpouring of being, creating all that exists without asking love in return. Knowledge of the One transcends philosophical rationality and requires intuitive and mystical understanding, involving the participation of the soul in divine life.
"Neither the God of the philosophers and scholars: the First Principle, the One from which, according to Plotinus, everything emanates, is a harmonious form, a perfect and self-sufficient substance, imprisoned by its own divinity and forever separated from the world, like Aristotle's God; nor the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob: The God of Plotinus, the eternal source of the gift of being (of being itself and not of "beings", that is, of things as they are), is a principle but not a person: he does not call the chosen one, he does not make an alliance with the one he chooses, whom he calls by name, he and all his descendants, he does not ask for love and does not want to be loved in return, he, exclusively, out of jealousy. Indifferent to all that is merely individual, participating only in the life of the Whole without ever becoming particularised in a material body, a singular soul, a determined idea, the One of Plotinus is the effusion that gives being, eternally fruitful and unconscious of itself, "beyond being" as well as beyond consciousness and concept. Unlike the divine for the Greeks, who could not love, who suffered no lack in himself and was self-sufficient and self-contained, Plotinus' God is love, not in the sense that he demands love, like the God of the Old Testament, from the creature he calls by name, but in the sense that he is superabundant grace, overflowing fullness, thus giving rise and time to the multiple existence that emanates from the One, like the rays of fire, the trickling of the spring or the branches of the trunk of the tree of life. The gratuitousness of the gift - we cannot account for existence, and "the rose is without a reason"; the perpetual creation of a world - that of ideas or intelligible forms, as well as that of sensitive or material forms - that would immediately fall back into nothingness if the divine gift did not eternally come to revive it; unconscious generation, generative fruition that gives birth to all that is, while always remaining in the infinite enjoyment of its fulfilment, in the infinite activity of its power.
Such a language necessarily takes us beyond the limits of philosophy as rational knowledge - reason being the mind left to itself, relying only on its own principles - as Plato, then Aristotle, but also the Epicurean and Stoic systems that followed them, had defined it. Plotinus' meditation, which revolves around the One, like the field of vision that expands from the blind spot, cannot demonstrate by concept the principle on which it is based. All knowledge is, in fact, knowledge of a specific object, and therefore requires that the subject be distinguished from the object, introducing a distance, a withdrawal, between the two concepts, without which the act of designating, and then defining, would be impossible. Reason can only recognise what it places before it (this is the literal meaning of the word "object") in order to impose its own categories on it and thus constitute it as an object of knowledge. The mind cannot act in this way towards the One, of which it is in a sense the creature and emanation, since it is in the One like an effusion of primary substance, traversed from side to side by the generative flow that brings it into existence. Rational knowledge, which assumes the distance necessary for objectification, must therefore be opposed here to intuitive knowledge, that is to say, immediate and non-demonstrative, the intimate and even very intimate experience of a divine and nourishing, almost maternal effusion that keeps us in the warmth of existence, and this without reason or purpose, through the perpetual and unreasonable flow of a grace without which nothing would be. Such an experience must be said to be mystical (the word comes from the initiation into the ancient mysteries to which Plotinus himself often refers), but by no means philosophical, in the sense that it is an invitation to a kind of participation of the created soul in the uncreated life that is in God, a transfiguration of the individual in the universal of which it is a part."
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D.2.1.b. THE CONCEPT OF AN ETERNALLY CREATIVE DIVINE POWER IS FUNDAMENTALLY AN ACT OF ABSOLUTE GIVING, ENTIRELY FREE AND SELF-SUFFICIENT, AND CANNOT ACCOMMODATE THE PRECEDENCE OF A PLAN.
"The One does not act in a way that resembles work. Rather, it is more about an effusion of enjoyment, a surplus of plenitude. It does not consciously imitate a model that it observes and follows. Instead, the activity of divine power is unconsciously exerted, manifesting as an overflow emanating from this eternal source, similar to how love radiates from the soul and body of a giver.
Creation is an absolute act of giving, entirely free and self-sufficient, and cannot accommodate the precedence of a plan. This concept of an eternally creative divine power, alien to the Greek mindset which perceives divinity only in terms of order mastery and harmony correctness, likely originates from the East, which clearly fascinated the minds of late antiquity."

E1.7.D.2.1.b_REF1_PH IN CONTRAST TO PLATO AND THE BIBLICAL GENESIS, IN WHICH CREATION IS A CONSCIOUS AND FINITE ACT, PLOTINUS BELIEVES THAT THE ONE CONTINUALLY CREATES THROUGH AN OUTBURST OF FULLNESS, WITHOUT PRECEDENT OR FINITE MODEL. 
"The going beyond the One into the multiple, which places the activity of power above the impotent perfection of the pure act, leads one to think of the life of God in terms of a perpetual creation. This is so new that it needs to be emphasised. The idea of creation is in fact foreign to Greek thought. [We have seen that Plotinus is looking for a principle that is "beyond being" and therefore truly creative, since it is posited as the principle of being itself: it is the activity of the infinite power that gives rise to existence. And although Plotinus often refers to the Timaeus, the creative activity of the One is in no way comparable to the work of the divine Demiurge, who, Plato tells us, has his eyes fixed on an intelligible model and, as a simple craftsman, is in a sense only an inferior deity. For Plotinus, nothing of the sort: the One is absolutely first, so it cannot imitate a model that would be prior to it. The One does not work, if one can put it that way (it is less a question of work than of the outpouring of a joy, of the overflowing of a fullness), consciously, by imitating a model that it has before its eyes and that it makes the rule; the activity of the divine power is, on the contrary, unconscious, it is the effect of the overflow that radiates from this eternal source, just as love radiates from the soul and body of the person who gives of himself. Creation is an act of giving that is absolute, totally free and self-sufficient: it does not allow for the existence of a plan. [...]
This idea of an eternally creative divine power, alien to the Greek mind, which recognises the divine only as the master of order and the rightness of harmony, undoubtedly came from the East and exercised an obvious fascination on the minds of late antiquity. Was Plotinus not himself born in Egypt, and did he not receive his philosophical training from Ammonios Saccas in the city of Alexandria, where all the influences of the East and the West came together? [As for the idea of divine creation, according to which being proceeds from a principle "beyond" it, if it is indeed a fundamental intuition in Plotinus' thought, it is in a completely different sense from that which inspires the biblical account. The God of Genesis created the world in six days. Then he rested, not in the sense of doing nothing, but in the sense of no longer creating: he only watched over the free development of his creation, intervening only to punish it when it strayed from its Creator, or even to destroy it when the evil was incurable, but not without giving it the possibility of rebirth. He is like a conscious artist who sees to it that his work, once abandoned to the world, is neither betrayed nor disfigured. In Plotinus, nothing of the sort: in the living hearth of the One, creation is not the first act of a long story, that of fall and redemption, but the continuing act of divine power. Plotinian creation is continuous, and without this inexhaustible outpouring of the One, all that has been given existence, incapable of maintaining itself in being by its own forces, would immediately return to non-being, like a dream that suddenly dissolves. In order for the world to be and to continue to be, it must be recreated every moment, instituted into being by the infinite power emanating from the One. Creation, according to Plotinus, is a perpetual genesis, without beginning or end. If Plotinus is not entirely alien to the wisdom of the East, if it is not impossible to find in him traces of the Christianity that was then beginning to spread in the ancient world, if the transmission of Jewish wisdom in the Latin world could not have been entirely indifferent to the Roman philosopher, the fact remains that the Enneads are first and foremost a reading and interpretation of Platonic philosophy, which is in fact a comprehensive, surpassing and uncompromising criticism of Aristotelian philosophy, which is incapable of rising to the true knowledge of the activity that is in the life of the divine."
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D.2.2. WE CANNOT IGNORE OUR MATERIAL OR PSYCHOLOGICAL CONDITIONING, BUT NEITHER CAN WE REDUCE OUR LIVES TO ANALYZABLE, MATHEMATICAL AND QUANTIFIABLE ASPECTS.
The unspeakable disrupts the familiar, confronting us with a mystery that we cannot incorporate into our daily lives. Consequently, our inner life will always embody conflict: it is neither pure ecstasy, pure reason, nor pure animality.

E1.7.D.2.2.a. "IGNORING OUR MATERIAL OR PSYCHOLOGICAL CONDITIONING LEADS TO SELF-MYSTIFICATION. There's a subtle yet equally tragic mystification in believing human life can be simplified to its analyzable, quantifiable, or expressible aspects.
This puts humans in a nearly untenable position. The unspeakable disrupts our comfortable, everyday life. We cannot fully immerse ourselves in it or find satisfaction in it. However, confronting this mystery is not a permanent solution. We'll soon have to revert to the comforting reality of everyday life. Our internal lives will never be completely harmonized; they will never be solely ecstasy, reason, or animality."

E1.7.D.2.2.a_REF1_PH PLOTINUS' MYSTICISM IS NOT AN ESCAPE FROM REALITY, BUT A RECOGNITION OF ALL DIMENSIONS OF HUMAN EXPERIENCE, INCLUDING ITS MYSTERIOUS AND TRANSCENDENT ASPECTS. "Seventeen centuries now separate us from Plotinus. And modern history is accelerating, taking us inexorably away from the sage who died alone in a villa in Campania. A great gulf has opened up between us and him. And yet, when we read certain pages of the Enneads, something awakens in us, an echo resonates deep within us. Bergson was right when he spoke of the call of the mystics: "They ask for nothing and yet they receive. They do not need to exhort, they only need to exist, their existence is a call". 
But modern man is wary of Plotinus' call. Seductive as a siren's song, is it not deceptive and dangerous? Modern man is afraid of being deceived. Whether he is a Marxist, a positivist, a Nietzschean or a Christian, he rejects the mirage of the "pure spiritual". He has discovered the power of matter, the power of that whole inferior world which Plotinus considered weak, powerless, close to nothingness. [...]

That Plotinus refuses, if he wishes, to identify himself with the "composite", with the "human animal". Modern man knows that Plotinus draws from this very composite the energy that sustains his spiritual activity. He discovered the power of social, psychological, biological and material infrastructures. Marxism and psychoanalysis have taught him the mechanism of mystification: the man who believes that he is detached from the human condition is merely the plaything of inferior motivations and wants to escape the demands of work and action. 
There is something healthy in this criticism of the "purely spiritual". For too long the disguises used to protect against class prejudice or psychological deficiencies have been mistaken for authentic values. But I think I have shown in this book that, despite certain formulations of Plotinus, his mysticism as he experienced it does not seem to be a "flight reaction". He is as intensely present to others as he is to the Spirit. [...]
No doubt we are deluding ourselves if we ignore our material, psychological or spiritual conditioning. But there is an equally tragic, if more subtle, delusion in thinking that human life can be reduced to its analysable, mathematical, quantifiable or expressible aspects. [...]
Man is thus in an almost untenable position. The unspeakable breaks through the familiar and comfortable fabric of everyday life. Man cannot, therefore, shut himself up in it, live in it completely, be satisfied with it. But if he dares to face the mystery, he will not be able to maintain this attitude: he will have to return quickly to the reassuring evidence of everyday life. The inner life of man will never be completely unified; it will never be pure ecstasy, pure reason, or pure animality. [...]

Modern man is even more divided within himself than the man of Plotinus. But he can still hear the call of Plotinus. It will not be a matter of slavishly repeating, in the middle of the 20th century, the spiritual journey described in the Enneads. That would be impossible and illusory. But it will be to embrace, with the same courage as Plotinus, all the dimensions of human experience and all that is mysterious, inexpressible and transcendent".
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.b. "WE ARE UNCONSCIOUS OF THAT HIGHER LEVEL OF OURSELVES, WHICH IS OUR EGO IN DIVINE THOUGHT. Or better yet, this is the divine thought of our ego, even though it's a part - the higher part - of our soul."

E1.7.D.2.2.b_REF1_PH "HERE WE FIND PLOTINUS' CENTRAL INTUITION: THE HUMAN SELF IS NOT IRREMEDIABLY SEPARATE FROM THE ETERNAL MODEL OF THE SELF AS IT EXISTS IN DIVINE THOUGHT. This true self, this self in God, is within us. In certain special experiences, which raise the level of our inner tension, we identify with Him, we become this Eternal Self; we are moved by His ineffable beauty and, identifying with Him, we identify with the divine thought itself in which He is contained. 
These special experiences therefore reveal to us that we do not cease, that we have never ceased to be in contact with our true Self. We are always in God: 

"And if we must have the audacity to say with greater clarity what seems right to me, contrary to the opinion of others, then our soul has not sunk completely into the sensible, but there is something of it that always remains in the spiritual world (IV 8, 8, 1.)".

If this is the case, then everything is in us and we are in everything. Our self extends from God to matter because we are up there at the same time as we are down here. 
As Plotinus says, using a Homeric expression, "our head remains fixed above the heavens". But immediately a doubt arises:

"If we have such great things within us, why are we not aware of them, why do we mostly remain without exercising these higher activities? Why do some people never exercise them? (V 1, 12.1.)"

Plotinus answers immediately: 

"It is not that everything in the soul is conscious, but that it reaches 'us' by reaching consciousness. If an activity of the soul is carried out without communicating anything to the consciousness, this activity does not reach the whole soul. It follows that "we" do not know anything about this activity, because "we" are connected with the consciousness, and "we" are not a part of the soul, but the whole soul (V 1, 12, 5.)".

We are therefore unaware of this higher level of ourselves which is our self in divine thought, or rather which is the divine thought of our self, even though it is a part - the higher part - of our soul".
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.b_REF1.1_PH PLOTIN DESCRIBES CONSCIOUSNESS AS AN INTERMEDIARY BETWEEN TWO SHADOW ZONES: THE UNCONSCIOUS LIFE IN GOD AND THE UNCONSCIOUS LIFE OF THE BODY. He argues that in order to truly be who we are, we must become aware of these higher and lower levels of our existence.
"Can we really say that we are things of which we are unconscious? And how can we account for this unconsciousness? [Consciousness is a point of view, a center of perspective. For us, our self coincides with this point, from which a perspective on the world or on our soul opens up: in other words, for a psychic activity to be ours, it must be conscious. Consciousness - and our self - is therefore located, like an intermediate environment or center, between two shadows that unfold above and below it: the silent and unconscious life of our self in God, the silent and unconscious life of the body. We can discover the existence of these upper and lower levels through reason. But we will not truly be what we are until we become aware of them. If we could become aware of the life of the spirit, perceive the pulsations of this eternal life that is within us, just as we can perceive the pulsations of our heart of flesh by paying attention, then the life of the spirit would enter the field of our consciousness, it would truly become ourselves, it would truly be our life. [...]

"If there is to be an awareness of the transcendent things so present [at the summit of the soul], then the consciousness must turn inward and direct its attention to the transcendent. It is like a man waiting for a voice he wants to hear: he would dismiss all other voices, he would strain his ear toward the sound he prefers above all others to find out if it is approaching; in the same way, we must exclude the perceptible sounds unless it is necessary to keep the soul's power of consciousness pure and ready to hear the sounds that come from above. (V 1, 12,12.)" [...]"
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.c. “PLOTINUS DOES NOT ADVOCATE FOR THE ABOLITION OF INDIVIDUALITY IN AWAKENING. Rather, the Plotinian experience reveals that our personal identity presupposes a profound absolute. This absolute serves as both the source and the manifestation of our identity.
This identity, the unity of the observer and the observed, is akin to a self-realization and self-awareness. Yet, it should be cautious not to stray from its essence due to an excessive desire to be self-aware. (V 8, 11, 23.)”
“The Plotinian inner experience uncovers distinct levels of our spiritual life. However, these levels do not negate each other. Instead, their totality and interplay constitute the inner life.”

E1.7.D.2.2.c._REF1_PH WE ARE, IRREMEDIABLY, CONSCIOUS AND SPLIT BEINGS "But these states [of communion with the One] cannot last: we are, irremediably, conscious and double beings. We want to seize these moments of unity, to fix them, to preserve them, but they escape us at the very moment when we think we are holding on to them: we fall back from presence to memory. 
Therefore, we can only ascend to the spiritual life through a kind of continual to and fro between the discontinuous levels of our inner tension. Turning our attention inward, we must prepare ourselves to experience the unity of the Spirit, then fall back to the level of consciousness to recognize that it is "we" who are "out there," and lose consciousness again to find our true self in God. More precisely, at the moment of ecstasy, we will have to resign ourselves to retaining only a confused awareness of ourselves: 

"This identity [of the one who sees and what he sees] is, in a sense, an understanding and awareness of the self, which must be careful not to stray from itself through an excessive desire for self-knowledge. (V 8, 11, 23.)" [Plotinus' inner experience thus reveals to us the discontinuous levels of our spiritual life. Dispersed in the worries and concerns of everyday life, we can first concentrate inwardly, directing our attention to things above, regaining awareness of ourselves. We will then discover that we can sometimes rise to a more perfect inner unity in which we reach our true living and real self in divine thought. Having reached this level, we may touch a state of indescribable oneness in which we mysteriously coincide with the absolute simplicity from which all life, all thought, and all consciousness emanates. 
But these levels do not cancel each other out: it is their totality, their interaction, that constitutes the inner life. Plotinus does not invite us to abolish personality in nirvana. On the contrary, Plotinus' experience shows us that our personal identity presupposes an inexpressible Absolute, of which it is both an emanation and an expression."
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.d. LIVING IN A STATE OF DUPLICATION, CALCULATION, AND PLANNING, HUMAN CONSCIOUSNESS BELIEVES THAT DISCOVERY ONLY FOLLOWS EFFORT.
“Plotinus, as Goethe said, dared to "believe in simplicity". He viewed life as a simple and immediate formative activity, beyond our analyses, a totality present all at once, inwardly focused, a Form shaping itself, knowledge that effortlessly achieves perfection.
This Plotinian approach exposes a critique of reflection and human reason. Living in a state of duplication, calculation, and project planning, human consciousness believes that discovery only follows search, that construction only results from assembling parts, that achieving a goal necessitates taking the means. It introduces mediation everywhere. Life, which finds without searching, invents the whole before the parts, and simultaneously serves as the end and means, in a word, is immediate and simple, therefore, eludes reflection. To reach it, or our pure self, we must transition from reflection to contemplation.”

E1.7.D.2.2.d_REF1_PH "PRESENCE [...] What then is the relationship between the world of forms and the sensible world? If the former can be seen through the latter, if the vision of the mind can extend the vision of the eye, it is because there is continuity between the two worlds, because they are the same thing, but on two different levels. Plotinus strongly emphasizes this continuity: "How could this world exist if it were separate from the spiritual world?"
Plotinus strongly opposes the anthropomorphism of Plato's Timaeus, which is also taken up by the Gnostic sects. For him, the material world is not the work of a Creator who made it by reasoning and reflection:

"If we admit that our universe takes its being and its being as it is from another, are we then to believe that its Creator first reflected upon the earth in himself and thought that it should be in the center, then the water and the water on the earth, and all the other things in their respective order up to the sky, then all the animals and for them such and such forms specific to each, as many as there are now, and for each of them the inner parts and the outer parts, and then, after he had put all these things in order in himself, did he set to work? Such a "reflection" is not possible: where would it have come from, since he had not yet seen the things that would be the object of his reflection? And even if he had received the vision of these things from another, it would not have been possible for him to set to work with his hands and tools, as craftsmen do now. For the hands and feet come long after. The fact remains that all this was in an Other [the spiritual world]; and since, by virtue of the proximity that exists in being between realities, one in relation to the other, there is no intermediary, it is as if a trace and an image of that Other appeared instantly... 
You can give the reason why the earth is in the center of the universe and why it is spherical and why the ecliptic is as it is; but in the divine mind it was not because things had to be that way that it was decided, after deliberation, to make them that way, but because things are as they are, these things are also good. (V 8,7, 1-40.)" [...]

He dared, as Goethe said, to "believe in simplicity". For him, life is a formative activity, simple and immediate, irreducible to all our analyses, a totality that is there all at once, internal to itself, a form that forms itself, an immediate knowledge that effortlessly attains perfection. [In this Plotinian approach we find a critique of human reflection and reason analogous to the critique of reflection and consciousness provoked by the discovery of the levels of the Self. In both cases, the simplicity of life escapes the grasp of reflection. Human consciousness, which lives in duplication, calculation, and planning, believes that one can only find after one has searched, that one can only build by assembling parts, and that one can only achieve an end by taking the means to it. Everywhere it introduces mediation. The life that finds without seeking, that invents the whole before the parts, that is at once end and means, in a word, that is immediate and simple, is therefore ungraspable by reflection. To reach it, as to reach our pure self, we must leave reflection for contemplation."
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.e. REMOVE THE ADDITION OF NON-BEING TO RECOVER THE WHOLE.

“By rejecting determination, you've achieved wholeness. Even before this, you were whole. However, because something extra was added to your wholeness, you became less than whole due to this addition. This addition wasn't an enhancement of your wholeness (after all, what could be added to that which is already whole?), but rather, it was an addition of non-being.”

E1.7.D.2.2.e_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO PLOTINUS, LIFE AT ALL ITS LEVELS IS CONTEMPLATION. NATURE IS THE PRODUCT OF SILENT CONTEMPLATION, AND THE SOUL, THOUGH OFTEN DIVERTED TO ACTION, ALWAYS ASPIRES TO CONTEMPLATION. Spirit is an inner vision in which there is no longer any distinction between external and internal perception. 
"It is that life itself, on all its levels, is contemplation. A violent paradox - but how Plotinian! 
Nature itself, the principle of the life of bodies, is already contemplation: 

"And if someone asked it why it produces, and it consented to pay attention to the one who questioned it and was willing to answer, it would say: 'You should not have asked me, but rather intuitively grasped by remaining silent yourself, as I myself am silent and am not in the habit of speaking. "But what was there to understand?" "That what I produce while remaining silent is what I contemplate, an object of contemplation produced by my nature, and that it is fitting for me, who myself was produced by contemplation, that my nature should be in love with contemplation. And it is that which contemplates in me that produces what I contemplate, just as surveyors draw by contemplating. But I do not draw, I only contemplate, and the lines of the bodies are formed as if they came from me". (III 8, 4, 1.) [...]

Undoubtedly, the soul often abandons contemplation for reasoning, research, and action, but it is ultimately out of love for contemplation: 

"When their power of contemplation weakens, men come to produce action, which is a shadow of contemplation and reason. Because contemplation is no longer enough for them, because of the weakness of their souls, they can no longer sufficiently grasp the object of their contemplation, and therefore they are dissatisfied; then, because they still desire to see this object, they turn to action in order to see with their eyes what they can no longer see with their minds. What is certain is that when they produce, they also want to see, contemplate and perceive this object (III 8.4, 31.)". [...]

"If we were to compare the Spirit [with which the world of forms is identified] to a sphere that lives a multiple and varied life, or to a reality made up only of faces and radiant with all these living faces... by representing it in this way, we would be looking at it from the outside, like one person looking at another. But in fact, having become spirit itself, one must become vision itself. (VI 7, 15, 24.)"

There is no longer any distinction between external perception and internal perception. The level of reflection and perception has been transcended to reach that of intuition and contemplation. One then experiences that life is direct contemplation of oneself. [...]

"He liked to make offerings and would go around the temples on new moons and holidays. One day he asked Plotinus to go with him. Plotinus replied, "It is for the gods to come to me, not for me to go to them. In what sense he spoke these words so haughtily, we could not understand, and we did not dare to ask him. (V. P. 10, 33.)"

The small group of disciples seems appalled by these words, which show contempt for traditional forms of worship. But how can we not recognize in them Plotinus' sense of the divine presence? To find God, it is not necessary to go to the temples where he is said to dwell. It is not necessary to travel to reach his presence. But one must become a living temple in which the divine presence can manifest itself. [Or, having become able to walk with it, better still, having attained the Whole itself, you will no longer seek anything; or, having renounced it, you will turn away from it towards something else and you will fall back, no longer seeing what is present to you because you are looking towards something else. But if you are in the case of "you will no longer seek," how will you experience it? Should it not be said that you have entered the All and have not remained in any of its parts, and that you no longer say of yourself: "So far it is I (1)". Rejecting determination, you became Everything. And yet, even before that, you were everything, but because something was added to you in addition to everything, you became less than everything by that very addition. For this addition was not an addition belonging to the order of Everything (what would you add to that which is Everything?), but an addition that was an addition of non-being (2). Having become someone, and precisely by adding non-being, you are no longer the Whole, unless you reject non-being. So you expand yourself by rejecting everything that is other than the Whole, and if you reject that, the Whole will be present to you. But if it is present to you when you reject it, and if it does not appear to you when you remain with particular things, it is because the Whole does not need to come to be present: if it is not present, it is because you have distanced yourself. And if you have distanced yourself, it is not that you have distanced yourself from it - for it is always present - but that, while it is present, you have turned in the opposite direction".

Life is a presence that always warns us. It is pre-existence; it is always already there. How could this be better expressed than by repeating Pascal's famous thought: "You would not be looking for me if you had not already found me." 

Notes:
(1) Literally, "I am of such and such a determined magnitude,. 
(2) Spinoza's axiom (Letter to J. Jelles, in Spinoza, Œuvres complètes, Paris, Gallimard, Bibliothèque de la Pléiade, 1954, p. 1287), "Omnis determinatio negatio est" [To determine everything is to reduce it], is fully valid in the Plotinian universe.
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.f. "THERE ARE ETERNALLY, TWO LEVELS AND TWO MODES OF THE MIND, THAT OF THE LOVING MIND, THAT OF THE THINKING MIND."

E1.7.D.2.2.f_REF1_PH FOR PLOTIN, THE MIND IN ITS BIRTH STATE IS IN DIRECT CONTACT WITH THE GOOD, CHARACTERIZED BY AN INNOCENCE OF LOVE AND PLEASURE. In its final state, the mind becomes determined and systematic, losing direct experience of the good to intellectual knowledge.
"Thus, the mind, in its nascent state, is in direct contact with the good, through a kind of 'touch'. Plotinus says of this mind, which is not yet mind, that it is "the mind filled with love, senseless because it is drunk with nectar, blossoming in enjoyment because of the state of intoxication in which it finds itself. This is probably an allusion to the myth of the birth of love in Plato's Symposium, where the father of love, Poros, sleeps in the garden of Zeus, drunk with nectar. If the nascent spirit is drunk with love and joy, it is because it is in direct contact with the Good, "living close to it," without the mediation of thought and forms. In its final state, on the contrary, the spirit will be fully determined and deployed: it will have produced, in its movement of self-consciousness, the multiplicity and systematic totality of forms. But then the thinking mind will no longer have the experience of loving contact with the Good: it will know it only intellectually, that is, in the duality of object and subject, in the multiplicity of forms. Thus, there are eternally two levels and two modes of mind, that of loving mind and that of thinking mind. [...]

The Platonic experience is constantly expressed in terms of light, radiance, transparency, clarity, and illumination. Should we conclude from this that it ignores the darkness and the nights of the spirit that characterize Christian mysticism? Insofar as the inner emptiness and austerity ("cut off all things") can appear to the soul as a kind of night, because it has the impression of losing the light to which it is accustomed, we must say that there is also a mystical night in Plotinus. But in so far as the night of the Christian mystics corresponds to the exercise of faith, even more, to the suffering of the crucified Christ who feels abandoned by the Father, it is all too obvious that all this is missing from Plotinus. Plotinus' spirituality is essentially luminous and serene. It is in peace and gentleness that the Plotinian soul "cuts off all things" and thus becomes a pure receptive capacity, waiting to be invaded by the presence of the good that is always already present."
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.2.g. A METAMORPHOSIS OF VISION.
”Plotinian virtue embodies a straightforward spiritual stance—it involves a constant focus on the divine and an ongoing acknowledgment of God's presence. This might be described as a metamorphosis of vision. Plotinian virtue pursues the divine presence in and around itself, and through all things. Due to this practice, union with God becomes continuous.”

E1.7.D.2.2.g_REF1_PH FOR PLOTIN, VIRTUE IS BORN FROM THE VISION OF THE BEAUTY OF INTELLIGENCE AND THE JOY OF DIVINE UNION, AND IT PURIFIES THE SOUL TO CONTINUALLY SUPPORT THAT UNION. "Such is the journey of the soul. Lifted up to the One by the free and gracious movement of the One, it could not remain on the summit of itself. It fell back. But having returned to practical life, to reflection, to consciousness, it finds in itself, here below, that trace of God, virtue, which makes it similar to God. Through the practice of virtue, the soul can once again ascend to the spirit, that is, to a purely spiritual life. Having reached this perfection, virtue becomes wisdom, it becomes a stable state from which the soul can once again be disposed to divine union. [...]

Virtue can only be born in the soul when it has glimpsed, even for a moment, the beauty of the intellect, when it has tasted, even for a flash, the joy of divine union. No doubt, because the soul is not sufficiently purified, it cannot endure these states. But the main task of virtue will be to purify the soul so that it can endure divine union continuously. [...]

The Plotinian virtue, then, consists in an extremely simple spiritual attitude [...] it is simply a constant attention to the divine, a constant practice of the presence of God. One can speak, if one wishes, of a metamorphosis of the gaze. In itself and around itself, through all things, the Plotinian virtue wants to see only the divine presence. Thanks to this practice, union with God becomes continuous. [...]"
(Pierre Hadot, Plotinus or the simplicity of vision, 1997)

E1.7.D.2.3. THE INITIATE'S SOUL IS TAKEN OVER BY THE INFINITE AND ABANDONS THOUGHT.
Plotinian initiation seeks to transcend intelligence by intuitively merging with the divine life within. In this fusion, consciousness is nearly lost, reduced to intuitive knowledge.

E1.7.D.2.3.a. “FAR FROM ACKNOWLEDGING ITS OWN POWER TO CONCEIVE, THE INITIATE'S SOUL YIELDS ITSELF TO THE INFINITE THAT SURROUNDS IT, FORSAKING THOUGHT IN ITS TRANSFIGURED INTUITION. Plotinian initiation aspires to transcend intelligence through an intuitive fusion that extinguishes self-consciousness.
This allows the soul, occasionally, to partake in the divine life, for this life also resides within the soul. The soul receives being by emanation from the primary source and can merge with it in a fusion where consciousness is nearly lost. This reduces consciousness to an immediate sense of intuitive knowledge, unable to mediate what it experiences through conceptual linking.
To elevate this experience to the level of consciousness, it must be temporarily left. However, not to abandon it, but to subsequently return in a rhythmic back-and-forth movement that mystically drives Plotinian ecstasy.”

E1.7.D.2.3.a_REF1_PH FOR PLOTINUS, THE SOUL CAN, ON RARE OCCASIONS, PARTICIPATE IN THE DIVINE LIFE THROUGH AN INTUITIVE AND FUSIONAL KNOWLEDGE, WITHOUT IMAGE OR REPRESENTATION, which requires a mystical to and fro between union and self-knowledge.
"In his Master's Life, which he has left us, Porphyry tells us that Plotinus was given to know such perfect bliss on several occasions: Following the path prescribed by Plato in the Symposium, he saw the God who has neither form nor essence, being beyond intelligence and the intelligible. It is this God that I myself have approached and with whom I have united only once, in my sixty-eighth year. Plotinus, for his part, "had the vision of the goal very close. For him, the end and the goal was intimate union with the God who is above all things. While I was with him [Porphyry remained with Plotinus for six years, from 263 to 268], he attained this goal four times, thanks to an ineffable act and not by force" (Life of Plotinus, § 23). Such ecstasy, in which the intellect is lost, goes beyond the limits of philosophy as defined by Plato and Aristotle. Far from recalling the power of conception within it, the soul of the initiate surrenders to the infinite that invades it and to the intuition that transfigures it, renouncing thought. The Socratic aporia aimed to provoke the concept, to shake the mind out of its torpor by adhering to the prevailing opinion or by naively believing in the sensible appearance. The Plotinian initiation, on the other hand, claims to transcend intelligence through a kind of intuitive fusion in which self-consciousness is abolished. Just as the One is beyond being, so the soul's communion with life, whose first principle is the Source, is beyond the "know thyself" that Plato had nevertheless placed at the foundation of all philosophical knowledge. [...]

The self-awareness of the mind, which Plato had made the very foundation of rationality, is annihilated in the soul that rises to the first principle. And while Aristotle defined the life of God as “thought of thought”, Plotinus, on the contrary, thinks of it as generative unconsciousness, a source devoid of thought as of consciousness from which Being eternally springs, that is to say, not created and determined things (in the manner of the god of Genesis), but Being itself, which means that all things that are, whatever their own being, also participate in what we call - rather enigmatically - “existence”. This is why ecstasy, which merges with the power that is in god, must abandon consciousness as well as intelligence, unworthy of the life of the first principle, which is beyond reason. [...]
It is thus possible for the soul to participate, on rare occasions, in the life that is in god, precisely because this life is also in it, which receives Being by emanation from the first source, and to unite with it in a fusion where consciousness is almost lost, reduced then to the immediate feeling of intuitive knowledge and incapable of mediating what it experiences through the sequence of concepts: “This identity [of the one who sees and what he sees] is, in a way, a grasping and a consciousness of the self, which must be careful not to stray from itself through an excessive desire for self-awareness” (V, 8, 11). All consciousness is in fact a doubling – like the two mirrors that the Platonic dialogue reciprocally places in abysses (First Alcibiades) – and therefore also division and separation: I can only conceive of what I place before me, and thus define as the object of my knowledge. Such a division – which is the condition of consciousness and like the critical distance that allows thought to perceive itself, to know itself – cannot take place in the One, in which everything merges into an ultimate principle that is “beyond Being”, that is to say, at the source from which Being itself comes. The fusional knowledge of the first principle is thus without image or representation, without thought or concept. Pour élever cette expérience à la dimension de la conscience, il faut donc en sortir, non toutefois pour s’en détourner, mais pour y revenir ensuite par un mouvement de va-et-vient qui rythme mystiquement l’extase plotinienne : « S’il écarte, bien qu’elle soit belle, cette image de lui-même uni au dieu, et qu’il parvient à être un avec l’esprit, sans plus se dédoubler, il est en même temps Un et Tout, avec ce dieu qui est présent dans le silence. Il est avec lui autant qu’il le peut et qu’il le veut. Mais si ensuite il se retourne pour devenir deux, il reste près du dieu, dans la mesure où il demeure pur, de sorte qu’il peut lui être présent de nouveau, si à nouveau il se retourne vers le dieu. Dans ce retournement, il gagne ceci : au début, il a conscience de lui-même, tant qu’il demeure différent du dieu ; mais lorsqu’il revient en hâte vers l’intérieur, il est dans un état de totalité et, laissant la conscience en arrière de crainte de rester différent du dieu, il est un, dans cet état transcendant (V, 8, 11)."
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D.2.3.a_REF2_PO "EXULTATION IS THE GOING 
Of an inland soul to sea -
Past the Houses -
Past the Headlands -
Into deep Eternity -

Bred as we, among the mountains, 
Can the sailor understand 
The divine intoxication 
Of the first league out from Land ?"
(Emily Dickinson, 1860)

E1.7.D.2.3.b. “WHEN EVERYTHING IS SIMPLE, HOW CAN ONE DEVELOP A DISCOURSE? All it requires is a touch of intelligence. When we are in the act of touching, we neither have the power nor the time to express anything. It's only afterwards that we will form logical arguments about what we have touched. Illuminated, the soul possesses what it was in search of. This is the true goal for the soul: to touch this light and to see that very thing by which it sees. For it must contemplate that by which it has been illuminated, just as the sun is not seen by any other light. So, how is this to be achieved? Remove all things”

E1.7.D.2.3.b_REF1_PH "WHICH IS TO SAY THAT THE GOD OF PLOTINUS IS NOT PURE INTELLIGENCE - LIKE THE GOD OF PLATO OR ARISTOTLE - BUT PURE PRESENCE, and that presence diminishes in intensity when consciousness, separating itself to become autonomous, sets up presence as its re-presentation (which means presence fallen and "bent" under the gaze of reflection). It is natural, then, that unlike the dialectical progression, which arrives gradually, that is to say, by following the articulations of reason, at the knowledge of its foundation, the knowledge of the divine, according to Plotinus, is achieved "suddenly" (exaiphnês), by the miracle of an encounter that cannot be explained by reason, in a kind of mystical marriage of the created with the uncreated: "If the soul has the good fortune to meet him, if he comes to her, even better: if he appears to her present, when she has turned away from present things [...] and suddenly sees him appear within her (for there is nothing between them and they are no longer two, but both are one: in fact, one can no longer distinguish him as long as he is there: the image of this is lovers and the beloved on earth who wish to merge together), at that moment the soul is no longer aware of her body or that she is in this body [...] Having sought him, when she has found him, the soul is no longer aware of her body or of her being in this body [...]....] Having sought it, when it is present, it goes to meet it, and it is He that it looks at instead of itself, and it does not have the leisure to see who it is that it looks at. As for the object of her vision, the seer, at that moment, neither sees it nor distinguishes it, and she does not realise that they are two, but has become in a different way and is no longer herself and to herself, but belongs entirely to what is above, and having become one, belongs to the One, as if she had made her centre coincide with the centre" (VI, 9, 10). The gaze of the seer is strangely confronted with the expansive power that emanates from the One: a gaze that does not see but, on the contrary, is lost in the infinity that absorbs it, a gaze that sees without seeing that it sees: "It is He whom the soul looks at instead of itself, and it does not have the leisure to see who it is". We can get an idea of this expanded view of transcendence by looking at the mosaics of Byzantium in the Justinian era, two centuries after Plotinus' text. [...The theory of vision has always been Plato's paradigm - if not the myth - of all knowledge: Just as the eyes of the body perceive material objects in the luminous environment, so the eyes of the soul perceive and define ideas (for example, geometric forms) in the mental light that bathes the interiority of the spirit, the spiritual cave in which the soul slumbers as long as it, fascinated by sensible objects, does not perceive this clarity that emanates from itself; And just as physical vision becomes aware of itself through the light of the sensible sun, so the mind perceives the Idea in the radiance of an "intelligible sun", the source of all spiritual life and the blind spot from which memory draws the Idea that comes to mind. Plotinus subjected this metaphysics of truth to a paradigm shift: according to the Neoplatonic thinker, it is not vision that is the visible image of the intelligible act of cognition, but touch. Indeed, vision presupposes this distance - we cannot see the end of our nose except through the mediation of a mirror placed in front of us - without which knowledge cannot be achieved, but through which, according to Plotinus, the unity of the first principle is also divided into the duality of subject and object, of seer and seen, of knower and known.

Touch, on the other hand, presupposes the abolition of distance, the establishment of contact between the concepts that speculative activity separates and opposes. All the more so since, in the perfect reciprocity of touch, it is not possible to determine who is object and who is subject, since each of the two is both touching and being touched. There is then no need for light as a mediator, which is necessary for the act of seeing: touch knows immediately, by contact, without having to go through the discursiveness of reason or the representation of the imagination. And if Plotinus still resorts to the metaphor of light, it is by attributing to it a kind of tactile sensitivity, so that the knowledge of the divine is analogous to the touch of light itself: "The soul runs around all truths, and yet here it flees from these truths, in which we have a part, as soon as it wants to speak and discourse, because to speak the discourse must go from term to term. Where everything is simple, how can one unfold a discourse? But all that is needed is a noetic touch [noerôs ephapsasthai: touch by intelligence]: when you touch, at the moment of touching you have neither the power nor the leisure to say anything; only afterwards will you make syllogisms about what you have touched [...]....] Enlightened, the soul possesses what it sought, and this is the true goal for the soul: the touch of this light [ephapsasthai phôtos] and the vision of this light by this light, not by another, the vision of that by which it sees. For that by which it has been illuminated is what it must contemplate, just as the sun is not seen by another light. So how can we achieve this? Cut away everything (aphele panta)" (V, III, 17)."
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D.2.3.c. “WHILE IT'S TRUE THAT THIS STATE OF ETERNITY DOESN'T LAST LONG, SOULS BOUND TO MORTAL BODIES QUICKLY BECOME TAINTED BY FATIGUE. This fatigue is perhaps what best distinguishes the created from the Uncreated. These souls soon turn away from the One, becoming lost in the labyrinth of both the sensible and the intelligible. Consequently, they live in exile from themselves, or in other words, from their principle.”

E1.7.D.2.3.c_REF1_PH "AT THE POINT OF CONTACT WHERE THE I, IN ITS ASCENT, TOUCHES THE INFINITE THAT IS IN GOD, that is, the generative activity, the perpetual giving, whose first principle is the Source, it finds itself deified and as if drawn into a mystical dance, like the choreutes dancing in the circle of the orchestra around the coryphaeius who leads them: "He who has seen knows what I am saying, and how the soul lives another life, going towards the One and participating in it, so disposed now that it knows that it is there, like the choreletes dancing in a circle around the Coryphaeus of authentic life, and knowing that it needs nothing more, but that, on the contrary, it is necessary to divest oneself of everything and remain in Him alone, cutting off all that is superfluous around us, so that we can't wait to leave here and are irritated to be bound to anything else" (VI, 9, 8). The soul, liberated by ecstasy, becomes divine and, as if captured by the One, revolves around Him in a sacred dance: "When it knows that the centre is the origin of the circle, it will revolve around the centre from which it came; it will cling to it; it will gather towards that point towards which all souls should tend, but towards which only the souls of the gods tend eternally. In moving towards Him, souls are gods. For to be a god is to be united with the One" (VI, 9, 8). And if this perfect union or divinisation happens "suddenly" (exaiphnês), it is because it is accomplished outside of all time, outside of space as well as time, in the eternity of the superabundant life that emanates from the One. It is true that this eternity does not last long, the souls attached to the mortal body soon get tired (tiredness is perhaps what best distinguishes the created from the uncreated) (1), they soon turn away from the One and turn towards the many, they get lost in the labyrinth of the sensible as well as the intelligible, and once again live in exile from themselves, that is, from their principle".
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.5.D.1.3.d.iii.wE1.7.D.2.3.c. THE FAILURE OF THOSE WHO CANNOT FORGET THEMSELVES OUT OF PRIDE

E1.7.D.2.4. "THE TRUE PURPOSE OF DISCOURSE ACCORDING TO PLOTINUS IS TO ELEVATE US PARADOXICALLY TO AN AWARENESS OF THE UNSPEAKABLE".

E1.7.D.2.4_REF1_PH "STRANGE PHILOSOPHY THAT LEADS TO THE ABANDONMENT OF PHILOSOPHY! The discourse of Plotinian meditation develops only to lead us to the silence into which the intellect must abandon itself, since here nothing can be said or even conceived. The true purpose of discourse, according to Plotinus, is paradoxically to bring us to the awareness of the unspeakable, and we learn to speak only in order to know what we must keep silent, which is the very source of all authentic speech, but which we can never chain to the order of our reasons: "When you speak his name or think of him, leave everything else; disregard everything; let him alone remain (monon auton). Seek nothing to add; but ask yourselves whether, in the thought you have of him, there is anything left which you have not yet put aside from him. For you may arrive at a principle of which it is no longer possible to say or conceive anything else; situated on the highest plane, he alone is truly free, because he is not a slave to himself; and he alone is truly himself, while each of the other beings is at once himself and something else" (VI, 8, 21, in fine). And Plotinus wrote again a little further on, and along the same lines: “If we see him, we will no longer dare to say that he is by accident, we will not even dare to utter a word: mental stupor (ekplageiê) would follow such audacity. As soon as we rush towards him, we cannot say where he is; he appears everywhere before the eyes of our soul; wherever it turns its gaze, it sees him, unless it abandons the divine to look elsewhere, and no longer thinks of him” (VI, 8, 19, l. 6-12).”
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D.2.5. THE INITIATED SOUL MUST TRANSITION FROM ECSTASY TO ATTAIN SELF-CONSCIOUS KNOWLEDGE. THIS REQUIRES PHILOSOPHICAL REFLECTION AND THE EXERCISE OF REASON. Unlike the Gnostics, Plotinus asserts that we cannot entirely shed our human condition to reach God. Moving towards the One is a challenging endeavor that demands philosophical wisdom and dialectical discipline. The soul ascends to the inconceivable through conceptual work, not mystical rapture. While philosophy may push its own boundaries, it does not abandon reasoning.

E1.7.D.2.5_REF1_PH ACCORDING TO PLOTINUS, THE HUMAN SOUL CANNOT REMAIN IN THE ABSOLUTE OF THE FIRST PRINCIPLE AND MUST TURN TOWARDS A CONSCIOUS KNOWLEDGE OF ITSELF THROUGH PHILOSOPHICAL REFLECTION AND PLATONIC DIALECTICS. Philosophy, even if it tends towards its limits, remains indispensable for the attainment of an intuitive and fusional knowledge of the divine life.
"The soul, having reached the summit of its initiation, cannot establish itself in the absolute of the first principle: it must emerge, not only because of its impotence [...] but also out of necessity, since the very movement of emanation forces the creature to tear itself away from the unconsciousness of ecstasy and to turn towards a self-aware science, the "know thyself" of discursive knowledge, the progressive development of Platonic dialectic. There is, according to Plotinus, a necessity specific to the human condition, which is also a necessity for philosophical reflection and the exercise of reason: "Against the Gnostics, who have the presumption to believe that the regenerated man can strip himself of all traces of this world, join God and become equal to him [...], Plotinus defends himself against the Gnostics, who have the presumption to believe that the regenerated man can strip himself of all traces of this world, join God and become equal to him [...]....] Plotinus maintains that we cannot completely escape our condition, and that the movement towards the One, which is a return to the principle, is always, apart from rare ecstasies, a difficult effort thwarted by the inertia inherent in all creatures. If we try too hard to be an angel, we would become a beast: philosophical wisdom and dialectical discipline are the necessary path that leads to that beyond discourse where the soul is lost in the One, in a non-reflective knowledge that is an amorous fusion and no longer a demonstration by concept. Plotinus, unlike the mystics, never asked us to economise on intelligence: According to him, the soul does not move towards God by renouncing reflection, but on the contrary by a very rigorous dialectical reasoning that leads to the point where discourse must give way to the ineffable, where discursive knowledge must be lost in an intuitive and fusional knowledge of the life that is in God. It is through the work of the concept that we ascend to the inconceivable, and not through a rapture that would be given to us as a grace and would distinguish us from the rest of humanity. Even if philosophy with Plotinus tends towards its own limit, it remains a philosophy and does not give up thinking".
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html


E1.7.D.2.5_REF1.1_PH PLOTINUS SUGGESTS THAT THE ASCENT TO THE ONE REQUIRES RIGOROUS DIALECTICAL REFLECTION, IN WHICH DISCURSIVE KNOWLEDGE GIVES WAY TO INTUITIVE AND FUSIONAL KNOWLEDGE, WITHOUT RENOUNCING INTELLIGENCE.
"But this dazzling richness is also, and necessarily, an increasing fall and decline: what emanates from the One cannot be the One itself, for then it would split and fall into multiplicity itself. The One cannot be confused with what it generates, and it can only generate lesser realities in which the intensity of existence progressively diminishes as they move away from the generating source. Thus, the expansion of divine energy eventually exhausts itself at the boundaries of creation and sinks into the nothingness of pure matter, like a light gradually dying in darkness. All beings that emanate from the One thus suffer from being separated from the principle that brought them into existence, and like Ulysses trying to return to Ithaca, they are tormented by nostalgia for their true home, striving to tear themselves away from the here and turn to the "there" from which they came. The descent, which emanates from the One and generates by emanation the totality of what is (by virtue of the nature of the One, which cannot be divided), thus corresponds to the loving ascent of the exiled souls, who, according to their degree of perfection, strive to rejoin the Father who begot them. Plotinus' universe is thus animated, like a great animal, by a dual movement of expansion and contraction, of diffusion (procession) and convergence (ascension), which gives it movement and life. Therefore, the soul that has reached the summit of its initiation cannot establish its abode in the Absolute of the First Principle: It must emerge from it, not only because of its impotence (fatigue distracts it from the divine, and the heaviness within it opposes the supreme grace that is in God), but also out of necessity, because the very movement of emanation compels the creature to tear itself away from the unconsciousness of ecstasy and turn to a self-aware science, the "know thyself" of discursive knowledge, the progressive development of Platonic dialectic.
