There is, according to Plotinus, a necessity specific to the human condition, which is also a necessity for philosophical reflection and the exercise of reason: against the Gnostics, who have the presumption to believe that the regenerated man can strip himself of all traces of this world, join God and become equal to him (they believe, writes Plotinus, that one escapes the human condition by flying close to God on the wings of dreams": II, 9, 9), Plotinus argues that we cannot completely escape our condition, and that the movement toward the One, which is a return to principle, is always, except in rare ecstasies, a difficult effort thwarted by the inertia inherent in all creatures. If we try too hard to be an angel, we would become a beast: philosophical wisdom and dialectical discipline are the necessary path that leads to that beyond discourse where the soul is lost in the One, in a non-reflective knowledge that is an amorous fusion and no longer a demonstration by concept. Plotinus, unlike the mystics, never asked us to economize on intelligence: in his view, the soul does not move toward God by renouncing reflection, but on the contrary through a very rigorous dialectical reasoning that leads to the point where discourse must give way to the ineffable, where discursive knowledge must be lost in an intuitive and fusional knowledge of the life that is in God. It is through the work of the concept that we rise to the unimaginable, and not through a rapture that would be given to us as a grace and would distinguish us from the rest of humanity. Even if philosophy, according to Plotinus, tends to its own limits, it remains philosophy and does not give up thinking: "To believe that one is already above intelligence is to fall below intelligence" (II, 9, 9, l. 51-52)".
(Jacques Darriulat, Plotinus, Master of Wisdom, II - From the One to the Many, 2013)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Plotin/Sagesse/Sagesse2.html

E1.7.D 3. PASCAL AND LEONARDO DA VINCI. It's not that the Western world will never again experience the "oceanic feeling of existence," but such experiences will be systematically translated into Christian vocabulary. This was the norm up until the Renaissance, during the 15th and early 16th centuries. Leonardo da Vinci's work, which embodied a deep spiritual connection with beings and things, was veiled with a Christian perspective, and Pascal's revelation was expressed in religious terms.

E1.7.D.3.1. BLAISE PASCAL (1623-1662)
"   The year of grace 1654,
        Monday the 23rd of November, St. Clement's day...
        From about half past ten in the evening until about half past twelve, midnight,

        FIRE

        God of Abraham. God of Isaac. God of Jacob 
        not of the philosophers and the wise. Certainty, joy, certainty, feeling, Joy, peace.""

E1.7.D.3.1_REF1_PH CERTAINTY, JOY, CERTAINTY, FEELING, JOY, PEACE "The terms in which a man interprets [the experience of cosmic consciousness] are naturally drawn from the religious and philosophical ideas of his culture, and their differences often conceal its basic identity. As water seeks the course of least resistance, so the emotions clothe themselves in the symbols that lie most readily to hand, and the association is so swift and automatic that the symbol may appear to be the very heart of the experience. Clarity - the disappearance of problems - suggests light, and in moments of such acute clarity there may be the physical sensation of light penetrating everything. To a theist this will naturally seem to be a glimpse of the presence of God, as in the celebrated testimony of Pascal:

        The year of grace 1654,
        Monday the 23rd of November, St. Clement's day...
        From about half past ten in the evening until about half past twelve, midnight,

        FIRE

        God of Abraham. God of Isaac. God of Jacob 
        not of the philosophers and the wise. Certainty, joy, certainty, feeling, Joy, peace."
(Alan Watts, This is it, 1958)

E1.7.D.3.1_REF2_PH "BLAISE PASCAL, TOWARD INFINITY AND BEYOND, PORTRAIT OF A JOYFUL MAN
The order in which Branschevick classified the thoughts does not respect the order of the bundles left by Pascal. The order proposed by Lafuma (the classified papers) gives it a less sinister height. For example, he had reserved a section on the greatness of man. The version that makes the wager a shaky solution to man's despair does not do justice to the complexity of Pascal's approach. There is no simple progression from despair to joy. For example, there are essays on amusement in all the sections. He presents himself as someone who says nothing new, but whose originality lies in the sequence of ideas (see the thought on real tennis).
There is no word joy in the thoughts, but there is laughter. All the texts on vanity are about the dysfunctions that make us laugh. The Vanity Bundle is a collection of funny stories. Vanity = absence of causes; followed by reason of effects = presence of a cause. If vanity could lead us to misery, the cause of the effects reveals to us a greatness: a celebration of man. It is the clash between the misanthrope and the philanthropist in Pascal that will nourish his thought.

In the Provincial Letters, Pascal justifies that nothing is due to vanity but laughter: a breast laughter. The laughter of despair of Nietzsche, Ionesco, Beckett? There is still room for joy in Pascal's thought; a serious Pascal who can be joyful without staying there for long. But the principle of joy remains central to Pascal. In the tradition of St. Augustine, he thinks of man as a being of pleasure, and will be harshly criticized for this by the Jansenists. There will be a prioritization of pleasures: he quotes St. Augustine, "One is always carried away by one's greatest pleasure. The debauched and the chaste in the same way. For Pascal, the greatest joy is the encounter with God. It is a joy that comes from elsewhere and can therefore fail, abandon us, hence the constant anxiety and gravity of this joy.
The text of the Memorial, the result of a revelation, recounts the experience of total joy. He wore this text sewn into his coat until his death. A text interwoven with jubilation and anguish. It gives way to feeling, which for Pascal is a form of knowledge: the certainty that feeling provides is true knowledge, something that reason struggles to provide in the Pensées. This certainty also guarantees him the "greatness of the human soul.

Why then the disproportion to the darkness of man in the Pensées? It is because Pascal wants to shake up the man who does not ask himself religious questions. He sets up a device to provoke a reaction. It is a rhetorical rather than an anthropological enterprise. He goes to the players to speak to them."
(Laurent Thirouin at the microphone of the program Les chemins de la philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 09/27/2018)

E1.7.D.4. MONTAIGNE, SPINOZA AND ROUSSEAU. Montaigne revived many intuitions of the pre-Socratic philosophers in the second half of the 16th century, without necessarily interpreting them religiously. Interestingly, Baruch Spinoza developed an ethics in the 17th century that resembled Asian philosophies, despite no contact with them. Yet both of them struggled to overcome the dissolution of the "self". Montaigne's free identity gave his Essays rhythm, cultivating his presence in the world through his "I". Spinoza's conatus sustained the ego. In the 18th century, Jean-Jacques Rousseau experimented with the "feeling of existence" late in life, while Goethe explored the "primal phenomenon".

E1.7.D.4.1. FOR MONTAIGNE (1533-1592), THERE IS NO COMMUNICATION WITH BEING. INSTEAD, THERE'S A RE-CONQUEST OF THE SELF BY MAINTAINING CLOSENESS TO ONESELF WHILE INTERACTING WITH OTHERS. Montaigne advises us to distance ourselves from harmful emotions by traveling and envisioning life beyond our daily routines. To him, wisdom isn't the pursuit of tranquility as it doesn’t bring peace. Rather, it's about accepting uncertainty and surrendering to situations. Montaigne doesn't strive to create a consistent image of himself. Instead, he relishes his inconsistency and cultivates his identity through relationships with others.

E1.7.D.4.1_REF1_PH "MONTAIGNE RECOGNISES THE IMPORTANCE OF SENSATIONS THAT HURT US. It is a matter of recomposing one's vision, of stepping back, of travelling, of distancing oneself from the familiar by looking at it from afar, by way of the imagination - and without condemning this as an escape from oneself. Montaigne does not maintain a relationship of knowledge to reality: his consoling thought is based on fantasy, on an aestheticization of existence.
More certainty does not bring more peace. Anxiety is caused by the absence of tranquillity, but tranquillity is an unattainable state of mind. To seek rest and tranquillity is to be "ingenious in mistreating oneself". Wisdom is to choose to be fickle. In making decisions, one relies not only on reason, but also on the resources of the body, on emotions, on fantasy, on dreams, on the unconscious. He agrees to be guided by the situation - a surprising form of wisdom in the West. Suffering often comes from trying to artificially construct a coherent image of ourselves. Montaigne delights in his inconsistency. He rejoices in transition, without disqualifying the present in favour of a future that would be better because it would be more complete. For Montaigne, the individual is constructed by exteriority, by fiction and imagination, by relations with others: it is a reconquest of oneself. We have no communication with others: we remain blind to ourselves when we are with others, without suffering from our inner emptiness".
(Sylvia Giocanti in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 05/17/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/linquietude-44-lame-agitee-de-michel-de-montaigne

E1.7.D.4.1_REF1.1_PH "ANXIETY, THE AGITATED SOUL OF MICHEL DE MONTAIGNE Montaigne presents himself as having no nature. He writes essays in order to gain a foothold, not because he has a particularly anxious temperament, but because it is man's fate to live in the flow of becoming, in the "perpetual motion". Man must organise his existence while being exposed to anxiety, to the "thorns of domesticity", because "life is a delicate thing and easily disturbed, [...] the troubles of life are irreparable". What may seem trivial, such as the management of one's property, can cause us as much anxiety as more serious matters (Montaigne lived at the time of the wars of religion) - it is not insignificant that he takes everyday examples. When we look at things closely, they hurt us the most. His philosophy is in opposition to the Stoics, who try to conform to a voluntary model of renunciation by reason of what does not depend on us (according to a prior division of reality). M. recognises the importance of the affect that hurts us. The aim is to recompose one's vision, to step back, to travel, to break with the familiar by means of the detour of distancing through the imagination - without this being condemned as an escape from oneself (again in contrast to the Stoics). The Stoics believe that there are true and false opinions about the problems we must be able to ignore. The sceptic is modern in that he refers to a thought of the subject and does not maintain a relationship of knowledge to reality. His idea of consolation is based on fantasy, on an aestheticization of existence.

If there is any point in talking about good and evil, it is in terms of consequences, when they cause suffering, whereas stoicism never takes respect for sensibility as a criterion. For the sceptic, getting rid of doubt is not an end in itself; certainty is not a better state than uncertainty, since our state is one of permanence and instability. More certainties do not lead to more peace of mind. Anxiety is the absence of rest, and rest is an inaccessible state of mind. To seek rest is to be "ingenious in mistreating oneself". Wisdom is to resolve oneself to indecision. To be able to choose, one relies not only on reason, but also on the resources of the body, emotions, imagination, dreams and the unconscious. One acts in a state of mind that Lao Tzu would have, valuing a form of passivity that does not doubt (because it does not rely on the will that is "buffeted by flies"). One accepts to be led by the situation - a form of wisdom that is surprising in the West. Suffering often comes from trying to artificially construct a coherent image of oneself. M. enjoys his inconsistency. He enjoys the moment without disqualifying the present in favour of the future, which would be better because it would be more complete. For M., the individual is constructed through the exterior, in the fiction and imagination of relationships with others: there is a reclaiming of the self. We have no communication with the self. We remain with ourselves, with others, without suffering from our inner emptiness.

For St Augustine, if man is anxious, it is because he can only rest in God. Where Montaigne is subversive is that he does not believe that the rest to which we aspire is a lie and that it is not found in God, because for that to be the case, the origin would have to be like a destination. Monsieur replaces God with meaningless objects; Pascal will criticise him for this and try to put some fear back into the Pensées. For Monsieur, there is a certain exhilaration in enjoying life to the point of losing it: he revels in the precariousness of our existence. Things have a price because we know we will lose them. The mistake philosophers make is to put death in front of our eyes, when death is integrated into the future; there is no need to think about death, it is enough to think about life".
(Sylvia Giocanti in the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 05/17/2018)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.franceculture.fr/emissions/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/linquietude-44-lame-agitee-de-michel-de-montaigne

E1.7.D.4.2. BARUCH SPINOZA (1632-1677)

In the fifth part of the Ethics, Spinoza introduces the "third kind of knowledge". This is a mental path that leads to conclusions similar to those found in Eastern philosophy before the concept of predication. This understanding of nature allows us to experience a certain kind of eternity, where rational thinking does not disrupt the contemplation of reality, leading to universal compassion. However, unlike some Eastern philosophies, Spinoza does not dissolve the self or the will.

We have previously discussed Spinoza's suggested transition from passive to active joys, a shift which is challenging to make, despite being grounded in rationality. In the fifth part of the Ethics, he encourages us to consider how this "third kind of knowledge" can align us with nature by downplaying consciousness, without eradicating our essence. This is a process that reconnects us with reality, as he states: "The more perfect each thing is, the more reality it has" (Ethics, Part Five, Demonstration of Proposition XL).

E1.7.D.4.3. JEAN-JACQUES ROUSSEAU (1712-1778)
"The feeling of existence stripped of all other affection is by itself a precious feeling of contentment and peace, which alone would suffice to make this existence dear and sweet."
In "Les Rêveries du promeneur solitaire", Jean-Jacques Rousseau seeks refuge from persecution for his free-thinking. On an island in Lake de Bienne, he enjoys several months of idleness. HHe seeks to rediscover a lost paradise and become one with nature. He lets the movement of water carry him, merging with the world to achieve a blissful existence that reaches the essence of being and transcends  temporary life into the taste of the fullness of eternity. This experience allows him to appreciate bare existence and find temporary inner peace.

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3.1_PH.wE1.7.D.4.3.

E1.7.B.1.1_REF3.2.a_PH.wE1.7.D.4.3.

E1.7.D.4.4. JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE (1749-1832)
"The supreme point to which man can reach is wonder; and when he stands in wonder before the primitive phenomenon (Urphänomen), he must be content; nothing higher can be granted him, and he must seek no further; this is his limit. But men are not usually content to contemplate the primitive phenomenon; they think they have to go even further, and they are like children who, after looking into a mirror, turn it upside down, to see what's on the other side".
(Goethe writing to Eckermann, :J.P. Eckermann, Gespräche mit Goethe, Wiesbaden, Insel-Verlag, 1955, p. 298)

E1.7.D.4.5_REF1_PO "I WANDERED
lonely as a cloud
That floats on high o’er vales and hills,

When all at once I saw a crowd,

A host, of golden daffodils;

Beside the lake, beneath the trees,

Fluttering and dancing in the breeze.

 

Continuous as the stars that shine

And twinkle on the milky way,

They stretched in never-ending line

Along the margin of a bay:

Ten thousand saw I at a glance,

Tossing their heads in sprightly dance.

 

The waves beside them danced; but they

Out-did the sparkling waves in glee:

A poet could not but be gay,

In such a jocund company:

I gazed—and gazed—but little thought

What wealth the show to me had brought:

 

For oft, when on my couch I lie

In vacant or in pensive mood,

They flash upon that inward eye

Which is the bliss of solitude;

And then my heart with pleasure fills,

And dances with the daffodils."

(William Wordsworth )

Listen to the poem: https://www.poetryfoundation.org/poems/45521/i-wandered-lonely-as-a-cloud

E1.7.D.5. THOREAU, WHITMAN, DICKINSON, NIETZSCHE AND RIMBAUD. The convergence of thought truly began in the 19th century, following the "death of God", with Nietzsche and poets like Thoreau, Whitman, Dickinson and Rimbaud. Nietzsche, in particular, grappled with the dissolution of the ego in his work, associating it with the Dionysian ideal.

E1.7.D.5.1. HENRY DAVID THOREAU (1817-1862)
"Perhaps the facts most astounding and most real are never communicated by man to man. The true harvest of my daily life is somewhat as intangible and indescribable as the tints of morning or evening."

E1.7.D.5.1_REF1_PH "IF THE DAY AND THE NIGHT ARE SUCH THAT YOU GREET THEM WITH JOY, and life emits a fragrance like flowers and sweet-scented herbs, is more elastic, more starry, more immortal, — that is your success. All nature is your congratulation, and you have cause momentarily to bless yourself. The greatest gains and values are farthest from being appreciated. We easily come to doubt if they exist. We soon forget them. They are the highest reality. Perhaps the facts most astounding and most real are never communicated by man to man. The true harvest of my daily life is somewhat as intangible and indescribable as the tints of morning or evening. It is a little Star-dust caught, a segment of the rainbow which I have clutched."
(Henry David Thoreau, Walden, 1854)

E1.7.D.5.1_REF2_PH "TIME IS BUT THE STREAM I GO A-FISHING IN. I drink at it; but while I drink I see the sandy bottom and detect how shallow it is. Its thin current slides away, but eternity remains. I would drink deeper; fish in the sky, whose bottom is pebbly with Stars. I cannot count one. I know not the first letter of the alphabet. I have always been regretting that I was not as wise as the day I was born. The intellect is a cleaver; it discerns and rifts its way into the secret of things. I do not wish to be any more busy with my hands than is necessary. My head is hands and feet. I feel all my best faculties concentrated in it."
(Henry David Thoreau, Walden, 1854)

E1.7.D.5.2. WALT WHITMAN (1819-1892)
"HAST NEVER COME TO THEE AN HOUR
Hast never come to thee an hour,
A sudden gleam divine, precipitating, bursting all these bubbles, fashions, wealth?
These eager business aims—books, politics, art, amours,
To utter nothingness?"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

"[Walt Whitman's] verses continue to stir the hearts and minds of readers two centuries hence. Their sublimest, most enduring gift springs from Whitman’s resolute insistence on embracing our variegated, inconstant, polyphonous selves — on harmonizing the individualistic and the egalitarian, nature and culture, the body and the soul. “Do I contradict myself?” he asked unselfconsciously. “Very well then I contradict myself, (I am large, I contain multitudes.)” His poems bellow the bold, countercultural assurance that in acknowledging the contradictions within us, we collapse the contradictions between us; that only by exploring the myriad facets of selfhood, from its brightest summits to its darkest recesses, can we begin to dissolve the illusion of separateness and the antagonisms of otherness that divide us from one another."
(Maria Popova, on Walt Whitman, The Marginalian)

Read it in The Marginalian: https://www.themarginalian.org/2019/05/31/helen-fagin-walt-whitman/?mc_cid=d201a1e393&mc_eid=0b6483decb

E1.7.D.5.2_REF1_PO "BEGINNING MY STUDIES

Beginning my studies the first step pleas'd me so much, 
The mere fact consciousness, these forms, the power of motion, 
The least insect or animal, the senses, eyesight, love, 
The first step I say awed me and pleas'd me so much, 
I have hardly gone and hardly wish'd to go any farther, 
But stop and loiter all the time to sing it in ecstatic songs."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF2_PO "SHUT NOT YOUR DOORS

Shut not your doors to me proud libraries, 
For that which was lacking on your well-fill'd shelves, yet needed on all most, I bring,
Forth from the war emerging, a book I have made, 
The words of my book nothing, the drift of it every thing, 
A book separate, not link'd with the rest nor felt by the intellect, 
But you ye untold latencies will thrill to every page."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF3_PO "TO BE IN ANY FORM, WHAT IS THAT?
(Round and round we go, all of us, and ever come back thither,)
If nothing lay more develop’d the quahaug in its callous shell were enough.

Mine is no callous shell,
I have instant conductors all over me whether I pass or stop,
They seize every object and lead it harmlessly through me.

I merely stir, press, feel with my fingers, and am happy,
To touch my person to some one else’s is about as much as I can stand.

Is this then a touch? quivering me to a new identity,
Flames and ether making a rush for my veins,
Treacherous tip of me reaching and crowding to help them,
My flesh and blood playing out lightning to strike what is hardly different from myself,
On all sides prurient provokers stiffening my limbs,
Straining the udder of my heart for its withheld drip,
Behaving licentious toward me, taking no denial,
Depriving me of my best as for a purpose,
Unbuttoning my clothes, holding me by the bare waist,
Deluding my confusion with the calm of the sunlight and pasture-fields,
Immodestly sliding the fellow-senses away,
They bribed to swap off with touch and go and graze at the edges of me,
No consideration, no regard for my draining strength or my anger,
Fetching the rest of the herd around to enjoy them a while,
Then all uniting to stand on a headland and worry me.

The sentries desert every other part of me,
They have left me helpless to a red marauder,
They all come to the headland to witness and assist against me.

I am given up by traitors,
I talk wildly, I have lost my wits, I and nobody else am the greatest traitor,
I went myself first to the headland, my own hands carried me there.

You villain touch! what are you doing? my breath is tight in its throat,
Unclench your floodgates, you are too much for me."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF4_PO "I KNOW I HAVE THE BEST OF TIME AND SPACE, AND WAS NEVER MEASURED AND NEVER WILL BE MEASURED.

I tramp a perpetual journey, (come listen all!)
My signs are a rain-proof coat, good shoes, and a staff cut from the woods,
No friend of mine takes his ease in my chair,
I have no chair, no church, no philosophy,
I lead no man to a dinner-table, library, exchange,
But each man and each woman of you I lead upon a knoll,
My left hand hooking you round the waist,
My right hand pointing to landscapes of continents and the public road.

Not I, not any one else can travel that road for you,
You must travel it for yourself.

It is not far, it is within reach,
Perhaps you have been on it since you were born and did not know,
Perhaps it is everywhere on water and on land.

[...] 

You are also asking me questions and I hear you,
I answer that I cannot answer, you must find out for yourself.

Sit a while dear son,
Here are biscuits to eat and here is milk to drink,
But as soon as you sleep and renew yourself in sweet clothes, I kiss you
with a good-by kiss and open the gate for your egress hence.

Long enough have you dream’d contemptible dreams,
Now I wash the gum from your eyes,
You must habit yourself to the dazzle of the light and of every moment of your life.

Long have you timidly waded holding a plank by the shore,
Now I will you to be a bold swimmer,
To jump off in the midst of the sea, rise again, nod to me, shout, and laughingly dash with your hair."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF5_PO "THERE IS THAT IN ME — I DO NOT KNOW WHAT IT IS — BUT I KNOW IT IS IN ME.

Wrench’d and sweaty — calm and cool then my body becomes,
I sleep—I sleep long.

I do not know it — it is without name — it is a word unsaid,
It is not in any dictionary, utterance, symbol.

Something it swings on more than the earth I swing on,
To it the creation is the friend whose embracing awakes me.

Perhaps I might tell more. Outlines! I plead for my brothers and sisters.

Do you see O my brothers and sisters?
It is not chaos or death—it is form, union, plan—it is eternal life — it is
Happiness."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF6_PO "SALUT AU MONDE!

O take my hand Walt Whitman!
Such gliding wonders! such sights and sounds!
Such join’d unended links, each hook’d to the next,
Each answering all, each sharing the earth with all.
What widens within you Walt Whitman? [...]

11 [...]
Each of us inevitable,
Each of us limitless—each of us with his or her right upon the earth,
Each of us allow’d the eternal purports of the earth,
Each of us here as divinely as any is here. [...]

13
My spirit has pass’d in compassion and determination around the whole earth,
I have look’d for equals and lovers and found them ready for me in all lands,
I think some divine rapport has equalized me with them.
You vapors, I think I have risen with you, moved away to distant
     continents, and fallen down there, for reasons,
I think I have blown with you you winds;
You waters I have finger’d every shore with you,
I have run through what any river or strait of the globe has run through,
I have taken my stand on the bases of peninsulas and on the high
     embedded rocks, to cry thence:
What cities the light or warmth penetrates I penetrate those cities myself,
All islands to which birds wing their way I wing my way myself."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF8_PO "ON THE BEACH AT NIGHT ALONE
On the beach at night alone,
As the old mother sways her to and fro singing her husky song,
As I watch the bright stars shining, I think a thought of the clef of the universes and of the future.

A vast similitude interlocks all,
All spheres, grown, ungrown, small, large, suns, moons, planets,
All distances of place however wide,
All distances of time, all inanimate forms,
All souls, all living bodies though they be ever so different, or in different worlds,
All gaseous, watery, vegetable, mineral processes, the fishes, the brutes,
All nations, colors, barbarisms, civilizations, languages,
All identities that have existed or may exist on this globe, or any globe,
All lives and deaths, all of the past, present, future,
This vast similitude spans them, and always has spann’d,
And shall forever span them and compactly hold and enclose them."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF9_PO "THOUGHTS
Of ownership—as if one fit to own things could not at pleasure enter
     upon all, and incorporate them into himself or herself [...]"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

This verse suggests that the concept of ownership is flawed because a person who believes they have the right to own things cannot fully comprehend that they have the ability to embrace and make a part of themselves anything they desire. In simpler terms, it implies that owning something does not necessarily mean that it belongs to us exclusively, as we can always assimilate it into our being.

To utter nothingness?"

E1.7.D.5.2_REF11_PO "WHO LEARNS MY LESSON COMPLETE?
[...]
I lie abstracted and hear beautiful tales of things and the reasons of things,
They are so beautiful I nudge myself to listen.

I cannot say to any person what I hear—I cannot say it to myself—it is very wonderful.

It is no small matter, this round and delicious globe moving so exactly
    in its orbit for ever and ever, without one jolt or the untruth of a single second"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF12_PO "SONG AT SUNSET
[...].
Open mouth of my soul uttering gladness,
Eyes of my soul seeing perfection,
Natural life of me faithfully praising things,
Corroborating forever the triumph of things.

Illustrious every one!
Illustrious what we name space, sphere of unnumber’d spirits,
Illustrious the mystery of motion in all beings, even the tiniest insect,
Illustrious the attribute of speech, the senses, the body,
Illustrious the passing light—illustrious the pale reflection on the new moon in the western sky,
Illustrious whatever I see or hear or touch, to the last.

Good in all,
In the satisfaction and aplomb of animals,
In the annual return of the seasons,
In the hilarity of youth,
In the strength and flush of manhood,
In the grandeur and exquisiteness of old age,
In the superb vistas of death.

Wonderful to depart!
Wonderful to be here!
The heart, to jet the all-alike and innocent blood!
To breathe the air, how delicious!
To speak—to walk—to seize something by the hand!
To prepare for sleep, for bed, to look on my rose-color’d flesh!
To be conscious of my body, so satisfied, so large!
To be this incredible God I am!
To have gone forth among other Gods, these men and women I love.

Wonderful how I celebrate you and myself
How my thoughts play subtly at the spectacles around!
How the clouds pass silently overhead!
How the earth darts on and on! and how the sun, moon, stars, dart on and on!
How the water sports and sings! (surely it is alive!)
How the trees rise and stand up, with strong trunks, with branches and leaves!
(Surely there is something more in each of the trees, some living soul.)
O amazement of things—even the least particle!
O spirituality of things! [...]"
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.2_REF13_PO "KOSMOS
Who includes diversity and is Nature,
Who is the amplitude of the earth, and the coarseness and sexuality of the earth, and the great charity of the earth and the equilibrium also,
Who has not look’d forth from the windows the eyes for nothing, or whose brain held audience with messengers for nothing,
Who contains believers and disbelievers, who is the most majestic lover,
Who holds duly his or her triune proportion of realism, spiritualism, and of the aesthetic or intellectual,
Who having consider’d the body finds all its organs and parts good,
Who, out of the theory of the earth and of his or her body understands by subtle analogies all other theories,
The theory of a city, a poem, and of the large politics of these States;
Who believes not only in our globe with its sun and moon, but in other globes with their suns and moons,
Who, constructing the house of himself or herself, not for a day but for all time, sees races, eras, dates, generations,
The past, the future, dwelling there, like space, inseparable together."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.5.4. FRIEDRICH NIETZSCHE (1844-1900)
"You must still have chaos in you to give birth to a dancing star"

E1.7.D.5.4.a. NIETZSCHE EXPLORES THE COMPLEXITY OF MORALITY AND TRUTH THROUGH THE PERSPECTIVE OF THE DIONYSIAN STATE. During Dionysian ecstasy, oblivion enables a separation between the world of everyday reality and that of radical truth, which is revealed by Dionysian art proclaiming excess as truth. The process produces a symbolic metamorphosis of the subject, leading to the disappearance of the empirical self in favor of a transcendent, cosmic reality reclaimed by man. Although noble and courageous, the solution proposed by Nietzsche may seem unrealistic, since it requires a confrontation with suffering.

E1.7.D.5.4.a.i. NIETZSCHE FEARS THAT EVERY MAN WILL NO LONGER TRY TO SURPASS HIMSELF, BUT RATHER IMITATE HIS NEIGHBOR
 Nietzsche associates Christian and Platonic values with the equalization promoted by the democratic model. He fears that, in these models, every man will no longer try to outdo himself, but rather imitate his neighbor - equalizing society from below.

E1.7.D.5.4.a.i_REF1_PH "CHRISTIANITY DYING AWAY IN MORALITY. - 'On n’est bon que par la pitie: il faut donc qu’il y ait quelque pitié dans tous nos sentiments'—so preaches morality in our days. And whence comes this? That sympathetic, disinterested, benevolent, social actions are now felt to be characteristics of the moral man—nay, perhaps be the most universal effect and change of tone which Christianity has brought about in Europe, though this has been neither its intention nor its doctrine. But it was the
residuum of Christian moods, when the very contrary, utterly selfish faith in the “ One thing is needful,” that is, the absolute importance of the eternal, personal
salvation, together with the dogmas on which it rested, had gradually receded, and the accompanying belief in “ love” and “ charity,” in accordance with the enormous
practice of the Church, was thereby pushed into the foreground. The further the dogmas were departed from, the more a justification for this departure was aimed at in a cult of philanthropy: not to fall short of the Christian ideal in this point, but, if possible, to excel it, was a secret stimulus to all French free-thinkers from Voltaire down to Auguste Comte; and the latter, with his famous moral formula of “ vivre pour autrui ” has indeed outchristianed Christianity! The doctrine of sympathetic affections and of the pity or utility of others, as the principle of action, has gained its greatest fame in Germany through Schopenhauer, in England through John Stuart Mill: hut they themselves were only echoes—from about the time of the French revolution, these doctrines have, with an enormous motor force, sprung up everywhere in the coarsest as well as the subtlest forms, and all socialistic principles involuntarily, as it were, took their stand on the common ground of this doctrine. Perhaps in our days no prejudice is more implicitly believed in than that we know what really constitutes morality. It seems that now everybody is pleased to hear that society is about to adapt the individual to the general requirements, and that the happiness and, at the same time, the sacrifice of the individual consists in feeling himself as a useful member and instrument of the whole: we have only our lingering doubts as to where to look for this whole; whether in an existing State or in one yet to he founded, or in the nation, or in an international brotherhood, or in small, new, economic communities. A great deal of meditation, doubt, fighting, much excitement and passion, are just now being exhibited on this head; hut wonderful and pleasant is the unanimity with which the “ ego” is requested to practise self denial, until, in form of adaptation to the whole, it again obtains its fixed sphere of rights and duties, until it has become something altogether new and different. Nothing less is aimed at—whether admitted or not—than
thorough transformation, nay, weakening and abrogation of the individual: there is an unceasing enumeration and accusing of all the wickedness and offensiveness, the lavishness, expense, and luxury of the traditional aspect of individual existence ; it is hoped that everything may he managed in a cheaper, less dangerous, more uniform, and harmonious way, provided only that there are nothing hut large bodies and their members. Everything which in any way corresponds to this all-productive craving and its subsidiary cravings, is considered as good—this is the moral ground-current of our age; sympathy and social feeling play into each other’s hands. [...]"
(Friedrich Nietzsche, The dawn of days, aphorism 132, 1881)

E1.7.D.5.4.a.ii. DOES NIETZSCHE FEEL CAPABLE OF PERFORMING THE DIONYSIAN METAMORPHOSIS HIMSELF?
The Apollonian-Dionysian process produces a symbolic metamorphosis of the subject, leading to the disappearance of the empirical self in favor of a transcendent, cosmic reality reclaimed by man. The subject then becomes the cosmic subject of excess, the only existing and eternal one. But does he feel capable of this himself?

E1.7.D.5.4.a.ii_REF1_PH DIONYSIAN ECSTASY ABOLISHES THE BARRIERS OF EVERYDAY EXISTENCE AND PLUNGES US INTO A FORGETFULNESS THAT IS EXPERIENCED AS TRUE KNOWLEDGE AND AWARENESS OF RADICAL TRUTH.
"The ecstasy of the Dionysian state, with its abolition of the usual barriers and limits of existence, contains during its duration a lethargic element into which everything personally experienced in the past is absorbed. This abyss of forgetting separates the world of everyday reality from the world of Dionysian reality (Kroner I, p. 55). Forgetting, however, is interpreted in the opposite sense to that in which psychoanalysis sees it today: not from the superficial reality of everyday life, but from the profound Dionysian reality or radical truth. [Dionysian ecstasy involves true knowledge and, above all, the consciousness of a truth".
(Angèle Kremer-Marietti, Introduction to The Birth of Tragedy by Nietzsche, 1994)

E1.7.D.5.4.a.ii_REF2_PH "DIONYSIAN ART ALONE PROCLAIMS RADICAL TRUTH, and what it reveals of truth is excess (Kroner I, p. 37). The true artist is the medium through which the only truly existing subject (not the cogito) celebrates salvation in appearance [...] what counts is artistic creation, because it brings man back to his most intimate and universal source".
(Angèle Kremer-Marietti, Introduction to The Birth of Tragedy by Nietzsche, 1994)

E1.7.D.5.4.a.ii_REF3_PH THE 'APOLLINIAN-DIONYSIAN' PROCESS PROCESS CAUSES A SYMBOLIC METAMORPHOSIS OF THE SUBJECT, LEADING TO THE DISSOLUTION OF THE EMPIRICAL SELF IN THE IMAGINARY COSMOS. This progressive negation of the individual self makes it possible to recover a transcendental and cosmic reality, thus reaching the primordial.
"The complex 'Apollonian-Dionysian' process does not, like the Apollonian process alone, produce a sublimation by substitution of appearance; it clearly produces a regression through a true symbolic metamorphosis of the subject itself; and once this metamorphosis begins, it tends immediately and irresistibly to complete itself in the complete disappearance of the subject, whose ego dissolves in the cosmos, reintegrated in the imagination. The ego has thus reached a certain centre of itself, which is none other than the negation of the empirical ego. But this progressive negation takes place in favour of a transcendental and cosmic reality that has been reconquered by man. This is where the Primordial One is located: at the centre of the self that denies itself as an individual subject to be anything other than the cosmic subject of excess, the only existing and eternal one".
(Angèle Kremer-Marietti, Introduction to The Birth of Tragedy by Nietzsche, 1994)

E1.7.D.5.4.a.iii. ZARATHUSTRA'S GREAT "YES" PRESUPPOSES THAT WE CONFRONT SUFFERING, THAT WE EVEN LOVE IT - AN IMPOSSIBLE CHALLENGE? Wishful thinking? "Couldn't we say that Nietzsche has diagnosed the limits of thought, but that the solution he proposes is unrealistic - albeit noble and courageous? He comes very close to the intuitions of Eastern ante-predictive philosophies, without considering the dissolution of the "self", reserved for the Dionysian.

E1.7.D.5.4.a.iii_REF1_PH 'THE GREAT FATIGUE' OF FRIEDRICH NIETZSCHE
"For Nietzsche, the weariness or fatigue of his contemporaries is the result of Christian and Platonic values whose origins go back to Socrates, a key figure in the history of European thought. The general exhaustion of modern man can be seen in the dejection, but also in the hyperactivity of those who must lose themselves in entertainment in order to bear themselves. However, the choice to tire oneself (through exercise or walking, for example) can regenerate energy and enable the creation of something new; it is a very different kind of tiredness from the tiredness we are talking about - the tiredness condemned by Nietzsche is independent of physical activity. The values of the "exhausted" are those that deny life. "We have called God that which weakens, teaches and inoculates weakness". The dominant values set themselves the radical goal of eradicating from man all the feelings they consider bad, placing him in a situation of flight from reality in which he has even less control over these feelings. N. advocates instead a recognition of who we are and an aestheticization of the self through the prioritisation and control of our impulses.
The rest of existence is the Dionysian that we do not want to face and that escapes the rationalist and Socratic vision.
"Nothing is more opposed to the purely aesthetic interpretation and justification of the world than the Christian doctrine, which is and wants to be only moral, and which with its absolute standards, and already for example with its divine truth, sends art, all art, back into the realm of lies, and thereby denies it, curses it, condemns it. Under such a way of thinking and judging, I have always felt a hostility towards life, a raging and vengeful antipathy towards life itself, because all life is based on appearance, art, illusion, optics, the necessity of perspective and error. From the very beginning, Christianity was essentially and fundamentally a dislike and weariness of life in relation to life, which was simply disguised, hidden, invented, behind the belief in another life, in a better life". Nietzsche, Preface to The Origin of Tragedy. Values of the exhausted who, instead of confronting reality and its contradictions, project themselves towards an Other that is supposedly better. 
Nietzsche associates Christian and Platonic values with the equalisation promoted by the democratic model. In this model, he fears that each person will no longer seek to surpass himself, but rather to imitate his neighbour, thus equalising society at the lowest level.
A great deal of suffering and metamorphosis is necessary for creation to take place. Zarathustra's great "yes" requires that we face suffering, that we even love it - an impossible challenge? Wishful thinking? "Could it not be said that Nietzsche has diagnosed the limits of thought, but that the solution he proposes is unrealistic - albeit noble and courageous? He comes close to the intuitions of Eastern ante-predictive philosophies, without considering the dissolution of the 'self' reserved for the Dionysian."
(Céline Denat on the program Les chemins de la philosophie, broadcast on France Culture on 12/18/2017)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/la-grande-fatigue-de-friedrich-nietzsche-5564489

E1.7.D.5.4.b. YOU MUST STILL HAVE CHAOS IN YOU TO GIVE BIRTH TO A DANCING STAR
"Shouldn't philosophy serve to make us better rather than to live better; that is, to work on one's interiority rather than focus on improving external conditions. Eastern ante-predictive philosophies encourage us to dare not to have an answer for everything, to dare to be aware by leaving our mental habits behind.
We spend our lives trying to improve ourselves, instead of understanding and accepting ourselves, so that we can surpass ourselves. This is where Zen helps us to bypass duality and the principle of non-contradiction so dear to rationalist philosophy: accepting ourselves in an amor fati while striving to live better - life is contradictory; as long as we accept our reality, progress may come in spite of ourselves. As Nietzsche says in Zarathustra, "You must still have chaos in you to give birth to a dancing star".

E1.7.D.5.4.b_REF1_PH IN FRIEDRICH NIETZSCHE'S "THUS SPOKE ZARATHUSTRA," THE CHARACTER ZARATHUSTRA DESCRIBES THE DECLINE OF HUMANITY INTO THE "LAST MAN," A STATE WHERE PEOPLE SEEK COMFORT AND MEDIOCRITY OVER GREATNESS AND AMBITION.
"Alas! there cometh the time when man will no longer launch the arrow of his longing beyond man — and the string of his bow will have unlearned to whizz!
I tell you: one must still have chaos in one, to give birth to a dancing star. I tell you: ye have still chaos in you.
Alas! There cometh the time when man will no longer give birth to any star. Alas! There cometh the time of the most despicable man, who can no longer despise himself. Lo! I show you THE LAST MAN.
« What is love? What is creation? What is longing? What is a star? » — so asketh the last man and blinketh.
The earth hath then become small, and on it there hoppeth the last man who maketh everything small. His species is ineradicable like that of the ground–flea; the last man liveth longest.
« We have discovered happiness » — say the last men, and blink thereby.
They have left the regions where it is hard to live; for they need warmth. One still loveth one’s neighbour and rubbeth against him; for one needeth warmth.
Turning ill and being distrustful, they consider sinful: they walk warily. He is a fool who still stumbleth over stones or men!
A little poison now and then: that maketh pleasant dreams. And much poison at last for a pleasant death.
One still worketh, for work is a pastime. But one is careful lest the pastime should hurt one. One no longer becometh poor or rich; both are too burdensome. Who still wanteth to rule? Who still wanteth to obey? Both are too burdensome.
No shepherd, and one herd! Every one wanteth the same; every one is equal: he who hath other sentiments goeth voluntarily into the madhouse.
« Formerly all the world was insane, » — say the subtlest of them, and blink thereby. They are clever and know all that hath happened: so there is no end to their raillery. People still fall out, but are soon reconciled—otherwise it spoileth their stomachs.
They have their little pleasures for the day, and their little pleasures for the night, but they have a regard for health.
« We have discovered happiness, » — say the last men, and blink thereby. —
And here ended the first discourse of Zarathustra, which is also called « The Prologue »: for at this point the shouting and mirth of the multitude interrupted him. « Give us this last man, O Zarathustra, » — they called out — « make us into these last men! Then will we make thee a present of the Superman! »” 
(Friedrich Nietzsche, Thus Spoke Zarathustra, 1883)

E1.7.D.5.4.b_REF2_PH "ISN'T THE PURPOSE OF PHILOSOPHY TO BECOME A BETTER PERSON RATHER THAN TO LIVE BETTER; that is, to work on one's interiority rather than focusing on improving exterior conditions? Eastern ante-predicative philosophies encourage us to dare not to having an answer to everything, to dare to be present by departing from our mental habits. Happiness is partly a personal asceticism and yet there is no possession of happiness. We must renounce all security: « The bad news is that we are falling, the good news is that there is no ground ». Oscillation between bliss and despair is inevitable: « It's a mess, but there is no problem! ». Let's not dramatize the tragic! Bukowski helps us to think that even in a state of dependence, if we accept not to judge our determinism, we can be free and joyful. Becoming aware of our own inner patterns and not to resist them is a first step towards liberation. We spend our lives trying to improve instead of understanding and accepting oneself, in order to be able to surpass oneself. This is where Zen helps us get around the duality of the principle of non-contradiction that is so dear to rationalist philosophy: to accept oneself in an amor fati while seeking to live better - life is contradictory; as long as one accepts his reality, progress may come in spite of oneself. As Nietzsche says through Zarathustra, « One must still have chaos in oneself to give birth to a dancing star »."
(Alexandre Jollien at the microphone of the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on 11/16/2018 on France Culture)

E1.7.D.5.4.b_REF2.1_MU L’ALLIANCE DU CHAOS ET D'UNE ÉTOILE QUI DANSE, Chopin, Ballade no. 1 g-minor op. 23, 1831

Écouter sur YouTube: https://youtu.be/ecC7fjnFiuk

E1.7.D.5.4.b_REF3_PH "AT THE POINT OF REST OF THE WHIRLING WORLD, neither flesh, nor deprivation of flesh, neither « coming from »  nor « going to », at the point of rest : there is the dance. Neither stop, nor move. Do not call it fixity - it is where past and future meet. Not « movement from » or « to », not ascent or decline, only the point of rest. There would be no dancing at all, if not for this point, whereas there is nothing but dancing." 
(Krishna to Arjuna, Bhagavadgītā)

E1.7.D.5.5. ARTHUR RIMBAUD (1854-1891)
"I know the skies in thunderbolts, a whirlwind’s might,
And the surf, and the currents, to dusks I have been,
And dawns as sublime as exalted doves in flight,
And I saw at times that which man but thinks he’s seen!"
(Arthur Rimbaud, The Drunken Boat, 1871)

E1.6.G.2.5_REF2_PO.wE1.7.D.5.5.

E1.7.D.5.5_REF1.1_PO "MY BOHEMIAN (FANTASY)

I went away, fists in my torn pockets;
My coat was, as well, becoming ideal;
I went under the sky, Muse, and I was your servant;
Oh! of what splendid loves I dreamt

My unique breaches had a large hole.
A dreaming Little-Tom-Thumb, I spewed forth, in my run,
Rhymes. My tavern was under the Great Bear.
My stars in the sky had a gentle frill

And I listened to them, sitting at the edge of roads
Those soft September nights where I felt drops
Of dew on my forehead like a vigorous wine;

Where, rhyming amidst the fantastic shadows,
Like lyres, I pulled elastics
From my injured shoes, one foot near my heart!
(Arthur Rimbaud, Douai Notebook, 1870)

"MA BOHÈME (FANTAISIE)

Je m'en allais, les poings dans mes poches crevées ; 
Mon paletot aussi devenait idéal; 
J'allais sous le ciel, Muse! et j'étais ton féal; 
Oh! là là ! que d'amours splendides j'ai rêvées!

Mon unique culotte avait un large trou. 
- Petit-Poucet rêveur, j'égrenais dans ma course 
Des rimes. Mon auberge était à la Grande-Ourse.
Mes étoiles au ciel avaient un doux frou-frou

Et je les écoutais, assis au bord des routes, 
Ces bons soirs de septembre où je sentais des gouttes 
De rosée à mon front, comme un vin de vigueur;

Où, rimant au milieu des ombres fantastiques, 
Comme des lyres, je tirais les élastiques 
De mes souliers blessés, un pied près de mon cœur !"
(Arthur Rimbaud, Cahier de Douai, 1870)

E1.7.D.5.5_REF1.2_PO "I HAVE STRETCHED ROPES FROM STEEPLE TO STEEPLE;
garlands from window to window;
golden chains from star to star, 
and I dance."
(Arhtur Rimbaud, Illuminations, 1886)

"J'AI TENDU DES CORDES DE CLOCHER A CLOCHER;
des guirlandes de fenêtre à fenêtre;
des chaînes d'or d'étoile à étoile, 
et je danse."
(Arhtur Rimbaud, Les Illuminations, 1886)

E1.7.D.5.5_REF1.3_PO "THE DRUNKEN BOAT

As I further down the impassive Rivers came,
No longer felt I guided by the bargemen’s hauls:
Screaming Redskins had used them to practice their aim,
And they had nailed them, naked, onto colored poles.

I didn’t much care for any of the crewmen,
The Flemish wheat, the English cotton in their tow.
As all that clamor died along with my bargemen,
The River let me sail at will upon its flow.

Straight into the furious lashing of the tide,
I, that other winter more deaf than toddler mind,
Ran! The Peninsulas, unmoored and set aglide,
Never knew a cry of triumph so unconfined.

The tempest blessed me as I woke unto the sea.
More lightweight than a cork I danced on crest and trough,
(Eternal rollers of victims they’re said to be,)
Ten nights without the lantern and its banal glow!

Sweeter than to children a sour apple’s meat,
The green water penetrated my pinewood frame,
And the stains of blue wine and splashes of vomit
It washed; my anchor and rudder the sea would claim.

And from that moment onwards I bathed in the Verse
Of the Sea, infused with stars and lactescent ink,
Ravaging green azures, where, pallid and submersed,
A delighted, drowned dreamer sometimes may sink;

Where, suddenly dyeing the blue, deranged fires,
And slow rhythms under the daylight’s shining stare,
Stronger than alcohol, vaster than our lyres,
Ferment for me the bitter redness of love’s glare!

I know the skies in thunderbolts, a whirlwind’s might,
And the surf, and the currents, to dusks I have been,
And dawns as sublime as exalted doves in flight,
And I saw at times that which man but thinks he’s seen!

I saw the low sun speckled with mystic horrors,
Illuminating long and solid purple straps,
Which remind of ancient dramas and their actors,
The waves rolling far off their trembling shutter-flaps!

I have dreamt of a green night in glittering snows,
A kiss reaching into the sea’s eye, slow and long,
The flowing of a lifeblood which no human knows,
And blue and yellow surging of phosphoric song!

I followed for months, like herds adrift on prairies,
Frantic, the swell that battles reefs until the death,
Never dreaming that the lustrous feet of Marys,
Could force the muzzle onto Oceans out of breath!

I crashed, do you know, into rare, exotic lands,
Where flowers fuse with panther eyes in skins of men,
Iridiscent rainbows stretched tight as bridle bands,
Neath the seas’ horizon, and to glaucous herds again!

I saw the enormous marshes seething, and nets
Where a Leviathan rots in the weed, grasp this!
And a sinkhole amidst the placid seas which lets
The flow collapse into a cascading abyss!

Glaciers, silver suns, pearl waves, skies of ember!
Hideous wrecks along the bottoms of brown bays,
Where gigantic snakes whom the vermin dismember,
Fall from contorted trees in a black, pungent haze!

I would have liked to show to children the dolphin
On a blue rising wave, that golden fish that sings.
Foams colored by flowers gently rocked my drifting,
And ineffable winds at times lent me their wings.

And sometimes, martyrs sick of poles and zones, mellow,
Their sobs sweetening my rolling, the weeping seas
Raised for me dark blossoms, suction cups all yellow,
And I hung out there like a woman on her knees…

Akin to an island, watching my sides ravaged
By quarrels and droppings of pale-eyed, noisy birds,
I wandered along as across my frayed cordage
Drowned men sank backwards into slumber without words!

Hurled by the hurricane into the birdless ether,
Lost under the hair of coves on shallow ground,
I, a boat whose wreck, a carcass drunk with water,
Neither merchant ships nor Monitors would have found;

Free, smoking, arisen from violet vapour,
I pierced the blushing sky like a wall gives to drill,
A luscious reward for good verse put on paper:
Lichens of sunlight and an azure spill,

I ran, flecked with lunulas, electric bows,
A rabid plank, trailed by sea-horses black as night,
When Julys carved with crushing bludgeon blows
Ultramarine skies into funnels set alight;

I who trembled to sense, at fifty leagues of distance,
Groaning Behemots rut, and Maelstroms broad and thick,
Eternal spinner of a blue inert persistence,
I miss Europe’s ancient parapet walls of brick!

I’ve seen astral islands, and swarms of stars, on deep
Delirious skies to which sailors set their course:
- In those endless nights, are you exiled, do you sleep,
One million golden birds, O future Vital Force?

But, truly, I’ve wept much! Every Dawn but saddens,
Every sun is bitter, every moon is rotten.
Rancid love has bloated me with stupor that maddens,
O may my keel dissolve! May I sink to bottom!

I want one water in Europe — shallow lying,
Black and cold, where into the scented dying day
A crouching child full of sorrow sends off flying
A boat as fragile as a butterfly in May!

I cannot anymore, O waves, bathed in your langours,
Undo the wake which cotton freighters spread in rays,
Nor endure the pride of the flags and the banners,
Nor swim under the floating bridges’ dreadful gaze!"
(Arthur Rimbaud, 1871)


"LE BATEAU IVRE

Comme je descendais des Fleuves impassibles,
Je ne me sentis plus guidé par les haleurs :
Des Peaux-Rouges criards les avaient pris pour cibles,
Les ayant cloués nus aux poteaux de couleurs.

J’étais insoucieux de tous les équipages,
Porteur de blés flamands ou de cotons anglais.
Quand avec mes haleurs ont fini ces tapages,
Les Fleuves m’ont laissé descendre où je voulais.

Dans les clapotements furieux des marées,
Moi, l’autre hiver, plus sourd que les cerveaux d’enfants,
Je courus ! Et les Péninsules démarrées
N’ont pas subi tohu-bohus plus triomphants.

La tempête a béni mes éveils maritimes.
Plus léger qu’un bouchon j’ai dansé sur les flots
Qu’on appelle rouleurs éternels de victimes,
Dix nuits, sans regretter l’oeil niais des falots !

Plus douce qu’aux enfants la chair des pommes sûres,
L’eau verte pénétra ma coque de sapin
Et des taches de vins bleus et des vomissures
Me lava, dispersant gouvernail et grappin.

Et dès lors, je me suis baigné dans le Poème
De la Mer, infusé d’astres, et lactescent,
Dévorant les azurs verts ; où, flottaison blême
Et ravie, un noyé pensif parfois descend ;

Où, teignant tout à coup les bleuités, délires
Et rhythmes lents sous les rutilements du jour,
Plus fortes que l’alcool, plus vastes que nos lyres,
Fermentent les rousseurs amères de l’amour !

Je sais les cieux crevant en éclairs, et les trombes
Et les ressacs et les courants : je sais le soir,
L’Aube exaltée ainsi qu’un peuple de colombes,
Et j’ai vu quelquefois ce que l’homme a cru voir !

J’ai vu le soleil bas, taché d’horreurs mystiques,
Illuminant de longs figements violets,
Pareils à des acteurs de drames très antiques
Les flots roulant au loin leurs frissons de volets !

J’ai rêvé la nuit verte aux neiges éblouies,
Baisers montant aux yeux des mers avec lenteurs,
La circulation des sèves inouïes,
Et l’éveil jaune et bleu des phosphores chanteurs !

J’ai suivi, des mois pleins, pareille aux vacheries
Hystériques, la houle à l’assaut des récifs,
Sans songer que les pieds lumineux des Maries
Pussent forcer le mufle aux Océans poussifs !

J’ai heurté, savez-vous, d’incroyables Florides
Mêlant aux fleurs des yeux de panthères à peaux
D’hommes ! Des arcs-en-ciel tendus comme des brides
Sous l’horizon des mers, à de glauques troupeaux !

J’ai vu fermenter les marais énormes, nasses
Où pourrit dans les joncs tout un Léviathan !
Des écroulements d’eaux au milieu des bonaces,
Et les lointains vers les gouffres cataractant !

Glaciers, soleils d’argent, flots nacreux, cieux de braises !
Échouages hideux au fond des golfes bruns
Où les serpents géants dévorés des punaises
Choient, des arbres tordus, avec de noirs parfums !

J’aurais voulu montrer aux enfants ces dorades
Du flot bleu, ces poissons d’or, ces poissons chantants.
– Des écumes de fleurs ont bercé mes dérades
Et d’ineffables vents m’ont ailé par instants.

Parfois, martyr lassé des pôles et des zones,
La mer dont le sanglot faisait mon roulis doux
Montait vers moi ses fleurs d’ombre aux ventouses jaunes
Et je restais, ainsi qu’une femme à genoux…

Presque île, ballottant sur mes bords les querelles
Et les fientes d’oiseaux clabaudeurs aux yeux blonds.
Et je voguais, lorsqu’à travers mes liens frêles
Des noyés descendaient dormir, à reculons !

Or moi, bateau perdu sous les cheveux des anses,
Jeté par l’ouragan dans l’éther sans oiseau,
Moi dont les Monitors et les voiliers des Hanses
N’auraient pas repêché la carcasse ivre d’eau ;

Libre, fumant, monté de brumes violettes,
Moi qui trouais le ciel rougeoyant comme un mur
Qui porte, confiture exquise aux bons poètes,
Des lichens de soleil et des morves d’azur ;

Qui courais, taché de lunules électriques,
Planche folle, escorté des hippocampes noirs,
Quand les juillets faisaient crouler à coups de triques
Les cieux ultramarins aux ardents entonnoirs ;

Moi qui tremblais, sentant geindre à cinquante lieues
Le rut des Béhémots et les Maelstroms épais,
Fileur éternel des immobilités bleues,
Je regrette l’Europe aux anciens parapets !

J’ai vu des archipels sidéraux ! et des îles
Dont les cieux délirants sont ouverts au vogueur :
– Est-ce en ces nuits sans fonds que tu dors et t’exiles,
Million d’oiseaux d’or, ô future Vigueur ?

Mais, vrai, j’ai trop pleuré ! Les Aubes sont navrantes.
Toute lune est atroce et tout soleil amer :
L’âcre amour m’a gonflé de torpeurs enivrantes.
Ô que ma quille éclate ! Ô que j’aille à la mer !

Si je désire une eau d’Europe, c’est la flache
Noire et froide où vers le crépuscule embaumé
Un enfant accroupi plein de tristesse, lâche
Un bateau frêle comme un papillon de mai.

Je ne puis plus, baigné de vos langueurs, ô lames,
Enlever leur sillage aux porteurs de cotons,
Ni traverser l’orgueil des drapeaux et des flammes,
Ni nager sous les yeux horribles des pontons."
(Arthur Rimbaud, 1871)

E1.7.D.5.5_REF2_PO “I WANT TO BE A POET, AND I'M WORKING ON BECOMING CALIRVOYANT: you won't understand at all, and I could hardly explain it to you. It's about reaching the unknown by disrupting all the senses. The suffering is enormous, but you have to be strong, born a poet, and I recognized myself as a poet. It's not my fault at all. It's wrong to say: I think: it should say: I am being thought. - Pardon the play on words. - I is another.”
(Arthur Rimbaud, Letter to Georges Izambard - May 13, 1871)

"For I am another. If the copper wakes up as a trumpet, it is not his fault. It is obvious to me: I am witnessing the flowering of my thoughts: I watch them, I listen to them: I strike the bow: the symphony stirs in the depths or leaps onto the stage. [...]
I say that one must be a clairvoyant, become a clairvoyant.
The poet becomes a clairvoyant through a long, immense and thoughtful deregulation of all the senses, all the forms of love, suffering, madness; he searches himself, he exhausts all the poisons within him to keep only the quintessences. An unspeakable torture in which he needs all his faith, all his superhuman strength, in which he becomes above all the great invalid, the great criminal, the great accursed and the supreme scholar! Because he reaches the unknown! Because he has cultivated his soul, already rich, more than anyone else! He reaches the unknown, and if, in a state of panic, he finally loses the meaning of his visions, he has seen them! Let him die leaping through the unheard-of and unspeakable: other terrible workers will come; they will begin with the horizons where the other has collapsed."
(Arthur Rimbaud, Letter to Paul Demeny - May 15, 1871)

E1.7.D.6. IT IS TRULY IN THE 20th CENTURY THAT A PROFUSION OF THINKERS AND ARTISTS CONVERGE AGAIN WITH THE INTUITIONS OF THE ANTE-PREDICATIVE PERIOD IN THE WEST. Claude Levi-Strauss wrote in Tristes Tropiques: "It's been two and a half thousand years since men discovered and formulated these truths. Since then, we have not discovered anything". However, this philosophy remains marginal today. In part, this is because it is still difficult to understand, lying as it does on the very edge of language. Also, its apparent detachment, which in no way implies disengagement from the world - as we have seen - is often interpreted as being in conflict with, or even a threat to, the productivity and result-oriented mindset that dominates our times.

E1.7.D.6.1. VIRGINIA WOOLF (1882-1941)
“That is the whole”

E1.7.D.6.1_REF1_LIT VIRGINIA WOOLF’S VIVID CHILDHOOD MEMORY OF OBSERVING A FLOWER "In the spring of 1939, looking back on her life, Virginia Woolf recounted [...] her most vivid childhood memory, also of a flower, in the garden by the large white house on the Celtic Sea coast where she grew up:
« I was looking at the flower bed by the front door; “That is the whole”, I said. I was looking at a plant with a spread of leaves; and it seemed suddenly plain that the flower itself was a part of the earth; that a ring enclosed what was the flower; and that was the real flower; part earth; part flower. It was a thought I put away as being likely to be very useful to me later. »"
(Maria Popova, The Marginalian)

E1.7.D.6.2. PAUL VALERY (1871-1945)
"Tenderness, the moment when the ego strips itself of everything that clothed it, disguised it, distinguished it from the tiny child that is in each of us, essential and hidden, the germ or pure feeling of living."

E1.7.A.3.3_REF2_PO.wE1.7.D.6.2.

E1.7.D.6.3. JOHN COWPER POWYS (1872-1963)
" I feel that the deepest thing in life is the soul’s individual struggle to reach an exultant peace in relation to more cosmic forces than any social system, just or unjust, can cope with or compass"

E1.7.B.1.1_REF4.2_PH.wE1.7.D.6.3.

E1.7.D.6.3_REF2_LIT "GLEEFUL WONDER AT THE SIMPLE FACT OF BEING ALIVE is an omnipresent theme in Leonardo da Vinci’s work, masterfully embodied in Saint John the Baptist. John Cowper Powys places the painter in a line of thinkers who started roughly in the same period with the preSocratics in the West and Indian and Chinese philosophy in the East : " I feel that the deepest thing in life is the soul’s individual struggle to reach an exultant peace in relation to more cosmic forces than any social system, just or unjust, can cope with or compass. What is wrong with so many clever people to-day is the fatal distrust lodged in their minds—and lodged there by a superstitious awe in the presence of transitory scientific theories—of the power in their own souls. What we need—and the key to it lies in ourselves—is a bold return to the magical view of life. I [mean] that kind of faith in the potentialities of the ego, with which all great poetry and all great philosophy has been concerned. That feeling of exultant liberation from the immediate pressure of practical life, which any “logos” from the arena of Goethe, or Spinoza, or Leonardo, or Plato, or Heraclitus, or Epictetus, or the old Chinese Taoists conveys, is what we need.”
(John Cowper Powys, Autobiography, 1934)

E1.7.D.6.4. BERTRAND RUSSELL (1872-1970)
"Make your interests gradually wider and more impersonal, until bit by bit the walls of the ego recede, and your life becomes increasingly merged in the universal life. An individual human existence should be like a river — small at first, narrowly contained within its banks, and rushing passionately past rocks and over waterfalls. Gradually the river grows wider, the banks recede, the waters flow more quietly, and in the end, without any visible break, they become merged in the sea, and painlessly lose their individual being."
(Bertrand Russell, “How to Grow Old,” Portraits from Memory and Other Essays, 1956)

E1.7.D.6.4_REF1_PO "TO OLD AGE
I see in you the estuary that enlarges and spreads itself grandly as it pours in the great sea."
(Walt Whitman, Leaves of grass, 1855)

E1.7.D.6.5. ROBERT MUSIL (1880-1942)
The characters in Robert Musil's "The Man Without Qualities" contemplate their experiences of awakening, yet choose to disregard them. It's essentially a chronicle of failure.

E1.7.D.6.5.a. " ONE WAS NO LONGER SUBJECT TO ANY OF THE SEPARATIONS THAT CHARACTERIZE HUMANITY [...] all life's problems and incidents took on an incomparable gentleness, tenderness, peace, and at the same time an entirely different meaning from the old one. [...]. Thanks to these silent experiences, everything that makes up ordinary life took on an overwhelming significance, whatever the circumstances."

E1.7.D.6.5.a_REF1_LIT ULRICH, IN A STATE OF CONTEMPLATION AND ONENESS WITH NATURE, FEELS A COMPLETE TRANSFORMATION IN HIS PERCEPTION OF LIFE. The distinctions between beings and objects fade away, creating a new intimacy and clarity. Even the most mundane experience takes on a profound and moving meaning.
"The forgotten, exceedingly important affair with the major’s wife. [...]
The sun drew him out of sleep in the mornings, and when the fishermen were out on the water, the women and children keeping to the houses, then it seemed that he and a donkey grazing among the shrubs and on hillocks between the two little villages on the island were the only higher specimens of life that existed in this quaint outpost
of the world. He followed Brother Ass’s example and climbed up on one of the hillocks or lay down on the shore in the surrounding company of sea, rock and sky. There was no
presumption in this feeling of companionship, for the difference in magnitude disappeared, as also did the difference between mind and nature, animate and inanimate; indeed, every kind of difference between things grew less in such communion. To put it quite soberly, these differences doubtless neither disappeared nor grew less, but they shed their significance and one was ‘no longer subject to any of the separations that are the mark of mankind’, one was in the very condition described by those believers in God who have entered the state of mystic love, those of whom the young cavalry lieutenant at that time knew nothing at all. Nor did he reflect on these  henomena—as another man, like a huntsman on the trail, might try to track an observation down by thinking along after it—indeed, he was probably not even aware of them.
But he was absorbing them. He was sub merging himself in the landscape, although one might equally well say that it was an unutterable state of being buoyed up and borne along; and when the world overbrimmed the threshold of his eyes, its meaning lapped against him from within, in soundless waves. He had drifted into the very heart of the world. From him to the distant beloved was as far as to the next tree. Inscape had linked the living beings where space was no more, as in dreams two living beings can pass through each other without intermingling; and this altered all their relations. Otherwise, however, this state of mind had nothing in common with dream. It was clear, and abounding in clear thoughts. Only nothing in it moved according to cause, purpose and physical desire, but everything went rippling out in circle upon circle, as
when a continuous jet plays upon a pool of water. And precisely this was what he described in his letters; nothing else.

It was an utterly changed form of life. Everything about it was shifted out of the focus of ordinary attention and had lost its sharp outlines. Seen in this way, it was all a little scattered and blurred, and yet manifestly there were still other centres filling it again with delicate certainty and clarity. For all life’s problems and events took on an incomparable mildness, softness and serenity, and at the same time an utterly transformed meaning. If, for instance, a beetle, there, ran past the hand of the man sunk in thought, it was not a coming nearer, a passing by and a dis appearing, and it was not beetle and man; it was a happening ineffably touching the heart, and yet not even a happening but, although it happened, a state. And, aided by such tranquil experiences, everything that generally goes to make up ordinary life was imbued with transforming significance, wherever Ulrich met with it."
(Robert Musil, The man without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.7.D.6.5.b. MUSIL REFLECTS ON PAUL ARNHEIM'S ORIGINAL COSMIC CONSCIOUSNESS EXPERIENCE. Arnheim is portrayed in a state where his soul overflows, leading to a heightened awareness, making real-world events seem distant. Despite this, he continues with his usual life. Musil questions whether this experience is an illusion or a reality that all religions affirm. However, over time, this experience fades and appears to have been a fleeting frenzy.

E1.7.D.6.5.b_REF1_LIT PAUL ARNHEIM, DESPITE HIS ORDINARY, COMMERCIAL BEHAVIOUR, USED TO EXPERIENCE A DEEP CONNECTION BETWEEN HIS INNER AND OUTER WORLDS. This experience, often associated with an original cosmic love, tended to fade with time, giving way to a different, more pragmatic reality that replaced wonder with routine.
"The Merchant King and the Fusion of Soul Trade Interests. Or again, all the paths of the mind start from the soul, but none leads back to it. [...] 
At that moment, even ambition fell silent, the events of the real world were as distant as the sounds heard behind a garden wall, it seemed to him that his soul had overflowed and was finally truly present. [...Even in this exalted state, it is true that the young Paul Arnheim, completely sure of himself, dined in some chic restaurant, frequented the best society, dressed with great care, and did everywhere what there was to do; But one could say that the distance from himself to himself was as great as from himself to beings to objects, that the outer world did not stop at his skin, and that the inner world did not shine only through the window of his reflection, but that these two worlds combined in an undivided presence and absence, as gentle, peaceful, and noble as a dreamless sleep. [...] Arnheim behaved exactly as usual, except that the events seemed to be charged with an elusive meaning: and behind the trembling flame, the inner man remained motionless, watching the outer man who, in front of it, was eating an apple while his tailor took the measurements of a suit.
Was it an illusion, or the shadow of a reality that we will never fully understand? The only answer that can be given is that all religions, at some stage in their evolution, have considered it a reality, as have all lovers, all romantics, and all men who have a special fondness for the moon, for spring, and for the radiant agony of the first days of autumn. But this feeling fades with time; one cannot say whether it evaporates or dries up, but one day one realizes that something else has taken its place, and one forgets it as quickly as one forgets unreal events, dreams and reveries. Since this experience of primordial cosmic love almost always goes hand in hand with first love, we believe that we know, not without relief, what value to attach to it, and we count it among the follies that one may commit only before obtaining the right to vote".
(Robert Musil, The man without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.7.D.6.5.c. "AND NOW, WHAT DO WE DO?"
"While our contemporaries wield the hammer and slide rule during working hours and behave outside them like a horde of kids swept from one extravagance into another under the pressure of "And now, what do we do?" which is basically nothing more than the expression of bitter disgust, they cannot deliver themselves from a persistent, secret voice urging them to conversion. To this voice, they apply the principle of the division of labor by maintaining intellectuals specialized in this kind of presentiment and inner jeremiad: penitents and confessors of their time, absolvers by profession, prophets and Lenten preachers in literature, who are always precious to know are there when one doesn't have the possibility, personally, of living according to their principles."

E1.7.D.6.5.c_REF1_LIT ARNHEIM IS TORN BETWEEN TWO INNER VOICES: ONE PRAISES THE IMPORTANCE OF MONEY, REASON, AND MORALITY, WHILE THE OTHER DEMANDS THAT THESE VALUES BE ABANDONED IN FAVOR OF A MORE SPIRITUAL AND AUTHENTIC EXISTENCE. This duality reflects the contradictions of his era, in which the worship of money and order coexisted with a desire for inner depth. Arnheim, like his contemporaries, is torn between his role as a technician and businessman and his spiritual aspirations, but this division fails at the crucial moment of decision.
"What is the object of modern man's faith? God or the Head of the Universe Corporation? Arnheim hesitated to answer. [...] 
Arnheim is said to have spoken in this way because a deep voice told him clearly that one could no more renounce money than one could renounce reason and morality. But another voice, equally deep, told him no less clearly that one should boldly renounce reason, morality, and all the rationalism of existence. It was precisely in those dizzying moments when he felt no other need than to rush, like a wandering satellite, into Diotima's lonely mass, that this voice almost prevailed. Then the growth of thought seemed as alien to him as that of nails and hair. A moral life seemed to him a dead life, and a hidden aversion to order and morality made him blush. Arnheim was like his own age. The latter worshiped money, order, knowledge, calculation, measurement and weighing, in short, the spirit of money and its family, and at the same time deplored them. While our contemporaries wield hammers and slide rules during working hours and behave outside of them like a horde of children driven from one extravagance to another under the pressure of the question, "And what shall we do now?" which is really nothing more than an expression of bitter disgust, they cannot free themselves from a persistent and secret voice urging them to convert. To this voice they apply the principle of the division of labor, maintaining intellectuals specialized in this kind of premonitions and inner lamentations: penitents and confessors of their time, professional absolvers, prophets and Lenten preachers in literature, who are always valuable to know about when one does not have the opportunity personally to live according to their principles; and it is more or less the same kind of moral ransom represented by the phrases and subsidies that the state swallows every year in bottomless cultural institutions. 
This division of labor could even be found in Arnheim himself. When he was sitting in his director's office checking a profit and loss account, he would have blushed to think otherwise than as a technician and businessman; but as soon as his company's money was no longer at stake, he would have blushed not to think otherwise and not to proclaim that it is necessary to give man the possibility of rising other than through the dead-end paths of regularity, prescriptions, standards, and other such things, the results of which are completely devoid of interiority and profoundly superficial. There is no doubt that this other way is what we call religion. Arnheim had written several books on the subject. In these books, he had also given this other way names: the myth, the return to simplicity, the kingdom of the soul, the spiritualization of the economy, the essence of the deed, and other similar formulas, because the problem had multiple aspects; in fact, it had exactly as many sides as Arnheim discovered when he examined himself with disinterestedness, as a man who has great duties before him must do. But it was obviously his destiny that this division of labor should lead to his downfall at the moment of decision. At the very moment when he felt the need to be as great, as whole as the figures of the earliest ages, as carefree as only true aristocrats can be, as integrally religious as the nature of love requires when it is deeply understood, at the very moment when he wanted to throw himself at Diotima's feet without regard for his trousers or his future, a voice held him back. It was the voice of reason, awakened at the wrong moment, or, as he said to himself with irritation, "the voice of calculation and greed, which today constantly opposes the edification of a noble life, the mystery of feeling".
(Robert Musil, The man without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)
