E1.7.D.6.5.d. ULRICH AND AGATHE DISCUSS MYSTICAL EXPERIENCE AND THE PERCEPTION OF REALITY. Ulrich states that one does not need to be a saint to experience transcendence. He suggests that something as simple as observing a herd of cows can transport someone to another life. In this state, typical perceptions are challenged, and details become familiar and intimate. The emerging relationships between all things are profound yet elusive. Within this state, evil has no place, and every action is driven by the love of this incredible state.

E1.7.D.6.5.d_REF1_LIT ULRICH DESCRIBES A TRANSCENDENTAL EXPERIENCE IN WHICH CONTEMPLATING A HERD OF COWS CAN MAKE ONE FEEL TRANSPORTED TO ANOTHER LIFE, FORGETTING PRACTICAL AND UTILITARIAN CONCERNS. This experience creates a new, intense but elusive relationship with the world, where everything seems intimately connected and where no heinous act can occur. This sense of tranquility and vastness is fragile and tends to dissipate.
"Sacred Conversations. Varied continuation. [...] 
[Ulrich] answered immediately: "You do not have to be a saint to have a similar experience! Just sitting on a felled tree or on a bench in the mountains and looking at a herd of grazing cows can make you feel as if you have suddenly been transported to another life! We forget ourselves and at the same time we find ourselves again: you have already spoken of this yourself. [...]
'Usually a herd is nothing more than grazing cattle in our eyes. Or a picturesque subject in front of a beautiful background. Or we hardly pay attention to it. The herds of cows on the mountain paths are part of the said paths, and one would only understand what one feels at the sight of them if there were a regulatory clock or an investment property in their place. In general, one wonders whether to sit or stand; one complains about the flies buzzing around the herd; one makes sure there isn't a bull in the middle; one wonders where the path leads: countless little intentions, little worries, little calculations, little perceptions that form like the paper on which the image of the herd is painted. We don't think about the paper, we just see the herd on it... - And suddenly the paper tears!
- That's it. Or rather, some habitual tissue in us is torn. So nothing edible is grazed; nothing pictorial; nothing stands in our way. You can't even form the words graze or browse, because that would require a number of practical, utilitarian concepts that you have suddenly lost. What remains on the surface could be described as a ripple of emotion, rising and falling, or breathing and extravagant, as if it filled the entire field of vision without having any precise contours. Of course, in this undulation we still find countless isolated perceptions, colors, horns, movements, smells, everything that is part of reality; but if we still take note of them, we no longer recognize them. I would say that these details are freed from the egoism that drew our attention to them, that they are fraternally and, in the literal sense of the word, intimately connected. Of course, it is not a question of the surface; somehow all things have lost their boundaries and have entered into you.' [...]
'Yes, the moment one escapes from inessential life, all things initiate new mutual relationships," Ulrich added. "One could almost say that there is no longer any relationship between them. For this new relationship is absolutely unknown; we have no experience of it at all, and all the others have been abolished; but this one, in spite of its obscurity, is so obvious that it cannot be denied. It is intense but elusive. Let us put it another way: usually, when we look at something, the gaze is like a stick or a taut thread, at both ends of which the eye leans on one side and the object looked at leans on the other, and one does not know what great weft of this kind is leaning every second; whereas, in this new relationship, there is rather something painfully sweet that keeps the rays of the gaze apart. 
"We no longer possess anything in the world, we no longer cling to anything. We are no longer held by anything," Agathe said. "Everything is like a great tree without a leaf stirring. In this state we cannot do anything bad. 
"It is said that nothing can happen in this state that is not in harmony with it," Ulrich continued. "The desire to surrender to this state is the sole motive, the sole form, the loving determination of every act and thought that occurs within it. It is something infinitely calm and infinitely vast, and everything that happens in it steadily increases its meaning, grows quietly. If it does not increase it, it is evil, but evil cannot happen, because at that moment the silence and clarity are torn apart and the wonderful state dissolves."
(Robert Musil, The man without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.6.C.2.13.c_REF1_PH.wE1.7.D.6.5.d

E1.7.D.6.5.e. ULRICH CLAIMS THE ESTABLISHMENT OF A GENERAL SECRETARIAT OF SOUL AND PRECISION. Ulrich's life is symbolized by two trees: one represents his relentless ardor to influence reality, while the other illustrates the inactive aspect of his being, evident in his belief in the temporary usefulness of his actions. He acknowledges that the conflict between literature and reality uncovers a basic choice between metaphor and the principle of identity. Merging these two thought processes, he suggests, could result in a more advanced humanity.

E1.7.D.6.5.e_REF1_LIT ULRICH IS TORN BETWEEN TWO APPROACHES TO LIFE: A FIERCE QUEST FOR PRECISION AND ACTION ON THE ONE HAND, AND A MORE DREAMY AND INTUITIVE DIMENSION ON THE OTHER. He struggles to reconcile these two aspects, but often finds himself in conflict. He must decide whether to live an ordinary life with attainable goals or to take his seemingly impossible aspirations seriously.
"The two trees of life. Ulrich called for the creation of a General Secretariat of Soul and Precision. [...] 
This need to attack and dominate life had always been very visible in Ulrich, appearing in the form of a rejection of the established order, a shifting aspiration for a new order, a moral or logical demand, or simply the need to keep his body in shape. Everything Ulrich had called over the years existentialism, a sense of possibility, imaginative precision as opposed to pedantic precision; everything he had wished for: that we should invent history, that we should experience the history of ideas instead of universal history, that we should take possession of what we cannot fully realise, and that at last one should live, perhaps not as a human being, but as a character in a book, stripped of all that is unimportant, so that the rest of the world forms a magical unity - all these variations of his thought, which by their extraordinary intransigence made them truly hostile to reality, had in common that they wanted to act on reality with an obvious and implacable eagerness.

More difficult to distinguish, because closer to the realm of shadow and dream, were the elements of the other tree in whose image his life was reflected. At its root, no doubt, was the deep memory of a childhood relationship with the world, of trust and abandonment; and this feeling had been prolonged by the intuition of having once glimpsed an immense land, later reduced to a little soil in the flowerpot where morality cultivates its miserable plants. [...] Perhaps this inactive half of his being had manifested itself above all in the involuntary conviction that the usefulness of the active and restless half was only temporary, a conviction that it projected like a shadow over the other. In everything he undertook (the passions of the body as well as those of the mind), Ulrich had felt himself a prisoner of preparation, always insufficient; with the years, the sense of necessity in his life had been exhausted like the oil in a lamp. His development had obviously split into two paths, one exposed in broad daylight and the other obscure and closed to traffic. The state of moral stagnation in which he found himself and which had oppressed him for a long time, perhaps more than was reasonable, could only have come about because he had never managed to merge these two paths into one. 
Now, remembering that the impossibility of their fusion had recently manifested itself to him in the tension between literature and reality, between comparison and truth, Ulrich suddenly realized that there was infinitely more to it than a simple accidental inspiration arising from those confused, aimless dialogues he had recently had with the interlocutors least able to understand him. No matter how far back in history we go, we find these two fundamental attitudes, one governed by metaphor, the other by the principle of identity. The principle of identity is the law of lucid thought and action; it manifests itself as much in the unassailable conclusion of a line of reasoning as in the brain of a blackmailer who pushes his victim before him step by step; it is a law imposed by the misery of our life, to which we would succumb if relations could not take an unambiguous form. The metaphor, on the other hand, is the way of associating images that reigns in the dream; it is the flexible logic of the soul to which, in the intuitions of art and religion, the kinship of all things corresponds. Ordinary likes and dislikes, approval and disapproval, admiration, subordination, domination, imitation and their opposites, these various relations of man to man and of man to nature, which are not yet and perhaps never will be purely objective, can only be grasped by metaphor. What is called higher humanity is undoubtedly an attempt to fuse together these two great halves of life, metaphor and truth, after they have been carefully separated. But when, in a metaphor, we separate everything that might be true from what is mere froth, we usually gain only a little truth while destroying all the value of the metaphor. This separation may have been inevitable in intellectual evolution, but it has had the same effect as boiling a substance to thicken it: the evaporation of the best of it in the process. Nowadays one sometimes has the strong impression that moral concepts and rules are nothing more than worn-out metaphors around which the unbearable kitchen smells of humanism waft. [...]

The way back [...] 

Ulrich felt that he finally had to make up his mind: either live like everyone else for an attainable goal, or take these 'impossibilities' seriously".
(Robert Musil, The man without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.7.D.6.5.e_REF1.1_LIT  FELIPE PINHO: ISN'T MUSIL PROPOSING A NEW CONCEPT IN 'THE MAN WITHOUT QUALITIES'? Isn't he leading us, through this philosophical fable, to the realization that man does not have to choose between the principle of identity and that of metaphor? None of the characters come to this conclusion on their own, but isn't it the reader's conclusion?
Pierre Schindler: But none of the characters are really happy, because they have all renounced their experiences of awakening by classifying them as futile. Isn't it a chronicle of that kind of failure?
(Conversation on April 10, 2019 with Felipe Pinho while jogging in Parque do Povo, São Paulo, Brazil)

E1.7.D.6.5.f. ULRICH REFLECTS ON FEELINGS, MUSIC, ACTIVITY AND PASSIVITY, AND THEIR RELATIONSHIP TO SPIRITUAL EVOLUTION. He begins to view change and progress as mere descriptors of the fact that human endeavors never reach absolute conviction. Furthermore, he suggests that knowledge may have expelled mankind from its original state, requiring countless experiments to find its way back to wisdom. In essence, Ulrich acknowledges the concepts of Original Sin and the Fall, but interprets them differently from traditional perspectives.

E1.7.D.6.5.f_REF1_LIT "ULRICH REPLIED SHARPLY: « WHEN I THINK OF MY VERY YOUNG AGE, I WOULD SAY THAT THE INSIDE AND THE OUTSIDE WERE THEN HARDLY DISTINCT. When I crawled towards an object, the object flew towards me; when an event looked important to my eyes, I was not the only one to be moved by it: things themselves began to bubble. [...] I did not yet own myself; deep down, "I" was not yet, my personal states were not yet clearly separated from those of the world. It may seem strange, yet it is true to say that my feelings, my inclinations, that I myself was not yet entirely within myself. Even stranger, I could also say that I wasn't quite far from myself yet. Today indeed, as you think you are in full possession of yourself, if you wonder who you are, in the end, you will discover that you always see yourself from the outside, as an object. You will perceive that one occasion makes you sad and another furious, as your coat is sometimes wet, sometimes warm. By observing yourself, with all the attention possible, you will succeed, at most, in ending up behind yourself, never inside. Whatever you do, you stay outside of yourself, except precisely for the rare moments when the others would say about you that you are outside yourself. To compensate, we adults have been able to say "I am" on any occasion, if it pleases us. You see a car and in a way you see at the same time, like a shadow, the sentence: I see a car. You love, or you are sad, and you see that you are. Strictly speaking, however, neither the car, nor your sadness, nor your love, nor you are entirely there. Nothing is there entirely like it was in childhood. Everything you touch, as soon as you have succeeded in being a personality, freezes even within your deep intimacy. All that remains is a thin thread of self-awareness and troubled self-esteem, enveloped in an entirely exterior life. So what is wrong? We have the feeling that something could still be fixed but we don't know what. In fact how can we say that a child has absolutely different experiences from those of an adult? I don't have a definitive answer to these questions, although one or two thoughts come to mind. But it's been a long time since I've responded in my own way: by losing all love for this way of being "someone" and for this kind of world. »"
(Robert Musil, The Man Without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.7.D.6.5.f_REF1.1_LIT ULRICH STRUGGLES WITH INTERNAL CONTRADICTIONS CONCERNING THE PURITY OF FEELINGS AND HUMAN ACTIVITY 
"Where we go from Koniatowski's critique of Danielli's principle to original sin, and from original sin to the emotional mystery of the sister. [...]
The feeling is quite rare. To preserve a certain temperature of feeling from the cold is probably to preserve the heat necessary for incubation, without which there is no spiritual evolution. When a man is torn away from the confusion of intelligent intentions that embarrass him with a thousand foreign objects, when he is placed for a few moments in a state of absolute disinterestedness, for example, when he listens to music, he almost returns to the state of flowers on which rain and sunlight pass. He was about to add that there is an eternity more eternal in the pauses and rest of the human spirit than in its activity; but he had been thinking sometimes of "feeling" and sometimes of "experience," which led to a contradiction. For there were also experiences of the will! There were also experiences of action at its height! No doubt one could admit that each of these experiences, when it reaches its greatest radiance of bitterness, is nothing more than feeling: but the contradiction would then be that the state of feeling, in its greatest purity, would be a rest, a fallout of activity! Or was there no contradiction at all? Was there a marvelous order in which the highest activity, at its center, would be immobility? It seemed that this series of inspirations was less an ulterior motive than an undesirable thought: for Ulrich, in a sudden reaction against the sentimental turn that the considerations in which he had become involved had taken, withdrew them. He had no intention of meditating on certain states, nor, in reflecting on feelings, of falling prey to them himself. [...]
That is why it also seemed to Ulrich that what we call change, or even progress of the times, was only a word to express that no attempt succeeds at the point where they all should unite, on the way to an absolutely total conviction, that is, towards the possibility of continuous development, lasting enjoyment, and that gravity of great beauty which now hardly ever casts a shadow on our lives. [...] When it came down to it, one was not far from thinking that Ulrich believed in the "Fall" and "Original Sin". In other words, he would have readily admitted that at some point an essential change had occurred in human behavior, like when the lover regains his composure: he sees "the whole truth," but something greater has been destroyed, and the truth is now nothing more than a remnant sewn back together as best it can. Perhaps it was indeed the fruit of "knowledge" that had caused this change in the mind, and had driven the human race from its original state, from which it could only find its way back after countless experiences and thanks to the wisdom that sin brings. Ulrich did not believe in these stories as they have been handed down to us, but as he had discovered them: he believed in them like a calculator who, having before his eyes the system of his feelings, concludes from the impossibility of justifying them separately to the necessity of introducing an imaginary hypothesis whose nature can only be sensed".
(Robert Musil, The Man Without Qualities, Volume 1, 1930)

E1.7.D.6.6. PIERRE TEILHARD DE CHARDIN (1881-1955)
The spiritual experiences we have can arise from atoms and molecules. At the same time, some of these experiences, and certainly their very personal and subjective nature, cannot be fully understood in terms of atoms and molecules.

E1.7.D.6.6_REF1_PH PIERRE TEILHARD DE CHARDIN DESCRIBES HIS LIFELONG SPIRITUAL JOURNEY, WHERE AN INNATE SPARK IGNITED A PROFOUND CONNECTION WITH THE UNIVERSE, TRANSFORMING HIS PERCEPTION OF MATTER INTO A DIVINE, LUMINOUS EXPERIENCE. He emphasizes the existence of an essential, higher order that encompasses all spiritual emotions and brings ultimate happiness and clarity.
"I shall try… to show how, starting from the point at which a spark was first struck, a point that was built into me congenitally, the World gradually caught fire for me, burst into flames; how this happened all during my life, and as a result of my whole life, until it formed a great luminous mass, lit from within that surrounded me. [...]
Within every being and every event there was a progressive expansion of a mysterious inner clarity which transfigured them… Crimson gleams of Matter, gliding imperceptibly into the gold of Spirit, ultimately to become transformed into the incandescence of a Universe that is Person… The Diaphany of the Divine at the heart of a glowing Universe, as I have experienced it through contact with the Earth — the Divine radiating from the depths of a blazing Matter. [...]
To be completely at home and completely happy, there must be the knowledge that “Something, essential by nature” exists, to which everything else is no more than an accessory or perhaps an ornament… which it is impossible (once one has experienced it) to confuse with any other spiritual emotion, whether joy in knowledge or discovery, joy in creation or in loving: and this not so much because it is different from all those emotions, but because it belongs to a higher order and contains them all."
(Pierre Teilhard de Chardin,  The Heart of Matter)

E1.7.D.6.6_REF2_PH "I’M A SCIENTIST AND HAVE ALWAYS HAD A SCIENTIFIC VIEW OF THE WORLD — by which I mean that the universe is made of material stuff, and only material stuff, and that stuff is governed by a small number of fundamental laws. Every phenomenon has a cause, which originates in the physical universe. I’m a materialist. Not in the sense of seeking happiness in cars and nice clothes, but in the literal sense of the word: the belief that everything is made out of atoms and molecules, and nothing more. Yet, I have transcendent experiences. I communed with two ospreys that summer in Maine. I have feelings of being part of things larger than myself. I have a sense of connection to other people and to the world of living things, even to the stars. I have a sense of beauty. I have experiences of awe. And I’ve had transporting creative moments. [...]
I believe that the spiritual experiences we have can arise from atoms and molecules. At the same time, some of these experiences, and certainly their very personal and subjective nature, cannot be fully understood in terms of atoms and molecules. I believe in the laws of chemistry and biology and physics — in fact, as a scientist I much admire those laws — but I don’t think they capture, or can capture, the first-person experience of making eye contact with wild animals and similar transcendent moments. Some human experiences are simply not reducible to zeros and ones. [...]

A common feature of all aspects of spirituality is a loss of self, a letting go, a willingness to embrace something outside of ourselves, a willingness to listen rather than talk, a recognition that we are small and the cosmos is large. […]

Most transcendent experiences are completely ego-free. In the moment, we lose track of time and space, we lose track of our bodies, we lose track of our selves. We dissolve. And yet… spirituality emerges from consciousness and the material brain. And the paramount signature of consciousness is a sense of self, an “I-ness” distinct from the rest of the cosmos. Thus, curiously, a thing centered on self creates a thing absent of self. […]

More self, less connection to the larger world."
(Alan Lightman, The Transcendent Brain: Spirituality in the Age of Science, 2023)

E1.7.D.6.6_REF2.1_PH NOT ALL THINGS ARE SUBJECT TO QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS "Although as a child I developed a scientific view of the world, I also understood that not all things were subject to quantitative analysis… I was about nine years old. It was a Sunday afternoon. I was alone in a bedroom of my home in Memphis, Tennessee, gazing out the window at the empty street, listening to the faint sound of a train passing a great distance away. Suddenly I felt that I was looking at myself from outside my body. For a brief few moments, I had the sensation of seeing my entire life, and indeed the life of the entire planet, as a brief flicker in a great chasm of time, with an infinite span of time before my existence and an infinite span of time afterward. My fleeting sensation included infinite space. Without body or mind, I was somehow floating in the gargantuan stretch of space, far beyond the solar system and even the galaxy, space that stretched on and on and on. I felt myself to be a tiny speck, insignificant. A speck in a huge universe that cared nothing about me or any living beings and their little dots of existence — a universe that simply was. And I felt that everything I had experienced in my young life, the joy and the sadness, and everything that I would later experience meant absolutely nothing in the grand scheme of things. It was a realization both liberating and terrifying at once… Despite the dismal feeling that the universe didn’t care a whit about me, I did feel connected to something far larger than myself."
(Alan Lightman, The Transcendent Brain: Spirituality in the Age of Science, 2023)

E1.7.D.6.7. WILLIAM CARLOS WILLIAMS  (1883-1963)
"So much depends
Upon

A red wheel
Barrow

Glazed with rain
Water

Beside the white
Chickens"

E1.7.D.6.7_REF1_PO "I WANTED TO WRITE A POEM THAT YOU COULD UNDERSTAND; what good would it do if I wrote you something that was not intelligible.
                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                   Here it is
can you see how are the little girls who linger playing on the sidewalk in the shadow
                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                    of dusk 
when they should be home 
                                                                                                                    for dinner?

I'm like that too."
(William Carlos Williams)

E1.7.D.6.8. D.H. LAWRENCE (1885-1930)
"The vast marvel is to be alive… The supreme triumph is to be most vividly, most perfectly alive."

E1.7.D.6.8_REF1_LIT "THE VAST MARVEL IS TO BE ALIVE… The supreme triumph is to be most vividly, most perfectly alive. Whatever the unborn and the dead may know, they cannot know the beauty, the marvel of being alive in the flesh. The dead may look after the afterwards. But the magnificent here and now of life in the flesh is ours, and ours alone, and ours only for a time. We ought to dance with rapture that we should be alive and in the flesh, and part of the living, incarnate cosmos. I am part of the sun as my eye is part of me. That I am part of the earth my feet know perfectly, and my blood is part of the sea. My soul knows that I am part of the human race, my soul is an organic part of the great human soul… There is nothing of me that is alone and absolute except my mind, and we shall find that the mind has no existence by itself, it is only the glitter of the sun on the surface of the waters."
(D.H. Lawrence, Apocalypse, 1931)

E1.7.D.6.9. T.S.ELIOT (1888-1965)
“At the still point of turning world. Neither flesh nor fleshness; 
Neither from nor towards; at the still point, there the dance is”

E1.7.D.6.9_REF1_PO "[...] AT THE STILL POINT OF TURNING WORLD. Neither flesh nor fleshness; 
Neither from nor towards; at the still point, there the dance is, 
But neither arrest nor movement. 
And do not call it fixity. 
Where past and future are gathered. Neither movement from nor towards. 
Neither ascent nor decline. Except for the point, the still point, 
There would be no dance, and there is only the dance. 
I can only say, there we have been: but I cannot say where. 
And I cannot say, how long, for that is to place it in time. 
The inner freedom from the practical desire. 
The release from action and suffering, release from the inner 
And the outer compulsion, yet surrounded 
By a grace of sense, a white light still and moving. 
Movement without motion, concentration 
Without elimination, both a new world 
And the old made explicit, understood 
In the completion of its partial ecstasy. 
The resolution of its partial horror [...]."
(T.S. Eliot, The Four Quartets, 1943)

E1.7.D.6.10. LUDWIG WITTGENSTEIN (1889-1951)
"My experience above all others? The best way to describe it would be to say that, when I experience it, I am filled with wonder at the existence of the world".

E1.7.D.6.10.a. THE TRACTATUS ENDS IN THE "MYSTICAL," WHICH CONSISTS OF THREE ELEMENTS: THE FEELING OF EXISTENCE, THE FEELING OF THE LIMITED WHOLE, AND THE FEELING OF THE INEFFABLE. These elements express the impossibility of giving meaning to the world, its existence and its totality from language and from within the world. The "mystical" begins when the indicative use of language provokes a feeling of limitation or totality in us. "Wittgenstein wants to describe the impression of strangeness (which can go as far as ecstasy) that we feel in front of Dasein (the fact that the world is)."

E1.7.D.6.10.a_REF1_PH WITTGENSTEIN HIGHLIGHTS THE IMPOSSIBILITY OF FINDING MEANING WITHIN THE WORLD AND LANGUAGE. Wittgenstein suggests that understanding the meaning of life is an indescribable experience, resonating with Éric Weil's notion that true comprehension transcends philosophical discourse and is only recognized in retrospect.
Here we find Wittgenstein's famous statement: 'Whoever understands me, recognizes my propositions as nonsense.' But the context puts this statement into a new perspective. All these propositions that conclude the Tractatus belong to the fourth kind of use of language: they try to show the inexpressible by their falsity. But insofar as they attempt to show the inexpressible, they appear to be nonsense. Insofar as they have a kind of meaning and truth, one might say, they appear to be nonsense. "By transcending them, we have the right vision of the world. Here we arrive at what Wittgenstein calls the mystical. [...] 
I think that Wittgenstein thinks that the "mystical" begins at the moment when the indicative use of language provokes in us a feeling of limitation or totality, which amounts to the same thing: "The feeling of the world as something limited is the mystical feeling. I think Wittgenstein wants to describe the feeling of strangeness (which can go as far as ecstasy) that we experience in the face of Dasein (the fact that the world is). [...]
In any case, the Tractatus ends in the 'mystical'. This 'mystical' seems to have three components: the sense of existence, the sense of the limited whole, and the sense of the inexpressible, that is, of something beyond language. These three components are in fact three different expressions of the same goal: the impossibility of giving meaning to the world, to its existence and its totality, from within the world and language. Wittgenstein tells us nothing more. Was his intuition of the meaninglessness of the world connected with the feeling of an inexpressible presence? Is that why he says that he who has discovered the meaning of life cannot say what it consists of (6.521)? Éric Weil seems to express a similar experience when he writes

'The man who lives in feeling and in the present, the man when he is there rather than when he lives there, has no need of philosophy, because he has no need. As soon as he is no longer there, he speaks of a "poetic moment," of a "mystical enlightenment," of a "flash," of an "eruption," or of an "irruption," and when he remembers his "inspired" language, his tat tvam asi, his cry in which the One is revealed, the symbols in which the unity of all life and all death appears, the love so penetrating that it is the light that devours, he knows that he has understood, that there has been understanding, the disappearance of all exteriority, of all strangeness, but he only knows this and no longer realizes that he has understood (1)'.

Note : (1) Weil, Logic of Philosophy. 
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.10.a_REF2_PH LUDWIG WITTGENSTEIN AFFIRMS THAT SKEPTICISM IS SENSELESS WHEN IT DOUBTS WHERE A QUESTION CANNOT BE ASKED. He stresses that the problems of life are not solved by scientific answers. He mentions the existence of the inexpressible, which manifests itself as the mystical, and concludes that the propositions of language must be transcended in order to obtain a correct vision of the world.
"Scepticism is not irrefutable, but palpably senseless, if it would doubt where a question cannot be asked. For doubt can only exist where there is a question; a question
only where there is an answer, and this only where something can be said. (6.51).
We feel that even if all possible scientic questions be answered, the problems of life have still not been touched at all. Of course there is then no question left, and just this is the answer. (6.52).
The solution of the problem of life is seen in the vanishing of this problem.
(Is not this the reason why men to whom after long doubting the sense of life became clear, could not then say wherein this sense consisted?) (6.521). 
There is indeed the inexpressible. This shows itself; it is the mystical. (6.522). 
The right method of philosophy would be this. To say nothing except what can be said, i.e. the propositions of natural science, i.e. something that has nothing to do with philosophy: and then always, when someone else wished to say something metaphysical, to demonstrate to him that he had given no meaning to certain signs in his ropositions. This method would be unsatisfying to the otherhe would not have the feeling that we were teaching him philosophybut it would be the only strictly correct method. My propositions are elucidatory in this way: he who understands me nally recognizes them as senseless, when he has climbed out through them, on them, over them. (He must so to speak throw away the ladder, after he has climbed up on it.) He must surmount these propositions; then he sees the world rightly. (6.54). 
Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent. (7)."
(Ludwig Wittgenstein, Tractatus philosophicus, 1921)

E1.7.D.6.10.a_REF3_PH “GOOD SNOWFLAKES. THEY DON’T FALL ANYWHERE ELSE.
—LAYMAN PANG (740–808)

Meditation originates and culminates in the everyday sublime. I have little interest in achieving states of sustained concentration in which the sensory richness of experience is replaced by pure introspective rapture. I have no interest in reciting mantras, visualizing Buddhas or mandalas, gaining out-of-body experiences, reading other people’s thoughts, practicing lucid dreaming, or channeling psychic energies through chakras, let alone letting my consciousness be absorbed in the transcendent perfection of the Unconditioned. Meditation is about embracing what is happening to this organism as it touches its environment in this moment. I do not reject the experience of the mystical. I reject only the view that the mystical is concealed behind what is merely apparent, that it is anything other than what is occurring in time and space right now. The mystical does not transcend the world but saturates it. “The mystical is not how the world is,” noted Ludwig Wittgenstein in 1921, “but that it is.”
As understood by Edmund Burke and the Romantic poets, the sublime exceeds our capacity for representation. The world is excessive: every blade of grass, every ray of sun, every falling leaf is excessive. None of these things can be adequately captured in concepts, images, or words. They overreach us, spilling beyond the boundaries of thought. Their sublimity brings the thinking, calculating mind to a stop, leaving one speechless, overwhelmed with either wonder or terror. Yet for we human animals who delight and revel in our place, who crave security, certainty, and consolation, the sublime is banished and forgotten. As a result, life is rendered opaque and flat. Each day is reduced to the repetition of familiar actions and events, which are blandly comforting but devoid of an intensity we both yearn for and fear.
To experience the everyday sublime requires that we dismantle the perceptual conditioning that insists on seeing ourselves and the world as essentially comfortable, permanent, solid, and “mine.” It means to embrace suffering and conflict rather than to shy away from them, to cultivate the embodied attention that contemplates the tragic, changing, empty, and impersonal dimensions of life, rather than succumbing to fantasies of self-glorification or self-loathing. This takes time. It is a lifelong practice.
The everyday sublime is our ordinary life experienced from the perspective of the fourfold task. As we have seen, this entails (1) an openhearted embrace of the totality of one’s existential situation, (2) a letting go of the habitual reactive patterns of thought and behavior triggered by that situation, (3) a conscious valorization of those moments in which such reactive patterns have stilled, and (4) a commitment to a way of life that emerges from such stillness and responds empathetically, ethically, and creatively to the situation at hand.
Understood in this way, meditation is not about gaining proficiency in technical procedures claimed to guarantee attainments that correspond to the dogmas of a particular religious orthodoxy. Nor is its goal to achieve a privileged, transcendent insight into the ultimate nature of reality, mind, or God. In the light of the fourfold task, meditation is the ongoing cultivation of a sensibility, a way of attending to every aspect of experience within a framework of ethical values and goals.
Over the course of history monks and yogins in Buddhist cultures have developed spiritual technologies to a high degree, resulting in levels of mental refinement, control, and absorption that may seem incredible to modern Westerners. Yet from a dharmic perspective the value of these attainments lies not in their being humanly possible but in their contribution to the practice of the fourfold task. It is not hard to imagine being highly accomplished in certain meditative techniques, yet still failing to embrace wholeheartedly the condition of dukkha that pervades the life of oneself and others, still failing to let go of self-centered reactions to dukkha, still failing to behold the stopping of such reactivity, and still failing to cultivate a radically different way of being in this world.
As a sensibility, meditation enables us to cultivate an understanding of moment-to-moment experience much as we develop an appreciation of art or poetry or nature. Grounded in the body and the senses, we value an open-mindedness to what is unfamiliar, probe our sensorium with relentless curiosity, listen attentively to what others have to say, are willing to suspend habitual attitudes and opinions, and question what is going on instead of simply taking things for granted. The disengagement of meditation is not an aloof regard (or disregard) but a perspective that engenders another kind of response to what is happening. And it begins with the breath, our primordial relationship to the fabric of the world in which we are embedded.”
(Stephen Batchelor, After Buddhism, rethinking the dharma for a secular age, 2016)

E1.7.D.6.10.a_REF4_PO CZESLAW MILOSZ SHARES THE WISDOM AND CLARITY HE REACHED AT THE VERY END OF HIS LIFE, NEAR HIS "NINETIETH YEAR."
“LATE RIPENESS
Not soon, as late as the approach of my ninetieth year,
I felt a door opening in me and I entered
the clarity of early morning.
One after another my former lives were departing,
like ships, together with their sorrow.
And the countries, cities, gardens, the bays of seas
assigned to my brush came closer,
ready now to be described better than they were before.
I was not separated from people,
grief and pity joined us.
We forget - I kept saying - that we are all children of the King.
For where we come from there is no division
into Yes and No, into is, was, and will be.
We were miserable, we used no more than a hundredth part of the gift we received for our long journey.
Moments from yesterday and from centuries ago -
a sword blow, the painting of eyelashes before a mirror
of polished metal, a lethal musket shot, a caravel
staving its hull against a reef - they dwell in us,
waiting for a fulfillment.
I knew, always, that I would be a worker in the vineyard,
as are all men and women living at the same time,
whether they are aware of it or not.”
(Czeslaw Milosz)

E1.7.D.6.10.b. "THE 'JUST VISION OF THE WORLD' CONSISTS IN ACHIEVING A NAÏVE CONTEMPLATION OF REALITY CLEAR OF PREJUDGEMENTS, liberated from pseudo-concepts and pseudo-problems. The liberation entails an analysis of language, which aids in rectifying its misuse. The philosopher's wisdom is an ineffable experience, a perspective of perceiving things devoid of hollow concepts. This critique of language leads to the unrepresentable and inexpressible, aligning with a realm beyond the world and language, a form of mysticism or ecstasy.”

E1.7.D.6.10.b_REF1_PH PHILOSOPHY, ACCORDING TO WITTGENSTEIN, IS LIKENED TO SELF-THERAPY OR PSYCHOANALYSIS OF LANGUAGE, LEADING TO INEXPRESSIBLE WISDOM. It culminates in a mysticism in which the inexpressible is revealed, suggesting an ecstasy beyond the limits of language and the world.
"Obviously," the reader will have thought, "if the propositions about objects, facts, propositions, etc., are nonsense because they sin against logical symbolism, then almost all the propositions of the Tractatus are such nonsense. One might say that philosophical activity consists in discovering one's own nonsense, in recognizing oneself as an illegitimate user of language. But the paradox remains. One wonders how one can understand these propositions of the Tractatus if, in the end, they make no sense. But the audacity of the paradox should make us think. 
What will be "this just vision of the world" of which Wittgenstein speaks? The result of going beyond philosophical propositions can only be a naive view of reality. This naive view can only be achieved after liberation from all prejudices, all pseudo-concepts, all pseudo-problems. The misuse of language has separated man from the world. In a kind of self-therapy, philosophy cures itself, through language, of this misuse of language. A kind of psychoanalysis is at work here. And once "analyzed", the philosopher is no longer a philosopher, he is no longer "in search of wisdom", he is "wise". His wisdom is a pure incommunicable experience, a way of looking at things that cannot be expressed. The critique of language eliminates the emptiness of concepts in favor of the fullness of lived existence. [...]
Wittgenstein's positivism thus leads to mysticism: 'There is no doubt that there is something inexpressible; it shows itself; that is the 'mystical' (6.522). Wittgenstein is desperately succinct here. We are reduced to interpreting as best we can. If we are in language, as we are in the world, the inexpressible corresponds to something beyond the limits of language and the limits of the world. The unrepresentable and the inexpressible that we encountered in logic gave us a glimpse of this beyond. The 'mystic' that Wittgenstein speaks of seems to correspond to an ecstasy in which we "see" a world beyond the world and language, its meaning, its significance, the fact of its existence. [...]"
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.10.b_REF2_MU ON THE DIFFICULTY OF ACKNOWLEDGING REALITY

Schubert Fantasie in F minor D940

Listen to it on YouTube: https://youtu.be/9_KYkpu1cBQ?feature=shared

E1.7.D.6.10.c. WHAT IS EXPRESSED IN LANGUAGE, WE CANNOT EXPRESS BY LANGUAGE
"It seems to me that the whole Tractatus can be summed up in this extraordinarily concise formula: 'What is expressed in language, we cannot express by language (4.121).' No one has ever expressed so clearly and so profoundly what must be called the mystery of language, which is identical to the mystery of the world."

E1.7.D.6.10.c_REF1_PH WITTGENSTEIN AFFIRMS THAT WHAT IS EXPRESSED IN LANGUAGE CANNOT BE EXPRESSED BY LANGUAGE ITSELF, THUS EMPHASIZING THE MYSTERY OF LANGUAGE AND THE WORLD. Philosophy must critique this intrinsic limitation.
"It seems to me that the whole of the Tractatus can be summed up in this extraordinarily succinct formula: 'What is expressed in language, we cannot express in language' (4.121). No one has ever expressed so clearly and profoundly what must be called the mystery of language, which is identical with the mystery of the world. This formula governs both Wittgenstein's logical symbolism and his "mysticism. In symbolism, we cannot express through signs what is shown in the signs themselves. We cannot represent the unrepresentable. And it is in this sense of the critique of language that philosophy must be. As for "mysticism," it corresponds to the following idea: the inexpressible is shown in language; the essence of language is to aim at the inexpressible by expressing the expressible; we cannot express in language what we see; the ultimate meaning of language cannot be expressed in language. No doubt we are in language; this situation is insurmountable. But it is precisely in this insurmountable situation that transcendence reveals itself to us. [...]"
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.10.d. "THE ASPECTS OF THINGS THAT FOR US ARE MOST IMPORTANT ARE HIDDEN FROM US BECAUSE THEY ARE SIMPLE AND "ORDINARY", (We can't notice them - because they're always right in front of our eyes.) The authentic foundations of man's search don't astonish him. They have only astonished him once. - This means: what, once recognized, is most astonishing and extraordinary, does not astonish us [anymore].”

E1.7.D.6.10.d_REF1_PH MARVELING AT SIMPLE, DAILY THINGS IS A KEY THEME FOR WITTGENSTEIN, who believed that the most important aspects of reality often escape us because they are familiar. 
"It is what is simplest and most familiar that should fill us with wonder: instead of the false glory of Solomon, we should prefer the splendor of the lilies of the field:
The aspects of things that are most important to us are hidden from us because they are simple and "everyday" (we cannot notice them because they are always in front of us). The authentic foundations of his research do not surprise man. Only once did it surprise him. - This means that what, once recognized, is most astonishing and extraordinary, does not astonish us (§ 129) We are certainly touching here on the deepest concerns of Wittgenstein, for whom wonder at reality was always a fundamental feeling:
"My experience par excellence? The best way to describe it would be to say that when I experience it, I am filled with wonder at the existence of the world".
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.10.d_REF1.1_PH "THERE IS NO PROBLEM. EVERYTHING is open before us, there is nothing to explain (§ 126). Wittgenstein's new philosophy would have nothing to do if it were not for traditional philosophies which, by their aberrant use of language, conceal the true substance of that language. [...]"
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.10.d_REF2_PH WITTGENSTEIN'S INSPIRATION AND GOAL REMAIN IDENTICAL THROUGHOUT HIS WORK: TO PROPOSE A CURE FOR PHILOSOPHY, WHICH HE REGARDED AS A DISEASE OF LANGUAGE. "Compared to the Tractatus logico-philosophicus, first published in 1921-1922, [...] the Philosophical Investigations, which constitute Wittgenstein's second major work, mark a profound evolution, and the author himself emphasizes this point in his preface: in his view, the second book constitutes a correction of the errors of the first. 
From one to the other, however, Wittgenstein's inspiration and purpose remained the same. A therapy is proposed to us. Philosophy is a disease of language; philosophical problems arise when "language takes a vacation" (§ 38). True philosophy, therefore, will consist in curing itself of philosophy, in making every philosophical problem disappear completely and definitively [...] 
Wittgenstein thus continues to devote himself to the same mission: to bring a radical and definitive peace to metaphysical anxiety."
(Pierre Hadot, Wittgenstein and the Limits of Language, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.11. MARTIN HEIDEGGER (1889-1976)
"We are truly Human only insofar as we are in the manifestation of Being".

E1.7.D.6.11_REF1_PH THE THOUGHT OF MARTIN HEIDEGGER SHOWS A STRIKING SIMILARITY TO THE BUDDHISM OF THE GREAT VEHICLE, SUGGESTING THAT IN THE 20th CENTURY, THE WESTERN WORLD MIGHT BE READY TO RECEIVE THIS MESSAGE, IGNORED FOR 2500 YEARS
"The question of Being and the unfolding of this question presuppose an interpretation of Dasein, that is, of the being of man. The idea underlying my thinking is precisely that Being, or the power of the manifestation of Being, needs man; and that, conversely, man is man only insofar as he is in the manifestation of Being. This should settle the question of the extent to which I am concerned only with Being and forget man. One cannot ask the question of being without asking the question of man's being," Martin Heidegger in 1969, illustrating the similarity of phenomenological thought to that of Great Vehicle Buddhism; replace "being" with "awakening," and the similarity is disturbing. Perhaps it is a question of time: from the 20th century onwards, the West may be ready to receive the message of Buddhism - whereas Victor Hugo, who had access to haiku, found it only "cute"."
(Alexis Lavis on the program Les Chemins de la Philosophie, broadcast on 03/14/2019 on France Culture)

Listen to the podcast in French:
https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/les-chemins-de-la-philosophie/l-avenir-appartient-a-ceux-qui-s-eveillent-tot-7763349

E1.7.D.6.12. KRISHNAMURTI (1895-1986)
"DO YOU REALIZE THAT THE MIND IS ONLY CAPABLE OF EXPERIENCING WHAT IT ALREADY KNOWS? THE MIND IS COMPLETELY INCAPABLE OF EXPERIENCING THE INEFFABLE."

E1.7.B.2.3.f.iv_REF1_PH.wE1.7.D.6.12.

E1.7.D.6.13. JACQUES LACAN (1901-1981)
"Between the real and the subject who formulates it, the relation is conflictual, each of the two terms opposing a resistance to the affirmation of the other"

E1.7.D.6.13_REF1_PH "BETWEEN REALITY AND THE SUBJECT THAT EXPRESSES IT, THE RELATIONSHIP IS CONFLICTIVE, with each of the two terms resisting the affirmation of the other. Thus, when the subject establishes itself as the master and possessor of the "real" - through measurement, surveying, or conquest - the real itself is dissolved in the symbolic, since it is then replaced by the words that designate it, the coordinates that delimit it, the maps that orient it. Conversely, when reality appears in its majesty, in the fullness of its manifestation, it is the subject that is annihilated by the power of its appearance. The subject or the object, the thought or the thing: you can't serve these two masters at the same time. The balance of appropriateness is a balance of terror, therefore infinitely improbable, and if one is the master - the real or the subject - the other will necessarily be domesticated or enslaved - the subject or the real. What is a subject who falls into the servitude of the Real, who succumbs to its revelation? It is a subject that loses its right to any subjectivity - what Lacan writes in his algebra $, which reads "barred subject" - that is, what remains of the subject when it loses thought, language and consciousness, which is not much, a residue, a "being there," a being that knows neither who it is nor what it should do. Faced with the emergence of reality, the subject falls into catalepsy, like certain animals that, unable to escape from a predator, freeze and play dead, as if anticipating in their imagination an imminent and very real death. When the Real appears, it is the subject that becomes the object; and conversely, when it is the subject that dictates the law, it is the Real that becomes the object that submits to the language that defines it. In this sense, the Real is an irrefutable presence, since the subject, who is its witness, not to say its martyr, is fascinated by this appearance, loses all its faculties and is therefore no longer able to refute anything. In this sense, for Lacan, the Real plays a role similar to that of death for Epicurus: when it is there, the sage said, I am no longer there, and when I am there, it has not yet come. Because of this analogy, the relationship of alternate occlusion, which applies to the subject and the real as well as to life and death, is not unrelated to the death instinct, which Lacan, unlike Freud, relates to the reality principle, that is, to the experience of the real. For Lacan, this principle cannot be reduced to the only adaptive mechanism that regulates, by trial and error, the homeostasis of living beings and their environment, of the subject and his world; on the contrary, it is necessary to posit an insurmountable maladjustment or inadequacy of the human subject - due to its prematurity - in its relationship to the real, because when the real shows itself in the fullness of its presence, the subject, disarmed, is immediately subjected. [...]

For Lacan, the relationship between the subject and the real is rather one of passage to the limit, and this is precisely why he can say that the real is "at the limit of our experience" and not beyond it: just as it should be said that the subject does not die, but experiences itself as dying, so it must be said that the subject experiences itself as fainting when it is exposed to the violence of the real, it feels itself disappearing according to the degree of intensity of the appearance of the real. Fear is the affect caused by this passage to the limit of being, to nothingness. The subject, then, is not a substance that establishes its own existence - based on the Cartesian model of "I think, therefore I am" - but the alienated term of an asymmetrical dipole, of which the Real is the other term. To explain this, Lacan draws on a notion he borrows from Ernest Jones, the subject's aphanisis, a Greek term meaning repression, disappearance, rather than fainting. Jones introduced this term in a 1927 article on female sexuality to denote a state in which sexual desire is abolished, a state long before the castration anxiety that intervenes in the unfolding of the Oedipus complex. But while for Jones aphanisis is an object of revulsion - fear of the disappearance of desire - for Lacan it is, conversely, a refuge for the subject terrorized by the advent of the real. Like the animal playing dead, the subject confronted with the real mimics its own disappearance by passing into unconsciousness. Lacan also speaks of the "fading" of the subject, from the English "to fade," to vanish, to fade away. This relationship of alternate disappearance - depending on whether one of the two terms, subject or real, dominates the other, real or subject - is still the one that, according to Lacan, defines fantasy, that is, the object of desire, which, in Lacanian algebra, is written: "$ ◇ a": "We symbolize the result, the limit, the end of that which constitutes the question of the subject by the symbol S crossed out in the presence of the small a ($ ◇ a), and we call it the fantasy." Common sense will object that this is a contradiction, since it would simultaneously state that the real is the object of terror and desire, of repulsion and attraction. This would be to misunderstand the ambivalence of desire - as well as its very act: transgression, crossing the line. For this reason, desire is necessarily divided between horror and terror, between lust and alarm, with an intensity that increases the closer it comes to its goal".
(Jacques Darriulat, Lacan, Listening to Madness, III- The Real, 2019)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Lacan/Ecouterlafolie/Ecouterlafolie3.html

E1.7.D.6.14. LOREN EISELEY (1907-1977)
"The time has to be right; one has to be, by chance or intention, upon the border of two worlds. And sometimes these two borders may shift or interpenetrate and one sees the miraculous."

E1.7.D.6.14_REF1_LIT LOREN EISELEY REFLECTS ON THE CONTINUITY OF LIFE AND THE TRANSFORMATION OF THE EARTH'S ELEMENTS OVER MILLIONS OF YEARS AS HE OBSERVES A FLOCK OF BIRDS MIGRATING AT SUNSET. The scene evokes a sense of wonder and the miraculous, highlighting the interconnectedness of life and the passage of time.
"The time has to be right; one has to be, by chance or intention, upon the border of two worlds. And sometimes these two borders may shift or interpenetrate and one sees the miraculous. [...]

It was a late hour on a cold, wind-bitten autumn day when I climbed a great hill spined like a dinosaur’s back and tried to take my bearings. The tumbled waste fell away in waves in all directions. Blue air was darkening into purple along the bases of the hills. I shifted my knapsack, heavy with the petrified bones of long-vanished creatures, and studied my compass. I wanted to be out of there by nightfall, and already the sun was going sullenly down in the west.

It was then that I saw the flight coming on. It was moving like a little close-knit body of black specks that danced and darted and closed again. It was pouring from the north and heading toward me with the undeviating relentlessness of a compass needle. It streamed through the shadows rising out of monstrous gorges. It rushed over towering pinnacles in the red light of the sun or momentarily sank from sight within their shade. Across that desert of eroding clay and wind-worn stone they came with a faint wild twittering that filled all the air about me as those tiny living bullets hurtled past into the night. [...]

It may not strike you as a marvel. It would not, perhaps, unless you stood in the middle of a dead world at sunset, but that was where I stood. Fifty million years lay under my feet, fifty million years of bellowing monsters moving in a green world now gone so utterly that its very light was traveling on the farther edge of space. The chemicals of all that vanished age lay about me in the ground. Around me still lay the shearing molars of dead titanotheres, the delicate sabers of soft-stepping cats, the hollow sockets that had held the eyes of many a strange, outmoded beast. Those eyes had looked out upon a world as real as ours; dark, savage brains had roamed and roared their challenges into the steaming night.

Now they were still here, or, put it as you will, the chemicals that made them were here about me in the ground. The carbon that had driven them ran blackly in the eroding stone. The stain of iron was in the clays. The iron did not remember the blood it had once moved within, the phosphorus had forgot the savage brain. The little individual moment had ebbed from all those strange combinations of chemicals as it would ebb from our living bodies into the sinks and runnels of oncoming time. [...]

I had lifted up a fistful of that ground. I held it while that wild flight of south-bound warblers hurtled over me into the oncoming dark. There went phosphorus, there went iron, there went carbon, there beat the calcium in those hurrying wings. Alone on a dead planet I watched that incredible miracle speeding past. It ran by some true compass over field and waste land. It cried its individual ecstasies into the air until the gullies rang. It swerved like a single body, it knew itself, and, lonely, it bunched close in the racing darkness, its individual entities feeling about them the rising night. And so, crying to each other their identity, they passed away out of my view.

I dropped my fistful of earth. I heard it roll inanimate back into the gully at the base of the hill: iron, carbon, the chemicals of life. Like men from those wild tribes who had haunted these hills before me seeking visions, I made my sign to the great darkness. It was not a mocking sign, and I was not mocked. As I walked into my camp late that night, one man, rousing from his blankets beside the fire, asked sleepily, “What did you see?”

“I think, a miracle,” I said softly, but I said it to myself. Behind me that vast waste began to glow under the rising moon."
(Loren Eiseley, The Judgment of the Birds published in The Star Thrower, 1957)

E1.7.D.6.15. RENÉ CHAR (1907-1988)
"I was the equal of things whose secret held under the ray of a wing. For most of us, the essential is never born, and those who possess it cannot exchange it without harming themselves. No one is willing to lose what he has conquered at the apex of his effort! Otherwise it would be youth and grace, source and delta would have the same purity. "

E1.7.D.6.15_REF1_PO "SEMI-DARKNESS
I was in one of those forests where the sun has no access but where, at night, the stars penetrate. This place was only allowed to exist, because the states' inquisition had forgotten it. The abandoned trails showed me their contempt. I had lost the compulsion to punish. In places, the memory of a force caressed the peasant fugue of the grass. I governed myself without doctrine, with a serene vehemence. I was the equal of things whose secret held under the ray of a wing. For most of us, the essential is never born, and those who possess it cannot exchange it without harming themselves. No one is willing to lose what he has conquered at the apex of his effort! Otherwise it would be youth and grace, source and delta would have the same purity. 

I was in one of those forests where the sun has no access but where, at night, the stars penetrate for implacable hostilities."
(René Char, The loyal adversaries)

E1.7.D.6.18. EUGÈNE IONESCO (1909-1994)
"This force seemed to have been hidden under the mask of things, and here it was bursting out, exploding."

E1.7.D.6.18_REF1_LIT “THIS FORCE SEEMED TO HAVE BEEN HIDDEN UNDER THE MASK OF THINGS, and now it burst forth, exploded. A single light: things had been the particular events of this light, the object-events in which it was particularized. [...] I had had the revelation of the essential; the rest was inessential.”

E1.7.D.6.18_REF2_LIT "I TOLD MYSELF THAT THIS WAS THE 'TRUTH' WITHOUT KNOWING HOW TO DEFINE THIS TRUTH. No doubt, if I had tried to define it, it would have vanished. [...] 
I told myself again that if this event had happened, and since I had experienced it, since I knew everything, I would never be unhappy again, because I had learned that one does not die: I only had to remember those moments to overcome all future worries and fears. I had the revelation of the essential, the rest was unimportant. [I wonder if this unusual feeling, this astonishment without an answer and almost without a question, is not a reaction of my deepest conscience. In truth, I don't ask: I know that this astonishment is my most authentic conscience. I know that the answer is precisely the impossibility of giving one. The non-answer is the best answer."
(Eugene Ionesco, Fragments of a Journal, 1967)

E1.7.D.6.19. ALBERT CAMUS (1913-1960)
Albert Camus' works "Noces à Tipasa" and "Retour à Tipasa" draw a contrast between the vibrancy of life in an earthly paradise and the aftermath of war. In the former, he celebrates the self-sufficiency of life, while in the latter, he documents his struggle to rekindle this sentiment after experiencing the savagery of war. Ultimately, he discovers a bond with nature that unveils a tragic understanding central to existence, a theme he continually explores in his writing.

E1.7.D.6.19.a. LIFE IS SELF-SUFFICIENT. THERE'S NO NEED TO SEARCH FOR A MEANING TO EXISTENCE, FOR IT IS FULL AND COMPLETE.
In "Noces à Tipasa", Albert Camus shares an experience that contrasts with Roquentin's metaphysical disgust in "La Nausée". In this tribute to a breathtaking landscape, he celebrates the fullness of life in an earthly paradise, where the gods are in the flowers and scenery. Here, life is self-sufficient. There's no need to search for a meaning to existence because it is already full and complete.

E1.7.B.1.3.d.ii_REF1_PH.wE1.7.D.6.19.a.

E1.7.D.6.19.b. CAMUS' COMMUNION WITH NATURE OPENED HIM UP TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF A TRAGIC KNOWLEDGE, WHICH LIES AT THE VERY HEART OF BEING, AND WHICH ENABLES US TO LIVE THE ENIGMA OF EXISTENCE IN THE LIGHT.
In 1952, Albert Camus wrote "Retour à Tipasa" (Return to Tipasa), chronicling his return to the same place two decades later. He had hoped to rediscover the freedom he had known back then, but the barbarity of war had tainted the world, and his first attempt was a failure. However, on his second attempt, Camus once again felt a communion with nature that melted away years of fury and darkness. This communion opened him up to the understanding of a tragic knowledge that lies at the very heart of Being, and enables us to live the Enigma of existence in the light. Camus spent his life attempting to articulate this elusive wisdom through his writing.

E1.7.B.1.3.d.iii_REF1_PH.wE1.7.D.6.19.b.

E1.7.D.6.19.b_REF2_PH CAMUS CELEBRATES THE FUSION OF LIFE AND DEATH, RUINS AND THE SUN, IN AN ODE TO THE EARTH AND THE UNBEARABLE PRESENCE OF NATURE. His visits to Tipasa symbolize the search for aesthetic purity and eternity rediscovered in the present, despite disappointments and obstacles.
"One could not end this long variation on the Nietzschean maxim without quoting the magnificent text of an inspired young man of twenty-three, who often said he was close to Nietzsche, and who, in a few sun-drenched pages, went straight to the heart of this imperative of the present that gives the maxim of authenticity its power. Camus drafted the text of Noces à Tipasa in 1936 and completed it the following year. Tipasa is a village west of Algiers, where Roman ruins rise among the flowers, closed on the land side by the high Chenoua massif, open to the boundless sea, overlooked by the ruins of the ancient city. This text is a prose poem, a kind of pagan ode to the glory of the earth and its unbearable presence. A marriage of life and death, of ruins and the sun. The evening epiphany of Silvaplana's ecstasy becomes here the incandescent embrace of desire with the world, the barbaric marriage of mortal man with the earth, the sun and the sea: "What hours I spent crushing absinthes, caressing ruins, trying to match my breathing to the tumultuous sighs of the world! Immersed in the wild scents and the concerts of sleepy insects, I open my eyes and my heart to the unbearable grandeur of this warm sky. It is not so easy to become who we are, to find our true measure. But as I gazed at the Chenoua Ridge, my heart was soothed by a strange certainty. I was learning to breathe, I was integrating, and I was fulfilling. I climbed one hill after another, each one holding a reward for me, like that temple whose columns measure the course of the sun and from which you can see the whole village, its white and pink walls and its green verandas" (Pléiade, Essays). It may well be that Camus only became a writer in order to rediscover (which he undoubtedly never managed to do) this poetry of pure immanence, this aesthetic enthusiasm that literally burns these few pages: "Once you have had the chance of a powerful love, life is spent again in search of that fervor and that light" (Return to Tipasa). In this "Fauvist" assumption of presence, in this carnal celebration of immanence, whose "kingdom" is entirely of this world, aesthetic intentionality is taken to its limit, to its critical reversal, on the threshold of the abolition of the phenomenon in the abstract vibration of pure light. The discovery of paradise on earth brings the aesthetic quest to an end. After the war, the author of The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and The Rebel (1951) returned to Tipasa in a kind of pilgrimage to the source that would give him a second life ("Return to Tipasa", in L'Eté, 1954). He returned twice. The first visit was disappointing: it was raining that morning and "the ruins were surrounded by barbed wire, which could only be crossed at authorized points. For reasons apparently approved by morality, it was also forbidden to go there at night; during the day one would meet a sworn guard. By chance, no doubt, it rained that morning over the entire area of the ruins" (Pléiade, 870). But a few days later, after a melancholy return to Algiers, when the rain stopped and the sun came out, "I set out again for Tipasa" (872). And then occurred the miracle of the eternal return and the reunion of eternity in the heart of time, eternity that is the sea that has gone with the sun. In that moment, as if time had stopped, the future and the past merge in the blaze of the present, and life finally becomes what it is: "I finally cross the barbed wire and find myself among the ruins. And in the glorious December light, as happens only once or twice in a life that can consider itself fulfilled after such moments, I found exactly what I had been looking for, and what, despite the weather and the world, had been offered to me, really only to me, in this deserted natural setting. From the olive grove plateau, I could see the village below. Not a sound came from it: only light smoke rose into the clear air. The sea, too, was silent, as if suffocated by the uninterrupted shower of a sparkling, cold light. A distant rooster crow from Chenoua was the only celebration of the fragile glory of dawn. On the side of the ruins, as far as the eye could see, there were only hail- and wormwood-strewn stones, trees, and columns, perfect in the transparency of the crystalline air. It seemed as if the morning had stood still, the sun stopped for an incalculable moment. In this light and silence, years of anger and night slowly melted away. I listened to an almost forgotten sound within me, as if my heart, which had stopped beating long ago, was slowly beginning to beat again" (872-873)."
(Jacques Darriulat, "Become what you are", 2007-2008)

Read the full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Nietzsche/Imperatifpresent/Imperatifpresent1.html

E1.7.D.6.19.b_REF3_PO “EARTH AGAIN
They are incomprehensible, the things of this earth.
The lure of waters. The lure of fruits.
Lure of two breasts and the long hair of a maiden.
In rouge, in vermillion, in that color of ponds
Found only in the Green Lakes near Wilno.
An ungraspable multitudes swarm, come together
In the crinkles of tree bark, in the telescope's eye,
For an endless wedding,
For the kindling of eyes, for a sweet dance
In the elements of air, sea, earth, and subterranean caves,
So that for a short moment there is no death
And time does not unreel like a skein of yarn
Thrown into an abyss.”
(Czeslaw Milosz)

E1.7.D.6.20. ALAN WATTS (1915-1973)
"There is a contradiction in wanting to be perfectly secure in a universe whose very nature is momentariness and fluidity. But the contradiction lies a little deeper than the mere conflict between the desire for security and the fact of change. If I want to be secure, that is, protected from the flux of life, I am wanting to be separate from life. Yet it is this very sense of separateness which makes me feel insecure. To be secure means to isolate and fortify the “I,” but it is just the feeling of being an isolated “I” which makes me feel lonely and afraid."

E1.7.D.6.20_REF1_PH ALAN WATTS HIGHLIGHTS THE CONTRADICTION IN SEEKING SECURITY IN A CONSTANTLY CHANGING UNIVERSE, noting that the desire for security stems from a sense of separation from life, which in turn creates insecurity. 
"Working rightly, the brain is the highest form of “instinctual wisdom.” Thus it should work like the homing instinct of pigeons and the formation of the fetus in the womb — without verbalizing the process or knowing “how” it does it. The self-conscious brain, like the self-conscious heart, is a disorder, and manifests itself in the acute feeling of separation between “I” and my experience. The brain can only assume its proper behavior when consciousness is doing what it is designed for: not writhing and whirling to get out of present experience, but being effortlessly aware of it. [...]
There is a contradiction in wanting to be perfectly secure in a universe whose very nature is momentariness and fluidity. But the contradiction lies a little deeper than the mere conflict between the desire for security and the fact of change. If I want to be secure, that is, protected from the flux of life, I am wanting to be separate from life. Yet it is this very sense of separateness which makes me feel insecure. To be secure means to isolate and fortify the “I,” but it is just the feeling of being an isolated “I” which makes me feel lonely and afraid. In other words, the more security I can get, the more I shall want.
To put it still more plainly: the desire for security and the feeling of insecurity are the same thing. To hold your breath is to lose your breath. A society based on the quest for security is nothing but a breath-retention contest in which everyone is as taut as a drum and as purple as a beet.
(Alan Watts, The Wisdom of Insecurity: A Message for an Age of Anxiety, 1951)

E1.7.D.6.20_REF1.1_PH “NOT TO BE AFRAID WHEN YOU ARE ALL ALONE IS THE ONLY TRUE WAY OF BEING NOT AFRAID. Where does your courage come in, when you cannot find it in your own self but always have to grasp God morally?”
(Olga Jacoby, Words in Pain: Letters on Life and Death, 1919)

E1.7.D.6.20_REF2_PH ALAN WATTS ARGUES THAT THE NOTION OF A SEPARATE SELF OR "I" DISTINCT FROM EXPERIENCE IS AN ILLUSION CREATED BY MEMORY AND THE RAPID CHANGE OF THOUGHTS. "The notion of a separate thinker, of an « I » distinct from the experience, comes from memory and from the rapidity with which thought changes. It is like whirling a burning stick to give the illusion of a continuous circle of fire. If you imagine that memory is a direct knowledge of the past rather than a present experience, you get the illusion of knowing the past and the present at the same time. This suggests that there is something in you distinct from both the past and the present experiences. You reason, « I know this present experience, and it is different from that past experience. If I can compare the two, and notice that experience has changed, I must be something constant and apart. »
But, as a matter of fact, you cannot compare this present experience with a past experience. You can only compare it with a memory of the past, which is a part of the present experience. When you see clearly that memory is a form of present experience, it will be obvious that trying to separate yourself from this experience is as impossible as trying to make your teeth bite themselves. There is simply experience. There is not something or someone experiencing experience! You do not feel feelings, think thoughts, or sense sensations any more than you hear hearing, see sight, or smell smelling. « I feel fine » means that a fine feeling is present. It does not mean that there is one thing called an « I » and another separate thing called a feeling, so that when you bring them together this « I » feels the fine feeling. There are no feelings but present feelings, and whatever feeling is present is « I. » No one ever found an « I » apart from some present experience, or some experience apart from an « I » - which is only to say that the two are the same thing.
As a mere philosophical argument this is a waste of time. We are not trying to have an « intellectual discussion. » We are being aware of the fact that any separate « I » who thinks thoughts and experiences experience is an illusion. To understand this is to realize that life is entirely momentary, that there is neither permanence nor security, and that there is no « I » which can be protected. [...]"
(Alan Watts, The wisdom of insecurity, 1951)

E1.7.D.6.21. ROLAND BARTHES (1915-1980)
"I felt like I was frozen in an ice field that stretched for thousands of miles, and inside me there was a feeling of absolute safety.”

E1.7.D.6.21_REF1_PH "OUTSIDE THE REALM OF RATIONALITY

[...] Zen satori does not come under language, and therefore definition, or even description; therefore, literally, it is untranslatable, because we would come up against Christian language: conversion, illumination, whereas satori is not a descent into a truth, a God, but rather a sudden emergence into the void: “enlightenment” is not a good word because it does not illuminate anything → contradiction: it dispels doubt, but not in favor of certainty. Satori: a kind of mental catastrophe that occurs all at once → experienced by the Buddha under the Bodhi tree = Nirvana achieved during earthly life.

Reports of satori are extremely rare (which is somewhat frustrating). This one, however, is the satori of Hakouin (modern Zen, 18th century Japan): Sudden, immense mental concentration: “I had the impression of being frozen in a field of ice that stretched for thousands of miles, and within me there was a feeling of absolute security” - this makes me think of Friedrich's painting, The Wreck of the HOPE Ship Grounded by the Ice: the intense desolation of this painting produces in me a kind of catastrophe, agony (primitive agony) (absolute, eternal abandonment, the loss of the mother); but perhaps satori is just the other side (or the place) of this catastrophe. In any case, for us [in the West], satoris are brief flashes, perhaps on the side of romanticism. [...]

“That's it”

The word of satori = the exclamation: That's it / Suzuki (II, 617): “The time will come when your mind will suddenly stop like an old rat caught in a dead end. Then there will be a leap into the unknown with the cry: ‘Ah, that's it!’”
(Roland Barthes, The Neutral, Course at the Collège de France, 1977-1978)

"Is that it?" The answer came to him immediately: No, that, nor anything else in the world can ever be anything but a name for "it", and names are just empty sounds. At that moment, the split between him and "it" disappeared; or, as the philosophers say, there was no longer any separation between subject and object."
