E1.7.D.6.24. THICH NHAT HANH (1926-2022)
Our environment can influence our emotions and make us mistake our societal "false self" for our authentic "true self." These inner conflicts can create turmoil, but surviving them leads to growth. Thich Nhat Hanh experienced such a storm while reading a book in a library. In a moment of insight, he realized he was devoid of ideals, hopes, viewpoints, or allegiances. He felt as carefree as a grasshopper on a blade of grass, where there is no division, resistance, or blame. Some life experiences go beyond study or rational thought; we must experience them, cherish them, and unite with them.

E1.7.D.6.24_REF1_PH THICH NHAT HANH REFLECTS ON A MOMENT OF PROFOUND INSIGHT IN A LIBRARY, WHERE HE FELT A DEEP CONNECTION WITH PAST BORROWERS OF A BOOK AND EXPERIENCED A SENSE OF EMPTINESS AND UNITY WITH THE UNIVERSE. This realization led him to understand the transient nature of identity and the importance of embracing life's difficulties to grow and find peace.
"It’s funny how much our surroundings influence our emotions. Our joys and sorrows, likes and dislikes are colored by our environment so much that often we just let our surroundings dictate our course. We go along with “public” feelings until we no longer even know our own true aspirations. We become a stranger to ourselves, molded entirely by society… Sometimes I feel caught between two opposing selves — the “false self” imposed by society and what I would call my “true self.” How often we confuse the two and assume society’s mold to be our true self. Battles between our two selves rarely result in a peaceful reconciliation. Our mind becomes a battlefield on which the Five Aggregates — the form, feelings, perceptions, mental formations, and consciousness of our being — are strewn about like debris in a hurricane. Trees topple, branches snap, houses crash.
These are our loneliest moments. Yet every time we survive such a storm, we grow a little. Without storms like these, I would not be who I am today. But I rarely hear such a storm coming until it is already upon me. It seems to appear without warning, as though treading silently on silk slippers. I know it must have been brewing a long time, simmering in my own thoughts and mental formations, but when such a frenzied hurricane strikes, nothing outside can help. I am battered and torn apart, and I am also saved. [...]
[He goes on to detail what occasioned the storm of his unselfing] The feeling began shortly before eleven o’clock at night on October first. I was browsing on the eleventh floor of Butler Library. I knew the library was about to close, and I saw a book that concerned the area of my research. I slid it off the shelf and held it in my two hands. It was large and heavy. I read that it had been published in 1892, and it was donated to the Columbia Library the same year. On the back cover was a slip of paper that recorded the names of borrowers and the dates they took it out of the library. The first time it had been borrowed was in 1915, the second time was in 1932. I would be the third. Can you imagine? I was only the third borrower, on October 1, 1962. For seventy years, only two other people had stood in the same spot I now stood, pulled the book from the shelf, and decided to check it out. I was overcome with the wish to meet those two people. I don’t know why, but I wanted to hug them. But they had vanished, and I, too, will soon disappear. Two points on the same straight line will never meet. I was able to encounter two people in space, but not in time. [...]
I feel as though I’ve lived a long time and have seen so much of life. I’m almost thirty-six, which is not young. But that night, while standing amidst the stacks at Butler Library, I saw that I am neither young nor old, existent nor nonexistent. My friends know I can be as playful and mischievous as a child. I love to kid around and enter fully into the game of life. I also know what it is to get angry. And I know the pleasure of being praised. I am often on the verge of tears or laughter. But beneath all of these emotions, what else is there? How can I touch it? If there isn’t anything, why would I be so certain that there is?
Still holding the book, I felt a glimmer of insight. I understood that I am empty of ideals, hopes, viewpoints, or allegiances. I have no promises to keep with others. In that moment, the sense of myself as an entity among other entities disappeared. I knew that this insight did not arise from disappointment, despair, fear, desire, or ignorance. A veil silently lifted effortlessly. That is all. If you beat me, stone me, or even shoot me, everything that is considered to be “me” will disintegrate. Then, what is actually there will reveal itself — faint as smoke, elusive as emptiness, and yet neither smoke nor emptiness, ugly, nor not ugly, beautiful, yet not beautiful. It is like a shadow on a screen. [...]
At that moment, I had the deep feeling that I had returned. My clothes, my shoes, even the essence of my being had vanished, and I was carefree as a grasshopper pausing on a blade of grass… When a grasshopper sits on a blade of grass, he has no thought of separation, resistance, or blame… The green grasshopper blends completely with the green grass… It neither retreats nor beckons. It knows nothing of philosophy or ideals. It is simply grateful for its ordinary life. Dash across the meadow, my dear friend, and greet yesterday’s child. When you can’t see me, you yourself will return. Even when your heart is filled with despair, you will find the same grasshopper on the same blade of grass… Some life dilemmas cannot be solved by study or rational thought. We just live with them, struggle with them, and become one with them… To live, we must die every instant. We must perish again and again in the storms that make life possible."
(Thich Nhat Hanh, Fragrant Palm Leaves: Journals, 1962–1966)

E1.7.D.6.25. FRANÇOIS CHENG (1929-2002)
TACIT CONCORD WITH REALITY
François Cheng introduces three fundamental notions of Chinese aesthetics for evaluating art. The highest quality a work can possess is divine resonance, a presence that aligns with divine spirit and enlightenment. The pinnacle of art suggests a deep connection between man and the divine [or the world, for non-believers]. This connection is referred to in Chinese as "tacit concord".

E1.7.A.3.3_REF4_PO.wE1.7.D.6.25.

E1.7.A.3.1_REF4.1_PO.wE1.7.D.6.25.

E1.7.A.3.1_REF4_PO.wE1.7.D.6.25.

E1.7.D.6.25_REF4_PO "WHEN THEY CAME HERE, THE GODS 
HAVE HIDDEN THEIR SECRET.
With our blood we have 
Traced the flowery paths. 
Then we ran 
From hill to hill 
Under the untouchable azure. 
*
To bury in you the most secret 
Of ourselves, in the depths of what we had not 
Dared to say nor hope for. The world is there. 
As it was in childhood, sprung 
From within, clear and round, round the sky. 
Round the earth. Plain-chanting fruits. 
In unison titmouse and waterfall. 
*
When the souls become songs, 
The world suddenly remembers. 
*
The night awakens to its dawn 
The breath retrieves its rhythm. 
Beyond death, Human
Summer rustles with resonance."
(François Cheng, When Souls Become Songs, 2018)

E1.7.D.6.25_REF2_LIT "THE PARADOXICAL EXPRESSION AN « INSTANT OF ETERNITY » CONVEYS AN EXTREMELY PRIVILEGED EXPERIENCE. Thus spoke Friedrich Nietzsche, quoted by the poet Jean Mambrino in L'Hespérie, Country of the Evening: « Let us admit that we say "yes" to a single moment, we would have said "yes" not only to ourselves, but to everything that exists. For nothing is isolated, neither in us nor in things, and if even once joy resounded in our soul, the whole preceeding eternity was needed to create the conditions for this one moment, and all of eternity was approved, justified in that one moment that we said "yes" ». We feel, confusedly but deeply convinced, that the moment as we have just evoked it is related, by its flavor of plenitude, to what eternity must be."
(François Cheng, Five meditations on death)

E1.7.D.6.25_REF3_PH FRANÇOIS CHENG EXAMINES THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN BEAUTY, THE GAZE AND MEMORY, EMPHASIZING THAT BEAUTY TAKES ON ITS FULL MEANING WHEN IT IS PERCEPTED: "On a higher level, another encounter occurs when this scene is captured by a gaze. If it is not captured by a gaze, beauty is not known; it is a "waste," it does not take on its full meaning. "Taking on meaning" here means that whenever the universe tends toward the state of beauty, it offers a chance - or revives a promise - of enjoyment. This gaze of a subject that captures the scene of beauty in the moment leads to a new encounter that is located on another level, that of memory.
In memory, or more precisely in its duration, each present gaze of a subject joins all the past gazes when the subject has encountered beauty; it also joins the gazes of other creators whose works the subject has admired. This is in accordance with the truth I mentioned earlier, that beauty attracts beauty, that it increases and elevates it. From there, from gaze to gaze, the subject, if inspired, can aspire to a supreme encounter that would connect it to the original gaze of the universe. Without the need for belief, it may instinctively feel that this universe, which was capable of creating beings endowed with a gaze, must itself have possessed a gaze. If the universe created itself, it must have 'seen' itself being created, and in the end it 'said' to itself, 'This is beautiful,' or more simply, 'This is it. If it hadn't said, "It's beautiful," would man ever have been able to say, "It's beautiful?

In the light of these few reflections, we understand that the act of looking is almost always linked to time, in the sense that this act, carried out by a subject, is immediately accompanied by another effort, that of recognition, which is linked to memory. In other words, what is looked at refers, in the subject, to all that he has looked at in the past or in the imagination, and, more deeply, to the intimate experience he has of a revelation of the self that is spread out over time.

In this regard, I would like to recall a remark by Henri Makliney, according to which the noun regard and the verb regarder are two words that many languages can envy French. The combination of re and garder is rich in connotations. More than the act of surreptitiously capturing a sight or an image, it evokes the resumption or renewal of something that has been preserved and that, with each new opportunity, demands to be developed as a future. Let us add that contemplation also carries the idea of respect; it always encourages the beholder to a deeper commitment.

Is there a source of the gaze that would be the gaze itself? Even a people as supposedly nonreligious as the Chinese, as we have said, instinctively feel that this universe that was capable of creating living beings with eyes must itself have been driven by the need to see and the ability to see. Moreover, in Chinese, the character that denotes the gaze holds the key to the sacred, to the divine. Moreover, an almost banal expression has taken root in the popular imagination: lao-tian-you-yan, "Heaven has its eyes. We have just seen that the entire poetic and artistic tradition in China was driven by this fundamental conviction. Later, Buddhism introduced the notion of the "eye of wisdom" or the "third eye. The gaze produced by this eye is a function of the universal consciousness that inhabits it, which it can acquire only after the experience of emptiness, rather than of the sagacity of the subject. [...] 

I will content myself with quoting a phrase of Meister Eckhart's, which later interested Hegel very much: "The eye with which I see God is the eye with which God sees me." [...] Ibn 'Arabi, one of the greatest Sufi poets of the 13th century, expressed in his own way the relationship between the gaze of the Creator and that of the creature. From a rather long poem, let us listen to the following four verses, which bring out a subtle dialectic of gaze. Here, too, it is the Creator who speaks:

I have created in you your perception to be the object of my perception.
Through my gaze you see me and I see you.
You cannot perceive me through yourself
On the other hand, if you perceive me, you perceive yourself.

[...] The second verse clarifies further: it is through the gaze of the Creator that the creature sees Him. The creature meets the gaze of the Creator and sees Him. And suddenly the Creator, meeting the gaze of the creature that sees Him, sees the creature.
The third verse confirms that the creature cannot see the Creator through itself. With the fourth verse, we understand that when the creature sees the Creator, it can truly see itself. What this verse says can also be reversed by suggesting that likewise, if the Creator sees the creature, it can truly see itself. In this long poem, if the Creator teaches the Creature the secret of the gaze, it is because He wants to enter into a relationship of love with the Creature. He asks the creature not to be content with looking through itself; he literally begs it to meet the Creator's gaze.

[...] In love, as in beauty, every gaze is a mutual gaze. This is why Merleau-Ponty defines perception with the concept of the chiasm, this interpenetration, precisely, between what looks and what is looked at. An isolated gaze can hardly achieve beauty. Only crossed gazes can provoke the spark that illuminates, and in the extreme case of Creator and creature, only they allow the divine light to reveal itself. But in an authentic experience of love and beauty, if not every creature is elevated to the dignity of the Creator, so is it true that exchanged gazes give birth to each other and make them be.

[...] At the next level, the gaze goes beyond the fascination of the figure and accesses the light of the soul, which is true presence. This light, received from the outside, penetrates within and becomes an inner light that allows us to see the soul of the other and our own soul in a vision of coming and going, like a fountain with intersecting jets. In this moment, you close your eyes as if in prayer or ecstasy.

I am thinking of the Khmer heads in the Guimet Museum. You can no longer see the eyes, but curiously, you can see the gaze turned toward the infinite space within. On the subject of this infinite space within, let us add a clarification. If we stick to the physical body, it is a terribly limited space. If we recognize and accept the spiritual body, that is, the body animated by the breath of spirit, the infinite is practically there, but the spiritual body must awaken and come into exchange, into resonance with the breath that animates the living universe, for the infinite is that which never ceases to spring forth into open life. But the space within is inevitable. You have to go through it, and from there everything can radiate again.

The dimension in question is truly that of the soul. It is in the depths of the inner space that we can hear the voice of the soul, that we can perceive the vision of the soul. When we look at a Khmer head in contemplation, we see that we have before us a face completely absorbed by the gaze, a face that becomes pure gaze and pure smile, a face of vision where the visible and the invisible nourish each other, where the source of beauty and the effective beauty become one. Turned inward and then turned outward again, this face will then be able to radiate another kind of light, that of transfiguration."
(François Cheng, Five meditations on beauty – Fourth meditation, 2006)

E1.7.D.6.25_REF4_PH "FOR MY PART, I WILL RETAIN THREE NOTIONS OF CHINESE AESTHETICS THAT SEEM FUNDAMENTAL TO ME. All three are primarily concerned with visual art, but are also valid for other art forms. They form a three-tiered "system" that can be used as a criterion for evaluating and judging works of art. I will present them in the following organic order: yin-yun, qi-yun and shen-yun.

1. As a basic level, yin-yun, "unifying interaction. [...] Thus, in the brush stroke that realizes yin-yun, the carnal relationship is forged between the feeling body of the artist and the feeling body of the landscape. All in all, yin-yun is indeed this intrinsic quality of a work: a unifying order that emerges from a multi-level interaction between the various elements that make up a material, between matter and spirit, and ultimately between the human subject and the living universe, which is itself a subject.

2. As an intermediate level, qi-yun, "rhythmic breath. [...] A rhythm does not unfold in time and space, it is the generator of its space-time. The emergence of a rhythmic space is one with the constitutive transformation of all the elements of a work of art into a moment of form, into a moment of rhythm. This rhythm is not before us; it is not something that can be had. We are in the rhythm. Present to it, we discover ourselves present to ourselves. We exist in this opening of being. Rhythm is a form of surprised existence... Rare are the works in which we have reason to be present.

3. Finally, at the highest level, shen-yun, "divine resonance. This term refers to the supreme quality a work must have: to be of the highest order. It seems difficult to grasp because it suggests something abstract and vague. The Chinese are careful not to fix it in an overly rigid definition, considering the quality it evokes as a state that can be experienced without being able to explain it. [...] 
The appearance of a presence - not in the figurative or anthropological sense of the word, which can be felt or sensed, but the very presence of the divine spirit. In all its invisible aspects, this presence corresponds to what Chinese theorists call xiang-wai-zhi-xiang, "the image beyond images. It is also close to what Chan spirituality experiences as enlightenment. When we stand in front of a natural scene, a tree in bloom, a bird flying with a cry, a ray of sunlight or moonlight illuminating a moment of silence, we suddenly go to the other side of the scene, we find ourselves beyond the screen of phenomena, and we experience the impression of a presence that is self-evident, It comes to us, whole, undivided, inexplicable and yet undeniable, like a generous gift that makes everything miraculously present, spreading a light of original color, whispering a song native from heart to heart, from soul to soul.

I have just said the word "soul"; it brings to mind the notion of yi-jing, "soul dimension," which we encountered in the second meditation on the rose, and which in Chinese aesthetic thought is somewhat equivalent to shen-yun, "divine resonance. Like shen, yi, "disposition of the heart, of the soul," is what both human beings and the living universe are endowed with. The yi-jing thus suggests a soul to soul understanding between man and the divine, which the Chinese language refers to as mo-qi, "tacit understanding.
An understanding that is never complete: there will always be a hiatus, a suspension, a gap to be filled. The sought-after infinity is in fact an un-infinity. The void that stretches across a scroll of Chinese painting is there to signify this. This breathless emptiness conceals an expectation, a listening, ready to welcome a new arrival, to herald a new understanding. For his part, the artist is always ready to endure pain and sorrow, hardship and ruin, to the point of being consumed by the fire of his act, of being drawn into the space of the work. He knows that beauty, more than a given, is the supreme gift of what has been offered. And that for man, more than an achievement, it will always be a challenge, a gamble".
(François Cheng, Five Meditations on Beauty, Fifth meditation, 2006)

E1.7.D.6.26. TONI MORRISON (1931-2019)
"It comes unadorned

Unadorned
Like a phrase
Strong enough to cast a spell;
It comes
Unbidden,
Like the turn of sun through hills
Or stars in wheels of song.
The jeweled feet of women dance the earth.
Arousing it to spring.
Shoulders broad as a road bend to share the weight of years.
Profiles breach the distance and lean
Toward an ordinary kiss.
Bliss.
It comes naked into the world like a charm."
(Toni Morrison)

E1.7.D.6.27. MARY OLIVER (1935-2019)
"Let me keep company always with those who say
“Look!” and laugh in astonishment,
and bow their heads."

E1.7.D.6.27_REF1_PO WHEN I AM AMONG THE TREES
When I am among the trees,
especially the willows and the honey locust,
equally the beech, the oaks and the pines,
they give off such hints of gladness.
I would almost say that they save me, and daily.

I am so distant from the hope of myself,
in which I have goodness, and discernment,
and never hurry through the world
    but walk slowly, and bow often.

Around me the trees stir in their leaves
and call out, “Stay awhile.”
The light flows from their branches.

And they call again, “It’s simple,” they say,
“and you too have come
into the world to do this, to go easy, to be filled
with light, and to shine.”
(Mary Oliver, Thirst, 2006)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF2_PO "I DON'T WANT TO BE DEMURE OR RESPECTABLE

I don't want to be demure or respectable.

was that way, asleep, for years.

That way, you forget too many important things.

How the little stones, even if you can't hear them, are singing.

How the river can't wait to get to the ocean and the sky, it's been there before.

What traveling is that!

It is a joy to imagine such distances.

I could skip sleep for the next hundred years.

There is a fire in the lashes of my eyes.

It doesn't matter where I am, it could be a small room.

The glimmer of gold Böhme saw on the kitchen pot was missed by everyone else in the house. [...]"
(Mary Oliver, Blue horses, 2014)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF3_PO "MYSTERIES, YES

"Truly, we live with mysteries too marvelous
to be understood.

How grass can be nourishing in the
mouths of the lambs.
How rivers and stones are forever
in allegiance with gravity
while we ourselves dream of rising.
How two hands touch and the bonds will
never be broken.
How people come, from delight or the
scars of damage,
to the comfort of a poem.

Let me keep my distance, always, from those
who think they have the answers.

Let me keep company always with those who say
“Look!” and laugh in astonishment,
and bow their heads."
(Mary Oliver, Evidence, 2009)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF4_PO "INVITATION

Oh do you have time
    to linger 
        for just a little while 
            out of your busy 

and very important day 
    for the goldfinches 
        that have gathered 
            in a field of thistles

for a musical battle, 
    to see who can sing 
        the highest note, 
            or the lowest,

or the most expressive of mirth, 
    or the most tender? 
        Their strong, blunt beaks
            drink the air

as they strive 
    melodiously 
        not for your sake 
            and not for mine

and not for the sake of winning 
    but for sheer delight and gratitude - 
        believe us, they say, 
            it is a serious thing

just to be alive 
    on this fresh morning 
        in this broken world. 
            I beg of you,

do not walk by 
    without pausing 
        to attend to this 
            rather ridiculous performance.

It could mean something.
    It could mean everything. 
        It could be what Rilke meant, when he wrote: 
            You must change your life."
(Mary Oliver, Red Bird, 2008)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF5_PO "TO BEGIN WITH, THE SWEET GRASS

[...]

The witchery of living 
is my whole conversation 
with you, my darlings.
All I can tell you is what I know.

Look, and look again.
This world is not just a little thrill for the eyes.

It's more than bones.
It's more than the delicate wrist with its personal pulse.
It's more than the beating of the single heart.
It's praising.
It's giving until the giving feels like receiving.
You have a life - just imagine that!
You have this day, and maybe another, and maybe still another. 

[...]

Let me ask you this.
Do you also think that beauty exists for some fabulous reason?

And, if you have not been enchanted by this adventure - 
your life - 
what would do for you?  

[...]

What I loved in the beginning, I think, was mostly myself. Never mind that I had to, since somebody had to.
That was many years ago.
Since then I have gone out from my confinements,
    though with difficulty. 

I mean the ones that thought to rule my heart.
I cast them out, I put them on the mush pile.
They will be nourishment somehow (everything is nourishment somehow or another).

And I have become the child of the clouds, and of hope.
I have become the friend of the enemy, whoever that is.
I have become older and, cherishing what I have learned,
I have become younger.

And what do I risk to tell you this, which is all I know? Love yourself. Then forget it. Then, love the world. "
(Mary Oliver, Evidence, 2009)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF6_PO "ON MEDITATING, SORT OF

Meditation, so I've heard, is best accomplished 
if you entertain a certain strict posture. 
Frankly, I prefer just to lounge under a tree. 
So why should I think I could ever be successful?

Some days I fall asleep, or land in that 
even better place-half-asleep-where the world, 
spring, summer, autumn, winter - 
flies through my mind in its 
hardy ascent and its uncompromising descent.

So I just lie like that, while distance and time 
reveal their true attitudes: they never 
heard of me, and never will, or ever need to.

Of course I wake up finally 
thinking, how wonderful to be who I am, 
made out of earth and water, 
my own thoughts, my own fingerprints- 
all that glorious, temporary stuff"
(Mary Oliver, Blue horses, 2014)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF6.1_PH "A NEW LOOK AT MEDITATION 

Spiritual experience, then, in the vision of things that it entails, does not resolve the contradictions: it undoes them while maintaining the concepts. In short, to use Ronald Laing's words, it sees through. So, after all that has been covered in this chapter, I propose to return to the example that raised the question in the first place - namely, meditation and the contradictory judgments to which it has been subjected. The previous considerations reveal a new point of view that seems contradictory, but may not be as contradictory as it seems. What if meditating and not meditating were not mutually exclusive? What if there were several ways to meditate, one of which was not to meditate? What if the non-meditation of the meditator was different from that of the non-meditator? What if there was a place from which the two possibilities appeared as variants of the same way of being? What if the pair meditate/not meditate was the binary designation of an inner position that cannot be named as a single entity? And what if this position is precisely the one the spiritual seeker is trying to reach? I leave the question open for now, in the hope that this book will help those who read (and write) it to discover their own answer."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF6.2_PH “THE PROGRESSION OF SPIRITUAL UNDERSTANDING [...]

A AND NON-A

But spiritual research takes those who practice it even further. Not only can it lead to a succession of opposing judgments by reversing the pros and cons, but, even more profoundly, it leads the seeker to a vision of things where what is being examined is not fully describable as either A or non-A. The least bad designation in logical terms would be paradoxical: A, provided that it is non-A; or non-A, it being understood that it is A. And even these formulations would be insufficient, because the dialectical process at work is even more radical, since it tends, as Anne Cheng writes, “to refute an idea as being, as not being, as being both being and not being, as being neither being nor not being”.

For now, it will suffice to stick to the first two stages. Since what we want to describe, which I will call "A-not-A", has the qualities of both A and not-A, it is not a matter of finding some kind of middle ground, but of accessing a place of vision that lies beyond their opposition and from which we perceive that it is a matter of a flattening of reality. When the mind is faced with an insurmountable difficulty, it is because it seeks the solution at the level of the contradiction: but another level must be reached. Not so that the problem finds its solution in the terms in which it was posed (which never happens in spiritual inquiry), but so that it disappears as such. Before it is overcome, the contradiction is a dead end; and those who try to go beyond it fall into another dead end, because it is not surmountable as such - at a higher level it simply has no place. And when the researcher looks back (which, in my opinion, he should do more often because it is instructive), it appears to him as a logical optical illusion due to the inadequacy of his vision."
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF6.3_PH "EXPRESSING OPPOSITES WHILE DELIVERING THEM FROM THEIR CONTRADICTION

It is not that language lacks precision, but that it has too much of it, because it discriminates according to its nature and function. Therefore, in order to describe the spiritual, one is often led to juxtapose contradictory qualities. Let us examine how this can be done.

The contradiction can unfold in successive moments, as in this well-known fragment of Heraclitus: "God: day-night, winter-summer, war-peace, satiety-famine." But if we imagine that the different phases of description or argumentation are condensed into a single statement that unites them, then we arrive at a different kind of formula, for example that of the well-known Mazu (Ma-Tsou), who proclaims that "Zen is the ordinary mind. Of course it is and it is not, while being it without being it... Let's say it is, under certain conditions that mean it is no longer what it seems to be. The grasping of the contradiction, which has lost its temporality, has united itself into a proof that seems paradoxical... or simply cunning. And the apparently simple affirmation that results says more than it expresses at first glance, because it captures the complexity of the path of understanding that led to it.

Such statements are often said to be provocative, but this qualifier is superficial. Even Dali, who comes so close to provocation in his famous phrases, goes beyond it, in my opinion. For example, if we look at one of Dali's famous statements, "The station of Perpignan is the center of the world," and we unfold its complex meaning, we realize that it condenses three different times:

1) To say this is absurd and deliberately provocative - Perpignan station, an ordinary place, is not, of course. 2) But yes, it is. All I have to do is decide, and the center of the world will be there, by virtue of an aesthetic decision. The world that spreads out from such a center will be seen differently. Reality will become surreal. 3) Yes, but it will still be Perpignan station, and it will be promoted as such. The tone of the sentence is, of course, subtly humorous. The irony is signaled and distanced, transcended while remaining present, and the sentence becomes unsettling and banal, surprising and ordinary. The contradictory stages that it implies have merged into a simultaneous formulation that presents them together without neutralizing them.

Admittedly, Dalí's references are far from Zen, but here he joins it in his own way. Through a completely surreal aesthetic operation, he sublimates banality to the point of dissolving it while respecting it, thus joining Mazu in his aforementioned formula; the first level of understanding is not rejected or fought against, but preserved and transcended at the same time, like a koan that accepts absurdity and strikes it down without ever opposing it".
(Jean-Luc Giribone, Beyond wisdom, How to live?, 2023)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF7_PO "IT WAS EARLY

It was early, 
    which has always been my hour 
        to begin looking 
            at the world

and of course, 
    even in the darkness, 
        to begin 
            listening into it,

especially 
    under the pines 
        where the owl lives 
            and sometimes calls out

as I walk by, 
    as he did 
        on this morning. 
            So many gifts!

What do they mean? 
    In the marshes 
        where the pink light 
            was just arriving

the mink
    with his bristle tail 
        was stalking 
            the soft-eared mice,

and in the pines
    the cones were heavy, 
        each one 
            ordained to open.

Sometimes 
    I need only to stand 
        wherever I am 
            to be blessed.

Little mink, let me watch you. 
    Little mice, run and run. 
        Dear pine cone, let me hold you 
            as you open."
(Mary Oliver, Evidence, 2009)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF8_PO "MINDFUL

Every day 
    I see or I hear 
        something 
            that more or less

kills me 
    with delight, 
        that leaves me 
            like a needle

in the haystack
    of light. 
        It is what I was born for- 
            to look, to listen,

to lose myself 
    inside this soft world- 
        to instruct myself 
            over and over

in joy, 
    and acclamation. 
        Nor am I talking 
            about the exceptional,

the fearful, the dreadful, 
    the very extravagant- 
        but of the ordinary, 
            the common, the very drab,

the daily presentations. 
    Oh, good scholar, 
        I say to myself, 
            how can you help

but grow wise 
    with such teachings 
        as these - 
            the untrimmable light

of the world, 
    the ocean's shine, 
        the prayers that are made 
            out of grass?"
(Mary Oliver, Why I wake early, 2004)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF9_PO "WHITE FLOWERS

Last night 
in the fields 
I lay down in the darkness
 to think about death, 
but instead I fell asleep, 
as if in a vast and sloping room 
filled with those white flowers 
that open all summer, 
sticky and untidy, 
in the warm fields. 
When I woke 
the morning light was just slipping 
in front of the stars, 
and I was covered 
with blossoms. 
I don't know 
how it happened -
I don't know 
if my body went diving down 
under the sugary vines 
in some sleep-sharpened affinity 
with the depths, or whether 
that green energy 
rose like a wave 
and curled over me, claiming me 
in its husky arms. 
I pushed them away, but I didn't rise. 
Never in my life had I felt so plush, 
or so slippery,
or so resplendently empty. 
Never in my life 
had I felt myself so near 
that porous line 
where my own body was done with 
and the roots and the stems and the flowers 
began."
(Mary Oliver, New and selected poems: Volume one, 1992)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF10_PO "RICE

It grew in the black mud. 
It grew under the tiger's orange paws. 
Its stems thinner than candles, and as straight. 
Its leaves like the feathers of egrets, but green. 
The grains cresting, wanting to burst. 
Oh, blood of the tiger.

I don't want you just to sit down at the table. 
I don't want you just to eat, and be content. 
I want you to walk out into the fields 
where the water is shining, and the rice has risen. 
I want you to stand there, far from the white tablecloth. 
I want you to fill your hands with the mud, like a blessing."
(Mary Oliver, New and selected poems: Volume one, 1992)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF11_PO "A MEETING

She steps into the dark swamp 
where the long wait ends.

The secret slippery package 
drops to the weeds.

She leans her long neck and tongues it 
between breaths slack with exhaustion

and after a while it rises and becomes a creature 
like her, but much smaller.

So now there are two. And they walk together 
like a dream under the trees.

In early June, at the edge of a field 
thick with pink and yellow flowers

I meet them. 
I can only stare.

She is the most beautiful woman 
I have ever seen.

Her child leaps among the flowers, 
the blue of the sky falls over me

like silk, the flowers burn, and I want 
to live my life all over again, to begin again,

to be utterly 
wild."
(Mary Oliver, Amercian primitive, 1983)

E1.7.D.6.27_REF12_PO "AT BLACKWATER POND

At Blackwater Pond the tossed waters have settled 
after a night of rain.
I dip my cupped hands. I drink 
a long time. It tastes 
like stone, leaves, fire. It falls cold 
into my body, waking the bones. I hear them 
deep inside me, whispering 
oh what is that beautiful thing 
that just happened?"
(Mary Oliver, Three rivers poetry journal, 1980)

E1.7.D.6.28. WENDELL BERRY born 1934
"One’s inner voices become audible. One feels the attraction of one’s most intimate sources.
In consequence, one responds more clearly to other lives. The more coherent one becomes within oneself as a creature, the more fully one enters into the communion of all creatures."

E1.7.D.6.28_REF1_PO "THE PEACE OF WILD THINGS

When despair for the world grows in me
and I wake in the night at the least sound
in fear of what my life and my children’s lives may be,
I go and lie down where the wood drake
rests in his beauty on the water, and the great heron feeds.
I come into the peace of wild things
who do not tax their lives with forethought
of grief. I come into the presence of still water.
And I feel above me the day-blind stars
waiting with their light. For a time
I rest in the grace of the world, and am free."
(Wendell Berry)

E1.7.D.6.28_REF2_PO "WE ENTER SOLITUDE, IN WHICH ALSO WE LOSE LONELINESS…
True solitude is found in the wild places, where one is without human obligation.
One’s inner voices become audible. One feels the attraction of one’s most intimate sources.
In consequence, one responds more clearly to other lives. The more coherent one becomes within oneself as a creature, the more fully one enters into the communion of all creatures. [...]
From the order of nature we return to the order — and the disorder — of humanity.
From the larger circle we must go back to the smaller, the smaller within the larger and dependent on it.
One enters the larger circle by willingness to be a creature, the smaller by choosing to be a human.
And having returned from the woods, we remember with regret its restfulness. For all creatures there are in place, hence at rest.
In their most strenuous striving, sleeping and waking, dead and living, they are at rest.
In the circle of the human we are weary with striving, and are without rest."
(Wendell Berry, What Are People For?)

E1.7.D.6.28_REF3_PO “FINALLY WILL IT NOT BE ENOUGH,
after much living, after 
much love, after much dying 
of those you have loved, 
to sit on the porch near sundown
 with your eyes simply open, 
watching the wind shape the clouds 
into the shapes of clouds?

Even then you will remember 
the history of love, shaped 
in the shapes of flesh, ever-changing
as the clouds that pass, the blessed 
yearning of the body for body, 
unending light. You will remember, watching 
the clouds, the future of love.”
(Wendell Berry, Sabbath poem, 1994)

E1.7.D.6.29. WIM WENDERS born 1945
Hirayama cleans Tokyo’s public toilets for a living, but he embodies the traits of a Zen master.

E1.7.A.1.1_REF3_CUR DESPITE HIS HUMBLE JOB, HIRAYAMA, A TOKYO PUBLIC TOILET CLEANER, REMAINS PATIENT, ADAPTABLE, AND WISE, EMBODYING THE TRAITS OF A ZEN MASTER.
Hirayama (Koji Yakusho) cleans Tokyo’s public toilets for a living, rising before dawn to gently water the seedlings he grows in his home and then drive off to begin his shift.Hirayama clearly derives enjoyment from performing his work well, but there’s more to his life than labor, and more to this movie than a simplistic celebration of manual toil.

Hirayama is a man of few words, observing Tokyo and its inhabitants with a tenderness and patience that, if overlooked, could be mistaken for a simple disposition. He deeply observes people and his surroundings, finding immense satisfaction in the beauty of nature, music, literature, and human interactions. His interactions are devoid of pride, even when overlooked due to his job, and he exhibits no anger, even when faced with adversity. He displays empathy and tenderness towards people of all ages, being able to establish intimate relationships inrecord time with kids, adolescent and adults alike.

Hirayama is an educated man who reads a classical literature book each month. He is adaptable, despite adhering to a routine. He understands that his wisdom cannot be conveyed merely through words, but through smiles, loving actions, jokes, and games. Hirayama embodies the traits of a Zen master.

Watch the trailer: https://www.imdb.com/title/tt27503384/?ref_=ext_shr_lnk

E1.7.D.6.30. JACQUES DARRIULAT born 1946
"In this immense drama - where energy unfolds in the life of matter like an expanding sphere and invention multiplies in the life of the spirit - we have a role that we can't ignore. However, we have the choice to either fully embrace that role or deny it, and to exist in the light of Being without ever comprehending the infinite power which grants us life and existence, and enables us to persist in that bliss, which is the enjoyment of simply existing."

E1.7.D.6.30_REF1_PH SPINOZA TEACHES THAT BLISS LIES IN THE UNDERSTANDING OF OUR ETERNAL ESSENCE AND THE ENJOYMENT OF THAT FULLNESS. "In this immense drama-the expanding sphere in which the energy that unfolds in the life of matter is actualized as invention that multiplies in the life of spirit-a role is assigned to us from which it is not in our power to depart. But we are given the choice of living it to the full, or of denying it and passing into the light of being without ever fathoming the infinite power that gives us life and existence and makes us persevere in that bliss which is the enjoyment of pure being. The lesson of ethics teaches us not to choose our destiny - a vain pretense of the imagination that dreams with open eyes and knows nothing of the life that is its essence - but to live it to the full and to enjoy knowing ourselves as an expression among the infinity of possibilities, all equally destined to a necessary realization, of the living God who animates, in varying degrees, all things that have been granted the incomprehensible gift of existence. "For what we have shown so far is quite general and also concerns human beings and other individuals, all of whom are animated, albeit to different degrees" (Scholia on II, 13). Life is not worth living because of the choices we could make, because of an imaginary freedom that we ascribe to ourselves only because we are unaware of the true causes that determine us and the necessity that leads us as if by the hand. Life is worth living because it offers us the experience, albeit limited in time and space, of participating in the life that is in God, of feeling and experiencing ourselves as such, by virtue of the very essence that makes us exist, eternally: "We feel and experience that we are eternal. […] 

We feel that our spirit, insofar as it embraces the essence of the body in the aspect of eternity - sub aeternitatis specie - is eternal, and that its existence cannot be defined by time, that is, explained by duration" (V, Scholium 23). Eternity is not immortality. The former places being in a dimension beyond time, a definition that is simply negative, not yet affirmative; the latter, on the contrary, prolongs indefinitely the time granted to this singular existence. In terms of bliss, the ancient Greeks knew only the second, and they knew nothing more divine than being immortal, to the point of making this attribute the very name of the deity, since they said of their gods that they were the Immortals. But the Jewish God knows Himself to be infinite, not indefinite, and it is not enough for Him to live indefinitely in time; He must live in the glory of eternity - which triumphs over the dissent and diaspora that form the intimate fabric of duration - and re-establish Himself in perfect conformity with Himself, in the bliss of being and knowing Himself fully, and of enjoying this fullness forever."
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the Fullness of this World, 6 - “We feel and experience that we are eternal”, 2017)

Read teh full essay in French: http://www.jdarriulat.net/Auteurs/Spinoza/Introduction/Introd6.html

E1.7.D.6.30_REF2_PH FOR SPINOZA, THE VALUE OF LIFE IS NOT IN THE FREEDOM OF CHOICE BUT IN THE EXPERIENCE OF FEELING ETERNAL. "In this immense drama - where energy unfolds in the life of matter like an expanding sphere and invention multiplies in the life of the spirit - we have a role that we can't ignore. However, we have the choice to either fully embrace that role or deny it, and to exist in the light of Being without ever comprehending the infinite power which grants us life and existence, and enables us to persist in that bliss, which is the enjoyment of simply existing. [Spinoza's] Ethic teaches us not how to choose our destiny - vain pretension of the imagination, which dreams with eyes opened and doesn't know anything of the real life which constitutes its essence - but how to live it in fullness, and to enjoy knowing ourselves, as an expression among the infinity of possibilities, all equally destined for necessary realization [...]
The value of our lives doesn't reside in the choices we would be able to make, because of an imaginary freedom that we attribute to ourselves only because we ignore the real causes that determine us as well as the necessity that lead us by the hand. The value of our lives reside in that it offers us the experience, although limited in time and in space, [...] to feel and experience ourselves eternal: « We feel and we experience that we are eternal. […] We feel that our spirit, insofar as it envelops the essence of the body under the aspect of eternity - sub aeternitatis specie - is eternal, and that its existence cannot be defined by time, in other words, be explained by duration » (Baruch Spinoza, Ethics V, scolie 23).”
(Jacques Darriulat, Spinoza and the plenitude of this world, 6 - “We feel and we experience that we are eternal”, 2017)


E1.7.D.6.30_REF3_PH JACQUES DARRIULAT EXPLORES THE NOTION OF EXISTENCE IN PHILOSOPHY, EMPHASIZING THE IMPORTANCE OF THE WONDER OF BEING ALIVE, WHICH IS OFTEN OVERLOOKED IN EVERYDAY LIFE. 
"At every moment, assaulted by existence, immersed in it, we no longer know how to be amazed by it. Everywhere and nowhere at once, always present and yet always absent, existence is forgotten, it remains incognito among us. Always the same, never questioned, existence therefore has no history for us. [...]

To follow the thread of Wittgenstein's meditation, the "world" tries to show its existence to the man who thinks it.
For about two centuries now, the "existence" of the world has no longer been taken for granted by modern man. To exist then means "to be real," not in an apparent but in an authentic reality. We can suspect that existence is not unrelated to truth. [What can it mean to think the nonsense of the world's existence "authentically"?
It was undoubtedly necessary for us to meet Heidegger on our way, since he was one of the first - the first to listen to him since Parmenides - to shift the axis of philosophical wonder from thinking to being. Plato founded philosophy in the sense that he was the first to think that there is nothing more astonishing for thought than the power to think and conceive that is in thought, a consciousness that he calls "reminiscence" or "anamnesis" in the sense that thought has always known it but has forgotten it. Heidegger begins the foundation of philosophy anew - at least that is his claim - by thinking that there is nothing more astonishing and more worthy of thought for thought than the fact of being, the single source from which all the determined, singular existences that come before our eyes, that come to hand, proceed. What he calls "Being" [...], multiple and diverse, while Being (capitalized) is necessarily one. What Heidegger calls the "decadence" of philosophy, or the "decay" of thought, comes from the fact that thought forgets to think - not thought itself, as the tradition of the classics taught - but being. Forgetting to think of Being, we no longer consider anything other than the beings that come to hand, which we can use as we please, like so many tools that we handle, and no longer as riddles that require thinking. There is no need to think: it is enough to work and master the world thanks to our technical skills. For philosophy to be reborn, therefore, we would have to go back to the beginning of philosophy, before the Platonic foundation, to the time of the Presocratics, mainly Heraclitus and Parmenides. [...]

Heidegger's question is not about the diversity of things, but about what they have in common and what unites them, namely "existence"."
(Jacques Darriulat, Being and Existence, 2-To Speak of Existence, 2020)

E1.7.D.6.31. ALAN LIGHTMAN born 1948
THE PERSONAL AND SUBJECTIVE NATURE OF OUR SPIRITUAL EXPERIENCES CANNOT BE FULLY UNDERSTOOD BY REASON
"The spiritual experiences we have can arise from atoms and molecules. At the same time, some of these experiences, and certainly their very personal and subjective nature, cannot be fully understood in terms of atoms and molecules."

E1.7.B.1.2.d.wE1.7.D.6.31. THE PERSONAL AND SUBJECTIVE NATURE OF OUR SPIRITUAL EXPERIENCES CANNOT BE FULLY UNDERSTOOD BY REASON
The spiritual experiences we have can arise from atoms and molecules. At the same time, some of these experiences, and certainly their very personal and subjective nature, cannot be fully understood in terms of atoms and molecules.


E1.7.D.6.32. JANE HIRSHFIELD born 1953
"A poem plucks the interconnection of the experiencing self and all being. In poetry’s words, life calls to life with the same inevitability and gladness that bird calls to bird, whale to whale, frog to frog. Listening across the night or ocean or pond, they recognize one another and are warmed by that knowledge."

E1.7.D.6.32_REF1_PO JANE HIRSHFIELD EXPLORES HOW GOOD ART, PARTICULARLY POETRY, TRANSFORMS AND ENLARGES HUMAN PERCEPTION. Art changes both the inner self and the outer world by creating new ways of seeing and understanding. 
"Good art is a truing of vision, in the way a saw is trued in the saw shop, to cut more cleanly. It is also a changing of vision. Entering a good poem, a person feels, tastes, hears, thinks, and sees in altered ways. Why ask art into a life at all, if not to be transformed and enlarged by its presence and mysterious means? … And by changing selves, one by one, art changes also the outer world that selves create and share…. Inside the intricate clockworks of language and music, event and life, what allows and invites us to feel and know as we do, and then increase our feeling and knowing? Such a question cannot be answered. “We” are different, from one another and, moment by moment, from even ourselves. “Art,” too, is a word deceptively single of surface. Still, following this question for thirty years has given me pleasure, and some sense of approaching more nearly a destination whose center cannot ever be mapped or reached. [...]

A mysterious quickening inhabits the depths of any good poem—protean, elusive, alive in its own right…. We feel something stir, shiver, swim its way into the world when a good poem opens its eyes. Poetry’s work is not simply the recording of inner or outer perception; it makes by words and music new possibilities of perceiving…. The eyes and ears must learn to abandon the habits of useful serving and take up instead a participatory delight in their own ends. A work of art is not a piece of fruit lifted from a tree branch: it is a ripening collaboration of artist, receiver, and world. […]

All writers recognize this surge of striking; in its energies the objects of the world are made new, alchemized by their passage through the imaginal, musical, world-foraging and word-forging mind.

This altered vision is the secret happiness of poems, of poets. It is as if the poem encounters the world and finds in it a hidden language, a Braille unreadable except when raised by the awakened imaginative mind. [...]


Poetry itself, when allowed to, becomes within us a playable organ of perception, sounding out its own forms of knowledge and forms of discovery. Poems do not simply express. They make, they find, they sound (in both meanings of that word) things undiscoverable by other means. […]

A poem is not the outer event or phenomenon it ostensibly describes, nor is it the feeling or insight it may seem to reveal or evoke. A poem may involve both, but is, more complexly, a living fabrication of new comprehension — “fabrication” meaning, not accidentally, both “lie,” “falsehood,” and, more simply and fundamentally, anything created and made: the bringing of something freshly into being. Fabric, whether of material or mind, is an interwoven invention: some substance — silk or cotton, wool or image — made stronger, larger than itself, by the dual-natured meeting of warp thread and weft thread. A work of art holds our lives as they are known when fully engaged with the multiple, crossing experience-strands of self, language, culture, emotion, senses, and mind. [...]

The desire of monks and mystics is not unlike that of artists: to perceive the extraordinary within the ordinary by changing not the world but the eyes that look… To form the intention of new awareness is already to transform and be transformed. [...]

A poem plucks the interconnection of the experiencing self and all being. In poetry’s words, life calls to life with the same inevitability and gladness that bird calls to bird, whale to whale, frog to frog. Listening across the night or ocean or pond, they recognize one another and are warmed by that knowledge."
(Jane Hirshfield,  Ten Windows: How Great Poems Transform the World, 2015)

E1.7.D.6.33. DAVID WHYTE born 1955
"The Journey

Above the mountains
the geese turn into
the light again

Painting their
black silhouettes
on an open sky.

Sometimes everything
has to be
enscribed across
the heavens

so you can find
the one line
already written
inside you.

Sometimes it takes
a great sky
to find that

small, bright
and indescribable
wedge of freedom
in your own heart.

Sometimes with
the bones of the black
sticks left when the fire
has gone out

someone has written
something new
in the ashes of your life.

You are not leaving
you are arriving."
(David Whyte)"

E1.7.D.6.34. VALÉRIE ROUZEAU born 1967

E1.7.A.3.3_REF6_PO.wE1.7.D.6.34.

E1.7.A.3.3_REF6.1_PO.wE1.7.D.6.34.

E1.7.A.3.3_REF6.1.a_PO.wE1.7.D.6.34.

E1.7.D.6.34_REF4_PO “To say it all in an Alexandrian verse
It will be within my range of my vocal range
You know the loneliness when you turn the pages 
I thus have come to the following conclusion 
I see on the horizon the tall trees rising 
The books sleeping on their shelves, the child who remains
Does not take his eyes off the distant pine trees 
How so sad it would be to look at one's own feet."
(Valérie Rouzeau, gleaned at the Marché de la poésie in Paris in 2018 - Valérie Rouzeau says that only the second verse is hers)


"UN JOUR JE DIRAI TOUT DANS UN ALEXANDRIN
Ce sera dans mes cordes dans mes vocales cordes
Tu sais la solitude quand tu tournes les pages 
J'en suis arrivée à la conclusion suivante 
Je vois sur l'horizon les grands arbres qui montent 
Les livres qui dorment sur leurs étagères 
L'enfant qui reste ne quitte pas les sapins des yeux 
Comme ce serait dommage de regarder ses pieds.”
(Valérie Rouzeau, glanés sur le Marché de la poésie à Paris en 2018 - Valérie Rouzeau dit que seul le deuxième vers est d’elle)

E1.7.D.6.35. TRISTAN GARCIA born 1981
"Forgetting the self doesn't make me truer, but happier".

E1.7.D.6.35_REF1_PH TRISTAN GARCIA SUGGESTS THAT WISDOM, WHETHER WESTERN OR EASTERN, IS AN ILLUSION THAT ATTEMPTS TO HARMONIZE WHO WE ARE WITH THAT PART OF THE WORLD WHICH WE ARE ABLE TO COMPREHEND. "Wisdom reveals to me, by comprehending things, that the world is equal. Then it teaches me that I must remain equal to the image of this flattened world. Since everything is equal, you are also equal, you can be neither more nor less, your character can be equal, you can seek out the flat world, and you can draw a single line on this plane and fold yourself over it. Wisdom articulates being and comprehension; it is necessary to be in the world in the image of what one comprehends of it.

All (Eastern and Western) wisdom is the illusion that it would be possible to bring what I am into accord with what I know. By some sleight of hand, wisdom attributes a unique and absolute meaning to what I comprehend of the world in me and to what I am myself in this world.

The world is equal, and because it is so it doesn’t matter whether I am equal to it or not. Will the sage be more and better in the world than the one who has lived without wisdom? Since sages shape themselves in the image of the world, in the image of what really is, do they think that they will be more, that they will be better than the one who has comprehended nothing?
The consciousness of truth never makes something more true."
(Tristan Garcia, Form and Object: A Treatise on things, 2011)

E1.7.D.6.36. MARIA POPOVA born 1984

when the constellation of starlings
flickers across the evening sky,
it is     enough

to stand here
for an irrevocable minute
     agape with wonder.

It is     eternity."

E1.7.D.6.36_REF1_PO "BUT WE HAD MUSIC

Right this minute
across time zones and opinions people are
making plans
making meals
making promises and poems

while

at the center of our galaxy
a black hole with the mass of four billion suns
screams its open-mouth kiss
     of oblivion.

Someday it will swallow
Euclid’s postulates and the Goldberg Variations,
swallow calculus and Leaves of Grass.

I know this.

And still
when the constellation of starlings
flickers across the evening sky,
it is     enough

to stand here
for an irrevocable minute
     agape with wonder.

It is     eternity."
(Maria Popova)

E1.7.D.6.37. ANNIE MURPHY PAUL born 1986
"In the space that is made available [when we contemplate nature], currently active thoughts can mingle with the deep stores of memories, emotions, and ideas already present in the brain, generating inspired collisions."

E1.7.D.6.37_REF1_SCI NATURAL SCENES ACTIVATE THE BRAIN'S "DEFAULT MODE NETWORK," ALLOWING FOR A RELAXED, ASSOCIATIVE STATE THAT FOSTERS UNEXPECTED CONNECTIONS AND INSIGHTS. This state, combined with the positive emotions nature evokes, leads to expansive and open-minded thinking, enabling the mingling of active thoughts with deep memories and ideas, thus generating inspiration.
"Scientists theorize that the “soft fascination” evoked by natural scenes engages what’s known as the brain’s “default mode network.” When this network is activated, we enter a loose associative state in which we’re not focused on any one particular task but are receptive to unexpected connections and insights. In nature, few decisions and choices are demanded of us, granting our minds the freedom to follow our thoughts wherever they lead. At the same time, nature is pleasantly diverting, in a fashion that lifts our mood without occupying all our mental powers; such positive emotion in turn leads us to think more expansively and open-mindedly. In the space that is thus made available, currently active thoughts can mingle with the deep stores of memories, emotions, and ideas already present in the brain, generating inspired collisions."
 (Annie Murphy Paul, The Extended Mind: The Power of Thinking Outside the Brain, 2021)

E1.7.D.6.38. ELLA FRANCES SANDERS
"A SENSE OF WONDER CAN FIND YOU IN MANY FORMS, sometimes loudly, sometimes as a whispering, sometimes even hiding inside other feelings — being in love, or unbalanced, or blue."

E1.7.C.1.2.b_REF1_LIT.wE1.7.D.6.38. "A SENSE OF WONDER CAN FIND YOU IN MANY FORMS, sometimes loudly, sometimes as a whispering, sometimes even hiding inside other feelings — being in love, or unbalanced, or blue.
For me, it is looking at the night for so long that my eyes ache and I’m stuck seeing stars for hours afterwards, watching the way the ocean sways itself to sleep, or as the sky washes itself in colors for which I know I will never have the words — a world made from layers of rock and fossil and glittered imaginings that keeps tripping me up, demanding I pay attention to one leaf at a time, ensuring I can never pick up quite where I left off. [...]
When one is considering the universe, unseen matter, our small backyard of the stuff, I think it is important, sensible even, to try and find some balance between laughter and uncontrollable weeping.
Cry because we cannot even begin to understand how beautiful it is, cry because we are terribly flawed as a species, cry because it all seems so shockingly improbable that maybe our existence could be nothing but a dreamscape — celestial elephants in rooms without walls. But then? Surely, we can laugh.
Laugh because being riddled head-to-toe with human emotions while trying to come to terms with just how indisputably tiny we are in the grand scheme of things, makes absolutely everything and everyone seem quite ridiculous, entirely farcical. We have heads? Ridiculous! There are arguments about who is in charge here? Ridiculous! The universe is expanding? Ridiculous! We feel it necessary to keep secrets? Ridiculous. [...]
Depending on where you look, what you touch, you are changing all the time. The carbon inside you, accounting for about 18 percent of your being, could have existed in any number of creatures or natural disasters before finding you. That particular atom residing somewhere above your left eyebrow? It could well have been a smooth, riverbed pebble before deciding to call you home.
You see, you are not so soft after all; you are rock and wave and the peeling bark of trees, you are ladybirds and the smell of a garden after the rain. When you put your best foot forward, you are taking the north side of a mountain with you. [...]
The idea of an unchanging “you” or “self” is inherently fraught with confusion and conflict, and if you consider the topic for too long it can begin to feel clammy, almost suspect. An apparent string running through all the previous versions of you — the one five minutes ago, a few hours ago, several years — the idea of “self” inevitably gets tangled up in things like the physical body and appearance, like memory. It’s clear that you cannot pin yourself down as any one particular “thing” but rather that you resemble a story line, an endless progression, variations on a theme, something that enables you to relate your present “self” to the past and future ones. [...]
We do seem to make sense of ourselves and the world as a part of a narrative — we think in terms of main characters, those we speak and interact with, and where the beginnings, the middles, and the endings are. [...]
A lot of our time is spent trying to tie up loose ends, trying to shape disorder into something recognizably smooth, trying to escape the very limits that hold us close, happily ignoring rough edges and the inevitable. We separate ourselves out into past, present, and future, if only to show that we have changed, that we know better, that we have understood something inherent; if only to draw neat lines from start to finish without looking back.
The problem is that chaos is always only ever sitting just across the table, frequently glancing up from its newspaper, from its coffee cup filled with discolored and imploding stars. Because chaos too waits. Waits for you to notice it, for you to realize it’s the most dazzling thing you’ve ever seen, for all of your atoms to collectively shriek in belated recognition and stare, mouth open, at how exquisitely embedded it is in everything. Because we are not designed to be more orderly than anything else; seams have a tendency to come apart with time — you and the universe are the same in this way, which makes for a delicately overwhelming struggle.
So, then, if you can’t ever end things neatly, can’t ever put them back quite the way you found them, surely the alternative is to remain stubbornly carbonated with possibility, to never rest from your rotation. To keep assembling stories between us, stories about how everything was everything, about how much we loved."
(Ella Frances Sanders,  Eating the Sun: Small Musings on a Vast Universe)

E1.7.D.6.39. JOHN KOENIG
Uncommonly lovely invented words for what we feel but cannot name. John Koenig “get to work redefining the world around us, until our language more closely matches the reality we experience.”

E1.7.D.6.39._REF1_LIT “IN LANGUAGE, ALL THINGS ARE POSSIBLE. Which means that no emotion is untranslatable. No sorrow is too obscure to define. We just have to do it. […]
Despite what dictionaries would have us believe, this world is still mostly undefined. […]"
(John Koenig, The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows, 2021)

E1.7.A.2_REF5.b_LIT.wE1.7.D.6.39. WORDS CAN CAPTURE THE FRAGILITY AND BEAUTY OF EXISTENCE, EMPHASIZING OUR VULNERABILITY AND LIMITED CONTROL OVER LIFE'S CIRCUMSTANCES. “There are words that anchor us in both the smallness and the grandeur of existence, its fierce fragility, its devastating beauty; words tasked with holding the hardest truth — that we are children of chance, born of a billion bright improbabilities that prevailed over the infinitely greater odds of nonexistence, living with only marginal and mostly illusory control over the circumstances of our lives and other people’s choices, forever vulnerable to the accidents of a universe insentient to our hopes.”
(Maria Popova's review of John Koenig's The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows, 2021 - in The Marginalian)

E1.7.D.6.40. ANDRÉE CHEDID
“I CAN'T HELP BUT BE AMAZED
At the lapping of the water
The chirping of the birds
The joys of the earth
I can't help but be amazed
At an invincible love
Always present

I can't help but be amazed
At this ardent love
That fears neither
The torrent of time
Nor the carnage
Of the days accrued

In my mirror
Faded
I still smile at myself
I remain amazed
Despite everything
Love has taken root
Once
And for all
I can't help but be amazed at this ardent love.”

(Andrée Chedid)

“Je reste émerveillée
Du clapotis de l’eau
Des oiseaux gazouilleurs
Ces bonheurs de la terre
Je reste émerveillée
D’un amour
Invincible
Toujours présent

Je reste émerveillée
De cet amour
Ardent
Qui ne craint
Ni le torrent du temps
Ni l’hécatombe
Des jours accumulés

Dans mon miroir
Défraîchi
Je me souris encore
Je reste émerveillée
Rien n’y fait
L’amour s’est implanté
Une fois 
Pour toutes.
De cet amour ardent je reste émerveillée.”
